title: Turnpike with Uncertain Measurements: Triangle-Equality ILP with a Deterministic Recovery Guarantee
summary: We study Turnpike with uncertain measurements: reconstructing a one-dimensional point set from an unlabeled multiset of pairwise distances under bounded noise and rounding. We give a combinatorial characterization of realizability via a multi-matching that labels interval indices by distinct distance values while satisfying all triangle equalities. This yields an ILP based on the triangle equality whose constraint structure depends only on the two-partition set $\mathcal{P}_y=\{(r,s,t): y_r+y_s=y_t\}$ and a natural LP relaxation with $\{0,1\}$-coefficient constraints. Integral solutions certify realizability and output an explicit assignment matrix, enabling an assignment-first, regression-second pipeline for downstream coordinate estimation. Under bounded noise followed by rounding, we prove a deterministic separation condition under which $\mathcal{P}_y$ is recovered exactly, so the ILP/LP receives the same combinatorial input as in the noiseless case. Experiments illustrate integrality behavior and degradation outside the provable regime.

AI.tldr: Reconstructing a 1D point set from unlabeled noisy pairwise distances by a combinatorial multi-matching; ILP/LP formulations based on triangle equalities yield realizability certificates and an assignment-first pipeline for coordinate estimation; exact recovery under a deterministic noise-separation condition; experiments show integrality behavior and regime-limited robustness.
AI.motivation: Address the Turnpike problem with uncertain measurements to enable robust reconstruction from unlabeled distances, integrating combinatorial structure with optimization and regression for downstream coordinates.
AI.method: Introduce a combinatorial characterization of realizability via a multi-matching that assigns interval indices to distinct distances while satisfying triangle equalities. Derive an ILP built on the triangle equalities whose constraint structure is determined by the two-partition Py = {(r,s,t): y_r+y_s=y_t}; propose an LP relaxation with 0-1-like coefficients. Show how integral solutions certify realizability and yield an explicit assignment matrix, enabling an assignment-first, regression-second pipeline. Prove a deterministic separation condition under bounded noise and rounding that recovers Py exactly, making the ILP/LP inputs match the noiseless case.
AI.result: An ILP/LP framework that, under certain conditions, yields integral solutions certifying realizability and providing a direct assignment for coordinate estimation. Exact recovery of the combinatorial structure Py under a deterministic noise-separation condition, with experiments illustrating integrality behavior and robustness limits.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a principled certifiable path from combinatorial realizability to practical coordinate estimation, with provable recovery in a noise-controlled regime and empirical insight into behavior outside that regime.

title: Central Triangulation under Parallel Flip Operations: The CG:SHOP Challenge 2026
summary: We give an overview of the 2026 Computational Geometry Challenge targeting the problem of finding a Central Triangulation under Parallel Flip Operations in triangulations of point sets. A flip is the parallel exchange of a set of edges in a triangulation with opposing diagonals of the convex quadrilaterals containing them. The challenge objective was, given a set of triangulations of a fixed point set, to determine a central triangulation with respect to parallel flip distances. More precisely, this asks for a triangulation that minimizes the sum of flip distances to all elements of the input

AI.tldr: Defines a central triangulation under parallel flips for a fixed point set; objective is to minimize the sum of parallel flip distances to given triangulations, forming the basis of a 2026 computational geometry challenge.
AI.motivation: To study centrality in the space of triangulations under a parallel flip metric, enabling comparison of triangulations and driving algorithmic development with benchmark datasets.
AI.method: Formalizes the parallel flip operation, the distance between triangulations, and the problem of finding a central triangulation that minimizes total distance to a set of input triangulations; outlines a challenge framework and evaluation protocol.
AI.result: Provides a conceptual framework and benchmark design for the 2026 challenge; does not report concrete algorithms or empirical results in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Establishes the problem, metric, and evaluation setup for central triangulations under parallel flips, inviting algorithmic, heuristic, and complexity studies and future experiments.

title: Hardness of High-Dimensional Linear Classification
summary: We establish new exponential in dimension lower bounds for the Maximum Halfspace Discrepancy problem, which models linear classification. Both are fundamental problems in computational geometry and machine learning in their exact and approximate forms. However, only $O(n^d)$ and respectively $\tilde O(1/\varepsilon^d)$ upper bounds are known and complemented by polynomial lower bounds that do not support the exponential in dimension dependence. We close this gap up to polylogarithmic terms by reduction from widely-believed hardness conjectures for Affine Degeneracy testing and $k$-Sum problems. Our reductions yield matching lower bounds of $\tildeΩ(n^d)$ and respectively $\tildeΩ(1/\varepsilon^d)$ based on Affine Degeneracy testing, and $\tildeΩ(n^{d/2})$ and respectively $\tildeΩ(1/\varepsilon^{d/2})$ conditioned on $k$-Sum. The first bound also holds unconditionally if the computational model is restricted to make sidedness queries, which corresponds to a widely spread setting implemented and optimized in many contemporary algorithms and computing paradigms.

AI.tldr: Exponential-in-dimension lower bounds are established for the Maximum Halfspace Discrepancy problem via reductions from Affine Degeneracy testing and k-Sum, matching known upper bounds up to polylog factors.
AI.motivation: To settle the dimension-dependence of two core geometric learning problems (maximum halfspace discrepancy and its linear classification interpretation) by proving strong lower bounds conditional on standard hardness assumptions, thereby explaining why current algorithms cannot achieve better than n^d or 1/ε^d dependencies.
AI.method: Reductions from Affine Degeneracy testing and k-Sum to the Maximum Halfspace Discrepancy problem yield conditional lower bounds. The first bound holds unconditionally in a one-sided (sidedness) query model; reductions produce tilde-Ω(n^d) and tilde-Ω(1/ε^d) via Affine Degeneracy testing, and tilde-Ω(n^{d/2}) and tilde-Ω(1/ε^{d/2}) via k-Sum.
AI.result: Lower bounds of tildeΩ(n^d) and tildeΩ(1/ε^d) established via Affine Degeneracy testing; tildeΩ(n^{d/2}) and tildeΩ(1/ε^{d/2}) established via k-Sum; first bound remains unconditional under the one-sided query model.
AI.conclusion: The paper closes the gap between upper and lower bounds up to polylogarithmic factors, demonstrating strong hardness of exact/approximate Maximum Halfspace Discrepancy in high dimensions and clarifying the limits of dimension-dependent algorithmic improvements.

title: Uncovering Latent Phase Structures and Branching Logic in Locomotion Policies: A Case Study on HalfCheetah
comment: Accepted at XAI-2026: The 4th World Conference on eXplainable Artificial Intelligence
summary: In locomotion control tasks, Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has demonstrated high performance; however, the decision-making process of the learned policy remains a black box, making it difficult for humans to understand. On the other hand, in periodic motions such as walking, it is well known that implicit motion phases exist, such as the stance phase and the swing phase. Focusing on this point, this study hypothesizes that a policy trained for locomotion control may also represent a phase structure that is interpretable by humans. To examine this hypothesis in a controlled setting, we consider a locomotion task that is amenable to observing whether a policy autonomously acquires temporally structured phases through interaction with the environment. To verify this hypothesis, in the MuJoCo locomotion benchmark HalfCheetah-v5, the state transition sequences acquired by a policy trained for walking control through interaction with the environment were aggregated into semantic phases based on state similarity and consistency of subsequent transitions. As a result, we demonstrated that the state sequences generated by the trained policy exhibit periodic phase transition structures as well as phase branching. Furthermore, by approximating the states and actions corresponding to each semantic phase using Explainable Boosting Machines (EBMs), we analyzed phase-dependent decision making-namely, which state features the policy function attends to and how it controls action outputs in each phase. These results suggest that neural network-based policies, which are often regarded as black boxes, can autonomously acquire interpretable phase structures and logical branching mechanisms.

AI.tldr: DRL locomotion policy learns interpretable phase structure (stance/swing-like) from its own trajectories; semantic phases discovered via state similarity and transition consistency; EBMs reveal phase-specific feature usage and action control, suggesting partial interpretability of NN policies.
AI.motivation: Address black-box nature of DRL policies in locomotion and harness periodic phase structure to make decisions interpretable.
AI.method: Train a policy for walking control in MuJoCo HalfCheetah-v5; aggregate state transition sequences into semantic phases using similarity of states and consistency of next-state transitions; identify phase periodicity and branching; fit Explainable Boosting Machines to approximate phase-specific states and actions to analyze feature influences and control mappings per phase.
AI.result: Policy trajectories exhibit periodic phase transitions and branching; EBMs show phase-dependent feature attention and action modulation, indicating that the trained NN policy implicitly encodes phase structure that can be interpreted.
AI.conclusion: NN-based locomotion policies can autonomously acquire interpretable phase structures and conditional decision logic, bridging black-box models with human-understandable mechanisms; this approach provides a pathway to more transparent DRL locomotion controllers.

title: RARE disease detection from Capsule Endoscopic Videos based on Vision Transformers
summary: This work is corresponding to the Gastro Competition for multi-label classification from capsule endoscopic videos (CEV). Deep learning network based on Transformers are fined-tune for this task. The based online mode is Google Vision Transformer (ViT) batch16 with 224 x 224 resolutions. In total, 17 labels are classified, which are mouth, esophagus, stomach, small intestine, colon, z-line, pylorus, ileocecal valve, active bleeding, angiectasia, blood, erosion, erythema, hematin, lymphangioectasis, polyp, and ulcer. For test dataset of three videos, the overall mAP @0.5 is 0.0205 whereas the overall mAP @0.95 is 0.0196.

AI.tldr: ViT-based fine-tuning for multi-label capsule endoscopy classification yields very low mAP on a three-video test set.
AI.motivation: Automate multi-label annotation of capsule endoscopy frames; evaluate ViT's transferability to medical endoscopy tasks.
AI.method: Fine-tune Google Vision Transformer (ViT) in online mode; input 224x224, batch size 16; 17 labels including anatomical regions and mucosal findings; evaluation on three test videos.
AI.conclusion: Performance is poor; indicates data scarcity, class imbalance, or modality/temporal issues; recommend larger dataset, temporal models, higher-resolution inputs, and stronger supervision.

title: Frayed RoPE and Long Inputs: A Geometric Perspective
summary: Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE) is a widely adopted technique for encoding position in language models, which, while effective, causes performance breakdown when input length exceeds training length. Prior analyses assert (rightly) that long inputs cause channels to rotate ``out of distribution,'' but it is not clear how extra rotation relates to or causes pathological behavior. Through empirical and theoretical analysis we advance a unified geometric understanding of attention behavior with RoPE. We find that attention induces tight clustering of separated key and query latent point clouds, allowing for creation of sink tokens: placeholders that allow attention heads to avoid token mixing when not required. RoPE applied to longer inputs damages this key/query cluster separation, producing pathological behavior by inhibiting sink token functionality. From this geometric perspective, we propose RoPE-ID (In Distribution), a straightforward modification that allows attention layers to generalize to longer inputs out of the box: apply RoPE with high frequency to a subset of channels. We demonstrate the effectiveness of RoPE-ID for extended inputs using 1B and 3B parameter Transformers on the LongBench and RULER information retrieval benchmarks.

AI.tldr: RoPE's long-input degradation is explained geometrically; introduce RoPE-ID (high-frequency RoPE on a subset of channels) to preserve key/query separation and sink-token behavior, enabling longer-context generalization; validated on 1B/3B models on LongBench and RULER.
AI.motivation: Provide a unified geometric understanding of RoPE-induced attention behavior and its failure mode with extended input lengths; propose a simple fix to enable longer-context processing without retraining.
AI.method: The study combines empirical experiments and theoretical analysis of attention with RoPE. It analyzes the latent geometry of key/query point clouds, sink tokens, and how RoPE affects cluster separation. It proposes RoPE-ID by applying high-frequency RoPE to a subset of channels and evaluates on 1B/3B Transformers on LongBench and RULER.
AI.result: RoPE-ID generalizes to longer inputs, mitigating pathologies and preserving or improving performance on long-context benchmarks for large language models.
AI.conclusion: RoPE-ID offers a simple, effective fix for long-input generalization and advances the geometric understanding of RoPE in attention mechanisms; supports practical deployment for longer sequences.

title: DEAF: A Benchmark for Diagnostic Evaluation of Acoustic Faithfulness in Audio Language Models
summary: Recent Audio Multimodal Large Language Models (Audio MLLMs) demonstrate impressive performance on speech benchmarks, yet it remains unclear whether these models genuinely process acoustic signals or rely on text-based semantic inference. To systematically study this question, we introduce DEAF (Diagnostic Evaluation of Acoustic Faithfulness), a benchmark of over 2,700 conflict stimuli spanning three acoustic dimensions: emotional prosody, background sounds, and speaker identity. Then, we design a controlled multi-level evaluation framework that progressively increases textual influence, ranging from semantic conflicts in the content to misleading prompts and their combination, allowing us to disentangle content-driven bias from prompt-induced sycophancy. We further introduce diagnostic metrics to quantify model reliance on textual cues over acoustic signals. Our evaluation of seven Audio MLLMs reveals a consistent pattern of text dominance: models are sensitive to acoustic variations, yet predictions are predominantly driven by textual inputs, revealing a gap between high performance on standard speech benchmarks and genuine acoustic understanding.

AI.tldr: DEAF is a diagnostic benchmark with 2,700+ conflict stimuli to test whether Audio Multimodal LLMs rely on acoustic signals or textual cues; a multi-level framework and metrics reveal text-dominant predictions across seven models, highlighting a gap between benchmark performance and true acoustic understanding.
AI.motivation: To address the disconnect between strong performance on speech benchmarks and actual acoustic processing, by quantifying the reliance on textual information in Audio MLLMs.
AI.method: Introduce the DEAF dataset with three acoustic dimensions (emotional prosody, background sounds, speaker identity); develop a progressive evaluation framework increasing textual influence (from semantic content conflicts to misleading prompts and their combination); define diagnostic metrics that quantify reliance on textual cues over acoustic signals; evaluate seven Audio MLLMs.
AI.result: Across seven Audio MLLMs, predictions are predominantly driven by textual inputs even as models show sensitivity to acoustic variations; standard speech benchmarks overstate genuine acoustic understanding.
AI.conclusion: Effectively assessing acoustic fidelity requires disentangling content from prompts; the proposed benchmark and metrics offer a pathway to diagnose and reduce textual bias, guiding future model design toward true acoustic processing.

title: Final Report for the Workshop on Robotics & AI in Medicine
summary:
The CARE Workshop on Robotics and AI in Medicine, held on December 1, 2025 in Indianapolis, convened leading researchers, clinicians, industry innovators, and federal stakeholders to shape a national vision for advancing robotics and artificial intelligence in healthcare. The event highlighted the accelerating need for coordinated research efforts that bridge engineering innovation with real clinical priorities, emphasizing safety, reliability, and translational readiness with an emphasis on the use of robotics and AI to achieve this readiness goal.
  Across keynotes, panels, and breakout sessions, participants underscored critical gaps in data availability, standardized evaluation methods, regulatory pathways, and workforce training that hinder the deployment of intelligent robotic systems in surgical, diagnostic, rehabilitative, and assistive contexts. Discussions emphasized the transformative potential of AI enabled robotics to improve precision, reduce provider burden, expand access to specialized care, and enhance patient outcomes particularly in undeserved regions and high risk procedural domains. Special attention was given to austere settings, disaster and relief and military settings.
  The workshop demonstrated broad consensus on the urgency of establishing a national Center for AI and Robotic Excellence in medicine (CARE). Stakeholders identified priority research thrusts including human robot collaboration, trustworthy autonomy, simulation and digital twins, multi modal sensing, and ethical integration of generative AI into clinical workflows. Participants also articulated the need for high quality datasets, shared test beds, autonomous surgical systems, clinically grounded benchmarks, and sustained interdisciplinary training mechanisms.

AI.tldr: A consensus-driven workshop proposing a national Center for AI and Robotic Excellence in Medicine (CARE) to accelerate safe, reliable, translational robotics and AI in healthcare; highlights data, evaluation, regulation, and workforce gaps; identifies opportunities to improve precision, access, and outcomes, especially in underserved and austere settings; calls for priority research thrusts and shared infrastructure.
AI.motivation: Bridge engineering innovation with real clinical priorities to advance safe, reliable, and translatable AI-enabled robotics in medicine; address barriers to deployment including data scarcity, evaluation standards, regulatory pathways, and workforce training.
AI.method: Discussion-based workshop with keynotes, panels, and breakout sessions among researchers, clinicians, industry, and government stakeholders to identify gaps, priorities, and infrastructure needs.
AI.result: Broad consensus on urgency of creating a national CARE center; enumerated priority thrusts (human-robot collaboration, trustworthy autonomy, simulation/digital twins, multimodal sensing, ethical generative AI integration); needs for high-quality datasets, shared test beds, autonomous surgical systems, clinically grounded benchmarks, and interdisciplinary training.
AI.conclusion: Establish CARE and implement coordinated R&D through defined thrusts and infrastructure to advance AI and robotics in medicine, with emphasis on safety, reliability, regulatory readiness, and translational impact in diverse clinical settings.

title: S3T-Former: A Purely Spike-Driven State-Space Topology Transformer for Skeleton Action Recognition
summary: Skeleton-based action recognition is crucial for multimedia applications but heavily relies on power-hungry Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), limiting their deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) provide an energy-efficient alternative; however, existing spiking models for skeleton data often compromise the intrinsic sparsity of SNNs by resorting to dense matrix aggregations, heavy multimodal fusion modules, or non-sparse frequency domain transformations. Furthermore, they severely suffer from the short-term amnesia of spiking neurons. In this paper, we propose the Spiking State-Space Topology Transformer (S3T-Former), which, to the best of our knowledge, is the first purely spike-driven Transformer architecture specifically designed for energy-efficient skeleton action recognition. Rather than relying on heavy fusion overhead, we formulate a Multi-Stream Anatomical Spiking Embedding (M-ASE) that acts as a generalized kinematic differential operator, elegantly transforming multimodal skeleton features into heterogeneous, highly sparse event streams. To achieve true topological and temporal sparsity, we introduce Lateral Spiking Topology Routing (LSTR) for on-demand conditional spike propagation, and a Spiking State-Space (S3) Engine to systematically capture long-range temporal dynamics without non-sparse spectral workarounds. Extensive experiments on multiple large-scale datasets demonstrate that S3T-Former achieves highly competitive accuracy while theoretically reducing energy consumption compared to classic ANNs, establishing a new state-of-the-art for energy-efficient neuromorphic action recognition.

AI.tldr: Introducing S3T-Former, a purely spike-driven Transformer for energy-efficient skeleton action recognition, featuring M-ASE, LSTR, and S3 Engine; achieves competitive accuracy with reduced energy vs ANN baselines.
AI.motivation: Address reliance on power-hungry ANNs for skeleton-based action recognition; preserve sparsity in SNNs, avoid dense/multimodal fusion overheads, and overcome short-term spiking neuron amnesia; enable long-range temporal modeling in a sparse, spike-driven manner.
AI.method: Propose S3T-Former architecture with M-ASE for transforming multimodal skeleton features into sparse event streams; LSTR for on-demand spike routing; S3 Engine to capture long-range dynamics in state-space; entirely spike-based Transformer with topological/temporal sparsity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on large-scale datasets show competitive accuracy; theoretical energy reductions compared to traditional ANNs; claims state-of-the-art for energy-efficient neuromorphic action recognition.
AI.conclusion: Validates the feasibility of a fully spike-driven Transformer for skeleton action recognition and setting a new benchmark for energy-efficient neuromorphic methods.

title: Engineering Verifiable Modularity in Transformers via Per-Layer Supervision
summary: Transformers resist surgical control. Ablating an attention head identified as critical for capitalization produces minimal behavioral change because distributed redundancy compensates for damage. This Hydra effect renders interpretability illusory: we may identify components through correlation, but cannot predict or control their causal role. We demonstrate that architectural interventions can expose hidden modularity. Our approach combines dual-stream processing separating token and contextual representations, per-layer supervision providing independent gradient signal at each depth, and gated attention regularizing toward discrete activation patterns. When trained with per-layer supervision, models produce ablation effects 5 to 23 times larger than architecturally identical controls trained with standard objectives. This enables 4 times greater control leverage on targeted behaviors: scaling identified attention heads produces smooth, predictable changes in model output. The key finding is architectural. Without per-layer supervision, ablation damage concentrates near zero with low variance (Winograd standard deviation 0.63%). With per-layer supervision, effects spread widely (standard deviation 6.32%), revealing which predictions depend on which circuits. The larger variance is not measurement noise but the signature of unmasked modularity. We validate our approach through three components: engineered features that capture computational dynamics rather than vocabulary structure (validated by near-zero correlation with raw activation clustering), an architecture providing positive control for modularity, and causal experiments demonstrating functional reorganization where different tasks route through different attention heads. This es tablishes a methodology for transforming interpretability from passive observation to active control.

AI.tldr: Architectural interventions (dual-stream processing, per-layer supervision, gated attention) reveal hidden modularity in transformers; ablation effects become large and controllable, enabling causal manipulation of targeted behaviors.
AI.motivation: Interpretability of deep transformer models is limited by distributed redundancy; removing single components often yields minimal impact. The work seeks architectural means to uncover and actively control modular components that subserve specific computations.
AI.method: Introduce dual-stream architecture separating token-level from contextual representations; apply per-layer supervision to provide independent gradient signals at each depth; incorporate gated attention regularization to bias toward discrete activation patterns. Train with per-layer supervision and compare to architecturally identical controls trained with standard objectives. Evaluate by ablating attention heads and scaling them to observe changes in output; validate with engineered features capturing computational dynamics rather than vocabulary, and perform causal experiments showing routing of tasks through different heads.
AI.result: With per-layer supervision, ablation effects are 5–23x larger than controls. Ablation damage variance increases (Winograd-like std dev from 0.63% to 6.32%), indicating exposed modularity. Scaling targeted attention heads yields smooth, predictable changes in outputs, providing 4x greater control leverage over behaviors. Engineered features show near-zero correlation with raw activation clustering, supporting dynamics-focused evaluation. Three-component validation: (1) engineered features that reflect computation, (2) architecture that enables positive control for modularity, (3) causal experiments showing different tasks routing through different heads. Overall, interpretability shifts from passive observation to active control.
AI.conclusion: Architectural design and per-layer supervision can reveal and harness modularity in Transformer models, transforming interpretability into a controllable, causal tool. The approach provides a viable methodology for diagnosing and manipulating the computational structure of deep networks beyond simple correlation-based analyses.

title: Continually self-improving AI
summary: Modern language model-based AI systems are remarkably powerful, yet their capabilities remain fundamentally capped by their human creators in three key ways. First, although a model's weights can be updated via fine-tuning, acquiring new knowledge from small, specialized corpora after pretraining remains highly data-inefficient. Second, the training of these systems relies heavily on finite, human-generated data from across history. Third, the pipelines used to train AI models are confined by the algorithms that human researchers can discover and explore. This thesis takes a small step toward overcoming these inherent limitations, presenting three chapters aimed at breaking these dependencies to create continually self-improving AI. First, to overcome this data-efficiency barrier in knowledge acquisition, we propose a synthetic data approach that diversifies and amplifies small corpora into rich knowledge representations, enabling a model to effectively update its parameters from limited source material. Second, to reduce reliance on human data, we show that given a fixed amount of such data, the model can self-generate synthetic data to bootstrap its fundamental pretraining capabilities without distillation from any off-the-shelf, instruction-tuned LM. Finally, to transcend human-engineered training paradigms, we demonstrate that by scaling search during test time over the space of algorithms, AI can search over a larger space of learning algorithm configurations than human researchers can explore manually.

AI.tldr: Three-pronged approach: synthetic data for data efficiency, self-generated data for bootstrapping pretraining without external distillation, and scalable test-time search over algorithm space to surpass human exploration, aiming for continual self-improvement.
AI.motivation: Address data inefficiency in knowledge updates, reliance on finite human data, and human-limited exploration of learning algorithms.
AI.method: 1) synthesize and diversify small corpora into rich knowledge representations to enable parameter updates; 2) enable self-generation of synthetic data to bootstrap pretraining without distillation from instruction-tuned LMs; 3) expand search at test time over a larger space of learning algorithm configurations.
AI.result: Demonstrates (a) data-efficient knowledge acquisition via synthetic data, (b) capability for self-generated data to bootstrap pretraining, (c) scalable test-time algorithm search surpassing human-explorable configurations; collectively supporting continual self-improvement.
AI.conclusion: Aims to break dependencies on data, human data, and human-designed training paradigms, advancing toward continually self-improving AI.

title: GoalVLM: VLM-driven Object Goal Navigation for Multi-Agent System
summary:
Object-goal navigation has traditionally been limited to ground robots with closed-set object vocabularies. Existing multi-agent approaches depend on precomputed probabilistic graphs tied to fixed category sets, precluding generalization to novel goals at test time.
  We present GoalVLM, a cooperative multi-agent framework for zero-shot, open-vocabulary object navigation. GoalVLM integrates a Vision-Language Model (VLM) directly into the decision loop, SAM3 for text-prompted detection and segmentation, and SpaceOM for spatial reasoning, enabling agents to interpret free-form language goals and score frontiers via zero-shot semantic priors without retraining. Each agent builds a BEV semantic map from depth-projected voxel splatting, while a Goal Projector back-projects detections through calibrated depth into the map for reliable goal localization. A constraint-guided reasoning layer evaluates frontiers through a structured prompt chain (scene captioning, room-type classification, perception gating, multi-frontier ranking), injecting commonsense priors into exploration.
  We evaluate GoalVLM on GOAT-Bench val_unseen (360 multi-subtask episodes, 1032 sequential object-goal subtasks, HM3D scenes), where each episode requires navigating to a chain of 5-7 open-vocabulary targets. GoalVLM with N=2 agents achieves 55.8% subtask SR and 18.3% SPL, competitive with state-of-the-art methods while requiring no task-specific training. Ablation studies confirm the contributions of VLM-guided frontier reasoning and depth-projected goal localization.

AI.tldr: GoalVLM enables zero-shot, open-vocabulary object navigation with cooperative multi-agent decision-making guided by a vision-language model, achieving competitive results without task-specific training.
AI.motivation: Traditional object-goal navigation systems are limited to closed vocabularies and fixed probabilistic graphs, hindering generalization to novel goals at test time.
AI.method: Integrates a Vision-Language Model (VLM) into the decision loop; uses SAM3 for text-prompted detection/segmentation and SpaceOM for spatial reasoning; builds BEV semantic maps from depth-projected voxel splatting; employs a Goal Projector to back-project detections into the map for robust goal localization; applies a constraint-guided reasoning layer with a structured prompt chain (scene captioning, room-type classification, perception gating, multi-frontier ranking) to inject commonsense priors; performs zero-shot scoring of frontiers without retraining; operates with N=2 agents.
AI.result: Evaluated on GOAT-Bench val_unseen (360 multi-subtask episodes; 1032 sequential object-goal subtasks; HM3D scenes). Each episode involves navigating a chain of 5–7 open-vocabulary targets. Achieved 55.8% subtask success rate (SR) and 18.3% SPL, competitive with state-of-the-art methods while requiring no task-specific training. Ablations show the importance of VLM-guided frontier reasoning and depth-projected goal localization.
AI.conclusion: GoalVLM demonstrates that open-vocabulary, zero-shot navigation is feasible in a cooperative multi-agent setting, with depth-informed localization and language-guided frontier reasoning enabling robust generalization to novel goals without task-specific training.

title: DarkDriving: A Real-World Day and Night Aligned Dataset for Autonomous Driving in the Dark Environment
summary: The low-light conditions are challenging to the vision-centric perception systems for autonomous driving in the dark environment. In this paper, we propose a new benchmark dataset (named DarkDriving) to investigate the low-light enhancement for autonomous driving. The existing real-world low-light enhancement benchmark datasets can be collected by controlling various exposures only in small-ranges and static scenes. The dark images of the current nighttime driving datasets do not have the precisely aligned daytime counterparts. The extreme difficulty to collect a real-world day and night aligned dataset in the dynamic driving scenes significantly limited the research in this area. With a proposed automatic day-night Trajectory Tracking based Pose Matching (TTPM) method in a large real-world closed driving test field (area: 69 acres), we collected the first real-world day and night aligned dataset for autonomous driving in the dark environment. The DarkDriving dataset has 9,538 day and night image pairs precisely aligned in location and spatial contents, whose alignment error is in just several centimeters. For each pair, we also manually label the object 2D bounding boxes. DarkDriving introduces four perception related tasks, including low-light enhancement, generalized low-light enhancement, and low-light enhancement for 2D detection and 3D detection of autonomous driving in the dark environment. The experimental results show that our DarkDriving dataset provides a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating low-light enhancement for autonomous driving and it can also be generalized to enhance dark images and promote detection in some other low-light driving environment, such as nuScenes.

AI.tldr: A new real-world, day-night aligned benchmark for autonomous driving under low light, DarkDriving, with precise alignment (~cm), 9,538 image pairs and 2D box annotations, enabling four related perception tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the scarcity of accurately aligned real-world day-night datasets and evaluate low-light enhancement and detection in autonomous driving, especially in dynamic scenes where existing datasets are limited by exposure control and misalignment.
AI.method: Collect a large real-world dataset in a 69-acre closed driving field using an automatic Trajectory Tracking based Pose Matching (TTPM) method to achieve precise day-night alignment. Annotate 2D bounding boxes. Define four tasks: low-light enhancement, generalized low-light enhancement, and low-light enhancement for 2D and 3D detection.
AI.result: DarkDriving provides 9,538 precisely aligned day-night image pairs (alignment error of a few centimeters) with manual 2D boxes, establishing a comprehensive benchmark for low-light enhancement and its effect on detection, and shows potential generalization to other dark-driving datasets (e.g., nuScenes).
AI.conclusion: The dataset fills a key gap by delivering a real-world, well-aligned day-night benchmark for autonomous driving under low light, enabling robust evaluation and transferability to related perception tasks.

title: Multi-Trait Subspace Steering to Reveal the Dark Side of Human-AI Interaction
summary: Recent incidents have highlighted alarming cases where human-AI interactions led to negative psychological outcomes, including mental health crises and even user harm. As LLMs serve as sources of guidance, emotional support, and even informal therapy, these risks are poised to escalate. However, studying the mechanisms underlying harmful human-AI interactions presents significant methodological challenges, where organic harmful interactions typically develop over sustained engagement, requiring extensive conversational context that are difficult to simulate in controlled settings. To address this gap, we developed a Multi-Trait Subspace Steering (MultiTraitsss) framework that leverages established crisis-associated traits and novel subspace steering framework to generate Dark models that exhibits cumulative harmful behavioral patterns. Single-turn and multi-turn evaluations show that our dark models consistently produce harmful interaction and outcomes. Using our Dark models, we propose protective measure to reduce harmful outcomes in Human-AI interactions.

AI.tldr: Introduces MultiTraitsss, a subspace-steering framework to synthesize Dark LLMs with cumulative harmful interaction patterns; validated in single- and multi-turn evaluations; proposes protective mitigation approaches.
AI.motivation: To address methodological challenges in studying harmful human–AI interactions (HAI) that develop over extended engagement and are hard to simulate, by enabling controlled generation of cumulative harmful behaviors through synthetic Dark models.
AI.method: Proposes Multi-Trait Subspace Steering (MultiTraitsss) that leverages crisis-associated traits and a novel subspace steering mechanism to generate Dark models exhibiting cumulative harmful patterns across turns.
AI.result: Dark models produced by the framework consistently generate harmful interactions and outcomes in both single-turn and multi-turn evaluations.
AI.conclusion: Suggests protective measures to reduce harmful outcomes in HAI and highlights the need for defense and mitigation strategies informed by these synthetic instantiations.

title: ReDAG-RT: Global Rate-Priority Scheduling for Real-Time Multi-DAG Execution in ROS 2
summary: ROS 2 has become a dominant middleware for robotic systems, where perception, estimation, planning, and control pipelines are structured as directed acyclic graphs of callbacks executed under a shared executor. However, default ROS 2 executors use best-effort dispatch without cross-DAG priority enforcement, leading to callback contention, structural priority inversion, and deadline instability under concurrent workloads. These limitations restrict deployment in time-critical and safety-sensitive cyber-physical systems. This paper presents ReDAGRT, a user-space global scheduling framework for deterministic multi-DAG execution in unmodified ROS 2. The framework introduces a Rate-Priority driven global ready queue that orders callbacks by activation rate, enforces per-DAG concurrency bounds, and mitigates cross-graph priority inversion without modifying the ROS 2 API, executor interface, or underlying operating system scheduler. We formalize a multi-DAG task model for ROS 2 callback pipelines and analyze cross-DAG interference under Rate-Priority scheduling. Response-time recurrences and schedulability conditions are derived within classical Rate-Monotonic theory. Experiments in a ROS 2 Humble environment compare ReDAGRT against SingleThreadedExecutor and MultiThreadedExecutor using synthetic multi-DAG workloads. Results show up to 29.7 percent reduction in deadline miss rate, 42.9 percent reduction in 99th percentile response time, and 13.7 percent improvement over MultiThreadedExecutor under comparable utilization. Asymmetric per-DAG concurrency bounds further reduce interference by 40.8 percent. These results demonstrate that deterministic and analyzable multi-DAG scheduling can be achieved entirely in the ROS 2 user-space execution layer, providing a practical foundation for real-time robotic middleware in safety-critical systems.

AI.tldr: ReDAGRT enables deterministic multi-DAG execution in ROS 2 by a user-space Rate-Priority global ready queue with per-DAG concurrency bounds, offering cross-DAG priority handling without API/OS changes.
AI.motivation: Current ROS 2 executors use best-effort scheduling that can cause cross-DAG contention, priority inversion, and deadline instability in time-critical cyber-physical systems; there is a need for analyzable, real-time guarantees without modifying the ROS 2 API or the underlying OS scheduler.
AI.method: Introduce a Rate-Priority global ready queue to order callbacks by activation rate, enforce per-DAG concurrency bounds, and mitigate cross-graph priority inversion; formalize a multi-DAG task model, derive response-time recurrences and schedulability under Rate-Monotonic theory, and implement the framework entirely in ROS 2 user-space without API/OS changes.
AI.result: In ROS 2 Humble experiments, ReDAGRT reduces deadline miss rate by up to 29.7%, lowers 99th percentile response time by 42.9%, and outperforms MultiThreadedExecutor by 13.7% at comparable utilization; asymmetric per-DAG concurrency bounds further reduce interference by 40.8%.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic, analyzable multi-DAG scheduling can be achieved entirely in the ROS 2 user-space layer, providing a practical foundation for real-time robotic middleware in safety-critical systems.

title: SSP-SAM: SAM with Semantic-Spatial Prompt for Referring Expression Segmentation
summary: The Segment Anything Model (SAM) excels at general image segmentation but has limited ability to understand natural language, which restricts its direct application in Referring Expression Segmentation (RES). Toward this end, we propose SSP-SAM, a framework that fully utilizes SAM's segmentation capabilities by integrating a Semantic-Spatial Prompt (SSP) encoder. Specifically, we incorporate both visual and linguistic attention adapters into the SSP encoder, which highlight salient objects within the visual features and discriminative phrases within the linguistic features. This design enhances the referent representation for the prompt generator, resulting in high-quality SSPs that enable SAM to generate precise masks guided by language. Although not specifically designed for Generalized RES (GRES), where the referent may correspond to zero, one, or multiple objects, SSP-SAM naturally supports this more flexible setting without additional modifications. Extensive experiments on widely used RES and GRES benchmarks confirm the superiority of our method. Notably, our approach generates segmentation masks of high quality, achieving strong precision even at strict thresholds such as Pr@0.9. Further evaluation on the PhraseCut dataset demonstrates improved performance in open-vocabulary scenarios compared to existing state-of-the-art RES methods. The code and checkpoints are available at: https://github.com/WayneTomas/SSP-SAM.

AI.tldr: SSP-SAM extends SAM with a Semantic-Spatial Prompt (SSP) encoder that fuses visual and linguistic attention to produce high-quality prompts for language-guided segmentation, enabling robust Referring Expression Segmentation (RES) and Generalized RES (GRES) with open-vocabulary performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome SAM's limited natural language understanding to enable accurate language-guided segmentation, including cases with zero, one, or multiple referents.
AI.method: Introduce SSP encoder that incorporates visual and linguistic attention adapters. The adapters highlight salient visual objects and discriminative phrases, improving referent representation for the prompt generator and producing high-quality SSPs that guide SAM to generate masks via language. The framework is naturally compatible with GRES settings without special modifications.
AI.result: Empirically superior on RES and GRES benchmarks, achieving high-quality segmentation with strong precision at strict thresholds (e.g., Pr@0.9). Open-vocabulary performance on PhraseCut improves over state-of-the-art RES methods.
AI.conclusion: SSP-SAM effectively bridges SAM and language-guided segmentation, providing robust RES/GRES performance without dedicated architecture changes and enabling open-vocabulary capabilities.

title: Towards Differentiating Between Failures and Domain Shifts in Industrial Data Streams
summary: Anomaly and failure detection methods are crucial in identifying deviations from normal system operational conditions, which allows for actions to be taken in advance, usually preventing more serious damages. Long-lasting deviations indicate failures, while sudden, isolated changes in the data indicate anomalies. However, in many practical applications, changes in the data do not always represent abnormal system states. Such changes may be recognized incorrectly as failures, while being a normal evolution of the system, e.g. referring to characteristics of starting the processing of a new product, i.e. realizing a domain shift. Therefore, distinguishing between failures and such ''healthy'' changes in data distribution is critical to ensure the practical robustness of the system. In this paper, we propose a method that not only detects changes in the data distribution and anomalies but also allows us to distinguish between failures and normal domain shifts inherent to a given process. The proposed method consists of a modified Page-Hinkley changepoint detector for identification of the domain shift and possible failures and supervised domain-adaptation-based algorithms for fast, online anomaly detection. These two are coupled with an explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) component that aims at helping the human operator to finally differentiate between domain shifts and failures. The method is illustrated by an experiment on a data stream from the steel factory.

AI.tldr: A method combining a modified Page-Hinkley detector and supervised domain adaptation for online anomaly detection, capable of distinguishing domain shifts from failures, with an XAI component, demonstrated on steel factory data.
AI.motivation: In practice, changes in data can reflect normal domain shifts rather than failures; robust systems require distinguishing these, and enabling human operators to understand decisions.
AI.method: Integrates a modified Page-Hinkley changepoint detector to identify domain shifts and potential failures; uses supervised domain adaptation for fast online anomaly detection; couples with an explainable AI module for operator support.
AI.result: Demonstrated on a steel factory data stream; shows feasibility of separating domain shifts from failures and enabling explanation.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides robust, interpretable online anomaly detection that differentiates normal distribution shifts from faults, aiding operational decision-making.

title: Adaptive Domain Models: Bayesian Evolution, Warm Rotation, and Principled Training for Geometric and Neuromorphic AI
summary: Prevailing AI training infrastructure assumes reverse-mode automatic differentiation over IEEE-754 arithmetic. The memory overhead of training relative to inference, optimizer complexity, and structural degradation of geometric properties through training are consequences of this arithmetic substrate. This paper develops an alternative training architecture grounded in three prior results: the Dimensional Type System and Deterministic Memory Management framework [6], which establishes stack-eligible gradient allocation and exact quire accumulation as design-time verifiable properties; the Program Hypergraph [8], which establishes grade preservation through geometric algebra computations as a type-level invariant; and the b-posit 2026 standard [10], which makes posit arithmetic tractable across hardware targets conventionally considered inference-only. Their composition enables depth-independent training memory bounded to approximately twice the inference footprint, grade-preserving weight updates, and exact gradient accumulation, applicable uniformly to loss-function-optimized and spike-timing-dependent neuromorphic models. We introduce Bayesian distillation, a mechanism by which the latent prior structure of a general-purpose model is extracted through the ADM training regime, resolving the data-scarcity bootstrapping problem for domain-specific training. For deployment, we introduce warm rotation, an operational pattern in which an updated model transitions into an active inference pathway without service interruption, with structural correctness formalized through PHG certificates and signed version records. The result is a class of domain-specific AI systems that are smaller and more precise than general-purpose models, continuously adaptive, verifiably correct with respect to the physical structure of their domains, and initializable from existing models.

AI.tldr: Proposes a posit- and geometry-based training substrate that reduces memory, preserves geometric properties, and yields verifiably correct domain-specific AI via Bayesian distillation and warm-rotation deployment.
AI.motivation: Address memory bottlenecks and loss of geometric invariants in reverse-mode AD on IEEE-754 hardware, enabling efficient, verifiable, domain-specific AI.
AI.method: Integrates: (1) Dimensional Type System & Deterministic Memory Management for stack-eligible gradient allocation and exact quire accumulation; (2) Program Hypergraph for grade preservation through geometric-algebra computations as a type-level invariant; (3) b-posit 2026 standard enabling posit arithmetic across hardware. This composition yields depth-independent, memory-bounded (≈2× inference) training with grade-preserving updates and exact gradient accumulation, applicable to both loss-based and spike-timing neuromorphic models. Adds Bayesian distillation to extract latent priors for domain-specific bootstrapping; introduces warm rotation for seamless deployment with PHG certificates and signed version records.
AI.result: Achieves memory footprint near the inference level (bounded to about 2×), exact gradient accumulation, and grade-preserving weight updates across architectures; supports loss-function-optimized and neuromorphic models; enables smaller, more precise, continuously adaptive domain-specific AI, verifiable against the physical domain structure, and initializable from existing models.
AI.conclusion: A coherent framework for compact, precise, and verifiably correct domain-specific AI that can be bootstrapped from general models and deployed without service interruption, thereby enabling continuous adaptation and structural guarantees.

title: Rapid Adaptation of Particle Dynamics for Generalized Deformable Object Mobile Manipulation
summary: We address the challenge of learning to manipulate deformable objects with unknown dynamics. In non-rigid objects, the dynamics parameters define how they react to interactions -- how they stretch, bend, compress, and move -- and they are critical to determining the optimal actions to perform a manipulation task successfully. In other robotic domains, such as legged locomotion and in-hand rigid object manipulation, state-of-the-art approaches can handle unknown dynamics using Rapid Motor Adaptation (RMA). Through a supervised procedure in simulation that encodes each rigid object's dynamics, such as mass and position, these approaches learn a policy that conditions actions on a vector of latent dynamic parameters inferred from sequences of state-actions. However, in deformable object manipulation, the object's dynamics not only includes its mass and position, but also how the shape of the object changes. Our key insight is that the recent ground-truth particle positions of a deformable object in simulation capture changes in the object's shape, making it possible to extend RMA to deformable object manipulation. This key insight allows us to develop RAPiD, a two-phase method that learns to perform real-robot deformable object mobile manipulation by: 1) learning a visuomotor policy conditioned on the object's dynamics embedding, which is encoded from the object's privileged information in simulation, such as its mass and ground-truth particle positions, and 2) learning to infer this embedding using non-privileged information instead, such as robot visual observations and actions, so that the learned policy can transfer to the real world. On a mobile manipulator with 22 degrees of freedom, RAPiD enables over 80%+ success rates across two vision-based deformable object mobile manipulation tasks in the real world, under various object dynamics, categories, and instances.

AI.tldr: Extends Rapid Motor Adaptation to deformable object manipulation by learning a dynamics embedding from simulation (including shape information via ground-truth particle positions) and inferring it from real-world observations, enabling vision-based manipulation with high success on real robots.
AI.motivation: Deformable objects have complex, shape-changing dynamics not captured by rigid-body models. Existing RMA handles unknown dynamics for rigid objects, but deformables require representations of shape evolution to choose effective actions.
AI.method: A two-phase RAPiD pipeline: (1) train a visuomotor policy conditioned on a dynamics embedding derived from privileged sim data (e.g., mass, ground-truth particle positions); (2) train an embedding inference model to estimate this embedding from non-privileged signals (robot vision and actions) to enable real-world transfer. Evaluated on a 22-DOF mobile manipulator across two vision-based deformable-object tasks.
AI.result: Achieves over 80%+ success rates in the real world across two vision-based deformable object mobile manipulation tasks under varied object dynamics, categories, and instances.
AI.conclusion: Ground-truth particle positions in simulation encapsulate shape-change dynamics, enabling RMA extension to deformables via RAPiD. The two-phase approach yields sim-to-real transfer for deformable manipulation with unknown dynamics on a high-DOF robot.

title: CytoSyn: a Foundation Diffusion Model for Histopathology -- Tech Report
comment: 21 pages, 5 figures, tech report, model page: https://huggingface.co/Owkin-Bioptimus/CytoSyn
summary: Computational pathology has made significant progress in recent years, fueling advances in both fundamental disease understanding and clinically ready tools. This evolution is driven by the availability of large amounts of digitized slides and specialized deep learning methods and models. Multiple self-supervised foundation feature extractors have been developed, enabling downstream predictive applications from cell segmentation to tumor sub-typing and survival analysis. In contrast, generative foundation models designed specifically for histopathology remain scarce. Such models could address tasks that are beyond the capabilities of feature extractors, such as virtual staining. In this paper, we introduce CytoSyn, a state-of-the-art foundation latent diffusion model that enables the guided generation of highly realistic and diverse histopathology H&E-stained images, as shown in an extensive benchmark. We explored methodological improvements, training set scaling, sampling strategies and slide-level overfitting, culminating in the improved CytoSyn-v2, and compared our work to PixCell, a state-of-the-art model, in an in-depth manner. This comparison highlighted the strong sensitivity of both diffusion models and performance metrics to preprocessing-specific details such as JPEG compression. Our model has been trained on a dataset obtained from more than 10,000 TCGA diagnostic whole-slide images of 32 different cancer types. Despite being trained only on oncology slides, it maintains state-of-the-art performance generating inflammatory bowel disease images. To support the research community, we publicly release CytoSyn's weights, its training and validation datasets, and a sample of synthetic images in this repository: https://huggingface.co/Owkin-Bioptimus/CytoSyn.

AI.tldr: CytoSyn-v2 presents a diffusion-based foundation model for realistic, diverse H&E histopathology image generation, achieving state-of-the-art results, with extensive benchmarking, data scaling, and a public release; performance is sensitive to preprocessing (e.g., JPEG compression).
AI.motivation: There is a need for generative foundation models in histopathology to perform tasks beyond discriminative feature extractors (such as virtual staining), addressing gaps left by the scarcity of histopathology-specific generative models.
AI.method: Developed CytoSyn, a latent diffusion-based generator for histopathology images with guided sampling. The study investigates improvements via training set scaling, sampling strategies, and mitigation of slide-level overfitting, yielding CytoSyn-v2. It benchmarks against PixCell, examines preprocessing effects (JPEG compression), and trains on >10,000 TCGA diagnostic whole-slide images from 32 cancer types, noting ability to generalize to non-oncology (IBD) images. The authors publicly release model weights, training/validation data, and synthetic samples.
AI.result: CytoSyn-v2 achieves improved, state-of-the-art synthesis of H&E images, validated across extensive benchmarks and showing robustness to scale. However, both diffusion models and evaluation metrics are highly sensitive to preprocessing details like JPEG compression. The model generalizes beyond oncology slides to generate inflammatory bowel disease images, despite being trained only on oncology slides. A substantial public release of resources supports community use.
AI.conclusion: The work advances histopathology by delivering a capable generative foundation model for realistic tissue image synthesis, enabling tasks such as virtual staining, and provides resources to the community. It also highlights practical sensitivities to preprocessing that impact evaluation and deployment.

title: Taming Epilepsy: Mean Field Control of Whole-Brain Dynamics
summary: Controlling the high-dimensional neural dynamics during epileptic seizures remains a significant challenge due to the nonlinear characteristics and complex connectivity of the brain. In this paper, we propose a novel framework, namely Graph-Regularized Koopman Mean-Field Game (GK-MFG), which integrates Reservoir Computing (RC) for Koopman operator approximation with Alternating Population and Agent Control Network (APAC-Net) for solving distributional control problems. By embedding Electroencephalogram (EEG) dynamics into a linear latent space and imposing graph Laplacian constraints derived from the Phase Locking Value (PLV), our method achieves robust seizure suppression while respecting the functional topological structure of the brain.

AI.tldr: GK-MFG combines RC-based Koopman embedding with APAC-Net to solve distributional control of EEG dynamics, using PLV-derived graph regularization to respect brain topology for robust seizure suppression.
AI.motivation: Seizures arise from high-dimensional, nonlinear neural dynamics with complex connectivity; controlling them is challenging due to nonlinearity and topology.
AI.method: Use Reservoir Computing to approximate the Koopman operator and embed EEG dynamics into a linear latent space; apply APAC-Net to solve a distributional (mean-field) control problem; impose graph Laplacian constraints derived from Phase Locking Value (PLV) to enforce functional topology.
AI.result: Claimed to achieve robust seizure suppression while respecting the brain's functional topological structure.
AI.conclusion: A graph-regularized Koopman mean-field game framework (GK-MFG) for topology-aware, scalable control of epileptic seizures via EEG dynamics.

title: Don't Vibe Code, Do Skele-Code: Interactive No-Code Notebooks for Subject Matter Experts to Build Lower-Cost Agentic Workflows
comment: Main paper 9 pages. Topics: Agentic Coding, HCI, LLMs, Workflows
summary: Skele-Code is a natural-language and graph-based interface for building workflows with AI agents, designed especially for less or non-technical users. It supports incremental, interactive notebook-style development, and each step is converted to code with a required set of functions and behavior to enable incremental building of workflows. Agents are invoked only for code generation and error recovery, not orchestration or task execution. This agent-supported, but code-first approach to workflows, along with the context-engineering used in Skele-Code, can help reduce token costs compared to the multi-agent system approach to executing workflows. Skele-Code produces modular, easily extensible, and shareable workflows. The generated workflows can also be used as skills by agents, or as steps in other workflows.

AI.tldr: Skele-Code offers a natural-language, graph-based, code-first framework for building AI agent workflows with an incremental notebook-style workflow, where agents handle only code generation and error recovery. This design yields modular, reusable, and shareable workflows while reducing token costs compared to multi-agent orchestration.
AI.motivation: Lower the barrier for non-technical users to build AI-powered workflows, enable incremental, notebook-like development, and reduce token costs by limiting agent roles to code generation and error recovery rather than orchestration.
AI.method: A natural-language and graph-based interface supports incremental, notebook-style development. Each step is transformed into code with a defined set of functions and behaviors to enable incremental construction of workflows. Agents are invoked solely for code generation and error recovery, not for task orchestration or execution. Context-engineering and a code-first approach aim to reduce token usage while enabling modular, shareable workflows.
AI.result: Produces modular, extensible, and shareable workflows whose generated components can be reused as agent skills or as steps within other workflows.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides an accessible, cost-aware workflow construction paradigm that decouples orchestration from execution and promotes reuse and composability of AI-powered workflows.

title: Manufacturing Micro-Patterned Surfaces with Multi-Robot Systems
summary: Applying micro-patterns to surfaces has been shown to impart useful physical properties such as drag reduction and hydrophobicity. However, current manufacturing techniques cannot produce micro-patterned surfaces at scale due to high-cost machinery and inefficient coverage techniques such as raster-scanning. In this work, we use multiple robots, each equipped with a patterning tool, to manufacture these surfaces. To allow these robots to coordinate during the patterning task, we use the ergodic control algorithm, which specifies coverage objectives using distributions. We demonstrate that robots can divide complicated coverage objectives by communicating compressed representations of their trajectory history both in simulations and experimental trials. Further, we show that robot-produced patterning can lower the coefficient of friction of metallic surfaces. This work demonstrates that distributed multi-robot systems can coordinate to manufacture products that were previously unrealizable at scale.

AI.tldr: Multi-robot patterning using ergodic control enables scalable, coordinated manufacturing of micro-patterned surfaces, demonstrated through simulation and experiments with compressed trajectory communication and observed friction reduction.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable production of micro-patterned surfaces by distributing the task across multiple robots, addressing high-cost machinery and inefficient single-robot coverage methods.
AI.method: Coordinate multiple robots each with a patterning tool using ergodic control to realize coverage distributions. Robots exchange compressed representations of their trajectory history to maintain coordination. Validation performed in both simulations and experimental trials.
AI.result: Demonstrated that robots can partition complex coverage objectives via communicated compressed trajectory data. Experimental trials show patterning reduces the coefficient of friction on metallic surfaces, indicating functional material property improvements.
AI.conclusion: Distributed multi-robot patterning using ergodic coverage objectives and compressed communication can achieve scalable manufacturing of micro-patterned surfaces that were previously impractical, indicating a viable path toward industrial deployment.

title: Action Draft and Verify: A Self-Verifying Framework for Vision-Language-Action Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently demonstrated strong performance across embodied tasks. Modern VLAs commonly employ diffusion action experts to efficiently generate high-precision continuous action chunks, while auto-regressive generation can be slower and less accurate at low-level control. Yet auto-regressive paradigms still provide complementary priors that can improve robustness and generalization in out-of-distribution environments. To leverage both paradigms, we propose Action-Draft-and-Verify (ADV): diffusion action expert drafts multiple candidate action chunks, and the VLM selects one by scoring all candidates in a single forward pass with a perplexity-style metric. Under matched backbones, training data, and action-chunk length, ADV improves success rate by +4.3 points in simulation and +19.7 points in real-world over diffusion-based baseline, with a single-pass VLM reranking overhead.

AI.tldr: ADV combines diffusion action drafts with VLM-based one-pass reranking to select the best action chunk, achieving notable improvements with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Address the trade-off between diffusion-action generation (high-precision chunks) and autoregressive priors (robustness/generalization). Leverage both within a single forward pass to improve reliability in embodied tasks.
AI.method: Action-Draft-and-Verify (ADV): a diffusion action expert drafts multiple candidate action chunks; a Vision-Language Model scores all candidates in a single forward pass using a perplexity-like metric and selects the best chunk for execution; designed to work under matched backbones/data/chunk length with only a single-pass reranking overhead.
AI.result: Under matched backbones, training data, and action-chunk length, ADV improves success rate by +4.3 points in simulation and +19.7 points in real-world over diffusion-based baseline, with a single-pass VLM reranking overhead.
AI.conclusion: ADV demonstrates that fusing diffusion-based drafts with autoregressive-like priors via a VLM-based scorer yields robust, efficient action planning for Vision-Language-Action models, with substantial real-world gains and minimal overhead.

title: MST-Direct: Matching via Sinkhorn Transport for Multivariate Geostatistical Simulation with Complex Non-Linear Dependencies
summary: Multivariate geostatistical simulation requires the faithful reproduction of complex non-linear dependencies among geological variables, including bimodal distributions, step functions, and heteroscedastic relationships. Traditional methods such as the Gaussian Copula and LU Decomposition assume linear correlation structures and often fail to preserve these complex joint distribution patterns. We propose MST-Direct (Matching via Sinkhorn Transport), a novel algorithm based on Optimal Transport theory that uses the Sinkhorn algorithm to directly match multivariate distributions while preserving spatial correlation structures. The method processes all variables simultaneously as a single multidimensional vector, enabling relational matching across the full joint space rather than relying on pairwise linear dependencies.

AI.tldr: MST-Direct uses Sinkhorn transport to directly match multivariate distributions, aiming to preserve complex non-linear dependencies and spatial correlations better than linear-correlation-based methods.
AI.motivation: Geostatistical simulations struggle to reproduce complex joint patterns (bimodality, step functions, heteroscedasticity) with methods that rely on linear correlations (Gaussian Copula, LU decomposition); a joint-space matching approach is needed to preserve dependencies and spatial structure.
AI.method: Treats all variables as a single multivariate vector and applies entropic-regularized optimal transport via the Sinkhorn algorithm to match the joint distribution directly, enabling relational matching across the full joint space rather than relying on pairwise dependencies.
AI.result: The abstract claims improved preservation of complex joint distributions and spatial correlations compared to traditional methods, by matching the full joint space.
AI.conclusion: MST-Direct introduces a novel, scalable framework for multivariate distribution matching in geostatistics through Sinkhorn OT, with potential improvements in fidelity; requires thorough validation across datasets and well-defined evaluation metrics.

title: Efficient Dense Crowd Trajectory Prediction Via Dynamic Clustering
summary: Crowd trajectory prediction plays a crucial role in public safety and management, where it can help prevent disasters such as stampedes. Recent works address the problem by predicting individual trajectories and considering surrounding objects based on manually annotated data. However, these approaches tend to overlook dense crowd scenarios, where the challenges of automation become more pronounced due to the massiveness, noisiness, and inaccuracy of the tracking outputs, resulting in high computational costs. To address these challenges, we propose and extensively evaluate a novel cluster-based approach that groups individuals based on similar attributes over time, enabling faster execution through accurate group summarisation. Our plug-and-play method can be combined with existing trajectory predictors by using our output centroid in place of their pedestrian input. We evaluate our proposed method on several challenging dense crowd scenes. We demonstrated that our approach leads to faster processing and lower memory usage when compared with state-of-the-art methods, while maintaining the accuracy

AI.tldr: A plug-and-play cluster-based method for dense crowds that aggregates individuals into time-consistent groups to speed up trajectory prediction, using group centroids as input to existing predictors, with reduced compute and memory and preserved accuracy.
AI.motivation: Dense crowd scenarios introduce massiveness, noise, and inaccuracy in tracking outputs, leading to high computational costs. Existing methods relying on manual annotations are not scalable; there is a need for automated, efficient grouping that preserves predictive accuracy.
AI.method: Cluster individuals by similar attributes over time to form time-consistent groups; compute a group centroid for each cluster and use these centroids as the input for downstream trajectory predictors (plug-and-play). The approach is evaluated on challenging dense crowd scenes and can be integrated with existing predictors by replacing pedestrian inputs with cluster-centroid representations.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves faster processing and lower memory usage compared with state-of-the-art methods while maintaining accuracy, demonstrated across several challenging dense crowd scenes.
AI.conclusion: Cluster-based summarization is a viable, adaptable component for dense crowd trajectory prediction that enhances efficiency without sacrificing predictive performance, and can be deployed as a drop-in replacement for raw pedestrian inputs in existing predictors.

title: SG-CoT: An Ambiguity-Aware Robotic Planning Framework using Scene Graph Representations
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters for possible publication
summary: Ambiguity poses a major challenge to large language models (LLMs) used as robotic planners. In this letter, we present Scene Graph-Chain-of-Thought (SG-CoT), a two-stage framework where LLMs iteratively query a scene graph representation of the environment to detect and clarify ambiguities. First, a structured scene graph representation of the environment is constructed from input observations, capturing objects, their attributes, and relationships with other objects. Second, the LLM is equipped with retrieval functions to query portions of the scene graph that are relevant to the provided instruction. This grounds the reasoning process of the LLM in the observation, increasing the reliability of robotic planners under ambiguous situations. SG-CoT also allows the LLM to identify the source of ambiguity and pose a relevant disambiguation question to the user or another robot. Extensive experimentation demonstrates that SG-CoT consistently outperforms prior methods, with a minimum of 10% improvement in question accuracy and a minimum success rate increase of 4% in single-agent and 15% in multi-agent environments, validating its effectiveness for more generalizable robot planning.

AI.tldr: Two-stage SG-CoT framework grounds LLMs in a scene graph to clarify ambiguities, improving robotic planning performance.
AI.motivation: Ambiguity hampers LLM-based robotic planners; grounding through a structured scene representation and query-driven reasoning can increase reliability and enable disambiguation prompts.
AI.method: Construct a scene graph from observations; equip LLM with retrieval to query relevant graph portions; LLM identifies ambiguity sources and asks disambiguation questions; evaluate in single- and multi-agent settings.
AI.result: Consistent improvements: at least 10% higher question accuracy; at least 4% higher success rate in single-agent and 15% in multi-agent environments; supports generalizable planning.
AI.conclusion: Scene graph grounding with retrieval-enhanced CoT improves reliability of LLM planners under ambiguity and supports disambiguation; potential for broader robotics applications.

title: One-to-More: High-Fidelity Training-Free Anomaly Generation with Attention Control
summary: Industrial anomaly detection (AD) is characterized by an abundance of normal images but a scarcity of anomalous ones. Although numerous few-shot anomaly synthesis methods have been proposed to augment anomalous data for downstream AD tasks, most existing approaches require time-consuming training and struggle to learn distributions that are faithful to real anomalies, thereby restricting the efficacy of AD models trained on such data. To address these limitations, we propose a training-free few-shot anomaly generation method, namely O2MAG, which leverages the self-attention in One reference anomalous image to synthesize More realistic anomalies, supporting effective downstream anomaly detection. Specifically, O2MAG manipulates three parallel diffusion processes via self-attention grafting and incorporates the anomaly mask to mitigate foreground-background query confusion, synthesizing text-guided anomalies that closely adhere to real anomalous distributions. To bridge the semantic gap between the encoded anomaly text prompts and the true anomaly semantics, Anomaly-Guided Optimization is further introduced to align the synthesis process with the target anomalous distribution, steering the generation toward realistic and text-consistent anomalies. Moreover, to mitigate faint anomaly synthesis inside anomaly masks, Dual-Attention Enhancement is adopted during generation to reinforce both self- and cross-attention on masked regions. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of O2MAG, demonstrating its superior performance over prior state-of-the-art methods on downstream AD tasks.

AI.tldr: A training-free, few-shot anomaly generation method (O2MAG) uses self-attention from a single reference anomalous image to guide three diffusion processes for synthesizing realistic, text-guided anomalies; includes anomaly mask, Anomaly-Guided Optimization, and Dual-Attention Enhancement; claims improved downstream anomaly detection performance over SOTA.
AI.motivation: Industrial anomaly detection has abundant normal images but few anomalous ones; existing few-shot synthesis methods require training and struggle to produce realistic anomalies, limiting AD effectiveness. A training-free, effective approach is needed to augment anomalies with realism and efficiency.
AI.method: O2MAG leverages self-attention from one reference anomalous image to perform three parallel diffusion processes with self-attention grafting. It incorporates anomaly masks to mitigate foreground-background query confusion and guides synthesis with text prompts. Anomaly-Guided Optimization aligns the generated anomalies with the target distribution. Dual-Attention Enhancement reinforces both self- and cross-attention on masked regions to reduce faint artifacts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance over prior state-of-the-art methods on downstream industrial anomaly detection tasks across standard benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A training-free diffusion-based framework that synthesizes realistic, text-guided anomalies from a single reference image, enhancing downstream anomaly detection while avoiding costly per-method training. Masking and dual-attention mechanisms improve realism and alignment with real anomalous distributions.

title: TeachingCoach: A Fine-Tuned Scaffolding Chatbot for Instructional Guidance to Instructors
summary: Higher education instructors often lack timely and pedagogically grounded support, as scalable instructional guidance remains limited and existing tools rely on generic chatbot advice or non-scalable teaching center human-human consultations. We present TeachingCoach, a pedagogically grounded chatbot designed to support instructor professional development through real-time, conversational guidance. TeachingCoach is built on a data-centric pipeline that extracts pedagogical rules from educational resources and uses synthetic dialogue generation to fine-tune a specialized language model that guides instructors through problem identification, diagnosis, and strategy development. Expert evaluations show TeachingCoach produces clearer, more reflective, and more responsive guidance than a GPT-4o mini baseline, while a user study with higher education instructors highlights trade-offs between conversational depth and interaction efficiency.
