Together, these results demonstrate that pedagogically grounded, synthetic data driven chatbots can improve instructional support and offer a scalable design approach for future instructional chatbot systems.

AI.tldr: Pedagogically grounded chatbot TeachingCoach improves instructional support by extracting pedagogical rules from resources and using synthetic data to fine-tune a specialized model for real-time guidance; expert evaluations show improvements over a GPT-4o mini baseline with trade-offs in depth vs efficiency.
AI.motivation: Instructors in higher education often lack timely, pedagogically grounded guidance; scalable instructional support is limited, and existing tools rely on generic chatbots or non-scalable human consultations.
AI.method: A data-centric pipeline that (1) extracts pedagogical rules from educational resources, (2) generates synthetic dialogue to fine-tune a specialized language model, and (3) guides instructors through problem identification, diagnosis, and strategy development in real time.
AI.result: Expert evaluations indicate TeachingCoach yields clearer, more reflective, and more responsive guidance than a GPT-4o mini baseline. A user study with higher education instructors reveals trade-offs between conversational depth and interaction efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Pedagogically grounded, synthetic data–driven chatbots can improve instructional support and provide a scalable design approach for future instructional chatbot systems.

title: Offload or Overload: A Platform Measurement Study of Mobile Robotic Manipulation Workloads
summary: Mobile robotic manipulation--the ability of robots to navigate spaces and interact with objects--is a core capability of physical AI. Foundation models have led to breakthroughs in their performance, but at a significant computational cost. We present the first measurement study of mobile robotic manipulation workloads across onboard, edge, and cloud GPU platforms. We find that the full workload stack is infeasible to run on smaller onboard GPUs, while larger onboard GPUs drain robot batteries several hours faster. Offloading alleviates these constraints but introduces its own challenges, as additional network latency degrades task accuracy, and the bandwidth requirement makes naive cloud offloading impractical. Finally, we quantify opportunities and pitfalls of sharing compute across robot fleets. We believe our measurement study will be crucial to designing inference systems for mobile robots.

AI.tldr: A measurement study evaluating mobile robotic manipulation workloads across onboard, edge, and cloud GPUs, revealing feasibility limits for onboard hardware, energy tradeoffs, latency and bandwidth challenges for offloading, and notable prospects and caveats for fleet-wide compute sharing.
AI.motivation: To empirically characterize compute, energy, and network constraints for foundation-model–driven mobile manipulation, guiding hardware choices and inference-system design for real-world deployment.
AI.method: Empirical benchmarking of mobile robotic manipulation workloads on multiple platforms (small and large onboard GPUs, edge GPUs, and cloud GPUs), varying compute placement, measuring latency, accuracy under network delays, battery impact, and fleet-sharing scenarios.
AI.result: Small onboard GPUs cannot run the full workload; larger onboard GPUs increase energy consumption and shorten robot runtime; offloading alleviates local compute but introduces network latency and bandwidth bottlenecks that can degrade accuracy and render cloud offloading impractical; fleet-wide compute sharing presents opportunities but also significant pitfalls that must be managed.
AI.conclusion: The study provides essential benchmarks to inform design choices for mobile-robot inference systems, emphasizing the tradeoffs between local versus remote compute and the need for robust offloading strategies and fleet collaboration protocols.

title: Q-Drift: Quantization-Aware Drift Correction for Diffusion Model Sampling
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is a practical path to deploy large diffusion models, but quantization noise can accumulate over the denoising trajectory and degrade generation quality. We propose Q-Drift, a principled sampler-side correction that treats quantization error as an implicit stochastic perturbation on each denoising step and derives a marginal-distribution-preserving drift adjustment. Q-Drift estimates a timestep-wise variance statistic from calibration, in practice requiring as few as 5 paired full-precision/quantized calibration runs. The resulting sampler correction is plug-and-play with common samplers, diffusion models, and PTQ methods, while incurring negligible overhead at inference. Across six diverse text-to-image models (spanning DiT and U-Net), three samplers (Euler, flow-matching, DPM-Solver++), and two PTQ methods (SVDQuant, MixDQ), Q-Drift improves FID over the corresponding quantized baseline in most settings, with up to 4.59 FID reduction on PixArt-Sigma (SVDQuant W3A4), while preserving CLIP scores.

AI.tldr: Q-Drift provides a sampler-side drift correction for post-training quantization (PTQ) in diffusion models. It treats quantization error as an implicit stochastic perturbation per denoising step and uses a marginal-distribution-preserving drift adjustment estimated from calibration. The method is plug-and-play with common samplers and PTQ techniques, incurring negligible overhead and improving FID while preserving CLIP scores.
AI.motivation: Quantization noise accumulates along the denoising trajectory in diffusion models under PTQ, degrading generation quality. Existing PTQ approaches focus on weights/activations but do not address sampler dynamics; a calibration-based, low-overhead correction at inference can preserve the diffusion process statistics and quality.
AI.method: Formulates a drift adjustment for the sampler by modeling quantization error as a stochastic perturbation at each denoising step and derives a marginal-distribution-preserving correction. Estimates a per-timestep variance from a small calibration set (as few as 5 paired FP/quantized runs) and applies the drift term during sampling. Compatible with Euler, flow-matching, and DPM-Solver++ samplers and with PTQ methods SVDQuant and MixDQ.
AI.result: Empirically, Q-Drift yields improvements in FID over quantized baselines across six text-to-image models (DiT and U-Net), three samplers, and two PTQ methods, with up to 4.59 FID reduction on PixArt-Sigma (SVDQuant W3A4); CLIP scores are preserved and inference overhead is negligible.
AI.conclusion: Q-Drift is a practical, plug-and-play sampler-side correction that mitigates quantization-induced drift in diffusion model sampling under PTQ, enabling higher-quality generation across diverse models, samplers, and PTQ methods with minimal overhead.

title: Quotient Geometry and Persistence-Stable Metrics for Swarm Configurations
summary: Swarm and constellation reconfiguration can be viewed as motion of an unordered point configuration in an ambient space. Here, we provide persistence-stable, symmetry-invariant geometric representations for comparing and monitoring multi-agent configuration data. We introduce a quotient formation space $\mathcal{S}_n(M,G)=M^n/(G\times S_n)$ and a formation matching metric $d_{M,G}$ obtained by optimizing a worst-case assignment error over ambient symmetries $g\in G$ and relabelings $σ\in S_n$. This metric is a structured, physically interpretable relaxation of Gromov--Hausdorff distance: the induced inter-agent metric spaces satisfy $d_{\mathrm{GH}}(X_x,X_y)\le d_{M,G}([x],[y])$. Composing this bound with stability of Vietoris--Rips persistence yields $d_B(Φ_k([x]),Φ_k([y]))\le d_{M,G}([x],[y])$, providing persistence-stable signatures for reconfiguration monitoring. We analyze the metric geometry of $(\mathcal{S}_n(M,G),d_{M,G})$: under compactness/completeness assumptions on $M$ and compact $G$ it is compact/complete and the metric induces the quotient topology; if $M$ is geodesic then the quotient is geodesic and exhibits stratified singularities along collision and symmetry strata, relating it to classical configuration spaces. We study expressivity of the signatures, identifying symmetry-mismatch and persistence-compression mechanisms for non-injectivity. Finally, in a phase-circle model we prove a conditional inverse theorem: under semicircle support and a gap-labeling margin, the $H_0$ signature is locally bi-Lipschitz to $d_{M,G}$ up to an explicit factor, yielding two-sided control. Examples on $\mathbb{S}^2$ and $\mathbb{T}^m$ illustrate satellite-constellation and formation settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces a symmetry-aware quotient formation space for multi-agent configurations and a matching metric d_{M,G} that yields stable, persistence-based signatures, with theory on metric geometry, and a phase-circle inverse theorem; applies to spheres and tori.
AI.motivation: To compare and monitor unordered multi-agent configurations robustly under ambient symmetries and relabelings, providing persistence-stable geometrical descriptors that are interpretable and mathematically grounded, and relate to Gromov–Hausdorff distance and persistent homology.
AI.method: Define S_n(M,G)=M^n/(G×S_n). Construct formation matching metric d_{M,G} by optimizing a worst-case assignment error over ambient symmetries g∈G and relabelings σ∈S_n. Prove d_GH(X_x,X_y)≤d_{M,G}([x],[y]). Use stability of Vietoris–Rips persistence to get d_B(Φ_k([x]),Φ_k([y]))≤d_{M,G}([x],[y]). Analyze metric geometry: under compact/complete M and compact G, the quotient is compact/complete and topology is quotient; if M is geodesic, the quotient is geodesic with stratified singularities along collision/symmetry strata. Examine expressivity: symmetry-mismatch and persistence-compression cause non-injectivity. Prove a conditional inverse theorem in a phase-circle model: with semicircle support and a gap-labeling margin, the H_0 signature is locally bi-Lipschitz to d_{M,G} up to explicit factor. Provide examples on S^2 and T^m (satellite-constellation and formation settings).
AI.result: The metric d_{M,G} acts as a structured, physically interpretable relaxation of the Gromov–Hausdorff distance, providing stability guarantees for processed formation signatures. The quotient topology and geometric properties (compactness/ completeness; geodesicity and stratified singularities) are established; persistence signatures are guaranteed to be stability-bounded; an inverse-type guarantee is demonstrated in a phase-circle model; concrete instantiations on S^2 and T^m illustrate applicability to real-world formation monitoring.
AI.conclusion: The framework yields persistence-stable, symmetry-invariant representations for reconfiguration monitoring with solid theoretical guarantees and clear geometric interpretation, connecting to classical configuration spaces and enabling practical analysis in satellite-constellation and formation scenarios.

title: Access Controlled Website Interaction for Agentic AI with Delegated Critical Tasks
summary: Recent studies reveal gaps in delegating critical tasks to agentic AI that accesses websites on the user's behalf, primarily due to limited access control mechanisms on websites designed for agentic AI. In response, we propose a design of website-based interaction for AI agents with fine-grained access control for delegated critical tasks. Our approach encompasses a website design and implementation, as well as modifications to the access grant protocols in an open-source authorization service to tailor it to agentic AI, with delegated critical tasks on the website. The evaluation of our approach demonstrates the capabilities of our access-controlled website used by AI agents.

AI.tldr: Proposes a website-based interface with fine-grained access control for agentic AI performing delegated critical tasks, using modifications to an open-source authorization service; evaluation demonstrates effective access control in AI-assisted websites.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap that agentic AI delegating critical tasks to websites suffers from limited, coarse-grained access control mechanisms designed for AI agents. Aims to enable safer delegation of high-stakes tasks via a dedicated website design and tailored authorization protocols.
AI.method: Design and implement a website-based interaction for AI agents with fine-grained access control; modify an open-source authorization service's access grant protocols to tailor them for agentic AI; evaluate the access-controlled website in the context of AI agents performing delegated tasks on the site.
AI.result: Demonstrates the capabilities of an access-controlled website that AI agents can use to perform delegated tasks, validating the practicality and effectiveness of the proposed access-control design.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical approach to secure agentic AI delegation through a website and customized authorization workflow, contributing a usable design and implementation pathway for safer AI-human collaboration.

title: Sparse3DTrack: Monocular 3D Object Tracking Using Sparse Supervision
summary: Monocular 3D object tracking aims to estimate temporally consistent 3D object poses across video frames, enabling autonomous agents to reason about scene dynamics. However, existing state-of-the-art approaches are fully supervised and rely on dense 3D annotations over long video sequences, which are expensive to obtain and difficult to scale. In this work, we address this fundamental limitation by proposing the first sparsely supervised framework for monocular 3D object tracking. Our approach decomposes the task into two sequential sub-problems: 2D query matching and 3D geometry estimation. Both components leverage the spatio-temporal consistency of image sequences to augment a sparse set of labeled samples and learn rich 2D and 3D representations of the scene. Leveraging these learned cues, our model automatically generates high-quality 3D pseudolabels across entire videos, effectively transforming sparse supervision into dense 3D track annotations. This enables existing fully-supervised trackers to effectively operate under extreme label sparsity. Extensive experiments on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrate that our method significantly improves tracking performance, achieving an improvement of up to 15.50 p.p. while using at most four ground truth annotations per track.

AI.tldr: First sparsely supervised framework for monocular 3D object tracking that splits the task into 2D query matching and 3D geometry estimation, using spatio-temporal cues to generate dense 3D pseudo-labels from very few annotations and enabling off-the-shelf trackers to operate under sparse supervision.
AI.motivation: Dense 3D annotations for monocular 3D tracking are expensive and hard to scale; existing methods rely on full supervision with long video sequences. A sparse-supervision paradigm could drastically reduce labeling effort while retaining performance.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) 2D query matching to identify corresponding objects across frames, and (2) 3D geometry estimation to recover 3D poses. Both stages exploit spatio-temporal consistency in image sequences to augment a sparse labeled set and learn rich 2D/3D representations. The model then auto-generates high-quality 3D pseudo-labels across whole videos, converting sparse supervision into dense 3D track annotations. This enables fully supervised trackers to operate under extreme label sparsity.
AI.result: On KITTI and nuScenes, the method yields substantial gains, achieving up to 15.50 percentage points improvement in tracking performance with at most four ground-truth annotations per track.
AI.conclusion: Sparse supervision can effectively generate dense 3D track annotations by leveraging learned 2D/3D cues and spatio-temporal consistency, enabling existing trackers to perform well under limited labeling. The approach demonstrates strong empirical gains on major benchmarks with minimal labeling per track.

title: Training-Only Heterogeneous Image-Patch-Text Graph Supervision for Advancing Few-Shot Learning Adapters
comment: Accepted at The IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026
summary: Recent adapter-based CLIP tuning (e.g., Tip-Adapter) is a strong few-shot learner, achieving efficiency by caching support features for fast prototype matching. However, these methods rely on global uni-modal feature vectors, overlooking fine-grained patch relations and their structural alignment with class text. To bridge this gap without incurring inference costs, we introduce a novel asymmetric training-only framework. Instead of altering the lightweight adapter, we construct a high-capacity auxiliary Heterogeneous Graph Teacher that operates solely during training. This teacher (i) integrates multi-scale visual patches and text prompts into a unified graph, (ii) performs deep cross-modal reasoning via a Modality-aware Graph Transformer (MGT), and (iii) applies discriminative node filtering to extract high-fidelity class features. Crucially, we employ a cache-aware dual-objective strategy to supervise this relational knowledge directly into the Tip-Adapter's key-value cache, effectively upgrading the prototypes while the graph teacher is discarded at test time. Thus, inference remains identical to Tip-Adapter with zero extra latency or memory. Across standard 1-16-shot benchmarks, our method consistently establishes a new state-of-the-art. Ablations confirm that the auxiliary graph supervision, text-guided reasoning, and node filtering are the essential ingredients for robust few-shot adaptation. Code is available at https://github.com/MR-Sherif/TOGA.git.

AI.tldr: TOGA introduces a training-only Heterogeneous Graph Teacher to enhance Tip-Adapter by learning cross-modal patch–text relations and transferring this knowledge into the cache, achieving state-of-the-art few-shot CLIP adaptation with no test-time overhead.
AI.motivation: Global uni-modal features used by adapter-based CLIP tuning miss fine-grained patch relations and their alignment with class text; a high-capacity training-time teacher can capture relational knowledge to improve prototypes without increasing inference cost.
AI.method: Construct a Heterogeneous Graph Teacher that fuses multi-scale visual patches and text prompts into a graph; use a Modality-aware Graph Transformer for cross-modal reasoning; apply discriminative node filtering to extract class features; employ a cache-aware dual-objective to supervise the graph’s relational knowledge into Tip-Adapter’s key–value cache; discard the graph at test time to keep latency unchanged.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on standard 1–16-shot benchmarks; ablations confirm the necessity of auxiliary graph supervision, text-guided reasoning, and node filtering; code released at the provided GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: Training-time relational supervision via a graph-based teacher can upgrade CLIP prototypes without increasing test-time cost, demonstrating the effectiveness of cross-modal graph reasoning for robust few-shot adaptation.

title: NANOZK: Layerwise Zero-Knowledge Proofs for Verifiable Large Language Model Inference
comment: 11 pages. Accepted at the VerifAI Workshop at ICLR 2026 (camera-ready version)
summary:
When users query proprietary LLM APIs, they receive outputs with no cryptographic assurance that the claimed model was actually used. Service providers could substitute cheaper models, apply aggressive quantization, or return cached responses - all undetectable by users paying premium prices for frontier capabilities. We present METHOD, a zero-knowledge proof system that makes LLM inference verifiable: users can cryptographically confirm that outputs correspond to the computation of a specific model.
  Our approach exploits the fact that transformer inference naturally decomposes into independent layer computations, enabling a layerwise proof framework where each layer generates a constant-size proof regardless of model width. This decomposition sidesteps the scalability barrier facing monolithic approaches and enables parallel proving. We develop lookup table approximations for non-arithmetic operations (softmax, GELU, LayerNorm) that introduce zero measurable accuracy loss, and introduce Fisher information-guided verification for scenarios where proving all layers is impractical.
  On transformer models up to d=128, METHOD generates constant-size layer proofs of 5.5KB (2.1KB attention + 3.5KB MLP) with 24 ms verification time. Compared to EZKL, METHOD achieves 70x smaller proofs and 5.7x faster proving time at d=128, while maintaining formal soundness guarantees (epsilon < 1e-37). Lookup approximations preserve model perplexity exactly, enabling verification without quality compromise.

AI.tldr: METHOD is a zero-knowledge proof system for verifiable LLM inference that decomposes transformer computation into per-layer proofs, enabling constant-size proofs per layer and fast verification; it uses lookup-table approximations for non-arithmetic ops and Fisher information-guided verification, achieving much smaller proofs and faster proving than EZKL for models up to d=128, with no perceptible accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Users of proprietary LLM APIs cannot cryptographically verify that the service actually used the claimed model; providers could substitute cheaper models or cache results. A verifiable inference mechanism would ensure integrity and provenance of the returned outputs.
AI.method: Transformers are decomposed into independent layer computations; each layer produces a constant-size proof regardless of model width, enabling parallel proving. The approach uses lookup-table approximations for softmax, GELU, and LayerNorm with no measurable accuracy loss, and applies Fisher information-guided verification for cases where proving every layer is impractical.
AI.result: For models up to d=128, METHOD generates constant-size per-layer proofs totaling 5.5 KB (2.1 KB for attention, 3.5 KB for MLP) with 24 ms verification time. It yields ~70x smaller proofs and ~5.7x faster proving than EZKL at d=128, while maintaining formal soundness (epsilon < 1e-37). Lookup-approximation preserves perplexity exactly, enabling verification without quality loss.
AI.conclusion: METHOD delivers scalable, sound zero-knowledge verification for transformer inference, enabling users to cryptographically confirm that outputs derive from a specific model, with substantial reductions in proof size and proving time compared with prior monolithic approaches.

title: A Computationally Efficient Learning of Artificial Intelligence System Reliability Considering Error Propagation
summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) systems are increasingly prominent in emerging smart cities, yet their reliability remains a critical concern. These systems typically operate through a sequence of interconnected functional stages, where upstream errors may propagate to downstream stages, ultimately affecting overall system reliability. Quantifying such error propagation is essential for accurate modeling of AI system reliability. However, this task is challenging due to: i) data availability: real-world AI system reliability data are often scarce and constrained by privacy concerns; ii) model validity: recurring error events across sequential stages are interdependent, violating the independence assumptions of statistical inference; and iii) computational complexity: AI systems process large volumes of high-speed data, resulting in frequent and complex recurrent error events that are difficult to track and analyze. To address these challenges, this paper leverages a physics-based autonomous vehicle simulation platform with a justifiable error injector to generate high-quality data for AI system reliability analysis. Building on this data, a new reliability modeling framework is developed to explicitly characterize error propagation across stages. Model parameters are estimated using a computationally efficient, theoretically guaranteed composite likelihood expectation - maximization algorithm. Its application to the reliability modeling for autonomous vehicle perception systems demonstrates its predictive accuracy and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: A data-generation plus composite likelihood EM framework to model error propagation across stages in AI systems, validated on autonomous vehicle perception.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity, interdependence of sequential errors, and computational complexity in reliability modeling of AI pipelines.
AI.method: 1) Use a physics-based autonomous vehicle simulator with a justifiable error injector to generate data capturing stage-wise errors; 2) Develop a reliability framework that explicitly models cross-stage error propagation; 3) Estimate parameters via a computationally efficient composite likelihood EM algorithm with theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: Demonstrates accurate predictive performance and computational efficiency in perception reliability, with scalable parameter estimation and explicit propagation characterization across stages.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical, scalable approach for quantifying and predicting AI system reliability in multi-stage pipelines, with potential applicability to other domains.

title: Proprioceptive-only State Estimation for Legged Robots with Set-Coverage Measurements of Learned Dynamics
summary: Proprioceptive-only state estimation is attractive for legged robots since it is computationally cheaper and is unaffected by perceptually degraded conditions. The history of joint-level measurements contains rich information that can be used to infer the dynamics of the system and subsequently produce navigational measurements. Recent approaches produce these estimates with learned measurement models and fuse with IMU data, under a Gaussian noise assumption. However, this assumption can easily break down with limited training data and render the estimates inconsistent and potentially divergent. In this work, we propose a proprioceptive-only state estimation framework for legged robots that characterizes the measurement noise using set-coverage statements that do not assume any distribution. We develop a practical and computationally inexpensive method to use these set-coverage measurements with a Gaussian filter in a systematic way. We validate the approach in both simulation and two real-world quadrupedal datasets. Comparison with the Gaussian baselines shows that our proposed method remains consistent and is not prone to drift under real noise scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proprioceptive-only state estimation for legged robots uses distribution-free set-coverage noise models with a Gaussian filter, yielding more robust, drift-resistant estimates than Gaussian-noise-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Gaussian noise assumptions in learned proprioceptive estimators are fragile with limited training data, leading to inconsistency or divergence. A distribution-free noise characterization can enhance robustness for legged robot state estimation.
AI.method: Characterize measurement noise with set-coverage statements (no distribution assumption) and integrate them with a Gaussian filter in a practical, computationally inexpensive framework. Use proprioceptive joint histories to infer dynamics and navigation; validate in simulation and two real quadrupedal datasets; compare against Gaussian baselines.
AI.result: The proposed set-coverage–based method remains consistent and is not prone to drift under real-noise conditions, outperforming Gaussian baselines in terms of consistency on both simulated and real data.
AI.conclusion: Distribution-free set-coverage noise modeling is a viable, robust approach for proprioceptive-only state estimation in legged robots, compatible with Gaussian filtering and effective under real-world noise.

title: From Concepts to Judgments: Interpretable Image Aesthetic Assessment
summary: Image aesthetic assessment (IAA) aims to predict the aesthetic quality of images as perceived by humans. While recent IAA models achieve strong predictive performance, they offer little insight into the factors driving their predictions. Yet for users, understanding why an image is considered pleasing or not is as valuable as the score itself, motivating growing interest in interpretability within IAA. When humans evaluate aesthetics, they naturally rely on high-level cues to justify their judgments. Motivated by this observation, we propose an interpretable IAA framework grounded in human-understandable aesthetic concepts. We learn these concepts in an accessible manner, constructing a subspace that forms the foundation of an inherently interpretable model. To capture nuanced influences on aesthetic perception beyond explicit concepts, we introduce a simple yet effective residual predictor. Experiments on photographic and artistic datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive predictive performance while offering transparent, human-understandable aesthetic judgments.

AI.tldr: An interpretable IAA model using a human-understandable concept subspace and a residual predictor; achieves competitive accuracy with transparent explanations.
AI.motivation: Current image aesthetic assessment (IAA) models are strong in prediction but lack interpretability. Humans rely on high-level cues when judging aesthetics, so there is a need for interpretable IAA that exposes understandable factors.
AI.method: Learn concepts in an accessible manner to form a subspace that underpins an inherently interpretable model. Include a simple residual predictor to capture nuanced influences beyond explicit concepts.
AI.result: On photographic and artistic datasets, the method achieves competitive predictive performance while providing transparent, human-understandable aesthetic judgments.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers interpretable aesthetic predictions without sacrificing accuracy, validating the use of human-understandable concepts and a residual component to capture nuances in IAA.

title: Fundamental Limits of Neural Network Sparsification: Evidence from Catastrophic Interpretability Collapse
summary: Extreme neural network sparsification (90% activation reduction) presents a critical challenge for mechanistic interpretability: understanding whether interpretable features survive aggressive compression. This work investigates feature survival under severe capacity constraints in hybrid Variational Autoencoder--Sparse Autoencoder (VAE-SAE) architectures. We introduce an adaptive sparsity scheduling framework that progressively reduces active neurons from 500 to 50 over 50 training epochs, and provide empirical evidence for fundamental limits of the sparsification-interpretability relationship. Testing across two benchmark datasets -- dSprites and Shapes3D -- with both Top-k and L1 sparsification methods, our key finding reveals a pervasive paradox: while global representation quality (measured by Mutual Information Gap) remains stable, local feature interpretability collapses systematically. Under Top-k sparsification, dead neuron rates reach $34.4\pm0.9\%$ on dSprites and $62.7\pm1.3\%$ on Shapes3D at k=50. L1 regularization -- a fundamentally different "soft constraint" paradigm -- produces equal or worse collapse: $41.7\pm4.4\%$ on dSprites and $90.6\pm0.5\%$ on Shapes3D. Extended training for 100 additional epochs fails to recover dead neurons, and the collapse pattern is robust across all tested threshold definitions. Critically, the collapse scales with dataset complexity: Shapes3D (RGB, 6 factors) shows $1.8\times$ more dead neurons than dSprites (grayscale, 5 factors) under Top-k and $2.2\times$ under L1. These findings establish that interpretability collapse under sparsification is intrinsic to the compression process rather than an artifact of any particular algorithm, training duration, or threshold choice.

AI.tldr: Sparsification preserves global representation but erodes local interpretability; dead neurons increase with sparsity and dataset complexity; effect appears intrinsic to compression.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether interpretable features survive extreme neural sparsification in hybrid VAE-SAE architectures and identify fundamental limits of the sparsification-interpretability trade-off.
AI.method: Adaptive sparsity scheduling reducing active neurons from 500 to 50 over 50 training epochs; evaluated on dSprites and Shapes3D with Top-k and L1 sparsification; measured global representation quality via Mutual Information Gap (MIG) and local interpretability via dead neuron rate; tested robustness across threshold definitions; extended training by 100 epochs to assess recoverability.
AI.result: Global representation quality (MIG) remains stable while local interpretability collapses. Dead neuron rates at k=50: 34.4±0.9% (dSprites) and 62.7±1.3% (Shapes3D) under Top-k. L1 regularization yields 41.7±4.4% (dSprites) and 90.6±0.5% (Shapes3D). Extended training does not recover dead neurons. Collapse robust to threshold definition. Shapes3D shows ~1.8× more dead neurons than dSprites under Top-k and ~2.2× under L1, indicating dataset complexity amplifies the effect.
AI.conclusion: Interpretability collapse under sparsification appears intrinsic to the compression process rather than an artifact of specific algorithms, training duration, or threshold choice; the severity scales with dataset complexity, revealing a fundamental limit to preserving interpretable features under aggressive sparsification.

title: Retrieval-Augmented LLM Agents: Learning to Learn from Experience
summary: While large language models (LLMs) have advanced the development of general-purpose agents, achieving robust generalization to unseen tasks remains a significant challenge. Current approaches typically rely on either fine-tuning or training-free memory-augmented generation using retrieved experience; yet both have limitations: fine-tuning often fails to extrapolate to new tasks, while experience retrieval often underperforms compared to supervised baselines. In this work, we propose to combine these approaches and systematically study how to train retrieval-augmented LLM agents to effectively leverage retrieved trajectories in-context. First, we establish a robust supervised fine-tuning (SFT) recipe using LoRA that outperforms several state-of-the-art agent training pipelines. Second, we provide a detailed analysis of key design choices for experience retrieval, identifying optimal strategies for storage, querying, and trajectory selection. Finally, we propose a pipeline that integrates experience retrieval into the fine-tuning process. Our results demonstrate that this combined approach significantly improves generalization to unseen tasks, providing a scalable and effective framework for building agents that learn to learn from experience.

AI.tldr: A combined training paradigm that uses supervised fine-tuning (via LoRA) together with retrieval-augmented experience trajectories to improve generalization of LLM agents to unseen tasks; includes a robust SFT recipe, a thorough study of retrieval design (storage, querying, trajectory selection), and a pipeline that integrates retrieval into fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of pure fine-tuning (poor extrapolation to new tasks) and standard retrieval-based methods (underperforming vs supervised baselines) by enabling agents to learn from retrieved trajectories during in-context learning and during fine-tuning.
AI.method: 1) Develop a robust supervised fine-tuning (SFT) recipe using LoRA that surpasses current agent-training pipelines. 2) Analyze design choices for experience retrieval, identifying optimal storage, querying, and trajectory selection strategies. 3) Propose and implement a pipeline that integrates experience retrieval into the fine-tuning process.
AI.result: The combined approach significantly improves generalization to unseen tasks compared with baselines, yielding a scalable and effective framework for agents that learn from experience.
AI.conclusion: Integrating retrieval-augmented experience into the fine-tuning loop with LoRA yields stronger generalization and a scalable framework for learning-to-learn agents.

title: DriveVLM-RL: Neuroscience-Inspired Reinforcement Learning with Vision-Language Models for Safe and Deployable Autonomous Driving
comment: 32 pages, 15 figures. Code and demo available online
summary: Ensuring safe decision-making in autonomous vehicles remains a fundamental challenge despite rapid advances in end-to-end learning approaches. Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) methods rely on manually engineered rewards or sparse collision signals, which fail to capture the rich contextual understanding required for safe driving and make unsafe exploration unavoidable in real-world settings. Recent vision-language models (VLMs) offer promising semantic understanding capabilities; however, their high inference latency and susceptibility to hallucination hinder direct application to real-time vehicle control. To address these limitations, this paper proposes DriveVLM-RL, a neuroscience-inspired framework that integrates VLMs into RL through a dual-pathway architecture for safe and deployable autonomous driving. The framework decomposes semantic reward learning into a Static Pathway for continuous spatial safety assessment using CLIP-based contrasting language goals, and a Dynamic Pathway for attention-gated multi-frame semantic risk reasoning using a lightweight detector and a large VLM. A hierarchical reward synthesis mechanism fuses semantic signals with vehicle states, while an asynchronous training pipeline decouples expensive VLM inference from environment interaction. All VLM components are used only during offline training and are removed at deployment, ensuring real-time feasibility. Experiments in the CARLA simulator show significant improvements in collision avoidance, task success, and generalization across diverse traffic scenarios, including strong robustness under settings without explicit collision penalties. These results demonstrate that DriveVLM-RL provides a practical paradigm for integrating foundation models into autonomous driving without compromising real-time feasibility. Demo video and code are available at: https://zilin-huang.github.io/DriveVLM-RL-website/

AI.tldr: DriveVLM-RL integrates vision-language models with reinforcement learning via a dual-pathway framework to enable safe, real-time autonomous driving without requiring VLM inference at deployment.
AI.motivation: Overcome RL's reliance on engineered rewards and sparse collision signals, and address VLMs' latency and hallucination risks, by combining semantic grounding with offline training to yield a deployable, safe driving policy.
AI.method: A two-path architecture: a Static Pathway that uses CLIP-like contrasting language goals for continuous spatial safety assessment; a Dynamic Pathway that performs attention-gated multi-frame semantic risk reasoning using a lightweight detector and a large VLM. Hierarchical reward synthesis fuses semantic cues with vehicle state. Training is asynchronous and offline, decoupling VLM inference from environment interaction; all VLM components are removed at deployment to ensure real-time feasibility.
AI.result: In CARLA, DriveVLM-RL yields significant improvements in collision avoidance, task success, and generalization across diverse traffic scenarios, with robustness in settings without explicit collision penalties.
AI.conclusion: DriveVLM-RL offers a practical framework for integrating foundation models into autonomous driving without compromising real-time performance or safety, demonstrated via offline-trained semantic guidance and deployable RL policies.

title: Insight-V++: Towards Advanced Long-Chain Visual Reasoning with Multimodal Large Language Models
comment: arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2411.14432
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable reliability and advanced capabilities through extended test-time reasoning. However, extending these capabilities to Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) remains a significant challenge due to a critical scarcity of high-quality, long-chain reasoning data and optimized training pipelines. To bridge this gap, we present a unified multi-agent visual reasoning framework that systematically evolves from our foundational image-centric model, Insight-V, into a generalized spatial-temporal architecture, Insight-V++. We first propose a scalable data generation pipeline equipped with multi-granularity assessment that autonomously synthesizes structured, complex reasoning trajectories across image and video domains without human intervention. Recognizing that directly supervising MLLMs with such intricate data yields sub-optimal results, we design a dual-agent architecture comprising a reasoning agent to execute extensive analytical chains, and a summary agent to critically evaluate and distill final outcomes. While our initial framework utilized Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), its off-policy nature fundamentally constrained reinforcement learning potential. To overcome these limitations, particularly for long-horizon video understanding, Insight-V++ introduces two novel algorithms, ST-GRPO and J-GRPO, which enhance spatial-temporal reasoning and improve evaluative robustness. Crucially, by leveraging reliable feedback from the summary agent, we guide an iterative reasoning path generation process, retraining the entire multi-agent system in a continuous, self-improving loop. Extensive experiments on base models like LLaVA-NeXT and Qwen2.5-VL demonstrate significant performance gains across challenging image and video reasoning benchmarks while preserving strong capabilities on traditional perception-focused tasks.

AI.tldr: A unified, self-improving multi-agent framework (Insight-V++) for image and video reasoning, featuring autonomous data generation, a dual-agent reasoning/summary architecture, and novel ST-GRPO/J-GRPO optimization, achieving strong gains on image/video benchmarks while preserving perception tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of high-quality long-chain reasoning data and optimized training pipelines for multi-modal LLMs; enable scalable, autonomous generation of complex reasoning trajectories across image and video domains.
AI.method: A scalable data generation pipeline with multi-granularity assessment; a dual-agent architecture (reasoning agent and summary agent) to generate and critically evaluate reasoning; replacement of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with ST-GRPO and J-GRPO to boost spatial-temporal long-horizon reasoning; iterative self-improving loop where the summary agent feedback guides retraining across the system; validated on base models LLaVA-NeXT and Qwen2.5-VL across image and video reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: Significant performance gains on challenging image and video reasoning benchmarks, with preservation of strong capabilities on traditional perception-focused tasks.
AI.conclusion: Extends image-centric Insight-V into a generalized spatial-temporal architecture (Insight-V++); demonstrates effective self-improving, multi-agent RL for long-horizon multimodal reasoning, scalable data generation, and robust performance across image and video domains.

title: Lightweight Adaptation for LLM-based Technical Service Agent: Latent Logic Augmentation and Robust Noise Reduction
summary: Adapting Large Language Models in complex technical service domains is constrained by the absence of explicit cognitive chains in human demonstrations and the inherent ambiguity arising from the diversity of valid responses. These limitations severely hinder agents from internalizing latent decision dynamics and generalizing effectively. Moreover, practical adaptation is often impeded by the prohibitive resource and time costs associated with standard training paradigms. To overcome these challenges and guarantee computational efficiency, we propose a lightweight adaptation framework comprising three key contributions. (1) Latent Logic Augmentation: We introduce Planning-Aware Trajectory Modeling and Decision Reasoning Augmentation to bridge the gap between surface-level supervision and latent decision logic. These approaches strengthen the stability of Supervised Fine-Tuning alignment. (2) Robust Noise Reduction: We construct a Multiple Ground Truths dataset through a dual-filtering method to reduce the noise by validating diverse responses, thereby capturing the semantic diversity. (3) Lightweight Adaptation: We design a Hybrid Reward mechanism that fuses an LLM-based judge with a lightweight relevance-based Reranker to distill high-fidelity reward signals while reducing the computational cost compared to standard LLM-as-a-Judge reinforcement learning. Empirical evaluations on real-world Cloud service tasks, conducted across semantically diverse settings, demonstrate that our framework achieves stability and performance gains through Latent Logic Augmentation and Robust Noise Reduction. Concurrently, our Hybrid Reward mechanism achieves alignment comparable to standard LLM-as-a-judge methods with reduced training time, underscoring the practical value for deploying technical service agents.

AI.tldr: A lightweight adaptation framework for LLMs in technical services using latent logic augmentation, robust multi-ground-truth noise reduction, and a hybrid reward signal to reduce training cost while maintaining alignment.
AI.motivation: Overcome absence of explicit cognitive chains and diverse valid responses, plus prohibitively expensive training, by enabling latent decision modeling, noise-resilient supervision, and cost-efficient reward-based fine-tuning.
AI.method: Three parts: (1) Latent Logic Augmentation: Planning-Aware Trajectory Modeling and Decision Reasoning Augmentation to align surface supervision with latent decision dynamics; (2) Robust Noise Reduction: build a Multiple Ground Truths dataset via dual-filtering to validate diverse responses; (3) Lightweight Adaptation: Hybrid Reward mechanism combining an LLM-based judge with a lightweight relevance-based reranker to distill high-fidelity rewards with lower cost than LLM-as-a-judge RL.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on real-world Cloud service tasks across semantically diverse settings shows stability and performance gains due to Latent Logic Augmentation and Robust Noise Reduction; Hybrid Reward achieves alignment close to LLM-as-a-judge with reduced training time.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers practical, cost-efficient adaptation with improved stability and performance for technical service agents.

title: EDM-ARS: A Domain-Specific Multi-Agent System for Automated Educational Data Mining Research
summary: In this technical report, we present the Educational Data Mining Automated Research System (EDM-ARS), a domain-specific multi-agent pipeline that automates end-to-end educational data mining (EDM) research. We conceptualize EDM-ARS as a general framework for domain-aware automated research pipelines, where educational expertise is embedded into each stage of the research lifecycle. As a first instantiation of this framework, we focus on predictive modeling tasks. Within this scope, EDM-ARS orchestrates five specialized LLM-powered agents (ProblemFormulator, DataEngineer, Analyst, Critic, and Writer) through a state-machine coordinator that supports revision loops, checkpoint-based recovery, and sandboxed code execution. Given a research prompt and a dataset, EDM-ARS produces a complete LaTeX manuscript with real Semantic Scholar citations, validated machine learning analyses, and automated methodological peer review. We also provide a detailed description of the system architecture, the three-tier data registry design that encodes educational domain expertise, the specification of each agent, the inter-agent communication protocol, and mechanisms for error-handling and self-correction. Finally, we discuss current limitations, including single-dataset scope and formulaic paper output, and outline a phased roadmap toward causal inference, transfer learning, psychometric, and multi-dataset generalization. EDM-ARS is released as an open-source project to support the educational research community.

AI.tldr: EDM-ARS is a domain-aware automated EDM research framework using five LLM-powered agents to automate end-to-end predictive modeling in education; outputs a LaTeX manuscript with real citations; open-source; includes architecture, data registry, limitations, and a roadmap.
AI.motivation: To automate and standardize educational data mining (EDM) research by embedding domain expertise into each stage of the research lifecycle, improving reproducibility and efficiency.
AI.method: A five-agent architecture (ProblemFormulator, DataEngineer, Analyst, Critic, Writer) controlled by a state-machine coordinator with revision loops, checkpoint-based recovery, and sandboxed code execution; a three-tier data registry encodes educational-domain knowledge; defined inter-agent communications and error-handling/self-correction mechanisms; generates a complete LaTeX manuscript with validated analyses and automated peer review.
AI.result: A complete, LaTeX-manuscript output with validated ML analyses and methodological peer review; detailed system architecture, data registry design, agent specifications, and communication protocol; open-source release.
AI.conclusion: EDM-ARS demonstrates a domain-aware automated EDM workflow; acknowledges current limitations (single-dataset, formulaic output) and outlines a roadmap toward causal inference, transfer learning, psychometrics, and multi-dataset generalization; intended to support the educational research community as open-source software.

title: ManiDreams: An Open-Source Library for Robust Object Manipulation via Uncertainty-aware Task-specific Intuitive Physics
comment: 9 pages, 10 figures. Project page at https://manidreams.github.io
summary: Dynamics models, whether simulators or learned world models, have long been central to robotic manipulation, but most focus on minimizing prediction error rather than confronting a more fundamental challenge: real-world manipulation is inherently uncertain. We argue that robust manipulation under uncertainty is fundamentally an integration problem: uncertainties must be represented, propagated, and constrained within the planning loop, not merely suppressed during training. We present and open-source ManiDreams, a modular framework for uncertainty-aware manipulation planning over intuitive physics models. It realizes this integration through composable abstractions for distributional state representation, backend-agnostic dynamics prediction, and declarative constraint specification for action optimization. The framework explicitly addresses three sources of uncertainty: perceptual, parametric, and structural. It wraps any base policy with a sample-predict-constrain loop that evaluates candidate actions against distributional outcomes, adding robustness without retraining. Experiments on ManiSkill tasks show that ManiDreams maintains robust performance under various perturbations where the RL baseline degrades significantly. Runnable examples on pushing, picking, catching, and real-world deployment demonstrate flexibility across different policies, optimizers, physics backends, and executors. The framework is publicly available at https://github.com/Rice-RobotPI-Lab/ManiDreams

AI.tldr: ManiDreams: an uncertainty-aware manipulation planning framework that integrates distributional state representations, backend-agnostic dynamics, and declarative action constraints via a sample–predict–constrain loop, showing robustness under perceptual, parametric, and structural uncertainty with open-source release.
AI.motivation: Real-world robotic manipulation is inherently uncertain. Prediction error alone is insufficient; uncertainty must be represented, propagated, and constrained within the planning loop. The paper targets robust manipulation under perceptual, parametric, and structural uncertainties.
AI.method: Introduce ManiDreams, a modular framework with composable abstractions: distributional state representations, backend-agnostic dynamics predictors, and declarative constraint specifications. It uses a sample-predict-constrain loop that evaluates candidate actions against distributional outcomes, wrapping any base policy to achieve robustness without retraining.
AI.result: On ManiSkill tasks, ManiDreams yields robust performance under perturbations where RL baselines fail, with runnable examples across pushing, picking, catching, and real-world deployment. Demonstrates flexibility across policy types, optimizers, physics backends, and executors; code is available.
AI.conclusion: ManiDreams provides an open-source, modular approach to uncertainty-aware manipulation planning that can be integrated with existing policies and backends, enabling robust manipulation in uncertain real-world settings.

title: VLM-AutoDrive: Post-Training Vision-Language Models for Safety-Critical Autonomous Driving Events
summary:
The rapid growth of ego-centric dashcam footage presents a major challenge for detecting safety-critical events such as collisions and near-collisions, scenarios that are brief, rare, and difficult for generic vision models to capture. While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) demonstrate strong general reasoning ability, they underperform in driving contexts due to domain and temporal misalignment.
  We introduce VLM-AutoDrive, a modular post-training framework for adapting pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to high-fidelity anomaly detection. The framework integrates metadata-derived captions, LLM-generated descriptions, visual question answering (VQA) pairs, and chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning supervision to enable domain-aligned and interpretable learning. Off-the-shelf VLMs such as NVIDIA's Cosmos-Reason1 7B (CR1) exhibit near-zero Collision recall in zero-shot settings; fine-tuning with VLM-AutoDrive improves Collision F1 from 0.00 to 0.69 and overall accuracy from 35.35% to 77.27%.
  VLM-AutoDrive offers a scalable recipe for adapting general-purpose VLMs to safety-critical, temporally localized perception tasks. Evaluated on real-world Nexar dashcam videos, it achieves substantial gains in Collision and Near-Collision detection while producing interpretable reasoning traces, bridging the gap between perception, causality, and decision reasoning in autonomous driving.

AI.tldr: VLM-AutoDrive fine-tunes pretrained Vision-Language Models for high-fidelity dashcam anomaly detection using multimodal supervision, achieving large gains in Collision/near-Collision detection and providing interpretable CoT traces.
AI.motivation: Ego-centric dashcam videos yield brief, rare safety-critical events; vanilla VLMs suffer from domain and temporal misalignment, limiting effectiveness in driving contexts.
AI.method: Post-training adaptation framework that integrates metadata-derived captions, LLM-generated descriptions, visual question answering (VQA) pairs, and chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning supervision to align VLMs with safety-critical, temporally localized perception tasks; uses off-the-shelf VLMs (e.g., NVIDIA Cosmos-Reason1 7B) and fine-tunes them to improve detection performance, with interpretable reasoning traces.
AI.result: Collision recall rises from near-zero (zero-shot) to 0.69; overall accuracy increases from 35.35% to 77.27% on real Nexar dashcam videos; notable gains in Collision and Near-Collision detection and provision of interpretable reasoning traces.
AI.conclusion: Offers a scalable recipe for adapting general-purpose VLMs to safety-critical, temporally localized perception in autonomous driving, bridging perception, causality, and decision reasoning and enabling interpretable decisions.

title: Variational Phasor Circuits for Phase-Native Brain-Computer Interface Classification
summary: We present the \textbf{Variational Phasor Circuit (VPC)}, a deterministic classical learning architecture operating on the continuous $S^1$ unit circle manifold. Inspired by variational quantum circuits, VPC replaces dense real-valued weight matrices with trainable phase shifts, local unitary mixing, and structured interference in the ambient complex space. This phase-native design provides a unified method for both binary and multi-class classification of spatially distributed signals. A single VPC block supports compact phase-based decision boundaries, while stacked VPC compositions extend the model to deeper circuits through inter-block pull-back normalization. Using synthetic brain-computer interface benchmarks, we show that VPC can decode difficult mental-state classification tasks with competitive accuracy and substantially fewer trainable parameters than standard Euclidean baselines. These results position unit-circle phase interference as a practical and mathematically principled alternative to dense neural computation, and motivate VPC as both a standalone classifier and a front-end encoding layer for future hybrid phasor-quantum systems.

AI.tldr: VPC is a deterministic classical learning architecture on the unit circle that uses trainable phase shifts and interference instead of dense weights, enabling compact phase-based decision boundaries for binary/multi-class tasks; it achieves competitive accuracy on synthetic BCI benchmarks with far fewer parameters and can serve as a standalone classifier or front-end encoding layer for phasor-quantum hybrids.
AI.motivation: To explore a phase-native, unit-circle architecture as an efficient, principled alternative to dense Euclidean neural networks, inspired by variational quantum circuits.
AI.method: Replace dense weight matrices with trainable phase shifts, local unitary mixing, and structured interference within the complex ambient space. A single VPC block yields compact phase-based decision boundaries; multiple blocks are formed via inter-block pull-back normalization to build deeper circuits.
AI.result: On synthetic brain-computer interface benchmarks, VPC achieves competitive accuracy on difficult mental-state classification tasks while using substantially fewer trainable parameters than standard Euclidean baselines.
AI.conclusion: Unit-circle phase interference offers a principled, practical alternative to dense neural computation. VPC can function as a standalone classifier and as a front-end encoding layer for future hybrid phasor-quantum systems.

title: CORE: Robust Out-of-Distribution Detection via Confidence and Orthogonal Residual Scoring
comment: 26 pages, 5 figures, includes supplementary material as appendix
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is essential for deploying deep learning models reliably, yet no single method performs consistently across architectures and datasets -- a scorer that leads on one benchmark often falters on another. We attribute this inconsistency to a shared structural limitation: logit-based methods see only the classifier's confidence signal, while feature-based methods attempt to measure membership in the training distribution but do so in the full feature space where confidence and membership are entangled, inheriting architecture-sensitive failure modes. We observe that penultimate features naturally decompose into two orthogonal subspaces: a classifier-aligned component encoding confidence, and a residual the classifier discards. We discover that this residual carries a class-specific directional signature for in-distribution data -- a membership signal invisible to logit-based methods and entangled with noise in feature-based methods. We propose CORE (COnfidence + REsidual), which disentangles the two signals by scoring each subspace independently and combines them via normalized summation. Because the two signals are orthogonal by construction, their failure modes are approximately independent, producing robust detection where either view alone is unreliable. CORE achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance across five architectures and five benchmark configurations, ranking first in three of five settings and achieving the highest grand average AUROC with negligible computational overhead.

AI.tldr: CORE disentangles confidence and residual signals in penultimate features for OOD detection by orthogonal subspaces, improving robustness across architectures and datasets.
AI.motivation: OOD detection methods struggle to generalize across architectures and benchmarks due to conflicting weaknesses of logit-based (confidence-only) and feature-based (entangled confidence and membership) approaches. There is an underexplored orthogonal decomposition in penultimate features.
AI.method: Decompose penultimate features into a classifier-aligned subspace (confidence) and a residual subspace (classifier-discarded). The residual carries a class-specific membership signal for in-distribution data. Score each subspace independently for OOD detection and combine via normalized summation. The two signals are orthogonal, leading to approximately independent failure modes with negligible overhead.
AI.result: CORE achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance across five architectures and five benchmarks, ranking first in 3 of 5 settings and achieving the highest grand average AUROC with negligible computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling confidence and residual signals yields robust OOD detection due to approximately independent failure modes; CORE provides strong, architecture- and dataset-robust performance with minimal overhead.

title: Shifting Uncertainty to Critical Moments: Towards Reliable Uncertainty Quantification for VLA Model
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models enable general-purpose robotic policies by mapping visual observations and language instructions to low-level actions, but they often lack reliable introspection. A common practice is to compute a token-level uncertainty signal and take its mean over a rollout. However, mean aggregation can dilute short-lived but safety-critical uncertainty spikes in continuous control. In particular, successful rollouts may contain localized high-entropy segments due to benign noise or non-critical micro-adjustments, while failure rollouts can appear low-entropy for most timesteps and only exhibit brief spikes near the onset of failure. We propose a unified uncertainty quantification approach for predicting rollout success versus failure that (1) uses max-based sliding window pooling to preserve transient risk signals, (2) applies motion-aware stability weighting to emphasize high-frequency action oscillations associated with unstable behaviors, and (3) performs DoF-adaptive calibration via Bayesian Optimization to prioritize kinematically critical axes. Experiments on the LIBERO benchmark show that our method substantially improves failure prediction accuracy and yields more reliable signals for failure detection, which can support downstream human-in-the-loop interventions.

AI.tldr: A unified uncertainty quantification approach for Vision-Language-Action robots that preserves transient risk signals and improves failure prediction via max-pooling, motion-aware weighting, and DoF-adaptive calibration, validated on LIBERO.
AI.motivation: Mean-aggregated uncertainty hides brief but critical risk spikes; current failure signals may be diluted; need faster, more reliable failure detection to enable human interventions.
AI.method: (1) max-based sliding window pooling of token-level uncertainty to preserve short-lived spikes; (2) motion-aware stability weighting to emphasize high-frequency action oscillations; (3) DoF-adaptive calibration via Bayesian optimization to prioritize kinematically critical axes.
AI.result: On LIBERO benchmark, substantially improved failure prediction accuracy and more reliable failure signals, enabling better human-in-the-loop interventions.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields safer, more responsive VLA policies by robust uncertainty aggregation and adaptivity, improving operational safety.

title: MicroVision: An Open Dataset and Benchmark Models for Detecting Vulnerable Road Users and Micromobility Vehicles
summary: Micromobility is a growing mode of transportation, raising new challenges for traffic safety and planning due to increased interactions in areas where vulnerable road users (VRUs) share the infrastructure with micromobility, including parked micromobility vehicles (MMVs). Approaches to support traffic safety and planning increasingly rely on detecting road users in images -- a computer-vision task relying heavily on the quality of the images to train on. However, existing open image datasets for training such models lack focus and diversity in VRUs and MMVs, for instance, by categorizing both pedestrians and MMV riders as "person", or by not including new MMVs like e-scooters. Furthermore, datasets are often captured from a car perspective and lack data from areas where only VRUs travel (sidewalks, cycle paths). To help close this gap, we introduce the MicroVision dataset: an open image dataset and annotations for training and evaluating models for detecting the most common VRUs (pedestrians, cyclists, e-scooterists) and stationary MMVs (bicycles, e-scooters), from a VRU perspective. The dataset, recorded in Gothenburg (Sweden), consists of more than 8,000 anonymized, full-HD images with more than 30,000 carefully annotated VRUs and MMVs, captured over an entire year and part of almost 2,000 unique interaction scenes. Along with the dataset, we provide first benchmark object-detection models based on state-of-the-art architectures, which achieved a mean average precision of up to 0.723 on an unseen test set. The dataset and model can support traffic safety to distinguish between different VRUs and MMVs, or help monitoring systems identify the use of micromobility. The dataset and model weights can be accessed at https://doi.org/10.71870/eepz-jd52.

AI.tldr: Introduces MicroVision, an open VRU/MMV-focused image dataset for detection from a VRU perspective, with 8k images, 30k annotated VRUs/MMVs, and baselines achieving up to 0.723 mAP; aims to improve traffic safety and planning.
AI.motivation: Addresses gaps in existing datasets, notably mislabeling MMVs as 'person', car-centric viewpoints, and lack of sidewalk/cycle-path data; targets VRUs and stationary MMVs to improve detection for safety analyses.
AI.method: Collect and annotate anonymized, full-HD images from Gothenburg over a year (~2k interaction scenes); annotate pedestrians, cyclists, e-scooterists, stationary bicycles/e-scooters; provide baseline detector benchmarks using state-of-the-art architectures; evaluate on unseen test set.
AI.result: Dataset contains >8k images with >30k VRUs/MMVs; mAP up to 0.723 on unseen test set; baseline models show feasibility of VRU/MMV detection; dataset and weights are publicly accessible.
AI.conclusion: MicroVision offers a resource to distinguish VRUs and MMVs, support safety analytics and monitoring; promotes research in micromobility perception; available via DOI.

title: SLEA-RL: Step-Level Experience Augmented Reinforcement Learning for Multi-Turn Agentic Training
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have shown strong results on multi-turn tool-use tasks, yet they operate in isolation during training, failing to leverage experiences accumulated across episodes. Existing experience-augmented methods address this by organizing trajectories into retrievable libraries, but they retrieve experiences only once based on the initial task description and hold them constant throughout the episode. In multi-turn settings where observations change at every step, this static retrieval becomes increasingly mismatched as episodes progress. We propose SLEA-RL (Step-Level Experience-Augmented Reinforcement Learning), a framework that retrieves relevant experiences at each decision step conditioned on the current observation. SLEA-RL operates through three components: (i) step-level observation clustering that groups structurally equivalent environmental states for efficient cluster-indexed retrieval; (ii) a self-evolving experience library that distills successful strategies and failure patterns through score-based admission and rate-limited extraction; and (iii) policy optimization with step-level credit assignment for fine-grained advantage estimation across multi-turn episodes. The experience library evolves alongside the policy through semantic analysis rather than gradient updates. Experiments on long-horizon multi-turn agent benchmarks demonstrate that SLEA-RL achieves superior performance compared to various reinforcement learning baselines.

AI.tldr: SLEA-RL introduces step-level retrieval for LLM-based agents in long-horizon, multi-turn tasks by clustering observations, maintaining a self-evolving experience library, and performing step-level credit assignment for policy optimization, achieving superior performance over RL baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing experience-augmented methods retrieve experiences only once per episode, causing misalignment as observations evolve; static libraries fail to leverage growing episodic information across steps.
AI.method: Three components: (i) step-level observation clustering to create cluster-indexed retrieval based on structurally equivalent states; (ii) a self-evolving, score-based experience library that admissions experiences and rate-limits extractions to distill strategies and failure patterns; (iii) policy optimization with step-level credit assignment for fine-grained advantage estimation across turns; the library evolves through semantic analysis rather than gradient updates.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on long-horizon multi-turn agent benchmarks shows SLEA-RL outperforms various RL baselines and other experience-augmented approaches.
AI.conclusion: Step-level, adaptive experience augmentation with an evolving semantic library improves alignment with current observations and enables finer credit assignment, yielding superior performance on challenging multi-turn tasks.

title: The Validity Gap in Health AI Evaluation: A Cross-Sectional Analysis of Benchmark Composition
summary:
Background: Clinical trials rely on transparent inclusion criteria to ensure generalizability. In contrast, benchmarks validating health-related large language models (LLMs) rarely characterize the "patient" or "query" populations they contain. Without defined composition, aggregate performance metrics may misrepresent model readiness for clinical use.
  Methods: We analyzed 18,707 consumer health queries across six public benchmarks using LLMs as automated coding instruments to apply a standardized 16-field taxonomy profiling context, topic, and intent.
  Results: We identified a structural "validity gap." While benchmarks have evolved from static retrieval to interactive dialogue, clinical composition remains misaligned with real-world needs. Although 42% of the corpus referenced objective data, this was polarized toward wellness-focused wearable signals (17.7%); complex diagnostic inputs remained rare, including laboratory values (5.2%), imaging (3.8%), and raw medical records (0.6%). Safety-critical scenarios were effectively absent: suicide/self-harm queries comprised <0.7% of the corpus and chronic disease management only 5.5%. Benchmarks also neglected vulnerable populations (pediatrics/older adults <11%) and global health needs.
  Conclusions: Evaluation benchmarks remain misaligned with real-world clinical needs, lacking raw clinical artifacts, adequate representation of vulnerable populations, and longitudinal chronic care scenarios. The field must adopt standardized query profiling--analogous to clinical trial reporting--to align evaluation with the full complexity of clinical practice.

AI.tldr: Benchmarks for health-related LLMs show a structural validity gap due to lack of patient/population profiling and realistic clinical data.
AI.motivation: Ensure benchmark relevance and generalizability for clinical deployment by aligning benchmarks with real-world patient contexts.
AI.method: Analyze 18,707 consumer health queries across six public benchmarks; use LLMs as automated coders to apply a standardized 16-field taxonomy profiling context, topic, and intent.
AI.result: Identify a structural validity gap: clinical composition misaligned with real-world needs; limited presence of objective data (42%), skewed toward wellness signals (17.7%); rare diagnostic inputs (labs 5.2%, imaging 3.8%, raw records 0.6%); safety-critical scenarios largely absent (suicide/self-harm <0.7%, chronic disease management 5.5%); underrepresentation of vulnerable groups (pediatrics/older adults <11%); neglect of global health needs. Recommend standardized query profiling to align evaluation with real-world clinical practice.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation benchmarks should incorporate raw clinical artifacts, balanced representation of populations, and longitudinal care contexts; adopt standardized query profiling akin to clinical trial reporting.

title: HRI-SA: A Multimodal Dataset for Online Assessment of Human Situational Awareness during Remote Human-Robot Teaming
comment: This work is currently under peer review
summary: Maintaining situational awareness (SA) is critical in human-robot teams. Yet, under high workload and dynamic conditions, operators often experience SA gaps. Automated detection of SA gaps could provide timely assistance for operators. However, conventional SA measures either disrupt task flow or cannot capture real-time fluctuations, limiting their operational utility. To the best of our knowledge, no publicly available dataset currently supports the systematic evaluation of online human SA assessment in human-robot teaming. To advance the development of online SA assessment tools, we introduce HRI-SA, a multimodal dataset from 30 participants in a realistic search-and-rescue human-robot teaming context, incorporating eye movements, pupil diameter, biosignals, user interactions, and robot data. The experimental protocol included predefined events requiring timely operator assistance, with ground truth SA latency of two types (perceptual and comprehension) systematically obtained by measuring the time between assistance need onset and resolution. We illustrate the utility of this dataset by evaluating standard machine learning models for detecting perceptual SA latencies using generic eye-tracking features and contextual features. Results show that eye-tracking features alone effectively classified perceptual SA latency (recall=88.91%, F1=67.63%) using leave-one-group-out cross-validation, with performance improved through contextual data fusion (recall=91.51%, F1=80.38%). This paper contributes the first public dataset supporting the systematic evaluation of SA throughout a human-robot teaming mission, while also demonstrating the potential of generic eye-tracking features for continuous perceptual SA latency detection in remote human-robot teaming.

AI.tldr: Introduction of HRI-SA, a multimodal dataset for online SA assessment in human-robot teaming, with 30 participants in a search-and-rescue scenario; provides ground-truth SA latency types (perceptual and comprehension); demonstrates baseline ML models using eye-tracking and contextual features to detect perceptual SA latency; performance improves with data fusion.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of publicly available datasets for evaluating online human SA assessment in HRI and the need for timely SA-gap detection to aid operators under high workload.
AI.method: Collect multimodal data (eye movements, pupil diameter, biosignals, user interactions, and robot data) from 30 participants in a realistic search-and-rescue HRI task; define ground-truth SA latency for perceptual and comprehension; evaluate ML models for detecting perceptual SA latency using generic eye-tracking features and contextual data with leave-one-group-out cross-validation.
AI.result: Eye-tracking features alone achieved perceptual SA latency detection with recall 88.91% and F1 67.63%; fusion with contextual features improved to recall 91.51% and F1 80.38%; first public dataset for systematic SA evaluation in HRI; demonstrates potential of generic eye-tracking features for continuous perceptual SA latency detection in remote HRI.
AI.conclusion: Public HRI-SA dataset enables systematic evaluation of SA during a human-robot teaming mission and supports development of online SA assessment tools, highlighting the utility of eye-tracking features for real-time perceptual SA latency detection.

title: Semantic Segmentation and Depth Estimation for Real-Time Lunar Surface Mapping Using 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Navigation and mapping on the lunar surface require robust perception under challenging conditions, including poorly textured environments, high-contrast lighting, and limited computational resources. This paper presents a real-time mapping framework that integrates dense perception models with a 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) representation. We first benchmark several models on synthetic datasets generated with the LuPNT simulator, selecting a stereo dense depth estimation model based on Gated Recurrent Units for its balance of speed and accuracy in depth estimation, and a convolutional neural network for its superior performance in detecting semantic segments. Using ground truth poses to decouple the local scene understanding from the global state estimation, our pipeline reconstructs a 120-meter traverse with a geometric height accuracy of approximately 3 cm, outperforming a traditional point cloud baseline without LiDAR. The resulting 3DGS map enables novel view synthesis and serves as a foundation for a full SLAM system, where its capacity for joint map and pose optimization would offer significant advantages. Our results demonstrate that combining semantic segmentation and dense depth estimation with learned map representations is an effective approach for creating detailed, large-scale maps to support future lunar surface missions.

AI.tldr: A real-time lunar mapping framework combining dense perception with 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to produce large-scale maps from synthetic data, achieving centimeter-level height accuracy over 120 m and enabling novel view synthesis and SLAM-ready maps.
AI.motivation: Robust perception and detailed, scalable maps are critical for lunar exploration under challenging lighting, textures, and limited computation. Dense, learned representations can improve map fidelity beyond traditional point clouds.
AI.method: Benchmark stereo dense depth estimation (GRU-based) and CNN-based semantic segmentation on LuPNT synthetic data. Use ground-truth poses to decouple local scene understanding from global state estimation, reconstruct a 120 m traverse with 3DGS, and compare against a LiDAR-free point cloud baseline. Demonstrate novel view synthesis and readiness for joint map-pose optimization within a SLAM framework.
AI.result: Geometric height accuracy about 3 cm over a 120 m traverse; 3DGS map enables novel view synthesis and serves as a foundation for SLAM with potential joint map-pose optimization. Outperforms traditional point cloud baseline without LiDAR.
