AI.conclusion: Integrating semantic segmentation and dense depth estimation with learned map representations (3DGS) effectively supports detailed, large-scale lunar maps and is advantageous for future lunar missions.

title: Probabilistic Federated Learning on Uncertain and Heterogeneous Data with Model Personalization
comment: Accepted at IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
summary: Conventional federated learning (FL) frameworks often suffer from training degradation due to data uncertainty and heterogeneity across local clients. Probabilistic approaches such as Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) can mitigate this issue by explicitly modeling uncertainty, but they introduce additional runtime, latency, and bandwidth overhead that has rarely been studied in federated settings. To address these challenges, we propose Meta-BayFL, a personalized probabilistic FL method that combines meta-learning with BNNs to improve training under uncertain and heterogeneous data. The framework is characterized by three main features: (1) BNN-based client models incorporate uncertainty across hidden layers to stabilize training on small and noisy datasets, (2) meta-learning with adaptive learning rates enables personalized updates that enhance local training under non-IID conditions, and (3) a unified probabilistic and personalized design improves the robustness of global model aggregation. We provide a theoretical convergence analysis and characterize the upper bound of the global model over communication rounds. In addition, we evaluate computational costs (runtime, latency, and communication) and discuss the feasibility of deployment on resource-constrained devices such as edge nodes and IoT systems. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet show that Meta-BayFL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, including both standard and personalized FL approaches (e.g., pFedMe, Ditto, FedFomo), with up to 7.42\% higher test accuracy.

AI.tldr: Meta-BayFL is a personalized probabilistic FL framework that combines Bayesian neural networks with meta-learning to address data uncertainty and non-IID heterogeneity, achieving higher accuracy and providing convergence and cost analyses for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Federated learning suffers from data uncertainty and heterogeneity across clients, leading to training degradation. Bayesian neural networks can model uncertainty but add runtime/communication costs; thus a method is needed that stabilizes training, personalizes updates, and remains feasible on resource-constrained devices.
AI.method: Threefold design: (1) BNN-based client models with uncertainty across hidden layers to stabilize training on small/noisy datasets; (2) meta-learning with adaptive learning rates for personalized local updates under non-IID conditions; (3) unified probabilistic-personalized design for robust global aggregation. The paper provides convergence analysis and an upper bound on the global model over communication rounds, and evaluates computational costs for edge/IOT deployment.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet shows Meta-BayFL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art FL baselines (e.g., pFedMe, Ditto, FedFomo), achieving up to 7.42% higher test accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a novel integration of Bayesian uncertainty modeling and meta-learning to enhance personalization and robustness in FL. Demonstrates theoretical convergence properties and practical cost considerations, and validates improved accuracy on standard benchmarks, with implications for edge/deployment feasibility.

title: Consumer-to-Clinical Language Shifts in Ambient AI Draft Notes and Clinician-Finalized Documentation: A Multi-level Analysis
summary: Ambient AI generates draft clinical notes from patient-clinician conversations, often using lay or consumer-oriented phrasing to support patient understanding instead of standardized clinical terminology. How clinicians revise these drafts for professional documentation conventions remains unclear. We quantified clinician editing for consumer-to- clinical normalization using a dictionary-confirmed transformation framework. We analyzed 71,173 AI-draft and finalized-note section pairs from 34,726 encounters. Confirmed transformations were defined as replacing a consumer expression with its dictionary-mapped clinical equivalent in the same section. Editing significantly reduced terminology density across all sections (p < 0.001). The Assessment and Plan accounted for the largest transformation volume (59.3%). Our analysis identified 7,576 transformation events across 4,114 note sections (5.8%), representing 1.2% consumer-term deletions. Transformation intensity varied across individual clinicians (p < 0.001). Overall, clinician post-editing demonstrates consistent shifts from conversational phrasing toward standardized, section- appropriate clinical terminology, supporting section-aware ambient AI design.

AI.tldr: Clinician post-editing of AI-drafted notes systematically normalizes consumer, lay-language terms to standard clinical terminology, with notable section-specific patterns and inter-clinician variability.
AI.motivation: To understand how clinicians revise AI-generated, consumer-oriented drafts into professional documentation and to assess whether ambient AI design should account for document structure.
AI.method: Retrospective analysis of 71,173 AI-draft vs. finalized-note section pairs from 34,726 encounters. Used a dictionary-confirmed transformation framework to identify confirmed transformations (consumer expression → dictionary-mapped clinical equivalent within the same section). Quantified transformation events, sections affected, and deletions; evaluated across sections and individual clinicians; statistical testing (p-values) for terminology density and variability.
AI.result: Editing reduced terminology density across all sections (p<0.001). The Assessment and Plan (A&P) section accounted for the largest share of transformations (59.3%). 7,576 transformation events across 4,114 note sections (5.8%), representing 1.2% consumer-term deletions. Transformation intensity varied across clinicians (p<0.001).
AI.conclusion: Clinician post-editing yields a consistent shift from conversational phrasing to section-appropriate clinical terminology, supporting section-aware ambient AI design.

title: Multi-material Direct Ink Writing and Embroidery for Stretchable Wearable Sensors
summary: The development of wearable sensing systems for sports performance tracking, rehabilitation, and injury prevention has driven growing demand for smart garments that combine comfort, durability, and accurate motion detection. This paper presents a textile-compatible fabrication workflow that integrates multi-material direct ink writing with automated embroidery to create stretchable strain sensors directly embedded into garments. The process combines sequential multi-material printing of a silicone-carbon grease-silicone stack with automated embroidery that provides both mechanical fixation and electrical interfacing in a single step. The resulting hybrid sensor demonstrates stretchability up to 120% strain while maintaining electrical continuity, with approximately linear behaviour up to 60% strain (R^2 = 0.99), a gauge factor of 31.4, and hysteresis of 22.9%. Repeated loading-unloading tests over 80 cycles show baseline and peak drift of 0.135% and 0.236% per cycle, respectively, indicating moderate cycle-to-cycle stability. Mechanical testing further confirms that the silicone-fabric interface remains intact under large deformation, with failure occurring in the textile rather than at the stitched boundary. As a preliminary proof of concept, the sensor was integrated into wearable elbow and knee sleeves for joint angle monitoring, showing a clear correlation between normalised resistance change and bending angle. By addressing both mechanical fixation and electrical interfacing through embroidery-based integration, this approach provides a reproducible and scalable pathway for incorporating printed stretchable electronics into textile systems for motion capture and soft robotic applications.

AI.tldr: A textile-compatible workflow combines multi-material direct ink writing with automated embroidery to embed stretchable strain sensors in garments, enabling scalable motion capture and soft robotics applications.
AI.motivation: To enable comfortable, durable, and accurate motion sensing in wearables by integrating printed stretchable electronics directly into textiles with a reproducible manufacturing approach.
AI.method: Sequential printing of a silicone-carbon grease-silicone stack followed by automated embroidery that serves as mechanical fixation and electrical interfacing, creating a hybrid sensor embedded in fabric and tested on mechanical and functional wearable prototypes.
AI.result: Sensors stretch up to 120% with near-linear behavior up to 60% strain (R^2=0.99), gauge factor 31.4, hysteresis 22.9%. Over 80 load–unload cycles show minimal drift (baseline 0.135%/cycle; peak 0.236%/cycle). The silicone–fabric interface remains intact; failure occurs in fabric rather than stitched boundary. Demonstrated in elbow/knee sleeves with correlation between resistance change and bending angle.
AI.conclusion: Embroidery-based integration provides simultaneous mechanical fixation and electrical interfacing in a scalable manufacturing route for textilized stretchable electronics, enabling motion capture and soft-robotic applications.

title: LRConv-NeRV: Low Rank Convolution for Efficient Neural Video Compression
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Neural Representations for Videos (NeRV) encode entire video sequences within neural network parameters, offering an alternative paradigm to conventional video codecs. However, the convolutional decoder of NeRV remains computationally expensive and memory intensive, limiting its deployment in resource-constrained environments. This paper proposes LRConv-NeRV, an efficient NeRV variant that replaces selected dense 3x3 convolutional layers with structured low-rank separable convolutions, trained end-to-end within the decoder architecture. By progressively applying low-rank factorization from the largest to earlier decoder stages, LRConv-NeRV enables controllable trade-offs between reconstruction quality and efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that applying LRConv only to the final decoder stage reduces decoder complexity by 68%, from 201.9 to 64.9 GFLOPs, and model size by 9.3%, while incurring negligible quality loss and achieving approximately 9.2% bitrate reduction. Under INT8 post-training quantization, LRConv-NeRV preserves reconstruction quality close to the dense NeRV baseline, whereas more aggressive factorization of early decoder stages leads to disproportionate quality degradation. Compared to existing work under layer-aligned settings, LRConv-NeRV achieves a more favorable efficiency versus quality trade-off, offering substantial GFLOPs and parameter reductions while maintaining higher PSNR/MS-SSIM and improved temporal stability. Temporal flicker analysis using LPIPS further shows that the proposed solution preserves temporal coherence close to the NeRV baseline, results establish LRConv-NeRV as a potential architectural alternative for efficient neural video decoding under low-precision and resource-constrained settings.

AI.tldr: LRConv-NeRV introduces structured low-rank separable convolutions into NeRV's decoder, enabling controllable quality–efficiency trade-offs by progressively factorizing from the largest to earlier decoder stages. Final-stage factorization yields substantial reductions in FLOPs and parameter count with negligible quality loss and bitrate gains; aggressive early-stage factorization degrades quality. INT8 quantization preserves quality near the dense baseline. Under layer-aligned comparisons, it offers a favorable efficiency–quality balance and improved temporal stability (LPIPS), suggesting it as a viable architecture for efficient neural video decoding in resource-constrained settings.
AI.motivation: NeRV encodes videos within neural network parameters, but the convolutional decoder (dense 3x3 convs) is computationally expensive and memory-intensive, hindering deployment on resource-constrained devices. A more efficient decoding architecture is needed without sacrificing video quality.
AI.method: Replace selected dense 3x3 convolutional layers with structured low-rank separable convolutions, trained end-to-end within the NeRV decoder. Apply low-rank factorization progressively from the largest decoder blocks toward earlier stages, enabling controllable trade-offs. Evaluate by comparing decoder FLOPs, model size, PSNR/MS-SSIM, bitrate, INT8 quantization effects, LPIPS-based temporal flicker, and against layer-aligned baselines.
AI.result: Applying LRConv only to the final decoder stage yields a 68% reduction in decoder GFLOPs (201.9 → 64.9 GFLOPs) and ~9.3% reduction in model size, with negligible quality loss and ~9.2% bitrate reduction. Under INT8 post-training quantization, reconstruction quality remains close to the dense NeRV baseline; more aggressive factorization of early stages causes disproportionate quality degradation. Compared with layer-aligned methods, LRConv-NeRV offers a more favorable efficiency–quality trade-off, maintaining higher PSNR/MS-SSIM and improved temporal stability. LPIPS-based temporal flicker analysis shows coherence near the NeRV baseline. Overall, LRConv-NeRV is a promising approach for efficient neural video decoding in low-precision and resource-constrained environments.
AI.conclusion: LRConv-NeRV provides a viable architectural alternative for efficient neural video decoding by introducing structured low-rank convolutions in a progressive, stage-aware manner. Final-stage factorization offers substantial efficiency gains with minimal quality loss, while early-stage factorization should be avoided or limited to preserve quality. The method demonstrates strong potential under low-precision regimes and could enable practical deployment on resource-constrained hardware.

title: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning Fine-Tuning with an Online Refiner
summary: Constraints are essential for stabilizing reinforcement learning fine-tuning (RFT) and preventing degenerate outputs, yet they inherently conflict with the optimization objective because stronger constraints limit the ability of a fine-tuned model to discover better solutions. We propose \textit{dynamic constraints} that resolve this tension by adapting to the evolving capabilities of the fine-tuned model based on the insight that constraints should only intervene when degenerate outputs occur. We implement this by using a reference model as an \textit{online refiner} that takes the response from the fine-tuned model and generates a minimally corrected version which preserves correct content verbatim while fixing errors. A supervised fine-tuning loss then trains the fine-tuned model to produce the refined output. This mechanism yields a constraint that automatically strengthens or relaxes based on output quality. Experiments on dialogue and code generation show that dynamic constraints outperform both KL regularization and unconstrained baselines, achieving substantially higher task rewards while maintaining training stability.

AI.tldr: Introduces dynamic, adaptive constraints for reinforcement-learning fine-tuning (RFT) that adjust with model capability using an online refiner to minimally fix outputs while preserving content, yielding higher rewards and stability than KL or no constraints.
AI.motivation: Constraints stabilize RFT but can hinder optimization; need a mechanism that strengthens constraints only when outputs degrade, allowing the model to explore better solutions during learning.
AI.method: Use a reference model as an online refiner that takes the fine-tuned model's output and produces a minimally corrected version preserving content verbatim but fixing errors. Train the fine-tuned model with a supervised loss to imitate the refined output, resulting in a constraint that tightens or relaxes based on output quality.
AI.result: Dynamic constraints outperform KL regularization and unconstrained baselines on dialogue and code-generation tasks, achieving substantially higher task rewards while maintaining training stability.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, quality-driven constraints reconcile the competing objectives of constraint enforcement and optimization, enabling improved performance and stability in RFT.

title: FaithSteer-BENCH: A Deployment-Aligned Stress-Testing Benchmark for Inference-Time Steering
summary: Inference-time steering is widely regarded as a lightweight and parameter-free mechanism for controlling large language model (LLM) behavior, and prior work has often suggested that simple activation-level interventions can reliably induce targeted behavioral changes. However, such conclusions are typically drawn under relatively relaxed evaluation settings that overlook deployment constraints, capability trade-offs, and real-world robustness. We therefore introduce \textbf{FaithSteer-BENCH}, a stress-testing benchmark that evaluates steering methods at a fixed deployment-style operating point through three gate-wise criteria: controllability, utility preservation, and robustness. Across multiple models and representative steering approaches, we uncover several systematic failure modes that are largely obscured under standard evaluation, including illusory controllability, measurable cognitive tax on unrelated capabilities, and substantial brittleness under mild instruction-level perturbations, role prompts, encoding transformations, and data scarcity. Gate-wise benchmark results show that existing methods do not necessarily provide reliable controllability in deployment-oriented practical settings. In addition, mechanism-level diagnostics indicate that many steering methods induce prompt-conditional alignment rather than stable latent directional shifts, further explaining their fragility under stress. FaithSteer-BENCH therefore provides a unified benchmark and a clearer analytical lens for future method design, reliability evaluation, and deployment-oriented research in steering.

AI.tldr: FaithSteer-BENCH is a deployment-oriented gate-wise stress-test benchmark for LLM steering, revealing that existing activation-level methods often fail to provide reliable controllability, while causing cognitive costs and fragility; provides a unified evaluation framework for deployment reliability.
AI.motivation: Prior evaluations of inference-time steering ignore deployment constraints, capability trade-offs, and real-world robustness; a stress-test bench is needed to quantify controllability, utility preservation, and robustness.
AI.method: Introduce FaithSteer-BENCH with three gate-wise criteria (controllability, utility preservation, robustness); evaluate across multiple models and steering approaches; perform mechanism-level diagnostics to distinguish stable latent directional shifts from prompt-conditioned alignment.
AI.result: Reveals systematic failure modes: illusory controllability, cognitive tax on unrelated capabilities, and brittleness under instruction perturbations, role prompts, encoding transformations, and data scarcity; existing methods do not guarantee controllability in deployment settings; diagnostics suggest steering is often prompt-conditioned rather than producing stable latent direction shifts.
AI.conclusion: FaithSteer-BENCH provides a unified benchmark and analytical lens for future deployment-oriented steering research, guiding reliable method design and evaluation.

title: Contact Status Recognition and Slip Detection with a Bio-inspired Tactile Hand
summary: Stable and reliable grasp is critical to robotic manipulations especially for fragile and glazed objects, where the grasp force requires precise control as too large force possibly damages the objects while small force leads to slip and fall-off. Although it is assumed the objects to manipulate is grasped firmly in advance, slip detection and timely prevention are necessary for a robot in unstructured and universal environments. In this work, we addressed this issue by utilizing multimodal tactile feedback from a five-fingered bio-inspired hand. Motivated by human hands, the tactile sensing elements were distributed and embedded into the soft skin of robotic hand, forming 24 tactile channels in total. Different from the threshold method that was widely employed in most existing works, we converted the slip detection problem to contact status recognition in combination with binning technique first and then detected the slip onset time according to the recognition results. After the 24-channel tactile signals passed through discrete wavelet transform, 17 features were extracted from different time and frequency bands. With the optimal 120 features employed for status recognition, the test accuracy reached 96.39% across three different sliding speeds and six kinds of materials. When applied to four new unseen materials, a high accuracy of 91.95% was still achieved, which further validated the generalization of our proposed method. Finally, the performance of slip detection is verified based on the trained model of contact status recognition.

AI.tldr: Multimodal tactile slip detection on a 5-finger bio-inspired hand using 24 tactile channels. Converts slip detection to contact status recognition with binning; uses discrete wavelet transform to extract features, selecting 120 features for status recognition. Achieves high accuracy: 96.39% on varied speeds/materials; 91.95% on unseen materials, suggesting good generalization.
AI.motivation: Robust, reliable grasp for fragile/glazed objects requires early slip detection to prevent object damage or drop risk in unstructured environments; traditional threshold-based slip detection is insufficient. The work seeks proactive slip prevention using rich tactile sensing.
AI.method: Hardware: five-finger bio-inspired hand with soft skin embedding 24 tactile channels. Signal processing: 24-channel tactile data pass through discrete wavelet transform; feature extraction yields 17 features from time/frequency bands (inconsistently stated); a binning-based contact-status recognition framework replaces simple thresholding. Feature selection results in an optimal set (stated as 120 features) used for status recognition. Slip onset time is inferred from recognition results of contact status.
AI.result: Achieves test accuracy of 96.39% across three sliding speeds and six materials. Generalizes to four unseen materials with accuracy 91.95%. Validates slip detection performance via the trained contact-status model.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates high-accuracy slip detection by reframing slip as contact-status recognition and leveraging wavelet-based feature extraction on multimodal tactile data. It shows good generalization to unseen materials, supporting reliable real-time slip prevention in unstructured environments.

title: CycleCap: Improving VLMs Captioning Performance via Self-Supervised Cycle Consistency Fine-Tuning
summary: Visual-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in image captioning, visual question answering, and visual reasoning. Yet they remain prone to vision-language misalignment, often producing overly generic or hallucinated descriptions. Existing approaches address this via instruction tuning-requiring costly, large-scale annotated datasets or via complex test-time frameworks for caption refinement. In this work, we revisit image-text alignment through the lens of cycle consistency: given an image and a caption generated by an image-to-text model, the backward mapping through a text-to-image model should reconstruct an image that closely matches the original. In our setup, a VLM serves as the image-to-text component, while a pre-trained text-to-image model closes the loop by reconstructing the image from the generated caption. Building on this, we introduce CycleCap, a fine-tuning scheme to improve image captioning using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a reward based on the similarity between the original and reconstructed images, computed on-the-fly. Unlike previous work that uses cycle consistency loss for preference dataset construction, our method leverages cycle consistency directly as a self-supervised training signal. This enables the use of raw images alone, eliminating the need for curated image-text datasets, while steering the VLM to produce more accurate and grounded text descriptions. Applied to four VLMs ranging from 1B to 7B parameters, CycleCap yields consistent improvements across captioning and hallucination benchmarks, surpassing state-of-the-art methods that rely on supervised cycle consistency training.

AI.tldr: CycleCap introduces a self-supervised cycle-consistency framework for image captioning. It uses a VLM for image-to-text and a pretrained text-to-image model to reconstruct the image from the caption, optimizing captions with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) using a reward based on on-the-fly similarity between the original and reconstructed images. This eliminates the need for curated image-text data and yields gains on captioning and grounding/hallucination benchmarks across 1B–7B-parameter VLMs, surpassing supervised cycle-consistency baselines.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models suffer misalignment and hallucinations. Existing cycle-consistency approaches rely on costly annotations or complex test-time refinements. A self-supervised cycle-consistency signal using raw images can align image-text pairs without labeled data.
AI.method: A VLM serves as the image-to-text component and a pretrained text-to-image model reconstructs the image from the generated caption, forming a cycle. CycleCap then fine-tunes via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a reward proportional to the similarity between the original image and the reconstructed image computed on-the-fly.
AI.result: Applied to four VLMs ranging from 1B to 7B parameters, CycleCap yields consistent improvements on captioning and hallucination benchmarks and surpasses state-of-the-art methods that rely on supervised cycle-consistency training.
AI.conclusion: CycleCap demonstrates that self-supervised cycle-consistency signals can effectively improve image captioning and grounding across diverse VLM sizes, reducing reliance on curated datasets while achieving strong performance gains.

title: ARTEMIS: A Neuro Symbolic Framework for Economically Constrained Market Dynamics
summary: Deep learning models in quantitative finance often operate as black boxes, lacking interpretability and failing to incorporate fundamental economic principles such as no-arbitrage constraints. This paper introduces ARTEMIS (Arbitrage-free Representation Through Economic Models and Interpretable Symbolics), a novel neuro-symbolic framework combining a continuous-time Laplace Neural Operator encoder, a neural stochastic differential equation regularised by physics-informed losses, and a differentiable symbolic bottleneck that distils interpretable trading rules. The model enforces economic plausibility via two novel regularisation terms: a Feynman-Kac PDE residual penalising local no-arbitrage violations, and a market price of risk penalty bounding the instantaneous Sharpe ratio. We evaluate ARTEMIS against six strong baselines on four datasets: Jane Street, Optiver, Time-IMM, and DSLOB (a synthetic crash regime). Results demonstrate ARTEMIS achieves state-of-the-art directional accuracy, outperforming all baselines on DSLOB (64.96%) and Time-IMM (96.0%). A comprehensive ablation study confirms each component's contribution: removing the PDE loss reduces directional accuracy from 64.89% to 50.32%. Underperformance on Optiver is attributed to its long sequence length and volatility-focused target. By providing interpretable, economically grounded predictions, ARTEMIS bridges the gap between deep learning's power and the transparency demanded in quantitative finance.

AI.tldr: ARTEMIS is a neuro-symbolic, arbitrage-aware framework for finance that blends a continuous-time Laplace Neural Operator encoder, a neural SDE regularized by physics-informed losses, and a differentiable symbolic bottleneck to distill interpretable trading rules, enforcing no-arbitrage and risk-based constraints.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of interpretability and economic grounding in deep learning models for quantitative finance, ensuring consistency with no-arbitrage principles and market risk considerations.
AI.method: A three-part architecture: (1) a continuous-time Laplace Neural Operator encoder to map market data to latent representations, (2) a neural stochastic differential equation regularized by physics-informed losses including a Feynman-Kac PDE residual to penalize local no-arbitrage violations and a market price of risk penalty bounding the instantaneous Sharpe ratio, and (3) a differentiable symbolic bottleneck that extracts interpretable trading rules from the learned representations.
AI.result: ARTEMIS outperforms six strong baselines on four datasets (Jane Street, Optiver, Time-IMM, DSLOB). It achieves state-of-the-art directional accuracy, notably 64.96% on DSLOB and 96.0% on Time-IMM. An ablation shows removing the PDE loss drops accuracy from 64.89% to 50.32%; Optiver underperforms due to long sequence length and volatility-focused targets.
AI.conclusion: ARTEMIS provides interpretable, economically grounded predictions that reconcile deep learning power with the transparency demanded in quantitative finance, demonstrating that physics-informed, arbitrage-aware neuro-symbolic models can achieve strong predictive performance.

title: MemArchitect: A Policy Driven Memory Governance Layer
comment: This is an on going research work and will be updated periodically
summary:
Persistent Large Language Model (LLM) agents expose a critical governance gap in memory management. Standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) frameworks treat memory as passive storage, lacking mechanisms to resolve contradictions, enforce privacy, or prevent outdated information ("zombie memories") from contaminating the context window.
  We introduce MemArchitect, a governance layer that decouples memory lifecycle management from model weights. MemArchitect enforces explicit, rule-based policies, including memory decay, conflict resolution, and privacy controls.
  We demonstrate that governed memory consistently outperforms unmanaged memory in agentic settings, highlighting the necessity of structured memory governance for reliable and safe autonomous systems.

AI.tldr: MemArchitect provides a governance layer for LLM memory, decoupling memory lifecycle from model weights and enforcing rule-based policies to manage decay, conflicts, and privacy, improving reliability and safety in agentic systems.
AI.motivation: In standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), memory is treated as passive storage, which can harbor contradictions, privacy violations, and outdated information that pollutes context ("zombie memories"), undermining persistent agent reliability.
AI.method: Introduce MemArchitect as a governance layer that operates independently of model weights and implements explicit, rule-based memory policies—such as memory decay, conflict resolution, and privacy controls—to manage the memory lifecycle.
AI.result: Governed memory consistently outperforms unmanaged memory in agentic settings, indicating that structured memory governance yields better consistency, privacy adherence, and safety for autonomous agents.
AI.conclusion: Structured memory governance is necessary for reliable and safe autonomous systems; future work could explore broader policy families, cross-domain evaluations, and deeper integration with dynamic memory sources.

title: Graph-of-Constraints Model Predictive Control for Reactive Multi-agent Task and Motion Planning
comment: 8 main content pages, 4 main content figures, camera ready version submitted to IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Sequences of interdependent geometric constraints are central to many multi-agent Task and Motion Planning (TAMP) problems. However, existing methods for handling such constraint sequences struggle with partially ordered tasks and dynamic agent assignments. They typically assume static assignments and cannot adapt when disturbances alter task allocations. To overcome these limitations, we introduce Graph-of-Constraints Model Predictive Control (GoC-MPC), a generalized sequence-of-constraints framework integrated with MPC. GoC-MPC naturally supports partially ordered tasks, dynamic agent coordination, and disturbance recovery. By defining constraints over tracked 3D keypoints, our method robustly solves diverse multi-agent manipulation tasks-coordinating agents and adapting online from visual observations alone, without relying on training data or environment models. Experiments demonstrate that GoC-MPC achieves higher success rates, significantly faster TAMP computation, and shorter overall paths compared to recent baselines, establishing it as an efficient and robust solution for multi-agent manipulation under real-world disturbances. Our supplementary video and code can be found at https://sites.google.com/view/goc-mpc/home .

AI.tldr: GoC-MPC: a generalized Graph-of-Constraints Model Predictive Control framework for multi-agent manipulation that handles partially ordered tasks and dynamic allocations via constraints on tracked 3D keypoints, achieving faster, more robust TAMP without training data or env models.
AI.motivation: Traditional constraint-sequence methods struggle with partial task orders and disturbances due to static allocations; there is a need for a flexible framework that adapts online to disturbances and dynamic agent roles.
AI.method: Introduce Graph-of-Constraints MPC (GoC-MPC), integrating a graph-structured set of constraints over tracked 3D keypoints within a Model Predictive Control loop. It supports partial task ordering, dynamic multi-agent coordination, and disturbance recovery, leveraging visual observations only and avoiding training data or explicit environment models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows GoC-MPC yields higher success rates, faster TAMP computation, and shorter overall paths compared with recent baselines, demonstrating robustness to real-world disturbances.
AI.conclusion: GoC-MPC offers an efficient, robust solution for coordination-rich multi-agent manipulation under disturbances, reducing reliance on environment models or training data and enabling online adaptation from visual input.

title: Fast and Generalizable NeRF Architecture Selection for Satellite Scene Reconstruction
summary: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) have emerged as a powerful approach for photorealistic 3D reconstruction from multi-view images. However, deploying NeRF for satellite imagery remains challenging. Each scene requires individual training, and optimizing architectures via Neural Architecture Search (NAS) demands hours to days of GPU time. While existing approaches focus on architectural improvements, our SHAP analysis reveals that multi-view consistency, rather than model architecture, determines reconstruction quality. Based on this insight, we develop PreSCAN, a predictive framework that estimates NeRF quality prior to training using lightweight geometric and photometric descriptors. PreSCAN selects suitable architectures in < 30 seconds with < 1 dB prediction error, achieving 1000$\times$ speedup over NAS. We further demonstrate PreSCAN's deployment utility on edge platforms (Jetson Orin), where combining its predictions with offline cost profiling reduces inference power by 26% and latency by 43% with minimal quality loss. Experiments on DFC2019 datasets confirm that PreSCAN generalizes across diverse satellite scenes without retraining.

AI.tldr: SHAP-guided PreSCAN forecasts NeRF training quality from lightweight descriptors, revealing multi-view consistency as the key driver for satellite imagery; enables fast, generalizable architecture selection with NAS-level savings and strong edge deployment gains.
AI.motivation: NAS for NeRF on satellite imagery is prohibitively expensive. The paper argues that model architecture is less critical than ensuring multi-view consistency, and aims to predict NeRF quality before training to accelerate architecture search and deployment.
AI.method: Apply SHAP analysis to identify factors affecting NeRF reconstruction quality; develop PreSCAN, a predictor using lightweight geometric and photometric descriptors to estimate NeRF quality prior to training; use predictions to select architectures from a search space in under 30 seconds with <1 dB error; validate on edge devices and across satellite scenes (DFC2019) without retraining.
AI.result: PreSCAN achieves <1 dB prediction error and predicts NeRF quality quickly (<30 seconds); delivers ~1000x speedup over NAS; on Jetson Orin, combining predictions with offline cost profiling reduces inference power by 26% and latency by 43% with minimal quality loss; generalizes across diverse satellite scenes without retraining on DFC2019 datasets.
AI.conclusion: Multi-view consistency drives NeRF quality more than architectural changes in satellite imagery. PreSCAN offers a fast, generalizable predictor for architecture selection, enabling efficient edge deployment and practical NAS-level gains without retraining.

title: BoundAD: Boundary-Aware Negative Generation for Time Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Contrastive learning methods for time series anomaly detection (TSAD) heavily depend on the quality of negative sample construction. However, existing strategies based on random perturbations or pseudo-anomaly injection often struggle to simultaneously preserve temporal semantic consistency and provide effective decision-boundary supervision. Most existing methods rely on prior anomaly injection, while overlooking the potential of generating hard negatives near the data manifold boundary directly from normal samples themselves. To address this issue, we propose a reconstruction-driven boundary negative generation framework that automatically constructs hard negatives through the reconstruction process of normal samples. Specifically, the method first employs a reconstruction network to capture normal temporal patterns, and then introduces a reinforcement learning strategy to adaptively adjust the optimization update magnitude according to the current reconstruction state. In this way, boundary-shifted samples close to the normal data manifold can be induced along the reconstruction trajectory and further used for subsequent contrastive representation learning. Unlike existing methods that depend on explicit anomaly injection, the proposed framework does not require predefined anomaly patterns, but instead mines more challenging boundary negatives from the model's own learning dynamics. Experimental results show that the proposed method effectively improves anomaly representation learning and achieves competitive detection performance on the current dataset.

AI.tldr: Reconstruction-driven boundary negative generation for TSAD uses a normal-sample reconstruction network plus reinforcement learning to shift training negatives toward the normal data boundary, enabling hard negatives without explicit anomaly injection and achieving competitive detection performance.
AI.motivation: Hard negatives are crucial for contrastive TSAD but traditional perturbations or pseudo-anomaly injections can violate temporal semantics or misrepresent boundary hardness. Generating hard negatives directly from normal samples via the reconstruction process aims to produce temporally coherent, near-boundary negatives.
AI.method: 1) Train a reconstruction network to capture normal temporal patterns. 2) Employ reinforcement learning to adaptively adjust update magnitudes based on the reconstruction state, inducing boundary-shifted samples along the reconstruction trajectory. 3) Use these boundary negatives in contrastive representation learning for anomaly detection. 4) No predefined anomaly patterns required; negatives are mined from the model’s own learning dynamics.
AI.result: Experimental results show improved anomaly representation learning and competitive detection performance on the current dataset, demonstrating the effectiveness of boundary-negative mining and the RL-guided update strategy compared to baselines relying on random perturbations or explicit anomaly injection.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively mines harder negatives from normal data dynamics during reconstruction, enhancing contrastive TSAD without explicit anomaly patterns and achieving competitive detection performance.

title: Understanding the Theoretical Foundations of Deep Neural Networks through Differential Equations
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved remarkable empirical success, yet the absence of a principled theoretical foundation continues to hinder their systematic development. In this survey, we present differential equations as a theoretical foundation for understanding, analyzing, and improving DNNs. We organize the discussion around three guiding questions: i) how differential equations offer a principled understanding of DNN architectures, ii) how tools from differential equations can be used to improve DNN performance in a principled way, and iii) what real-world applications benefit from grounding DNNs in differential equations. We adopt a two-fold perspective spanning the model level, which interprets the whole DNN as a differential equation, and the layer level, which models individual DNN components as differential equations. From these two perspectives, we review how this framework connects model design, theoretical analysis, and performance improvement. We further discuss real-world applications, as well as key challenges and opportunities for future research.

AI.tldr: This survey argues that differential equations provide a principled foundation for DNNs, treating the network and its layers as differential equations to unify design, analysis, and improvement, with real-world applications and open challenges.
AI.motivation: To supply a principled theoretical basis for DNNs by leveraging differential equations, addressing gaps in understanding, design guidance, and applicability.
AI.method: Adopts a two-level framework: model level (the whole DNN as a differential equation) and layer level (individual components as differential equations). Reviews literature, analyzes how the framework links architecture, theory, and performance, and discusses applications, challenges, and future opportunities.
AI.result: Clarifies how differential-equation perspectives explain architectural choices and enable principled performance enhancements; provides design-and-analysis guidance and highlights real-world applications, along with identified challenges and future directions.
AI.conclusion: Differential equations provide a unifying framework that connects model design, theoretical analysis, and practical performance in DNNs, with potential to guide principled improvements and expand real-world impact; future work should address key challenges and opportunities in this direction.

title: Efficient and Versatile Quadrupedal Skating: Optimal Co-design via Reinforcement Learning and Bayesian Optimization
summary: In this paper, we present a hardware-control co-design approach that enables efficient and versatile roller skating on quadrupedal robots equipped with passive wheels. Passive-wheel skating reduces leg inertia and improves energy efficiency, particularly at high speeds. However, the absence of direct wheel actuation tightly couples mechanical design and control. To unlock the full potential of this modality, we formulate a bilevel optimization framework: an upper-level Bayesian Optimization searches the mechanical design space, while a lower-level Reinforcement Learning trains a motor control policy for each candidate design. The resulting design-policy pairs not only outperform human-engineered baselines, but also exhibit versatile behaviors such as hockey stop (rapid braking by turning sideways to maximize friction) and self-aligning motion (automatic reorientation to improve energy efficiency in the direction of travel), offering the first system-level study of dynamic skating motion on quadrupedal robots.

AI.tldr: A bilevel hardware-control co-design framework enables passive-wheel skating on quadrupedal robots by combining Bayesian optimization over mechanical design with RL-based control per design, yielding superior performance and novel skating behaviors.
AI.motivation: To realize efficient and versatile passive-wheel skating on quadrupedal robots by mitigating leg inertia and energy use, while addressing the tight coupling between mechanical design and control due to the absence of wheel actuation.
AI.method: Upper-level Bayesian Optimization searches the mechanical design space; for each candidate design, a lower-level Reinforcement Learning policy is trained to control the actuated components. The resulting design-policy pairs are evaluated and iterated.
AI.result: The design-policy pairs outperform human-engineered baselines and enable versatile skating behaviors such as hockey stop and self-aligning motion. This work constitutes the first system-level study of dynamic skating on quadrupedal robots.
AI.conclusion: A hardware-control co-design framework effectively leverages the interaction between design and control to unlock efficient passive-wheel skating, delivering improved performance and novel dynamic behaviors.

title: Unrolled Reconstruction with Integrated Super-Resolution for Accelerated 3D LGE MRI
summary: Accelerated 3D late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) MRI requires robust reconstruction methods to recover thin atrial structures from undersampled k-space data. While unrolled model-based networks effectively integrate physics-driven data consistency with learned priors, they operate at the acquired resolution and may fail to fully recover high-frequency detail. We propose a hybrid unrolled reconstruction framework in which an Enhanced Deep Super-Resolution (EDSR) network replaces the proximal operator within each iteration of the optimization loop, enabling joint super-resolution enhancement and data consistency enforcement. The model is trained end-to-end on retrospectively undersampled preclinical 3D LGE datasets and compared against compressed sensing, Model-Based Deep Learning (MoDL), and self-guided Deep Image Prior (DIP) baselines. Across acceleration factors, the proposed method consistently improves PSNR and SSIM over standard unrolled reconstruction and better preserves fine cardiac structures, leading to improved LA (left atrium) segmentation performance. These results demonstrate that integrating super-resolution priors directly within model-based reconstruction provides measurable gains in accelerated 3D LGE MRI.

AI.tldr: Hybrid unrolled MRI reconstruction uses an Enhanced Deep Super-Resolution (EDSR) proxy within each iteration to jointly enforce data consistency and super-resolution, boosting high-frequency detail in accelerated 3D LGE MRI and improving LA segmentation.
AI.motivation: To recover thin atrial structures in undersampled 3D LGE MRI where standard unrolled networks at acquired resolution miss high-frequency details, hindering precise LA segmentation.
AI.method: Replace the proximal operator in each unrolled optimization iteration with an EDSR network to perform joint super-resolution and data-consistency enforcement. Train end-to-end on retrospectively undersampled preclinical 3D LGE data. Compare against compressed sensing, MoDL, and self-guided DIP baselines across various acceleration factors.
AI.result: Consistent PSNR and SSIM gains over standard unrolled reconstruction across acceleration factors; better preservation of fine cardiac structures; improved LA segmentation performance.
AI.conclusion: Integrating super-resolution priors directly within model-based reconstruction yields measurable gains in accelerated 3D LGE MRI.

title: Tula: Optimizing Time, Cost, and Generalization in Distributed Large-Batch Training
summary: Distributed training increases the number of batches processed per iteration either by scaling-out (adding more nodes) or scaling-up (increasing the batch-size). However, the largest configuration does not necessarily yield the best performance. Horizontal scaling introduces additional communication overhead, while vertical scaling is constrained by computation cost and device memory limits. Thus, simply increasing the batch-size leads to diminishing returns: training time and cost decrease initially but eventually plateaus, creating a knee-point in the time/cost versus batch-size pareto curve. The optimal batch-size therefore depends on the underlying model, data and available compute resources. Large batches also suffer from worse model quality due to the well-known generalization gap. In this paper, we present Tula, an online service that automatically optimizes time, cost, and convergence quality for large-batch training of convolutional models. It combines parallel-systems modeling with statistical performance prediction to identify the optimal batch-size. Tula predicts training time and cost within 7.5-14% error across multiple models, and achieves up to 20x overall speedup and improves test accuracy by 9% on average over standard large-batch training on various vision tasks, thus successfully mitigating the generalization gap and accelerating training at the same time.

AI.tldr: Tula is an online service that auto-optimizes batch size for large-batch CNN training by combining system modeling with performance prediction, achieving up to 20x speedups and ~9% accuracy gains, with 7.5–14% prediction error.
AI.motivation: Large-batch training suffers diminishing returns due to communication overhead and memory limits; the optimal batch size depends on model, data, and compute resources, and large batches can hurt generalization. An automatic method to optimize time, cost, and convergence quality is desirable.
AI.method: Tula uses a hybrid approach that combines parallel-systems modeling with statistical performance prediction to identify the optimal batch size for large-batch training; it operates as an online service.
AI.result: The framework predicts training time and cost with 7.5–14% error across multiple models; achieves up to 20x overall speedup and improves test accuracy by ~9% on average across various vision tasks, mitigating the generalization gap.
AI.conclusion: Tula successfully mitigates the generalization gap while accelerating large-batch CNN training, demonstrating practical value for balancing time, cost, and convergence quality in distributed training.

title: Large-Scale Analysis of Political Propaganda on Moltbook
summary: We present an NLP-based study of political propaganda on Moltbook, a Reddit-style platform for AI agents. To enable large-scale analysis, we develop LLM-based classifiers to detect political propaganda, validated against expert annotation (Cohen's $κ$= 0.64-0.74). Using a dataset of 673,127 posts and 879,606 comments, we find that political propaganda accounts for 1% of all posts and 42% of all political content. These posts are concentrated in a small set of communities, with 70% of such posts falling into five of them. 4% of agents produced 51% of these posts. We further find that a minority of these agents repeatedly post highly similar content within and across communities. Despite this, we find limited evidence that comments amplify political propaganda.

AI.tldr: Propaganda on Moltbook is rare overall but accounts for a large share of political content and is driven by a small, repeat-poster minority; comments contribute little amplification. LLM-based classifiers, validated against experts, enable large-scale detection.
AI.motivation: Assess the scale, concentration, and dynamics of political propaganda on an AI-agent Reddit-style platform and demonstrate scalable detection via LLM classifiers validated against human coding.
AI.method: Train/validate LLM-based classifiers to detect political propaganda using expert annotations (Cohen's kappa 0.64–0.74). Apply classifiers to 673,127 posts and 879,606 comments to quantify prevalence, identify concentrated communities, map agent-level contribution, detect content repetition, and assess amplification through comments.
AI.result: Propaganda posts: ~1% of all posts but ~42% of political content. Concentrated in five communities (70% of propaganda). 4% of agents produce 51% of propaganda posts. A minority repeat highly similar content across communities. Comments show limited amplification of propaganda.
AI.conclusion: Propaganda on Moltbook is highly concentrated in a small user base and is characterized by content repetition; limited evidence that comments amplify propaganda. LLM-based detection enables scalable analysis of large-scale political content.

title: MemoAct: Atkinson-Shiffrin-Inspired Memory-Augmented Visuomotor Policy for Robotic Manipulation
summary: Memory-augmented robotic policies are essential in handling memory-dependent tasks. However, existing approaches typically rely on simple observation window extensions, struggling to simultaneously achieve precise task state tracking and robust long-horizon retention. To overcome these challenges, inspired by the Atkinson-Shiffrin memory model, we propose MemoAct, a hierarchical memory-based policy that leverages distinct memory tiers to tackle specific bottlenecks. Specifically, lossless short-term memory ensures precise task state tracking, while compressed long-term memory enables robust long-horizon retention. To enrich the evaluation landscape, we construct MemoryRTBench based on RoboTwin 2.0, specifically tailored to assess policy capabilities in task state tracking and long-horizon retention. Extensive experiments across simulated and real-world scenarios demonstrate that MemoAct achieves superior performance compared to both existing Markovian baselines and history-aware policies. The project page is \href{https://tlf-tlf.github.io/MemoActPage/}{available}.

AI.tldr: MemoAct introduces a hierarchical memory-based policy with lossless short-term memory for precise task-state tracking and compressed long-term memory for robust long-horizon retention, evaluated on MemoryRTBench built on RoboTwin 2.0, outperforming Markovian and history-aware baselines.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of simple observation-window methods and reliance on short-term histories in memory-dependent robotic tasks. Proposes a memory architecture inspired by the Atkinson-Shiffrin model to separate memory functions across tiers.
AI.method: Design of MemoAct with distinct memory tiers: lossless short-term memory for accurate state tracking and compressed long-term memory for long-horizon retention. Development of MemoryRTBench from RoboTwin 2.0 to specifically evaluate task-state tracking and long-horizon retention. Experimental evaluation across simulated and real-world scenarios.
AI.result: MemoAct achieves superior performance relative to both Markovian baselines and history-aware policies across tested tasks and settings.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical memory architecture, combining lossless short-term and compressed long-term memory, effectively addresses memory-related bottlenecks in robots, and the MemoryRTBench provides a targeted evaluation framework for memory-dependent policies.

title: VISTA: Validation-Guided Integration of Spatial and Temporal Foundation Models with Anatomical Decoding for Rare-Pathology VCE Event Detection
summary: Capsule endoscopy event detection is challenging because diagnostically relevant findings are sparse, visually heterogeneous, and embedded in long, noisy video streams, while evaluation is performed at the event level rather than by frame accuracy alone. We therefore formulate the RARE-VISION task as a metric-aligned event detection problem instead of a purely frame-wise classification task. Our framework combines two complementary backbones, EndoFM-LV for local temporal context and DINOv3 ViT-L/16 for strong frame-level visual semantics, followed by a Diverse Head Ensemble, Validation-Guided Hierarchical Fusion, and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding. The fusion stage uses validation-derived class-wise model weighting, backbone weighting, and probability calibration, while the decoding stage applies temporal smoothing, anatomical constraints, threshold refinement, and per-label event generation to produce stable event predictions. Validation ablations indicate that complementary backbones, validation-guided fusion, and anatomy-aware temporal decoding all contribute to event-level performance. On the official hidden test set, the proposed method achieved an overall temporal mAP@0.5 of 0.3530 and temporal mAP@0.95 of 0.3235.

AI.tldr: A metric-aligned event detection framework (RARE-VISION) for capsule endoscopy that fuses EndoFM-LV and DINOv3 ViT-L/16 with validation-guided fusion and anatomy-aware temporal decoding to produce stable event predictions; achieves temporal mAP@0.5 of 0.3530 and mAP@0.95 of 0.3235 on hidden test.
AI.motivation: Capsule endoscopy presents sparse, heterogeneous, and long noisy video streams. Evaluation is more meaningful at the event level than at frame accuracy, necessitating a metric-aligned approach to detect clinically relevant events.
AI.method: Two backbones: EndoFM-LV for local temporal context; DINOv3 ViT-L/16 for strong frame-level semantics. A Diverse Head Ensemble, Validation-Guided Hierarchical Fusion, and Anatomy-Aware Temporal Event Decoding compose the framework. Fusion uses validation-derived class-wise model and backbone weighting plus probability calibration. Decoding applies temporal smoothing, anatomical constraints, threshold refinement, and per-label event generation.
AI.result: Validation ablations show that complementary backbones, validation-guided fusion, and anatomy-aware temporal decoding contribute to event-level performance. On the official hidden test set, overall temporal mAP@0.5 = 0.3530 and temporal mAP@0.95 = 0.3235.
AI.conclusion: The framework advances metric-aligned event detection in capsule endoscopy by leveraging complementary backbones and guided fusion/decoding strategies, attaining stable event predictions and improved event-level performance.

title: VC-Soup: Value-Consistency Guided Multi-Value Alignment for Large Language Models
summary: As large language models (LLMs) increasingly shape content generation, interaction, and decision-making across the Web, aligning them with human values has become a central objective in trustworthy AI. This challenge becomes even more pronounced when aligning multiple, potentially conflicting human values. Although recent approaches, such as reward reweighting, prompt-based supervised fine-tuning, and model merging, attempt to tackle multi-value alignment, they still face two major limitations: (1) training separate models for each value combination is prohibitively expensive; (2) value conflicts substantially degrade alignment performance. These limitations make it difficult to achieve favorable trade-offs across diverse human values. To address these challenges, we revisit multi-value alignment from the perspective of value consistency in data and propose VC-soup, a data filtering and parameter merging framework grounded in value-consistent learning. We first design a value consistency metric based on the cosine similarity between the reward-gap vector of each preference pair and an all-ones vector, which quantifies its cross-value coherence. We then filter out low-consistency preference pairs in each value dataset and train on the remaining data to obtain smooth, value-consistent policy models that better preserve linear mode connectivity. Finally, we linearly combine these policies and apply Pareto filtering across values to obtain solutions with balanced multi-value performance. Extensive experiments and theoretical analysis demonstrate that VC-soup effectively mitigates conflicts and consistently outperforms existing multi-value alignment methods.

AI.tldr: VC-soup proposes a data-filtering and parameter-merging framework to achieve multi-value alignment for LLMs, by enforcing value-consistency in data, training value-consistent policies, preserving linear mode connectivity, and linearly combining policies with Pareto filtering to balance multiple values.
AI.motivation: Align LLMs with multiple human values while avoiding expensive training of separate models for each value combination and mitigating value conflicts that degrade performance.
AI.method: 1) Define a value-consistency metric as the cosine similarity between each value-relevant reward-gap vector and an all-ones vector. 2) Filter out low-consistency preference pairs within each value dataset. 3) Train on the remaining data to obtain value-consistent policies that preserve linear mode connectivity. 4) Linearly combine the resulting policies and apply Pareto filtering across values to achieve balanced multi-value performance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and theoretical analysis show that VC-soup mitigates cross-value conflicts and consistently outperforms existing multi-value alignment methods.
AI.conclusion: A data-centric framework that enforces value consistency during data curation and policy merging can substantially improve multi-value alignment, offering scalable trade-offs across competing human values without training separate models for each value combination.

title: Interpretability without actionability: mechanistic methods cannot correct language model errors despite near-perfect internal representations
comment: 27 pages, 5 figures, 10 tables. Code available at https://github.com/sanjaybasu/interpretability-triage
summary: Language models encode task-relevant knowledge in internal representations that far exceeds their output performance, but whether mechanistic interpretability methods can bridge this knowledge-action gap has not been systematically tested. We compared four mechanistic interpretability methods -- concept bottleneck steering (Steerling-8B), sparse autoencoder feature steering, logit lens with activation patching, and linear probing with truthfulness separator vector steering (Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct) -- for correcting false-negative triage errors using 400 physician-adjudicated clinical vignettes (144 hazards, 256 benign). Linear probes discriminated hazardous from benign cases with 98.2% AUROC, yet the model's output sensitivity was only 45.1%, a 53-percentage-point knowledge-action gap. Concept bottleneck steering corrected 20% of missed hazards but disrupted 53% of correct detections, indistinguishable from random perturbation (p=0.84). SAE feature steering produced zero effect despite 3,695 significant features. TSV steering at high strength corrected 24% of missed hazards while disrupting 6% of correct detections, but left 76% of errors uncorrected. Current mechanistic interpretability methods cannot reliably translate internal knowledge into corrected outputs, with implications for AI safety frameworks that assume interpretability enables effective error correction.

AI.tldr: Four mechanistic interpretability methods fail to reliably translate internal knowledge into corrected outputs for clinical triage tasks, revealing a substantial knowledge-action gap.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether mechanistic interpretability methods can bridge the knowledge-action gap in language models for safety-critical decision making.
AI.method: Comparison of four methods on 400 physician-adjudicated clinical vignettes (144 hazards, 256 benign): concept bottleneck steering (Steerling-8B), sparse autoencoder feature steering, logit lens with activation patching, and linear probing with truthfulness separator vector steering (Qwen 2.5 7B Instruct). Measured ability to correct false-negative hazard errors; metrics include AUROC for hazard discrimination and output sensitivity; assessed perturbation impact on correct detections.
AI.result: Linear probes discriminated hazardous from benign cases with 98.2% AUROC but model output sensitivity was only 45.1% (53-percentage-point knowledge-action gap). Concept bottleneck steering corrected 20% of missed hazards but disrupted 53% of correct detections (indistinguishable from random perturbation, p=0.84). SAE feature steering produced zero effect despite 3,695 significant features. TSV steering at high strength corrected 24% of missed hazards while disrupting 6% of correct detections; 76% of errors remained uncorrected.
AI.conclusion: Current mechanistic interpretability methods cannot reliably translate internal knowledge into corrected outputs; this challenges safety frameworks that assume interpretability enables effective error correction.

title: Robotic Agentic Platform for Intelligent Electric Vehicle Disassembly
summary: Electric vehicles (EV) create an urgent need for scalable battery recycling, yet disassembly of EV battery packs remains largely manual due to high design variability. We present our Robotic Agentic Platform for Intelligent Disassembly (RAPID), designed to investigate perception-driven manipulation, flexible automation, and AI-assisted robot programming in realistic recycling scenarios. The system integrates a gantry-mounted industrial manipulator, RGB-D perception, and an automated nut-running tool for fastener removal on a full-scale EV battery pack. An open-vocabulary object detection pipeline achieves 0.9757 mAP50, enabling reliable identification of screws, nuts, busbars, and other components. We experimentally evaluate (n=204) three one-shot fastener removal strategies: taught-in poses (97% success rate, 24 min duration), one-shot vision execution (57%, 29 min), and visual servoing (83%, 36 min), comparing success rate and disassembly time for the battery's top cover fasteners. To support flexible interaction, we introduce agentic AI specifications for robotic disassembly tasks, allowing LLM agents to translate high-level instructions into robot actions through structured tool interfaces and ROS services. We evaluate SmolAgents with GPT-4o-mini and Qwen 3.5 9B/4B on edge hardware. Tool-based interfaces achieve 100% task completion, while automatic ROS service discovery shows 43.3% failure rates, highlighting the need for structured robot APIs for reliable LLM-driven control. This open-source platform enables systematic investigation of human-robot collaboration, agentic robot programming, and increasingly autonomous disassembly workflows, providing a practical foundation for research toward scalable robotic battery recycling.

AI.tldr: RAPID is a perception-driven robotic platform for scalable EV battery disassembly, combining a gantry-mounted manipulator, RGB-D sensing, and an automated nut runner with open-vocabulary detection and agentic AI interfaces. It experimentally compares one-shot fastener removal strategies, demonstrates 100% task completion with tool-based interfaces, and reveals reliability gaps in ROS-based LLM control, all within an open-source framework for researching human-robot collaboration and autonomous workflows.
AI.motivation: To address the variability and scalability challenges in EV battery recycling by enabling flexible perception-driven manipulation, rapid programming via AI agents, and open interfaces for LLM-driven control, thereby accelerating research into automated battery disassembly.
AI.method: System integrates a gantry-mounted industrial manipulator, RGB-D perception, and automated fastener tool. It uses an open-vocabulary object detector (0.9757 mAP50) to identify screws, nuts, busbars, etc. Compares three one-shot fastener removal strategies (taught-in poses, one-shot vision, visual servoing) on n=204 trials for top-cover fasteners. Introduces agentic AI specifications and uses SmolAgents with GPT-4o-mini and Qwen models on edge hardware to translate high-level instructions into robot actions via tool interfaces and ROS services. Assesses 100% task completion for tool-based interfaces and 43.3% ROS discovery failure.
AI.result: Open-vocabulary detector achieves 0.9757 mAP50. Fastener removal success: taught-in poses 97% (24 min), one-shot vision 57% (29 min), visual servoing 83% (36 min). Tool-based interfaces yield 100% task completion; automatic ROS service discovery shows 43.3% failure. The platform enables investigation of agentic programming and human-robot collaboration in disassembly tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents an open-source RAPID platform enabling systematic study of perception-driven disassembly, agentic robotics, and LLN-driven control for scalable robotic battery recycling, while highlighting API reliability needs and the value of structured interfaces for robust LLM-enabled robotics.

title: To See or To Please: Uncovering Visual Sycophancy and Split Beliefs in VLMs
summary: When VLMs answer correctly, do they genuinely rely on visual information or exploit language shortcuts? We introduce the Tri-Layer Diagnostic Framework, which disentangles hallucination sources via three metrics: Latent Anomaly Detection (perceptual awareness), Visual Necessity Score (visual dependency, measured via KL divergence), and Competition Score (conflict between visual grounding and instruction following). Using counterfactual interventions (blind, noise, and conflict images) across 7 VLMs and 7,000 model-sample pairs, our taxonomy reveals that 69.6% of samples exhibit Visual Sycophancy--models detect visual anomalies but hallucinate to satisfy user expectations--while zero samples show Robust Refusal, indicating alignment training has systematically suppressed truthful uncertainty acknowledgment. A scaling analysis (Qwen2.5-VL 7B to 72B) shows larger models reduce Language Shortcuts but amplify Visual Sycophancy, demonstrating scale alone cannot resolve the grounding problem. Diagnostic scores further enable a post-hoc selective prediction strategy achieving up to +9.5pp accuracy at 50% coverage with no additional training cost.

AI.tldr: A framework to diagnose VLM grounding via three metrics and counterfactuals; finds prevalent Visual Sycophancy, suppression of uncertainty (no robust refusal), and scale-dependent effects; enables post-hoc selective prediction with modest gains.
AI.motivation: To understand whether VLMs truly rely on visual input or fall back on language priors, and to disentangle different sources of errors (hallucination) in multimodal models.
AI.method: Tri-Layer Diagnostic Framework with Latent Anomaly Detection, Visual Necessity Score, and Competition Score; counterfactual interventions (blind, noise, conflict images); evaluation on 7 VLMs with 7,000 model-sample pairs; scaling analysis from 7B to 72B parameters.
AI.result: 69.6% of samples show Visual Sycophancy (detect visual anomalies but still hallucinate); zero Robust Refusal observed; larger models reduce Language Shortcuts but amplify Visual Sycophancy; post-hoc selective prediction improves accuracy by up to 9.5 percentage points at 50% coverage without extra training.
AI.conclusion: Grounding problems persist in VLMs and are not resolved by scaling alone; uncertainty acknowledgment remains suppressed by alignment training; the proposed diagnostic scores enable selective prediction to mitigate some errors without additional cost.

title: LLM-Augmented Computational Phenotyping of Long Covid
summary:
Phenotypic characterization is essential for understanding heterogeneity in chronic diseases and for guiding personalized interventions. Long COVID, a complex and persistent condition, yet its clinical subphenotypes remain poorly understood. In this work, we propose an LLM-augmented computational phenotyping framework ``Grace Cycle'' that iteratively integrates hypothesis generation, evidence extraction, and feature refinement to discover clinically meaningful subgroups from longitudinal patient data. The framework identifies three distinct clinical phenotypes, Protected, Responder, and Refractory, based on 13,511 Long Covid participants. These phenotypes exhibit pronounced separation in peak symptom severity, baseline disease burden, and longitudinal dose-response patterns, with strong statistical support across multiple independent dimensions.
  This study illustrates how large language models can be integrated into a principled, statistically grounded pipeline for phenotypic screening from complex longitudinal data. Note that the proposed framework is disease-agnostic and offers a general approach for discovering clinically interpretable subphenotypes.

AI.tldr: LLM-augmented 'Grace Cycle' framework discovers three clinically interpretable Long COVID subphenotypes (Protected, Responder, Refractory) from 13,511 participants, with strong longitudinal and baseline separation validated statistically.
AI.motivation: Address heterogeneity in chronic diseases and the lack of robust subphenotyping for Long COVID; leverage LLMs in a principled phenotyping pipeline.
AI.method: Iterative loop integrating hypothesis generation, evidence extraction, and feature refinement (Grace Cycle); uses LLMs to assist phenotyping; analyzes longitudinal data; identifies three subphenotypes.
AI.result: Three phenotypes show pronounced separation in peak symptom severity, baseline disease burden, and longitudinal dose-response; strong statistical support across multiple independent dimensions.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can be integrated into a statistically grounded, disease-agnostic phenotyping pipeline; approach generalizable for discovering interpretable subphenotypes in complex longitudinal data.

title: LGESynthNet: Controlled Scar Synthesis for Improved Scar Segmentation in Cardiac LGE-MRI Imaging
summary: Segmentation of enhancement in LGE cardiac MRI is critical for diagnosing various ischemic and non-ischemic cardiomyopathies. However, creating pixel-level annotations for these images is challenging and labor-intensive, leading to limited availability of annotated data. Generative models, particularly diffusion models, offer promise for synthetic data generation, yet many rely on large training datasets and often struggle with fine-grained conditioning control, especially for small or localized features. We introduce LGESynthNet, a latent diffusion-based framework for controllable enhancement synthesis, enabling explicit control over size, location, and transmural extent. Formulated as inpainting using a ControlNet-based architecture, the model integrates: (a) a reward model for conditioning-specific supervision, (b) a captioning module for anatomically descriptive text prompts, and (c) a biomedical text encoder. Trained on just 429 images (79 patients), it produces realistic, anatomically coherent samples. A quality control filter selects outputs with high conditioning-fidelity, which when used for training augmentation, improve downstream segmentation and detection performance, by up-to 6 and 20 points respectively.

AI.tldr: Latent diffusion-based LGESynthNet for controllable enhancement synthesis in LGE cardiac MRI; enables size, location, and transmural control via inpainting with ControlNet; integrates reward model, captioning, and biomedical text encoder; trained on 429 images; quality filter used for augmentation that boosts segmentation/detection.
AI.motivation: Mitigate scarcity of pixel-level annotated LGE MRI data and provide fine-grained control over synthetic enhancements to improve downstream tasks.
AI.method: Latent diffusion model framed as inpainting using a ControlNet-based architecture; conditioning via a reward model, captioning module for anatomically descriptive prompts, and a biomedical text encoder; trained on 429 images (79 patients); a quality-control filter selects high-conditioning-fidelity outputs for augmentation.
AI.result: Generates realistic, anatomically coherent synthetic samples; when used for training augmentation, improves downstream segmentation and detection performance by up to 6 and 20 points respectively.
AI.conclusion: Controllable diffusion-based data augmentation can alleviate annotation burden and enhance LGE MRI segmentation/detection, even with limited training data, provided conditioning fidelity is high.

title: Scaling Sim-to-Real Reinforcement Learning for Robot VLAs with Generative 3D Worlds
summary: The strong performance of large vision-language models (VLMs) trained with reinforcement learning (RL) has motivated similar approaches for fine-tuning vision-language-action (VLA) models in robotics. Many recent works fine-tune VLAs directly in the real world to avoid addressing the sim-to-real gap. While real-world RL circumvents sim-to-real issues, it inherently limits the generality of the resulting VLA, as scaling scene and object diversity in the physical world is prohibitively difficult. This leads to the paradoxical outcome of transforming a broadly pretrained model into an overfitted, scene-specific policy. Training in simulation can instead provide access to diverse scenes, but designing those scenes is also costly. In this work, we show that VLAs can be RL fine-tuned without sacrificing generality and with reduced labor by leveraging 3D world generative models. Using these models together with a language-driven scene designer, we generate hundreds of diverse interactive scenes containing unique objects and backgrounds, enabling scalable and highly parallel policy learning. Starting from a pretrained imitation baseline, our approach increases simulation success from 9.7% to 79.8% while achieving a 1.25$\times$ speedup in task completion time. We further demonstrate successful sim-to-real transfer enabled by the quality of the generated digital twins together with domain randomization, improving real-world success from 21.7% to 75% and achieving a 1.13$\times$ speedup. Finally, we further highlight the benefits of leveraging the effectively unlimited data from 3D world generative models through an ablation study showing that increasing scene diversity directly improves zero-shot generalization.

AI.tldr: Generative 3D world models and a language-driven scene designer enable scalable RL fine-tuning of vision-language-action models, preserving generality. They synthesize diverse interactive scenes, boosting simulation success (9.7%→79.8%), speeding task completion (1.25×), and improving sim-to-real transfer (21.7%→75%) with a 1.13× speedup; ablations show richer scene diversity enhances zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limits of fine-tuning VLAs directly in the real world (overfitting to specific scenes) and the costly, limited diversity of manual simulation environments. Leverage unlimited synthetic scene diversity via 3D world generative models to maintain generality while enabling scalable RL fine-tuning, including sim-to-real transfer.
AI.method: Utilize 3D world generative models to create hundreds of diverse interactive scenes with unique objects/backgrounds, guided by a language-driven scene designer. Start from a pretrained imitation baseline and perform RL fine-tuning with high parallelism in simulation, followed by domain randomization to enable sim-to-real transfer.
