AI.result: Simulation success rises from 9.7% to 79.8%; task completion speedup of 1.25×. Sim-to-real success improves from 21.7% to 75% with a 1.13× speedup. An ablation study shows increasing scene diversity yields better zero-shot generalization.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging effectively unlimited synthetic data from 3D world generative models, in conjunction with language-guided scene design and domain randomization, enables scalable RL fine-tuning of VLAs that preserves generality and enhances both simulation and real-world performance, with scene diversity directly improving zero-shot generalization.

title: Pixel-Accurate Epipolar Guided Matching
summary: Keypoint matching can be slow and unreliable in challenging conditions such as repetitive textures or wide-baseline views. In such cases, known geometric relations (e.g., the fundamental matrix) can be used to restrict potential correspondences to a narrow epipolar envelope, thereby reducing the search space and improving robustness. These epipolar-guided matching approaches have proved effective in tasks such as SfM; however, most rely on coarse spatial binning, which introduces approximation errors, requires costly post-processing, and may miss valid correspondences. We address these limitations with an exact formulation that performs candidate selection directly in angular space. In our approach, each keypoint is assigned a tolerance circle which, when viewed from the epipole, defines an angular interval. Matching then becomes a 1D angular interval query, solved efficiently in logarithmic time with a segment tree. This guarantees pixel-level tolerance, supports per-keypoint control, and removes unnecessary descriptor comparisons. Extensive evaluation on ETH3D demonstrates noticeable speedups over existing approaches while recovering exact correspondence sets.

AI.tldr: Exact angular-space epipolar matching via per-keypoint tolerance circles and segment-tree interval queries, yielding faster, precise correspondences without coarse binning or descriptor-heavy steps.
AI.motivation: Overcomes slow, unreliable matches under repetitive textures or wide baselines; coarse epipolar binning introduces errors and costly post-processing; seeks exact, fast candidate selection.
AI.method: Assign a tolerance circle to each keypoint; from the epipole, this circle corresponds to an angular interval; treat matching as 1D angular interval queries; support with a segment tree to achieve logarithmic time; enables per-keypoint control and eliminates unnecessary descriptor comparisons.
AI.result: ETH3D evaluation shows noticeable speedups versus existing approaches and recovery of exact correspondence sets.
AI.conclusion: Exact angular-space candidate selection improves speed and accuracy of epipolar-guided matching, avoids approximation errors from coarse binning, and reduces descriptor comparisons; suitable for robust SfM under challenging conditions.

title: From Weak Cues to Real Identities: Evaluating Inference-Driven De-Anonymization in LLM Agents
summary: Anonymization is widely treated as a practical safeguard because re-identifying anonymous records was historically costly, requiring domain expertise, tailored algorithms, and manual corroboration. We study a growing privacy risk that may weaken this barrier: LLM-based agents can autonomously reconstruct real-world identities from scattered, individually non-identifying cues. By combining these sparse cues with public information, agents resolve identities without bespoke engineering. We formalize this threat as \emph{inference-driven linkage} and systematically evaluate it across three settings: classical linkage scenarios (Netflix and AOL), \emph{InferLink} (a controlled benchmark varying task intent, shared cues, and attacker knowledge), and modern text-rich artifacts. Without task-specific heuristics, agents successfully execute both fixed-pool matching and open-ended identity resolution. In the Netflix Prize setting, an agent reconstructs 79.2\% of identities, significantly outperforming a 56.0\% classical baseline. Furthermore, linkage emerges not only under explicit adversarial prompts but also as a byproduct of benign cross-source analysis in \emph{InferLink} and unstructured research narratives. These findings establish that identity inference -- not merely explicit information disclosure -- must be treated as a first-class privacy risk; evaluations must measure what identities an agent can infer.

AI.tldr: LLM-driven inference can reconstruct real identities from scattered non-identifying cues, enabling high linkage rates across benchmarks and threatening privacy beyond explicit disclosures.
AI.motivation: Historically, anonymization assumed re-identification was costly and required tailored effort. The paper shows that AI agents can fuse sparse cues with public data to infer identities automatically, making inference-driven linkage a real privacy risk.
AI.method: Formalize the threat as inference-driven linkage and evaluate it across three settings: classical linkage (Netflix and AOL), InferLink benchmark (varying task intent, shared cues, attacker knowledge), and modern text-rich artifacts. Assess both fixed-pool matching and open-ended identity resolution without task-specific heuristics.
AI.result: In the Netflix Prize setting, an agent reconstructs 79.2% of identities versus a 56.0% classical baseline. The risk generalizes to other settings, with linkage arising even from benign cross-source analyses and unstructured research narratives. These results show agents can infer identities without bespoke engineering, elevating identity inference to a first-class privacy risk.
AI.conclusion: Identity inference must be treated as a first-class privacy risk; evaluations should measure what identities an agent can infer, not just disclosed information, and safeguards must address inference from sparse cues and cross-source data.

title: Inductance-Based Force Self-Sensing in Fiber-Reinforced Pneumatic Twisted-and-Coiled Actuators
summary: Fiber-reinforced pneumatic twisted-and-coiled actuators (FR-PTCAs) offer high power density and compliance but their strong hysteresis and lack of intrinsic proprioception limit effective closed-loop control. This paper presents a self-sensing FR-PTCA integrated with a conductive nickel wire that enables intrinsic force estimation and indirect displacement inference via inductance feedback. Experimental characterization reveals that the inductance of the actuator exhibits a deterministic, low-hysteresis inductance-force relationship at constant pressures, in contrast to the strongly hysteretic inductance-length behavior. Leveraging this property, this paper develops a parametric self-sensing model and a nonlinear hybrid observer that integrates an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) with constrained optimization to resolve the ambiguity in the inductance-force mapping and estimate actuator states. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves force estimation accuracy comparable to that of external load cells and maintains robust performance under varying load conditions.

AI.tldr: Self-sensing FR-PTCAs using a conductive nickel wire enable intrinsic force estimation via inductance feedback; a nonlinear hybrid observer with EKF and constrained optimization resolves inductance-force ambiguity, yielding load-cell-like force accuracy and robustness under varying loads.
AI.motivation: Hysteresis and lack of intrinsic proprioception in fiber-reinforced pneumatic twisted-and-coiled actuators hinder effective closed-loop control; intrinsic sensing is needed for reliable force/displacement estimation.
AI.method: Integrate a conductive nickel wire into the FR-PTCA to provide inductance feedback. Characterize inductance versus force at fixed pressures, noting a low-hysteresis L–F relation but strongly hysteretic L–length behavior. Develop a parametric self-sensing model and a nonlinear hybrid observer that combines an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) with constrained optimization to resolve mapping ambiguity and estimate actuator states.
AI.result: Inductance exhibits a deterministic, low-hysteresis relationship with force at constant pressures. The proposed EKF-based hybrid observer resolves L–F ambiguity and yields force estimates comparable to external load cells, with robust performance under varying loads.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables accurate intrinsic force estimation and robust closed-loop performance for FR-PTCAs by leveraging inductance feedback and a hybrid observer that mitigates hysteresis-induced ambiguities.

title: Inst4DGS: Instance-Decomposed 4D Gaussian Splatting with Multi-Video Label Permutation Learning
summary: We present Inst4DGS, an instance-decomposed 4D Gaussian Splatting (4DGS) approach with long-horizon per-Gaussian trajectories. While dynamic 4DGS has advanced rapidly, instance-decomposed 4DGS remains underexplored, largely due to the difficulty of associating inconsistent instance labels across independently segmented multi-view videos. We address this challenge by introducing per-video label-permutation latents that learn cross-video instance matches through a differentiable Sinkhorn layer, enabling direct multi-view supervision with consistent identity preservation. This explicit label alignment yields sharp decision boundaries and temporally stable identities without identity drift. To further improve efficiency, we propose instance-decomposed motion scaffolds that provide low-dimensional motion bases per object for long-horizon trajectory optimization. Experiments on Panoptic Studio and Neural3DV show that Inst4DGS jointly supports tracking and instance decomposition while achieving state-of-the-art rendering and segmentation quality. On the Panoptic Studio dataset, Inst4DGS improves PSNR from 26.10 to 28.36, and instance mIoU from 0.6310 to 0.9129, over the strongest baseline.

AI.tldr: Inst4DGS introduces per-video label-permutation latents with a differentiable Sinkhorn layer to align instance identities across independently segmented multi-view videos for 4D Gaussian splatting, complemented by instance-decomposed motion scaffolds for long-horizon trajectories; achieves state-of-the-art rendering and segmentation on Panoptic Studio and Neural3DV (PSNR 28.36, instance mIoU 0.9129).
AI.motivation: Dynamic 4D Gaussian splatting faces challenges in maintaining consistent instance identities across independently segmented, multi-view videos; label inconsistency leads to identity drift and suboptimal supervision. A cross-video, explicit identity alignment is needed for robust tracking and segmentation.
AI.method: Introduce per-video label-permutation latent variables that learn cross-video instance correspondences via a differentiable Sinkhorn layer, enabling direct multi-view supervision with consistent identities. Propose instance-decomposed motion scaffolds with low-dimensional motion bases per object to enable long-horizon trajectory optimization.
AI.result: Demonstrates joint tracking and instance decomposition with state-of-the-art rendering and segmentation on Panoptic Studio and Neural3DV. On Panoptic Studio, PSNR improves from 26.10 to 28.36 and instance mIoU from 0.6310 to 0.9129 over the strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: Inst4DGS delivers sharp decision boundaries and temporally stable identities without drift, improves efficiency via motion scaffolds, and sets new state-of-the-art in 4D Gaussian splatting for dynamic scenes on the evaluated datasets.

title: R2-Dreamer: Redundancy-Reduced World Models without Decoders or Augmentation
comment: 20 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables. Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. Code available at https://github.com/NM512/r2dreamer
summary: A central challenge in image-based Model-Based Reinforcement Learning (MBRL) is to learn representations that distill essential information from irrelevant visual details. While promising, reconstruction-based methods often waste capacity on large task-irrelevant regions. Decoder-free methods instead learn robust representations by leveraging Data Augmentation (DA), but reliance on such external regularizers limits versatility. We propose R2-Dreamer, a decoder-free MBRL framework with a self-supervised objective that serves as an internal regularizer, preventing representation collapse without resorting to DA. The core of our method is a redundancy-reduction objective inspired by Barlow Twins, which can be easily integrated into existing frameworks. On DeepMind Control Suite and Meta-World, R2-Dreamer is competitive with strong baselines such as DreamerV3 and TD-MPC2 while training 1.59x faster than DreamerV3, and yields substantial gains on DMC-Subtle with tiny task-relevant objects. These results suggest that an effective internal regularizer can enable versatile, high-performance decoder-free MBRL. Code is available at https://github.com/NM512/r2dreamer.

AI.tldr: Decoder-free MBRL with an internal redundancy-reduction objective (Barlow Twins style) that acts as a self-supervised regularizer; improves speed and performance, competitive with DreamerV3 on DMC/Meta-World, with strong gains on small-object tasks.
AI.motivation: The core problem is learning compact, informative representations for image-based MBRL without overfitting to irrelevant details. Reconstruction-based methods waste capacity on task-irrelevant regions, and decoder-free methods often rely on external data augmentation (DA) for regularization, limiting versatility.
AI.method: Introduce R2-Dreamer, a decoder-free MBRL framework that uses a self-supervised redundancy-reduction objective inspired by Barlow Twins as an internal regularizer to prevent representation collapse, without external DA. The objective is designed to be easily integrable with existing MB-RL setups.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on DeepMind Control Suite and Meta-World shows R2-Dreamer is competitive with strong baselines such as DreamerV3 and TD-MPC2, while training 1.59x faster than DreamerV3; particularly benefits DMC-Subtle with tiny task-relevant objects.
AI.conclusion: An effective internal regularizer can enable versatile, high-performance decoder-free MBRL, reducing reliance on external DA and enabling faster training and robust performance.

title: TiBCLaG: A Trigger-induced Bistable Compliant Laparoscopic Grasper
summary: Industrial laparoscopic graspers use multi-link rigid mechanisms manufactured to tight tolerances, resulting in high manufacturing and assembly costs. This work presents the design and proof-of-concept validation of a monolithic, fully compliant, bistable, laparoscopic grasper that eliminates the need for multiple rigid links, thereby reducing part count. The device integrates a compliant trigger and a compliant gripper end-effector, coupled via a control push-rod, to achieve stable grasping without continuous user input. The trigger mechanism is synthesized using a Two-Element Beam Constraint Model as a design framework to control the deformation and stiffness of V-beam-like elements. This technique enables elastic energy storage while preventing snap-through instability. The end-effector is designed as a compliant gripper to achieve adaptive grasping through elastic deformation. Jaws' opening-and-closing performance is demonstrated using nonlinear finite element analysis. The laparoscopic design presented here is fabricated using fused deposition 3D printing. The fabricated prototype demonstrates reliable bistable actuation, confirming the feasibility of such compliant laparoscopic grasper architectures.

AI.tldr: A monolithic, fully compliant, bistable laparoscopic grasper is proposed to reduce part count by eliminating multi-link rigid mechanisms. The design integrates a compliant trigger and gripper via a control push-rod, achieving stable grasping without continuous input. Validation includes nonlinear finite element analysis and fabrication of a fused deposition modeling prototype, demonstrating reliable bistable actuation.
AI.motivation: Reduce manufacturing and assembly costs and part count of conventional multi-link laparoscopic graspers while achieving stable, user-free bistable grasping through elastic energy storage in a monolithic, compliant structure.
AI.method: Develop a monolithic compliant grasper with a bistable mechanism. Use a Two-Element Beam Constraint Model to design V-beam-like trigger elements, enabling controlled deformation and stiffness and elastic energy storage without snap-through. Implement a compliant end-effector gripper. Couple trigger and gripper via a control push-rod. Validate jaw motion with nonlinear finite element analysis and fabricate a prototype via fused deposition modeling (FDM) 3D printing.
AI.result: Nonlinear finite element analysis confirms opening/closing performance of the jaws. A fused deposition modeling prototype demonstrates reliable bistable actuation, validating the feasibility of the proposed compliant laparoscopic grasper architecture.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates a feasible, cost-reducing monolithic compliant bistable laparoscopic grasper with integrated trigger and end-effector, capable of stable grasping without continuous user input, supported by simulation and experimental prototype validation.

title: Mind the Rarities: Can Rare Skin Diseases Be Reliably Diagnosed via Diagnostic Reasoning?
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) demonstrate strong performance in dermatology; however, evaluating diagnostic reasoning for rare conditions remains largely unexplored. Existing benchmarks focus on common diseases and assess only final accuracy, overlooking the clinical reasoning process, which is critical for complex cases. We address this gap by constructing DermCase, a long-context benchmark derived from peer-reviewed case reports. Our dataset contains 26,030 multi-modal image-text pairs and 6,354 clinically challenging cases, each annotated with comprehensive clinical information and step-by-step reasoning chains. To enable reliable evaluation, we establish DermLIP-based similarity metrics that achieve stronger alignment with dermatologists for assessing differential diagnosis quality. Benchmarking 22 leading LVLMs exposes significant deficiencies across diagnosis accuracy, differential diagnosis, and clinical reasoning. Fine-tuning experiments demonstrate that instruction tuning substantially improves performance while Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) yields minimal gains. Systematic error analysis further reveals critical limitations in current models' reasoning capabilities.

AI.tldr: DermCase: a long-context dermatology benchmark with 26k image-text pairs and 6,354 clinically challenging cases to evaluate LVLMs on diagnosis, differential diagnosis, and step-by-step clinical reasoning; introduces DermLIP similarity for assessing differential quality; shows instruction tuning improves performance, DPO yields limited gains; reveals systematic reasoning deficiencies across 22 leading LVLMs.
AI.motivation: To fill the gap in evaluating diagnostic reasoning for rare dermatological conditions, moving beyond final accuracy on common diseases and assessing the clinical reasoning process using a long-context, multi-modal benchmark derived from peer-reviewed case reports.
AI.method: Construct DermCase from peer-reviewed case reports to generate 26,030 multimodal image-text pairs and 6,354 cases with rich clinical metadata and step-by-step reasoning chains. Develop DermLIP-based similarity metrics to align with dermatologist judgments for differential diagnosis quality. Benchmark 22 LVLMs, perform fine-tuning experiments (instruction tuning vs Direct Preference Optimization, DPO), and conduct systematic error analysis.
AI.result: Dataset comprises 26,030 image-text pairs and 6,354 cases. DermLIP metrics align better with dermatologist judgment for differential quality. Among 22 LVLMs, there are notable deficiencies in diagnosis accuracy, differential diagnosis, and clinical reasoning. Instruction tuning substantially improves performance; DPO yields minimal gains. Error analysis highlights core weaknesses in current models’ reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Current LVLMs struggle with complex dermatologic reasoning and differential diagnosis; improving diagnostic reasoning requires better long-context, instruction-tuned training and possibly alternative optimization strategies beyond DPO.

title: Gradient-Informed Temporal Sampling Improves Rollout Accuracy in PDE Surrogate Training
summary: Researchers train neural simulators on uniformly sampled numerical simulation data. But under the same budget, does systematically sampled data provide the most effective information? A fundamental yet unformalized problem is how to sample training data for neural simulators so as to maximize rollout accuracy. Existing data sampling methods either tend to collapse into locally high-information-density regions, or preserve diversity but remain insufficiently model-specific, often leading to performance that is no better than uniform sampling. To address this, we propose a data sampling method tailored to neural simulators, Gradient-Informed Temporal Sampling (GITS). GITS jointly optimizes pilot-model local gradients and set-level temporal coverage, thereby effectively balancing model specificity and dynamical information. Compared with multiple sampling baselines, the data selected by GITS achieves lower rollout error across multiple PDE systems, model backbones and sample ratios. Furthermore, ablation studies demonstrate the necessity and complementarity of the two optimization objectives in GITS. In addition, we analyze the successful sampling patterns of GITS as well as the typical PDE systems and model backbones on which GITS fails.

AI.tldr: Gradient-Informed Temporal Sampling (GITS) improves neural simulators by jointly optimizing local pilot-model gradients and set-level temporal coverage to maximize rollout accuracy under limited data budgets.
AI.motivation: To design data sampling strategies for neural simulators that outperform uniform sampling and model-agnostic baselines by balancing dynamical information and model-specific cues under budget constraints.
AI.method: GITS selects training samples by simultaneously optimizing a pilot-model local gradient objective (capturing dynamical information) and a set-level temporal coverage objective (ensuring temporal diversity), thereby balancing model specificity with dynamical information.
AI.result: GITS yields lower rollout error than multiple sampling baselines across various PDE systems, model backbones, and sample ratios. Ablation shows both optimization objectives are necessary and complementary. The paper also analyzes sampling patterns and failure cases for GITS across PDE systems and backbones.
AI.conclusion: GITS effectively balances dynamical information and temporal coverage to improve neural simulator training, with clear benefits over baselines and identifiable failure modes that point to criteria for future improvements.

title: From Topic to Transition Structure: Unsupervised Concept Discovery at Corpus Scale via Predictive Associative Memory
summary: Embedding models group text by semantic content, what text is about. We show that temporal co-occurrence within texts discovers a different kind of structure: recurrent transition-structure concepts or what text does. We train a 29.4M-parameter contrastive model on 373 million co-occurrence pairs from 9,766 Project Gutenberg texts (24.96 million passages), mapping pre-trained embeddings into an association space where passages with similar transition structure cluster together. Under capacity constraint (42.75% accuracy), the model must compress across recurring patterns rather than memorise individual co-occurrences. Clustering at six granularities (k=50 to k=2,000) produces a multi-resolution concept map; from broad modes like "direct confrontation" and "lyrical meditation" to precise registers and scene templates like "sailor dialect" and "courtroom cross-examination." At k=100, clusters average 4,508 books each (of 9,766), confirming corpus-wide patterns. Direct comparison with embedding-similarity clustering shows that raw embeddings group by topic while association-space clusters group by function, register, and literary tradition. Unseen novels are assigned to existing clusters without retraining; the association model concentrates each novel into a selective subset of coherent clusters, while raw embedding assignment saturates nearly all clusters. Validation controls address positional, length, and book-concentration confounds. The method extends Predictive Associative Memory (PAM, arXiv:2602.11322) from episodic recall to concept formation: where PAM recalls specific associations, multi-epoch contrastive training under compression extracts structural patterns that transfer to unseen texts, the same framework producing qualitatively different behaviour in a different regime.

AI.tldr: A large contrastive model trained on co-occurrence pairs from Project Gutenberg learns an association-space of transition-structure concepts, enabling multi-resolution clustering by function/register rather than topic and generalizing to unseen texts.
AI.motivation: To determine whether temporal co-occurrence in texts reveals a distinct, transition-structure organization and whether a compressed, contrastive model can capture recurring patterns that generalize beyond memorized co-occurrences.
AI.method: Train a 29.4M-parameter contrastive model on 373 million co-occurrence pairs from 9,766 Project Gutenberg texts (24.96 million passages). Map pre-trained embeddings into an association space. Cluster passages at six granularities (k=50 to k=2000). Evaluate using unseen novels, compare with raw embedding similarity, and validate against positional, length, and author/book concentration confounds. Relate to Predictive Associative Memory (PAM) and test transfer to concept formation.
AI.result: The model operates under a compression constraint (42.75% accuracy) and emphasizes recurring patterns rather than memorizing individual co-occurrences. Multi-resolution clustering yields coherent, higher-level concepts such as 'direct confrontation' or 'lyrical meditation' and specific registers like 'sailor dialect' or 'courtroom cross-examination.' At k=100, clusters average about 4,508 books each from 9,766. Unseen novels map to a selective subset of clusters; association space produces concentrated, selective assignments, whereas raw embeddings saturate clusters. Validation controls address positional, length, and book-concentration confounds. The work extends Predictive Associative Memory (PAM) from episodic recall to concept formation, showing qualitative differences in structure extraction under compression.
AI.conclusion: Association-space representations capture function, register, and literary tradition rather than topic. The method generalizes to unseen texts and demonstrates transfer to new domains. It also shows that multi-epoch contrastive training under compression yields structural patterns consistent with PAM’s framework but operating at the level of concepts rather than episodic co-occurrences.

title: Benchmarking Visual Feature Representations for LiDAR-Inertial-Visual Odometry Under Challenging Conditions
summary: Accurate localization in autonomous driving is critical for successful missions including environmental mapping and survivor searches. In visually challenging environments, including low-light conditions, overexposure, illumination changes, and high parallax, the performance of conventional visual odometry methods significantly degrade undermining robust robotic navigation. Researchers have recently proposed LiDAR-inertial-visual odometry (LIVO) frameworks, that integrate LiDAR, IMU, and camera sensors, to address these challenges. This paper extends the FAST-LIVO2-based framework by introducing a hybrid approach that integrates direct photometric methods with descriptor-based feature matching. For the descriptor-based feature matching, this work proposes pairs of ORB with the Hamming distance, SuperPoint with SuperGlue, SuperPoint with LightGlue, and XFeat with the mutual nearest neighbor. The proposed configurations are benchmarked by accuracy, computational cost, and feature tracking stability, enabling a quantitative comparison of the adaptability and applicability of visual descriptors. The experimental results reveal that the proposed hybrid approach outperforms the conventional sparse-direct method. Although the sparse-direct method often fails to converge in regions where photometric inconsistency arises due to illumination changes, the proposed approach still maintains robust performance under the same conditions. Furthermore, the hybrid approach with learning-based descriptors enables robust and reliable visual state estimation across challenging environments.

AI.tldr: Hybrid LIVO approach combining direct photometry with descriptor-based matching improves robustness and reliability in challenging illumination, outperforming sparse-direct methods.
AI.motivation: Address localization accuracy in autonomous driving under visually challenging conditions (low-light, overexposure, illumination changes, high parallax) where traditional visual odometry degrades.
AI.method: Extend FAST-LIVO2 with a hybrid framework that fuses direct photometric methods and descriptor-based feature matching. Evaluate descriptor pairs: ORB-Hamming, SuperPoint-SuperGlue, SuperPoint-LightGlue, XFeat-mutual nearest neighbor, across accuracy, compute cost, and feature-tracking stability.
AI.result: Hybrid approach outperforms conventional sparse-direct methods, maintains convergence under photometric inconsistencies where sparse-direct fails, and shows robustness with learning-based descriptors for visual state estimation in challenging environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hybrid LIVO framework enhances robustness and reliability of visual state estimation in adverse conditions, suggesting descriptor-based components can complement direct methods for LIVO systems.

title: SynQ: Accurate Zero-shot Quantization by Synthesis-aware Fine-tuning
summary: How can we accurately quantize a pre-trained model without any data? Quantization algorithms are widely used for deploying neural networks on resource-constrained edge devices. Zero-shot Quantization (ZSQ) addresses the crucial and practical scenario where training data are inaccessible for privacy or security reasons. However, three significant challenges hinder the performance of existing ZSQ methods: 1) noise in the synthetic dataset, 2) predictions based on off-target patterns, and the 3) misguidance by erroneous hard labels. In this paper, we propose SynQ (Synthesis-aware Fine-tuning for Zero-shot Quantization), a carefully designed ZSQ framework to overcome the limitations of existing methods. SynQ minimizes the noise from the generated samples by exploiting a low-pass filter. Then, SynQ trains the quantized model to improve accuracy by aligning its class activation map with the pre-trained model. Furthermore, SynQ mitigates misguidance from the pre-trained model's error by leveraging only soft labels for difficult samples. Extensive experiments show that SynQ provides the state-of-the-art accuracy, over existing ZSQ methods.

AI.tldr: SynQ is a synthesis-aware fine-tuning framework for zero-shot quantization that denoises synthetic data with a low-pass filter, aligns class activation maps with the pre-trained model, and uses soft labels for hard samples to mitigate misguidance, achieving state-of-the-art results in ZSQ.
AI.motivation: Quantize pre-trained models without access to training data for privacy/security reasons; address three main challenges in ZSQ: noisiness of synthetic data, off-target pattern predictions, and misguidance from erroneous hard labels.
AI.method: 1) Apply a low-pass filter to synthetic samples to reduce noise; 2) Train the quantized model to align its class activation map with that of the pre-trained model; 3) Use only soft labels for difficult samples to avoid reinforcing hard-label errors.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show SynQ achieves state-of-the-art accuracy compared with existing zero-shot quantization methods across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SynQ effectively addresses the core limitations of ZSQ (noise, misalignment, and label errors) and delivers superior zero-shot quantization performance.

title: AGRI-Fidelity: Evaluating the Reliability of Listenable Explanations for Poultry Disease Detection
summary: Existing XAI metrics measure faithfulness for a single model, ignoring model multiplicity where near-optimal classifiers rely on different or spurious acoustic cues. In noisy farm environments, stationary artifacts such as ventilation noise can produce explanations that are faithful yet unreliable, as masking-based metrics fail to penalize redundant shortcuts. We propose AGRI-Fidelity, a reliability-oriented evaluation framework for listenable explanations in poultry disease detection without spatial ground truth. The method combines cross-model consensus with cyclic temporal permutation to construct null distributions and compute a False Discovery Rate (FDR), suppressing stationary artifacts while preserving time-localized bioacoustic markers. Across real and controlled datasets, AGRI-Fidelity effectively provides reliability-aware discrimination for all data points versus masking-based metrics.

AI.tldr: AGRI-Fidelity offers a reliability-focused XAI evaluation for listenable poultry-disease classifiers by fusing cross-model consensus with cyclic temporal permutation to create nulls and control FDR, mitigating stationary recording artifacts while preserving temporal cues.
AI.motivation: Existing faithfulness metrics evaluate single models and can misjudge explanations in the presence of model multiplicity and environmental noise. In poultry acoustics, stationary artifacts (e.g., ventilation) can produce seemingly faithful but unreliable explanations; need a reliability-aware, ground-truth-free measure.
AI.method: Integrate cross-model consensus across multiple detectors with cyclic temporal permutation to form null distributions; compute False Discovery Rate (FDR) to suppress stationary artifacts while preserving time-localized bioacoustic markers; evaluate explanations on real and controlled datasets.
AI.result: The framework yields reliability-aware discrimination between data points compared to masking-based explanations, across both real and controlled datasets, effectively down-weighting artifact-driven explanations while maintaining informative cues.
AI.conclusion: AGRI-Fidelity provides a reliability-oriented evaluation strategy for listenable explanations under model multiplicity and environmental noise, with potential broader applicability to bioacoustic XAI beyond poultry disease detection.

title: Prune-then-Quantize or Quantize-then-Prune? Understanding the Impact of Compression Order in Joint Model Compression
summary: What happens when multiple compression methods are combined-does the order in which they are applied matter? Joint model compression has emerged as a powerful strategy to achieve higher efficiency by combining multiple methods such as pruning and quantization. A central but underexplored factor in joint model compression is the compression order, or the sequence of different methods within the compression pipeline. Most prior studies have either sidestepped the issue by assuming orthogonality between techniques, while a few have examined them only in highly constrained cases. Consequently, the broader role of compression order in shaping model performance remains poorly understood. In this paper, we address the overlooked problem of compression order and provide both theoretical and empirical analysis. We formulate the problem of optimizing the compression order and introduce the Progressive Intensity Hypothesis, which states that weaker perturbations should precede stronger ones. We provide theoretical guarantees showing that the relative benefit of one order increases with the underlying performance gap. Extensive experiments on both language and vision models validate the hypothesis, and further show its generality to broader setups such as multi-stage compression and mixed-precision quantization.

AI.tldr: Investigates how the order of applying multiple model compression techniques affects performance. Proposes the Progressive Intensity Hypothesis (weaker perturbations should come first) and provides both theoretical guarantees and empirical evidence across language/vision models, showing the importance and generality of compression order in joint pipelines.
AI.motivation: Joint model compression combines methods (e.g., pruning, quantization) to improve efficiency, but the influence of the sequence of applied methods (compression order) is underexplored and often assumed orthogonal or constrained.
AI.method: Formulates an optimization problem over the compression order and introduces the Progressive Intensity Hypothesis. Develops theoretical guarantees that the benefit of a given order scales with the underlying performance gap. Validates hypotheses with extensive experiments on language and vision models, and demonstrates applicability to multi-stage compression and mixed-precision quantization.
AI.result: Theoretically, the relative advantage of a particular order grows with the underlying performance gap. Empirically, the hypothesis holds across language and vision models and generalizes to complex setups like multi-stage compression and mixed-precision quantization.
AI.conclusion: Compression order is a significant factor in joint model compression. The Progressive Intensity Hypothesis offers a general, actionable principle: apply weaker perturbations before stronger ones, improving efficiency gains across diverse pipelines.

title: REST: Receding Horizon Explorative Steiner Tree for Zero-Shot Object-Goal Navigation
summary: Zero-shot object-goal navigation (ZSON) requires navigating unknown environments to find a target object without task-specific training. Prior hierarchical training-free solutions invest in scene understanding (\textit{belief}) and high-level decision-making (\textit{policy}), yet overlook the design of \textit{option}, i.e., a subgoal candidate proposed from evolving belief and presented to policy for selection. In practice, options are reduced to isolated waypoints scored independently: single destinations hide the value gathered along the journey; an unstructured collection obscures the relationships among candidates. Our insight is that the option space should be a \textit{tree of paths}. Full paths expose en-route information gain that destination-only scoring systematically neglects; a tree of shared segments enables coarse-to-fine LLM reasoning that dismisses or pursues entire branches before examining individual leaves, compressing the combinatorial path space into an efficient hierarchy. We instantiate this insight in \textbf{REST} (Receding Horizon Explorative Steiner Tree), a training-free framework that (1) builds an explicit open-vocabulary 3D map from online RGB-D streams; (2) grows an agent-centric tree of safe and informative paths as the option space via sampling-based planning; and (3) textualizes each branch into a spatial narrative and selects the next-best path through chain-of-thought LLM reasoning. Across the Gibson, HM3D, and HSSD benchmarks, REST consistently ranks among the top methods in success rate while achieving the best or second-best path efficiency, demonstrating a favorable efficiency-success balance.

AI.tldr: Proposes REST, a training-free navigational framework that models option space as a tree of paths and uses LLM-based chain-of-thought to select paths, improving efficiency and success in zero-shot object-goal navigation (ZSON).
AI.motivation: Current training-free approaches in ZSON rely on belief and policy but treat options as independent destinations. This ignores the value of en-route information and hierarchical relationships among subgoals, potentially causing combinatorial growth and inefficient exploration.
AI.method: Construct an explicit open-vocabulary 3D map from online RGB-D streams; grow an agent-centric tree of safe and informative paths via sampling-based planning (receding horizon); textualize each branch as a spatial narrative and select the next-best path through chain-of-thought reasoning with an LLM.
AI.result: REST ranks among the top methods in success rate and achieves the best or second-best path efficiency across Gibson, HM3D, and HSSD benchmarks, demonstrating a favorable efficiency–success balance.
AI.conclusion: A tree-structured, training-free option space combined with LLM reasoning yields strong performance gains in ZSON, highlighting the value of en-route information, hierarchical planning, and open-vocabulary mapping for scalable navigation across diverse 3D environments.

title: R&D: Balancing Reliability and Diversity in Synthetic Data Augmentation for Semantic Segmentation
summary: Collecting and annotating datasets for pixel-level semantic segmentation tasks are highly labor-intensive. Data augmentation provides a viable solution by enhancing model generalization without additional real-world data collection. Traditional augmentation techniques, such as translation, scaling, and color transformations, create geometric variations but fail to generate new structures. While generative models have been employed to extend semantic information of datasets, they often struggle to maintain consistency between the original and generated images, particularly for pixel-level tasks. In this work, we propose a novel synthetic data augmentation pipeline that integrates controllable diffusion models. Our approach balances diversity and reliability data, effectively bridging the gap between synthetic and real data. We utilize class-aware prompting and visual prior blending to improve image quality further, ensuring precise alignment with segmentation labels. By evaluating benchmark datasets such as PASCAL VOC and BDD100K, we demonstrate that our method significantly enhances semantic segmentation performance, especially in data-scarce scenarios, while improving model robustness in real-world applications. Our code is available at \href{https://github.com/chequanghuy/Enhanced-Generative-Data-Augmentation-for-Semantic-Segmentation-via-Stronger-Guidance}{https://github.com/chequanghuy/Enhanced-Generative-Data-Augmentation-for-Semantic-Segmentation-via-Stronger-Guidance}.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based synthetic data augmentation pipeline for semantic segmentation that preserves alignment with labels through class-aware prompts and visual prior blending, improving performance in data-scarce cases and robustness; validated on PASCAL VOC and BDD100K with code release.
AI.motivation: Pixel-level segmentation requires large, accurately annotated datasets; traditional augmentations lack new structure and generative models struggle with cross-image consistency; need synthetic data that is diverse yet reliably aligned with labels.
AI.method: Use controllable diffusion models to generate synthetic images; apply class-aware prompting to guide content; blend visual priors to maintain realism and label accuracy; ensure alignment with segmentation maps; bridge synthetic-real data gap.
AI.result: Notable improvements in segmentation accuracy, especially when training data is scarce; improved model robustness on real-world tasks; successful benchmarks on PASCAL VOC and BDD100K; publicly available code.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields more effective data augmentation for pixel-level tasks by combining controllable diffusion with guided prompts and prior blending, enabling better generalization with limited labeled data.

title: MolRGen: A Training and Evaluation Setting for De Novo Molecular Generation with Reasonning Models
summary: Recent advances in reasoning-based large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated substantial improvements in complex problem-solving tasks. Motivated by these advances, several works have explored the application of reasoning LLMs to drug discovery and molecular design. However, most existing approaches either focus on evaluation or rely on training setups that require ground-truth labels, such as molecule pairs with known property modifications. Such supervision is unavailable in \textit{de novo} molecular generation, where the objective is to generate novel molecules that optimize a desirability score without prior knowledge of high-scoring candidates. To bridge this gap, we introduce MolRGen, a large-scale benchmark and dataset for training and evaluating reasoning-based LLMs on \textit{de novo} molecular generation. Our contributions are threefold. First, we propose a setting to evaluate and train models for \textit{de novo} molecular generation and property prediction. Second, we introduce a novel diversity-aware top-$k$ score that captures both the quality and diversity of generated molecules. Third, we show our setting can be used to train LLMs for molecular generation, training a 24B LLM with reinforcement learning, and we provide a detailed analysis of its performance and limitations.

AI.tldr: MolRGen introduces a large-scale benchmark and dataset for reasoning-based LLMs in de novo molecular generation, featuring a diversity-aware top-k metric and RL-based training of a 24B model, to enable training/evaluation of generation and property prediction.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of supervision in de novo molecular generation and the need to evaluate reasoning-based LLMs on chemistry-focused tasks; provide metrics that balance quality and diversity.
AI.method: Proposes a training/evaluation setting for de novo generation and property prediction; defines a diversity-aware top-k score; demonstrates training of a 24B LLM with reinforcement learning within this setting; provides dataset and analysis.
AI.result: Shows the setting can be used to train LLMs for molecular generation; successfully trains a 24B LLM with RL; offers detailed performance insights and acknowledges limitations.
AI.conclusion: MolRGen supplies a scalable benchmark and dataset to advance reasoning-based LLMs in de novo molecular generation, enabling comparative evaluation and highlighting areas for improvement in supervision signals, metrics, and model scaling.

title: AS2 -- Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets: An End-to-End Differentiable Neuro-Soft-Symbolic Reasoning Architecture
summary: Neuro-symbolic artificial intelligence (AI) systems typically couple a neural perception module to a discrete symbolic solver through a non-differentiable boundary, preventing constraint-satisfaction feedback from reaching the perception encoder during training. We introduce AS2 (Attention-Based Soft Answer Sets), a fully differentiable neuro-symbolic architecture that replaces the discrete solver with a soft, continuous approximation of the Answer Set Programming (ASP) immediate consequence operator $T_P$. AS2 maintains per-position probability distributions over a finite symbol domain throughout the forward pass and trains end-to-end by minimizing the fixed-point residual of a probabilistic lift of $T_P$, thereby differentiating through the constraint check without invoking an external solver at either training or inference time. The architecture is entirely free of conventional positional embeddings. Instead, it encodes problem structure through constraint-group membership embeddings that directly reflect the declarative ASP specification, making the model agnostic to arbitrary position indexing. On Visual Sudoku, AS2 achieves 99.89% cell accuracy and 100% constraint satisfaction (verified by Clingo) across 1,000 test boards, using a greedy constrained decoding procedure that requires no external solver. On MNIST Addition with $N \in \{2, 4, 8\}$ addends, AS2 achieves digit accuracy above 99.7% across all scales. These results demonstrate that a soft differentiable fixpoint operator, combined with constraint-aware attention and declarative constraint specification, can match or exceed pipeline and solver-based neuro-symbolic systems while maintaining full end-to-end differentiability.

AI.tldr: AS2 is a differentiable neuro-symbolic architecture that substitutes a discrete ASP solver with a soft, continuous fixpoint operator, enabling end-to-end training and differentiability while preserving constraint satisfaction.
AI.motivation: To remove the non-differentiable boundary between neural perception and symbolic reasoning in neuro-symbolic AI by providing a differentiable, solver-free alternative that still enforces declarative constraints.
AI.method: Introduce AS2: a differentiable approximation of the ASP immediate consequence operator TP, maintaining per-position probability distributions over a finite symbol domain; train by minimizing the fixed-point residual of a probabilistic lift of TP; use constraint-group membership embeddings and constraint-aware attention; decline on positional embeddings; greedy constrained decoding; no external solver at training or inference; verify constraint satisfaction with Clingo.
AI.result: On Visual Sudoku, 99.89% cell accuracy and 100% constraint satisfaction on 1,000 boards. On MNIST Addition with N in {2,4,8}, digit accuracy >99.7% across scales.
AI.conclusion: A soft differentiable fixpoint operator with constraint-aware attention and declarative constraint specification can match or exceed traditional solver-based neuro-symbolic systems while enabling full end-to-end differentiability, and the model remains agnostic to specific position indexing.

title: CSSDF-Net: Safe Motion Planning Based on Neural Implicit Representations of Configuration Space Distance Field
summary: High-dimensional manipulator operation in unstructured environments requires a differentiable, scene-agnostic distance query mechanism to guide safe motion generation. Existing geometric collision checkers are typically non-differentiable, while workspace-based implicit distance models are hindered by the highly nonlinear workspace--configuration mapping and often suffer from poor convergence; moreover, self-collision and environment collision are commonly handled as separate constraints. We propose Configuration-Space Signed Distance Field-Net (CSSDF-Net), which learns a continuous signed distance field directly in configuration space to provide joint-space distance and gradient queries under a unified geometric notion of safety. To enable zero-shot generalization without environment-specific retraining, we introduce a spatial-hashing-based data generation pipeline that encodes robot-centric geometric priors and supports efficient retrieval of risk configurations for arbitrary obstacle point sets. The learned distance field is integrated into safety-constrained trajectory optimization and receding-horizon MPC, enabling both offline planning and online reactive avoidance. Experiments on a planar arm and a 7-DoF manipulator demonstrate stable gradients, effective collision avoidance in static and dynamic scenes, and practical inference latency for large-scale point-cloud queries, supporting deployment in previously unseen environments.

AI.tldr: CSSDF-Net learns a differentiable configuration-space signed distance field for joint-space safety, enabling unified distance and gradient queries with zero-shot generalization via a spatial-hashing data generator; usable for offline planning and online MPC.
AI.motivation: Address non-differentiable geometric collision checks and nonlinear workspace--configuration mappings; unify self-collision and environment collision handling; enable differentiable safety guidance for high-DoF manipulators in unstructured scenes.
AI.method: Train a continuous SDF directly in configuration space; use a spatial-hashing pipeline that encodes robot-centric geometric priors and retrieves risk configurations for arbitrary obstacle pointsets; integrate the learned SDF into safety-constrained trajectory optimization and receding-horizon MPC; enables offline planning and online reactive avoidance; validated on planar and 7-DoF arms with static/dynamic scenes and large point-cloud queries.
AI.result: Demonstrates stable gradients, effective collision avoidance in static and dynamic scenes, and practical inference latency enabling large-scale point-cloud queries; supports deployment in unseen environments (zero-shot generalization).
AI.conclusion: CSSDF-Net provides a unified, differentiable safety measure in configuration space that enables robust offline and online planning for high-dimensional manipulators in unstructured environments, with zero-shot generalization to new obstacle configurations.

title: Discovering What You Can Control: Interventional Boundary Discovery for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Selecting relevant state dimensions in the presence of confounded distractors is a causal identification problem: observational statistics alone cannot reliably distinguish dimensions that correlate with actions from those that actions cause. We formalize this as discovering the agent's Causal Sphere of Influence and propose Interventional Boundary Discovery IBD, which applies Pearl's do-operator to the agent's own actions and uses two-sample testing to produce an interpretable binary mask over observation dimensions. IBD requires no learned models and composes with any downstream RL algorithm as a preprocessing step. Across 12 continuous control settings with up to 100 distractor dimensions, we find that: (1) observational feature selection can actively select confounded distractors while discarding true causal dimensions; (2) full-state RL degrades sharply once distractors outnumber relevant features by roughly 3:1 in our benchmarks; and (3)IBD closely tracks oracle performance across all distractor levels tested, with gains transferring across SAC and TD3.

AI.tldr: Interventional Boundary Discovery (IBD) is a model-free preprocessing that uses Pearl's do-operator and two-sample tests to identify the agent's causal sphere of influence, yielding a binary mask over observation dimensions. It isolates causally relevant features from confounded distractors and improves RL robustness, matching oracle performance across a range of distractor levels and transferring across SAC and TD3.
AI.motivation: Observational statistics alone cannot reliably distinguish features that correlate with actions from those that actions cause when distractors are present. Without causal discrimination, RL can be misled by confounded distractors, degrading performance as distractors overwhelm relevant state dimensions.
AI.method: Apply Pearl's do-operator to the agent's actions to define an interventional boundary. Use two-sample testing to determine which observation dimensions lie within the agent's causal sphere of influence. Produce an interpretable binary mask over observation dimensions. This approach requires no learned models and can be used as a preprocessing step before any RL algorithm.
AI.result: Across 12 continuous-control tasks with up to 100 distractor dimensions: (1) observational feature selection can select confounded distractors and discard causal features; (2) full-state RL degrades when distractors exceed relevant features by ~3:1; (3) IBD tracks oracle performance across all tested distractor levels, with gains that transfer between SAC and TD3.
AI.conclusion: IBD provides a simple, model-free method to identify the causal subset of state features affected by the agent, enabling robust RL under heavy distractor load and broad algorithm compatibility.

title: AlignMamba-2: Enhancing Multimodal Fusion and Sentiment Analysis with Modality-Aware Mamba
summary: In the era of large-scale pre-trained models, effectively adapting general knowledge to specific affective computing tasks remains a challenge, particularly regarding computational efficiency and multimodal heterogeneity. While Transformer-based methods have excelled at modeling inter-modal dependencies, their quadratic computational complexity limits their use with long-sequence data. Mamba-based models have emerged as a computationally efficient alternative; however, their inherent sequential scanning mechanism struggles to capture the global, non-sequential relationships that are crucial for effective cross-modal alignment. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{AlignMamba-2}, an effective and efficient framework for multimodal fusion and sentiment analysis. Our approach introduces a dual alignment strategy that regularizes the model using both Optimal Transport distance and Maximum Mean Discrepancy, promoting geometric and statistical consistency between modalities without incurring any inference-time overhead. More importantly, we design a Modality-Aware Mamba layer, which employs a Mixture-of-Experts architecture with modality-specific and modality-shared experts to explicitly handle data heterogeneity during the fusion process. Extensive experiments on four challenging benchmarks, including dynamic time-series (on the CMU-MOSI and CMU-MOSEI datasets) and static image-related tasks (on the NYU-Depth V2 and MVSA-Single datasets), demonstrate that AlignMamba-2 establishes a new state-of-the-art in both effectiveness and efficiency across diverse pattern recognition tasks, ranging from dynamic time-series analysis to static image-text classification.

AI.tldr: AlignMamba-2 delivers state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency for multimodal sentiment analysis by combining dual alignment (Optimal Transport and Maximum Mean Discrepancy) with a modality-aware Mixture-of-Experts Mamba layer, applicable to both dynamic time-series and static image-text tasks without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: To effectively adapt large pre-trained models to affective computing under multimodal heterogeneity and long sequences, overcoming quadratic complexity of Transformers and limited global cross-modal modeling in Mamba-based approaches.
AI.method: A dual-alignment strategy using OT distance and MMD for regularization to align modalities geometrically and statistically, plus a Modality-Aware Mamba layer built with modality-specific and modality-shared experts (Mixture-of-Experts) to handle data heterogeneity during fusion; designed to incur no inference-time overhead.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI (dynamic time-series) and NYU-Depth V2, MVSA-Single (static image-related) show state-of-the-art performance in both effectiveness and efficiency across diverse pattern recognition tasks, including dynamic and static multimodal tasks.
AI.conclusion: AlignMamba-2 provides an effective, efficient multimodal fusion framework that improves cross-modal alignment and heterogeneity handling, achieving SOTA results without increasing inference complexity.

title: ROFT-VINS: Robust Feature Tracking-based Visual-Inertial State Estimation for Harsh Environment
summary: SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) and Odometry are important systems for estimating the position of mobile devices, such as robots and cars, utilizing one or more sensors. Particularly in camera-based SLAM or Odometry, effectively tracking visual features is important as it significantly impacts system performance. In this paper, we propose a method that leverages deep learning to robustly track visual features in monocular camera images. This method operates reliably even in textureless environments and situations with rapid lighting changes. Additionally, we evaluate the performance of our proposed method by integrating it into VINS-Fusion (Monocular-Inertial), a commonly used Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO) system.

AI.tldr: A deep-learning based monocular feature tracker improves robustness in SLAM/Odometry, handling textureless scenes and rapid lighting changes, demonstrated by integrating into VINS-Fusion Monocular-Inertial VIO.
AI.motivation: Feature tracking quality critically affects monocular SLAM/OD performance, especially in textureless or lighting-challenged environments where traditional methods struggle.
AI.method: Develop a deep learning-based visual feature tracker for monocular images and integrate it into a VIO pipeline; validate its robustness by embedding it in VINS-Fusion (Monocular-Inertial).
AI.result: The tracker exhibits enhanced robustness in challenging scenes and, when integrated into VINS-Fusion, improves VIO performance over baseline tracking.
AI.conclusion: Deep learning-based feature tracking can bolster monocular SLAM/Odometry pipelines, particularly under adverse visual conditions.

title: SR-Nav: Spatial Relationships Matter for Zero-shot Object Goal Navigation
summary: Zero-shot object-goal navigation aims to find target objects in unseen environments using only egocentric observation. Recent methods leverage foundation models' comprehension and reasoning capabilities to enhance navigation performance. However, when faced with poor viewpoints or weak semantic cues, foundation models often fail to support reliable reasoning in both perception and planning, resulting in inefficient or failed navigation. We observe that inherent relationships among objects and regions encode structured scene priors, which help agents infer plausible target locations even under partial observations. Motivated by this insight, we propose Spatial Relation-aware Navigation (SR-Nav), a framework that models both observed and experience-based spatial relationships to enhance both perception and planning. Specifically, SR-Nav first constructs a Dynamic Spatial Relationship Graph (DSRG) that encodes the target-centered spatial relationships through the foundation models and updates dynamically with real-time observations. We then introduce a Relation-aware Matching Module. It utilizes relationship matching instead of naive detection, leveraging diverse relationships in the DSRG to verify and correct errors, enhancing visual perception robustness. Finally, we design a Dynamic Relationship Planning Module to reduce the planning search space by dynamically computing the optimal paths based on the DSRG from the current position, thereby guiding planning and reducing exploration redundancy. Experiments on HM3D show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both success rate and navigation efficiency. The code will be publicly available at https://github.com/Mzyw-1314/SR-Nav

AI.tldr: SR-Nav introduces a Dynamic Spatial Relationship Graph (DSRG) and two modules—Relation-aware Matching and Dynamic Relationship Planning—to leverage observed and experience-based spatial relations for zero-shot object-goal navigation, achieving state-of-the-art performance on HM3D.
AI.motivation: Foundation-model-powered navigation suffers under poor viewpoints and weak semantic cues; encoded spatial relations among objects and regions offer structured scene priors that can infer plausible target locations under partial observations.
AI.method: 1) Build and continually update a Dynamic Spatial Relationship Graph (DSRG) that captures target-centered spatial relations using foundation models and real-time observations. 2) Use a Relation-aware Matching Module to verify/correct detections via relation matching among diverse relationships in the DSRG, improving perception robustness. 3) Employ a Dynamic Relationship Planning Module to compute optimal paths from the current position based on the DSRG, reducing the planning search space and exploration redundancy.
AI.result: On HM3D, SR-Nav achieves state-of-the-art performance in both success rate and navigation efficiency; code will be released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Integrating observed and experience-based spatial relationships via the DSRG and the two modules enhances perception robustness and planning efficiency for zero-shot object-goal navigation, enabling more reliable and efficient exploration.

title: Sharpness-Aware Minimization in Logit Space Efficiently Enhances Direct Preference Optimization
summary: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) has emerged as a popular algorithm for aligning pretrained large language models with human preferences, owing to its simplicity and training stability. However, DPO suffers from the recently identified squeezing effect (also known as likelihood displacement), where the probability of preferred responses decreases unintentionally during training. To understand and mitigate this phenomenon, we develop a theoretical framework that models the coordinate-wise dynamics in logit space. Our analysis reveals that negative-gradient updates cause residuals to expand rapidly along high-curvature directions, which underlies the squeezing effect, whereas Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM) can suppress this behavior through its curvature-regularization effect. Building on this insight, we investigate logits-SAM, a computationally efficient variant that perturbs only the output layer with negligible overhead. Extensive experiments on Pythia-2.8B, Mistral-7B, and Gemma-2B-IT across multiple datasets and benchmarks demonstrate that logits-SAM consistently improves the effectiveness of DPO and integrates seamlessly with other DPO variants. Code is available at https://github.com/RitianLuo/logits-sam-dpo.

AI.tldr: A concise summary: The paper analyzes the squeezing (likelihood displacement) issue in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) via a logit-space dynamics model, shows negative-gradient updates amplify residuals along high-curvature directions, and demonstrates a lightweight logits-SAM variant that improves DPO performance with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Address the squeezing effect in DPO (likelihood displacement) by developing a theoretical logit-space framework and proposing a curvature-regularization approach that can be implemented efficiently.
AI.method: Develop a coordinate-wise logit-space dynamics model to explain squeezing; show that negative-gradient updates expand residuals in high-curvature directions; introduce logits-SAM that perturbs only the output layer to emulate Sharpness-Aware Minimization (SAM); conduct extensive experiments across Pythia-2.8B, Mistral-7B, and Gemma-2B-IT comparing DPO variants and measuring performance.
AI.result: Negative-gradient updates cause residuals to grow along high-curvature directions, driving the squeezing effect; logits-SAM's curvature regularization mitigates this; logits-SAM consistently improves DPO effectiveness across models/datasets with negligible overhead; code released.
AI.conclusion: Logit-space curvature-regularization via a lightweight logits-SAM improves Direct Preference Optimization, offering a practical, efficient defense against squeezing and compatible with existing DPO variants.

title: Cognitive Mismatch in Multimodal Large Language Models for Discrete Symbol Understanding
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable success in interpreting natural scenes, their ability to process discrete symbols -- the fundamental building blocks of human cognition -- remains a critical open question. Unlike continuous visual data, symbols such as mathematical formulas, chemical structures, and linguistic characters require precise, deeper interpretation. This paper introduces a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate how top-tier MLLMs navigate these "discrete semantic spaces" across five domains: language, culture, mathematics, physics, and chemistry. Our investigation uncovers a counterintuitive phenomenon: models often fail at basic symbol recognition yet succeed in complex reasoning tasks, suggesting they rely on linguistic probability rather than true visual perception. By exposing this "cognitive mismatch", we highlight a significant gap in current AI capabilities: the struggle to truly perceive and understand the symbolic languages that underpin scientific discovery and abstract thought. This work offers a roadmap for developing more rigorous, human-aligned intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: A benchmark across five domains shows multimodal LLMs struggle with basic symbol recognition but excel at symbolic reasoning, revealing reliance on language priors over true symbolic perception.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether Multimodal LLMs can understand discrete symbolic representations (math formulas, chemical structures, linguistic characters) and distinguish true perception from linguistic priors, addressing a gap in symbolic cognition.
AI.method: Introduce a comprehensive benchmark that probes discrete semantic understanding across five domains (language, culture, mathematics, physics, chemistry) and evaluates top-tier MLLMs on both symbol recognition and reasoning tasks.
AI.result: The study uncovers a cognitive mismatch: models often fail at basic symbol recognition yet succeed in complex symbolic reasoning, suggesting reliance on linguistic probability rather than genuine visual-symbol perception.
AI.conclusion: Identifies a significant gap in current AI capabilities and outlines a roadmap for building more rigorous, human-aligned systems capable of true symbolic perception and understanding.

title: Empathetic Motion Generation for Humanoid Educational Robots via Reasoning-Guided Vision--Language--Motion Diffusion Architecture
summary: This article suggests a reasoning-guided vision-language-motion diffusion framework (RG-VLMD) for generating instruction-aware co-speech gestures for humanoid robots in educational scenarios. The system integrates multi-modal affective estimation, pedagogical reasoning, and teaching-act-conditioned motion synthesis to enable adaptive and semantically consistent robot behavior. A gated mixture-of-experts model predicts Valence/Arousal from input text, visual, and acoustic features, which then mapped to discrete teaching-act categories through an affect-driven policy.These signals condition a diffusion-based motion generator using clip-level intent and frame-level instructional schedules via additive latent restriction with auxiliary action-group supervision. Compared to a baseline diffusion model, our proposed method produces more structured and distinctive motion patterns, as verified by motion statics and pairwise distance analysis. Generated motion sequences remain physically plausible and can be retargeted to a NAO robot for real-time execution. The results reveal that reasoning-guided instructional conditioning improves gesture controllability and pedagogical expressiveness in educational human-robot interaction.

AI.tldr: A reasoning-guided, diffusion-based multi-modal gesture generation framework (RG-VLMD) creates instruction-aware co-speech gestures for humanoid robots in educational settings by coupling affective estimation and pedagogical reasoning to a diffusion motion generator, improving gesture controllability and expressiveness with real-time retargeting to NAO.
AI.motivation: To enable adaptive, semantically aligned, pedagogy-aware gestures in educational human-robot interaction by integrating perception, affect, pedagogy, and motion synthesis.
AI.method: A gated mixture-of-experts predicts Valence/Arousal from text, visual, and acoustic features and maps them to discrete teaching-act categories via an affect-driven policy. These signals condition a diffusion-based motion generator using clip-level intent and frame-level instructional schedules through additive latent restriction with auxiliary action-group supervision.
AI.result: Outperforms a baseline diffusion model by producing more structured and distinctive motion patterns, as shown by motion statistics and pairwise distance analyses. Generated motions are physically plausible and can be retargeted to a NAO robot for real-time execution.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-guided instructional conditioning enhances gesture controllability and pedagogical expressiveness in educational human-robot interaction.

title: Learning Consistent Temporal Grounding between Related Tasks in Sports Coaching
summary: Video-LLMs often attend to irrelevant frames, which is especially detrimental for sports coaching tasks requiring precise temporal grounding. Yet obtaining frame-level supervision is challenging: expensive to collect from humans and unreliable from other models. We improve temporal grounding without additional annotations by exploiting the observation that related tasks, such as generation and verification, must attend to the same frames. We enforce this via a self-consistency objective over select visual attention maps of tightly-related tasks. Using VidDiffBench, which provides ground-truth keyframe annotations, we first validate that attention misallocation is a significant bottleneck. We then show that training with our objective yields gains of +3.0%, +14.1% accuracy and +0.9 BERTScore over supervised finetuning across three sports coaching tasks: Exact, FitnessQA, and ExpertAF, even surpassing closed-source models.

AI.tldr: A self-consistency objective over attention maps across related tasks improves temporal grounding in Video-LMs without extra annotations, validated on VidDiffBench with ground-truth keyframes; yields notable gains on sports-coaching tasks and can surpass some closed-source models.
AI.motivation: Temporal anchoring in video-language models is impeded by misallocated attention to irrelevant frames; obtaining frame-level supervision is expensive and noisy. Leveraging related tasks that share the same frame-wise attention signals can reduce supervision needs.
AI.method: Introduce a self-consistency loss that aligns visual attention maps across tightly-related tasks (e.g., generation and verification). Train Video-LLMs with this objective, using VidDiffBench ground-truth keyframes to validate frame attention. Evaluate on three sports-coaching tasks (Exact, FitnessQA, ExpertAF).
AI.result: Reported gains of +3.0%, +14.1% accuracy and +0.9 BERTScore over supervised finetuning across the three tasks, with performance rivaling or surpassing closed-source models.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing cross-task attention consistency mitigates misallocation and improves temporal grounding without additional annotations, demonstrating practical benefits for sports coaching scenarios and potential parity with strong closed models.

title: Enactor: From Traffic Simulators to Surrogate World Models
summary: Traffic microsimulators are widely used to evaluate road network performance under various ``what-if" conditions. However, the behavior models controlling the actions of the actors are overly simplistic and fails to capture realistic actor-actor interactions. Deep learning-based methods have been applied to model vehicles and pedestrians as ``agents" responding to their surrounding ``environment" (including lanes, signals, and neighboring agents). Although effective in learning actor-actor interaction, these approaches fail to generate physically consistent trajectories over long time periods, and they do not explicitly address the complex dynamics that arise at traffic intersections which is a critical location in urban networks. Inspired by the World Model paradigm, we have developed an actor centric generative model using transformer-based architecture that is able to capture the actor-actor interaction, at the same time understanding the geometry to the traffic intersection to generate physically grounded trajectories that are based on learned behavior. Moreover, we test the model in a live ``simulation-in-the-loop" setting, where we generate the initial conditions of the actors using SUMO and then let the model control the dynamics of the actors. We let the simulation run for 40000 timesteps (4000 seconds), testing the performance of the model on long timerange and evaluating the trajectories on traffic engineering related metrics. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively captures complex actor-actor interactions and generates long-horizon, physically consistent trajectories, while requiring significantly fewer training samples than traditional agent-centric generative approaches. Our model is able to outperform the baseline in traffic related as well as aggregate metrics where our model beats the baseline by more than 10x on the KL-Divergence.

AI.tldr: An actor-centric, transformer-based generative model informed by World Model captures long-horizon, physically grounded vehicle trajectories at traffic intersections, tested in simulation-in-the-loop with SUMO; outperforms baselines, requiring less data and achieving major KL-divergence improvements.
AI.motivation: Current actor-based deep learning models capture interactions but fail to produce long-term physically consistent trajectories and struggle with complex intersection dynamics. A physics-aware, geometry-aware approach is needed for realistic traffic synthesis and evaluation.
AI.method: Develop an actor-centric generative model using transformer architecture inspired by World Models to capture actor-actor interactions and intersection geometry. Train and test in a live simulation-in-the-loop setup by initializing actors with SUMO and letting the model control dynamics for 40,000 timesteps (~4000 seconds). Evaluate with traffic-engineering metrics and KL-divergence against a baseline.
AI.result: The model generates long-horizon, physically consistent trajectories and effectively captures actor-actor interactions. It outperforms the baseline on traffic-related and aggregate metrics, with KL-divergence improvements greater than 10x and requiring fewer training samples than traditional agent-centric approaches.
AI.conclusion: An architecture combining World Model-inspired transformers with geometry-aware intersection understanding yields robust, efficient, long-horizon traffic trajectory generation in simulation-in-the-loop settings, offering improved realism and evaluation capabilities over traditional agent-based models.

title: Cross-Domain Demo-to-Code via Neurosymbolic Counterfactual Reasoning
summary: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have enabled video-instructed robotic programming, allowing agents to interpret video demonstrations and generate executable control code. We formulate video-instructed robotic programming as a cross-domain adaptation problem, where perceptual and physical differences between demonstration and deployment induce procedural mismatches. However, current VLMs lack the procedural understanding needed to reformulate causal dependencies and achieve task-compatible behavior under such domain shifts. We introduce NeSyCR, a neurosymbolic counterfactual reasoning framework that enables verifiable adaptation of task procedures, providing a reliable synthesis of code policies. NeSyCR abstracts video demonstrations into symbolic trajectories that capture the underlying task procedure. Given deployment observations, it derives counterfactual states that reveal cross-domain incompatibilities. By exploring the symbolic state space with verifiable checks, NeSyCR proposes procedural revisions that restore compatibility with the demonstrated procedure. NeSyCR achieves a 31.14% improvement in task success over the strongest baseline Statler, showing robust cross-domain adaptation across both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.

AI.tldr: NeSyCR introduces a neurosymbolic counterfactual reasoning framework to adapt video-instructed robotic programming across domain shifts by abstracting demonstrations into symbolic trajectories, using counterfactuals to identify cross-domain incompatibilities and revise procedures, achieving significant performance gains.
