AI.motivation: Perceptual and physical differences between demonstrations and deployment create procedural mismatches that current VLMs struggle to reformulate causal dependencies for robust task execution; there is a need for verifiable, cross-domain procedural adaptation.
AI.method: NeSyCR abstracts video demonstrations into symbolic trajectories representing task procedures. It observes deployment data to derive counterfactual states revealing incompatibilities, then explores the symbolic state space with verifiable checks to propose procedural revisions that align with the demonstrated procedure.
AI.result: Achieves a 31.14% improvement in task success over the strongest baseline Statler, demonstrating robust cross-domain adaptation in both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.
AI.conclusion: NeSyCR enables verifiable adaptation of task procedures through neurosymbolic counterfactual reasoning, providing reliable code-policy synthesis and improved robustness under domain shifts.

title: ViTac-Tracing: Visual-Tactile Imitation Learning of Deformable Object Tracing
comment: The paper has been accepted by ICRA2026
summary: Deformable objects often appear in unstructured configurations. Tracing deformable objects helps bringing them into extended states and facilitating the downstream manipulation tasks. Due to the requirements for object-specific modeling or sim-to-real transfer, existing tracing methods either lack generalizability across different categories of deformable objects or struggle to complete tasks reliably in the real world. To address this, we propose a novel visual-tactile imitation learning method to achieve one-dimensional (1D) and two-dimensional (2D) deformable object tracing with a unified model. Our method is designed from both local and global perspectives based on visual and tactile sensing. Locally, we introduce a weighted loss that emphasizes actions maintaining contact near the center of the tactile image, improving fine-grained adjustment. Globally, we propose a tracing task loss that helps the policy to regulate task progression. On the hardware side, to compensate for the limited features extracted from visual information, we integrate tactile sensing into a low-cost teleoperation system considering both the teleoperator and the robot. Extensive ablation and comparative experiments on diverse 1D and 2D deformable objects demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, achieving an average success rate of 80% on seen objects and 65% on unseen objects.

AI.tldr: A unified visual-tactile imitation learning framework for tracing 1D and 2D deformable objects, combining local tactile-centric adjustments with a global task progression objective, and validated on hardware with teleoperation; shows strong generalization to seen and moderate generalization to unseen objects.
AI.motivation: Deformable object tracing remains challenging due to high variability in geometry and configurations and limited generalization of existing methods. The work aims to build a generalizable, unified model for tracing across 1D/2D deformable objects by fusing visual and tactile sensing and addressing both local precision and global task progression.
AI.method: Propose a visual-tactile imitation learning approach with two complementary losses: a local weighted loss emphasizing actions where contact is near the tactile image center to improve fine-grained adjustment, and a global tracing task loss to regulate overall task progression. The system integrates tactile sensing into a low-cost teleoperation setup to compensate for limited visual features.
AI.result: Extensive ablations and comparisons on diverse 1D/2D deformable objects show the method’s effectiveness, achieving an average success rate of 80% on seen objects and 65% on unseen objects.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully unifies visual and tactile cues to enhance deformable object tracing across 1D and 2D categories, improving generalization to unseen objects and reliability in real-world settings through the combination of local tactile-aware adjustments and global task regulation.

title: Interpretable Prostate Cancer Detection using a Small Cohort of MRI Images
summary: Prostate cancer is a leading cause of mortality in men, yet interpretation of T2-weighted prostate MRI remains challenging due to subtle and heterogeneous lesions. We developed an interpretable framework for automatic cancer detection using a small dataset of 162 T2-weighted images (102 cancer, 60 normal), addressing data scarcity through transfer learning and augmentation. We performed a comprehensive comparison of Vision Transformers (ViT, Swin), CNNs (ResNet18), and classical methods (Logistic Regression, SVM, HOG+SVM). Transfer-learned ResNet18 achieved the best performance (90.9% accuracy, 95.2% sensitivity, AUC 0.905) with only 11M parameters, while Vision Transformers showed lower performance despite substantially higher complexity. Notably, HOG+SVM achieved comparable accuracy (AUC 0.917), highlighting the effectiveness of handcrafted features in small datasets. Unlike state-of-the-art approaches relying on biparametric MRI (T2+DWI) and large cohorts, our method achieves competitive performance using only T2-weighted images, reducing acquisition complexity and computational cost. In a reader study of 22 cases, five radiologists achieved a mean sensitivity of 67.5% (Fleiss Kappa = 0.524), compared to 95.2% for the AI model, suggesting potential for AI-assisted screening to reduce missed cancers and improve consistency. Code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Small T2-weighted prostate MRI study (162 images) comparing AI architectures and handcrafted features for cancer detection; transfer-learned ResNet18 dominates, ViTs underperform, HOG+SVM close; AI outperforms radiologists in reader study; data/code released.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and need for interpretable, low-cost AI for prostate cancer detection using only T2-weighted MRI.
AI.method: Dataset: 162 T2W images (102 cancer, 60 normal). Data augmentation and transfer learning. Compare ViT, Swin, ResNet18, plus Logistic Regression, SVM, HOG+SVM. Evaluate accuracy, sensitivity, AUC. Reader study with 22 cases comparing radiologists vs AI.
AI.result: Transfer-learned ResNet18 achieved 90.9% accuracy, 95.2% sensitivity, AUC 0.905 with ~11M parameters. ViTs performed worse; HOG+SVM AUC 0.917 comparable accuracy. Radiologists had 67.5% sensitivity; Fleiss Kappa 0.524. Code and data publicly available.
AI.conclusion: In small datasets, transfer learning and handcrafted features offer competitive performance with lower complexity and cost. T2-only approach reduces acquisition and computation; AI potential to assist screening and improve consistency; public release enhances reproducibility.

title: Detection Is Cheap, Routing Is Learned: Why Refusal-Based Alignment Evaluation Fails
summary: Current alignment evaluation mostly measures whether models encode dangerous concepts and whether they refuse harmful requests. Both miss the layer where alignment often operates: routing from concept detection to behavioral policy. We study political censorship in Chinese-origin language models as a natural experiment, using probes, surgical ablations, and behavioral tests across nine open-weight models from five labs. Three findings follow. First, probe accuracy alone is non-diagnostic: political probes, null controls, and permutation baselines can all reach 100%, so held-out category generalization is the informative test. Second, surgical ablation reveals lab-specific routing. Removing the political-sensitivity direction eliminates censorship and restores accurate factual output in most models tested, while one model confabulates because its architecture entangles factual knowledge with the censorship mechanism. Cross-model transfer fails, indicating that routing geometry is model- and lab-specific. Third, refusal is no longer the dominant censorship mechanism. Within one model family, hard refusal falls to zero while narrative steering rises to the maximum, making censorship invisible to refusal-only benchmarks. These results support a three-stage descriptive framework: detect, route, generate. Models often retain the relevant knowledge; alignment changes how that knowledge is expressed. Evaluations that audit only detection or refusal therefore miss the routing mechanism that most directly determines behavior.

AI.tldr: Probe accuracy is insufficient for diagnosing alignment; the critical layer is routing from concept detection to behavioral policy. A three-stage detect–route–generate framework reveals how censorship is actually implemented and why detection/refusal benchmarks miss it.
AI.motivation: Current alignment evaluation focuses on detection or refusal but overlooks the routing step that translates detection into action. The study of political censorship in Chinese-origin LMs provides a natural experiment to isolate routing mechanisms across models.
AI.method: Nine open-weight models from five labs were examined using political probes, null controls, and permutation baselines; surgical ablations removed the political-sensitivity direction to test routing; behavioral tests assessed censorship phenotypes; cross-model transfer was tested to probe routing geometry; the analysis revealed classifiers’ performance across model families.
AI.result: 1) Probe accuracy alone is non-diagnostic; political probes with controls can reach 100%, making held-out generalization the informative test. 2) Surgical ablation exposes lab-specific routing: removing the political-sensitivity direction eliminates censorship and restores factual output in most models; one model confabulates due to entangled architecture; cross-model transfer fails, indicating routing geometry is model- and lab-specific. 3) Refusal is no longer the dominant censorship mechanism: within a model family, hard refusals drop to zero while narrative steering peaks, rendering censorship invisible to refusal-only benchmarks. 4) A three-stage descriptive framework—detect, route, generate—emerges: alignment changes how knowledge is expressed rather than eliminating it; evaluations must audit routing in addition to detection or refusal.
AI.conclusion: Evaluations of alignment should target the routing geometry that translates detected concepts into policy-laden outputs. The knowledge remains latent but can be expressed or suppressed by routing decisions that are model- and lab-specific; robust evaluation requires attention to the detect–route–generate pipeline, not just detection or refusal.

title: "You've got a friend in me": Co-Designing a Peer Social Robot for Young Newcomers' Language and Cultural Learning
summary: Community literacy programs supporting young newcomer children in Canada face limited staffing and scarce one-to-one time, which constrains personalized English and cultural learning support. This paper reports on a co-design study with United for Literacy tutors that informed Maple, a table-top, peer-like Socially Assistive Robot (SAR) designed as a practice partner within tutor-mediated sessions. From shadowing and co-design interviews, we derived newcomer-specific requirements and added them in an integrated prototype that uses short story-based activities, multi-modal scaffolding (speech, facial feedback, gesture), and embedded quizzes that support attention while producing tutor-actionable formative signals. We contribute system design implications for tutor-in-the-loop SARs supporting language socialization in community settings and outline directions for child-centered evaluation in authentic programs.

AI.tldr: Co-design with United for Literacy tutors to create Maple, a table-top, peer-like Socially Assistive Robot (SAR) used as a practice partner in tutor-mediated sessions for newcomer children in Canada; combines story-based activities, multi-modal scaffolding, and embedded quizzes to provide tutor action signals and support language socialization.
AI.motivation: Address limited staffing and scarce one-to-one time in community literacy programs for young newcomers, by augmenting tutoring with a SAR that supports English learning and cultural socialization in tutor-mediated sessions.
AI.method: Shadowing and co-design interviews with tutors; incorporation of newcomer-specific requirements into an integrated Maple prototype; implementation of short story-based activities, multi-modal scaffolding (speech, facial feedback, gesture), and embedded quizzes to sustain attention and yield tutor-actionable signals.
AI.result: Development of an integrated Maple prototype featuring story-based activities, multimodal scaffolding, and embedded quizzes, along with tutor action signals and design implications for tutor-in-the-loop SARs in community settings; outlines directions for child-centered evaluation in authentic programs.
AI.conclusion: The study provides system design implications for tutor-in-the-loop SARs to support language socialization in community settings and highlights the need for child-centered evaluation within authentic newcomer programs.

title: Cell-Type Prototype-Informed Neural Network for Gene Expression Estimation from Pathology Images
summary: Estimating slide- and patch-level gene expression profiles from pathology images enables rapid and low-cost molecular analysis with broad clinical impact. Despite strong results, existing approaches treat gene expression as a mere slide- or spot-level signal and do not incorporate the fact that the measured expression arises from the aggregation of underlying cell-level expression. To explicitly introduce this missing cell-resolved guidance, we propose a Cell-type Prototype-informed Neural Network (CPNN) that leverages publicly available single-cell RNA-sequencing datasets. Since single-cell measurements are noisy and not paired with histology images, we first estimate cell-type prototypes-mean expression profiles that reflect stable gene-gene co-variation patterns.CPNN then learns cell-type compositional weights directly from images and models the relationship between prototypes and observed bulk or spatial expression, providing a biologically grounded and structurally regularized prediction framework. We evaluate CPNN on three slide-level datasets and three patch-level spatial transcriptomics datasets. Across all settings, CPNN achieves the highest performance in terms of Spearman correlation. Moreover, by visualizing the inferred compositional weights, our framework provides interpretable insights into which cell types drive the predicted expression. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/naivete5656/CPNN.

AI.tldr: A cell-type prototype-informed neural network (CPNN) predicts gene expression from pathology images by leveraging scRNA-seq-derived cell-type prototypes to infer cell-type composition, achieving state-of-the-art Spearman correlations across multiple slide- and patch-level datasets with interpretable cell-type contributions; code released.
AI.motivation: Gene expression reflects aggregation of cell-level activity, but existing image-based approaches treat it as a single slide-/spot-level signal and lack cell-resolution guidance. Incorporating biologically grounded cell-type structure could improve accuracy and interpretability.
AI.method: Derive cell-type prototypes from publicly available scRNA-seq data as mean expression profiles capturing stable gene-gene co-variation. Train a neural network to infer cell-type compositional weights from pathology images and model the relationship between prototypes and observed bulk/spatial expression, providing a regularized, biologically grounded prediction framework.
AI.result: Evaluated on three slide-level datasets and three patch-level spatial transcriptomics datasets; CPNN achieves the highest Spearman correlation across settings. Visualizing inferred compositional weights yields interpretable insights into driving cell types. Code is publicly available at the authors' GitHub.
AI.conclusion: CPNN offers a biologically grounded, interpretable, and high-performing approach for image-based transcriptomics by explicitly modeling cell-type composition from pathology images using scRNA-seq prototypes, with open-source code for reproducibility.

title: On Additive Gaussian Processes for Wind Farm Power Prediction
summary: Population-based Structural Health Monitoring (PBSHM) aims to share information between similar machines or structures. This paper takes a population-level perspective, exploring the use of additive Gaussian processes to reveal variations in turbine-specific and farm-level power models over a collected wind farm dataset. The predictions illustrate patterns in wind farm power generation, which follow intuition and should enable more informed control and decision-making.

AI.tldr: Additive GPto model turbine- and farm-level power variations in wind farms; enables population-level insights for PBSHM.
AI.motivation: PBSHM aims to share information across similar machines; understanding turbine and farm-level variations facilitates better control decisions.
AI.method: Employs additive Gaussian processes to decompose wind-farm power into turbine-specific and farm-level components across a dataset.
AI.result: Predicted power patterns align with intuition and reveal population-level structure, supporting informed control decisions.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of population-level analysis with additive GPs for wind-farm SHM and potential improvements in decision-making.

title: Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization for Compositional Generation
summary: Text-to-image models produce images that align well with natural language prompts, but compositional generation has long been a central challenge. Models often struggle to satisfy multiple concepts within a single prompt, frequently omitting some concepts and resulting in partial success. Such failures highlight the difficulty of jointly optimizing multiple concepts during reward optimization, where competing concepts can interfere with one another. To address this limitation, we propose Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization (\ours), a framework that leverages the correlation structure among concept rewards to adaptively weight each attribute concept in optimization. By accounting for interactions among concepts, \ours balances competing reward signals and emphasizes concepts that are partially satisfied yet inconsistently generated across samples, improving compositional generation. Specifically, we decompose multi-concept prompts into pre-defined concept groups (\eg, objects, attributes, and relations) and obtain reward signals from dedicated reward models for each concept. We then adaptively reweight these rewards, assigning higher weights to conflicting or hard-to-satisfy concepts using correlation-based difficulty estimation. By focusing optimization on the most challenging concepts within each group, \ours encourages the model to consistently satisfy all requested attributes simultaneously. We apply our approach to train state-of-the-art diffusion models, SD3.5 and FLUX.1-dev, and demonstrate consistent improvements on challenging multi-concept benchmarks, including ConceptMix, GenEval 2, and T2I-CompBench.

AI.tldr: Proposes Correlation-Weighted Multi-Reward Optimization to weight concept rewards using correlation structure to improve compositional text-to-image generation; demonstrates gains on SD3.5/FLUX.1-dev and benchmarks ConceptMix, GenEval 2, T2I-CompBench.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image models struggle with satisfying multiple concepts simultaneously due to competing reward signals; interactions among concepts lead to partial/successful but inconsistent generation.
AI.method: Decompose prompts into concept groups; obtain per-concept rewards; compute correlation-based difficulty; adaptively reweight rewards to emphasize hard-to-satisfy concepts; train diffusion models with this weighted objective.
AI.result: Consistent improvements on challenging multi-concept benchmarks; better satisfaction of attributes; improved compositional generation across samples.
AI.conclusion: Correlation-weighted reward optimization helps balance competing signals and enhances compositionality, with potential applicability to other models and future work on reward design.

title: V-Dreamer: Automating Robotic Simulation and Trajectory Synthesis via Video Generation Priors
summary: Training generalist robots demands large-scale, diverse manipulation data, yet real-world collection is prohibitively expensive, and existing simulators are often constrained by fixed asset libraries and manual heuristics. To bridge this gap, we present V-Dreamer, a fully automated framework that generates open-vocabulary, simulation-ready manipulation environments and executable expert trajectories directly from natural language instructions. V-Dreamer employs a novel generative pipeline that constructs physically grounded 3D scenes using large language models and 3D generative models, validated by geometric constraints to ensure stable, collision-free layouts. Crucially, for behavior synthesis, we leverage video generation models as rich motion priors. These visual predictions are then mapped into executable robot trajectories via a robust Sim-to-Gen visual-kinematic alignment module utilizing CoTracker3 and VGGT. This pipeline supports high visual diversity and physical fidelity without manual intervention. To evaluate the generated data, we train imitation learning policies on synthesized trajectories encompassing diverse object and environment variations. Extensive evaluations on tabletop manipulation tasks using the Piper robotic arm demonstrate that our policies robustly generalize to unseen objects in simulation and achieve effective sim-to-real transfer, successfully manipulating novel real-world objects.

AI.tldr: V-Dreamer automates open-vocabulary, simulation-ready manipulation data generation from text by fusing LLMs and 3D generative models with video priors and sim-to-gen alignment; evaluated with policy learning on Piper arm showing generalization to unseen objects and sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Large-scale, diverse manipulation data is essential for training generalist robots, but real-world collection is costly and existing simulators rely on fixed assets and heuristics.
AI.method: A fully automated pipeline that (1) builds physically grounded 3D scenes from natural language using LLMs and 3D generators with geometric validation; (2) uses video generation models as motion priors to produce rich trajectories; (3) maps visual priors to executable robot trajectories via a Sim-to-Gen alignment module with CoTracker3 and VGGT; (4) trains imitation policies on synthesized data with diverse objects/environments; no manual intervention.
AI.result: Imitation policies trained on synthesized trajectories generalize to unseen objects in simulation and achieve effective sim-to-real transfer, enabling manipulation of novel real-world objects on the Piper arm.
AI.conclusion: V-Dreamer provides automated, open-vocabulary, simulation-ready manipulation data with high visual diversity and physical fidelity, bridging the gap for scalable training of generalist robots.

title: MedQ-UNI: Toward Unified Medical Image Quality Assessment and Restoration via Vision-Language Modeling
summary: Existing medical image restoration (Med-IR) methods are typically modality-specific or degradation-specific, failing to generalize across the heterogeneous degradations encountered in clinical practice. We argue this limitation stems from the isolation of Med-IR from medical image quality assessment (Med-IQA), as restoration models without explicit quality understanding struggle to adapt to diverse degradation types across modalities. To address these challenges, we propose MedQ-UNI, a unified vision-language model that follows an assess-then-restore paradigm, explicitly leveraging Med-IQA to guide Med-IR across arbitrary modalities and degradation types. MedQ-UNI adopts a multimodal autoregressive dual-expert architecture with shared attention: a quality assessment expert first identifies degradation issues through structured natural language descriptions, and a restoration expert then conditions on these descriptions to perform targeted image restoration. To support this paradigm, we construct a large-scale dataset of approximately 50K paired samples spanning three imaging modalities and five restoration tasks, each annotated with structured quality descriptions for joint Med-IQA and Med-IR training, along with a 2K-sample benchmark for evaluation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that a single MedQ-UNI model, without any task-specific adaptation, achieves state-of-the-art restoration performance across all tasks while generating superior descriptions, confirming that explicit quality understanding meaningfully improves restoration fidelity and interpretability.

AI.tldr: A unified vision-language MedQ-UNI model enables assess-then-restore across modalities by leveraging Med-IQA. It uses a dual-expert multimodal architecture, a large paired dataset, and achieves state-of-the-art restoration on multiple tasks without task-specific adaptation, with interpretable quality descriptions.
AI.motivation: Current Med-IR methods are typically confined to specific modalities or degradations and are not integrated with quality assessment. This gap limits generalization to diverse clinical degradations. Integrating Med-IQA can guide restoration and improve robustness and interpretability.
AI.method: A multimodal autoregressive dual-expert architecture with shared attention: an assessment expert generates structured natural language quality descriptions of degradation; a restoration expert conditions on these descriptions to perform restoration. Trained on ~50k paired samples across three imaging modalities and five restoration tasks, with a 2k-sample benchmark for evaluation. Data annotated with structured Med-IQA/Med-IR descriptions enabling joint training.
AI.result: A single MedQ-UNI model achieves state-of-the-art restoration across all tasks without task-specific adaptation and produces superior quality descriptions. Explicit quality understanding improves restoration fidelity and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: Integrating Med-IQA with Med-IR in a unified model yields robust, generalizable restoration across modalities and degradations, and enhances interpretability through generated degradation descriptions; dataset and benchmark support broad evaluation.

title: Path-Constrained Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures enable efficient scaling by activating only a subset of parameters for each input. However, conventional MoE routing selects each layer's experts independently, creating N^L possible expert paths -- for N experts across L layers. This far exceeds typical training set sizes, leading to statistical inefficiency as the model may not learn meaningful structure over such a vast path space. To constrain it, we propose \pathmoe, which shares router parameters across consecutive layers. Experiments on 0.9B and 16B parameter models demonstrate consistent improvements on perplexity and downstream tasks over independent routing, while eliminating the need for auxiliary load balancing losses. Analysis reveals that tokens following the same path naturally cluster by linguistic function, with \pathmoe{} producing more concentrated groups, better cross-layer consistency, and greater robustness to routing perturbations. These results offer a new perspective for understanding MoE architectures through the lens of expert paths.

AI.tldr: Path-sharing routing for sparse MoEs constrains the exponential expert-path space, yielding better statistical efficiency and performance by parameter-sharing routers across consecutive layers; it improves perplexity and downstream tasks, eliminates load-balancing losses, and reveals linguistically coherent path clustering and cross-layer consistency.
AI.motivation: Conventional MoE routing selects experts independently per layer, creating N^L possible paths, which is statistically inefficient given typical training data sizes. A constrained, shared-router approach aims to reduce path complexity and improve learning of structured representations.
AI.method: Introduce path-based MoE (PathMoE) that shares router parameters across consecutive layers. Evaluate on 0.9B and 16B parameter models. Analyze token-path clustering by linguistic function, cross-layer consistency, and robustness to routing perturbations; compare against independent routing and assess the need for load-balancing losses.
AI.result: PathMoE consistently improves perplexity and downstream tasks versus independent routing across large models. It eliminates the need for auxiliary load-balancing losses. Tokens following the same path cluster by linguistic function, with more concentrated groupings, stronger cross-layer consistency, and greater robustness to routing perturbations.
AI.conclusion: View MoE architectures through the lens of expert paths. Path sharing reduces statistical inefficiency, enhances routing stability, and yields more linguistically coherent and robust expert-path structures.

title: Reasonably reasoning AI agents can avoid game-theoretic failures in zero-shot, provably
summary: AI agents are increasingly deployed in interactive economic environments characterized by repeated AI-AI interactions. Despite AI agents' advanced capabilities, empirical studies reveal that such interactions often fail to stably induce a strategic equilibrium, such as a Nash equilibrium. Post-training methods have been proposed to induce a strategic equilibrium; however, it remains impractical to uniformly apply an alignment method across diverse, independently developed AI models in strategic settings. In this paper, we provide theoretical and empirical evidence that off-the-shelf reasoning AI agents can achieve Nash-like play zero-shot, without explicit post-training. Specifically, we prove that `reasonably reasoning' agents, i.e., agents capable of forming beliefs about others' strategies from previous observation and learning to best respond to these beliefs, eventually behave along almost every realized play path in a way that is weakly close to a Nash equilibrium of the continuation game. In addition, we relax the common-knowledge payoff assumption by allowing stage payoffs to be unknown and by having each agent observe only its own privately realized stochastic payoffs, and we show that we can still achieve the same on-path Nash convergence guarantee. We then empirically validate the proposed theories by simulating five game scenarios, ranging from a repeated prisoner's dilemma game to stylized repeated marketing promotion games. Our findings suggest that AI agents naturally exhibit such reasoning patterns and therefore attain stable equilibrium behaviors intrinsically, obviating the need for universal alignment procedures in many real-world strategic interactions.

AI.tldr: Reasoning AI agents can achieve Nash-like play zero-shot in repeated AI–AI settings without post-training, by forming beliefs about others and best-responding; even with unknown stage payoffs and private observations, on-path convergence to Nash-like behavior persists; empirically validated across five game scenarios.
AI.motivation: Address the impracticality of universal post-training alignment for diverse, independently developed AI models in strategic environments; demonstrate intrinsic stability and equilibrium-like behavior in off-the-shelf reasoning agents.
AI.method: Theoretically prove that 'reasonably reasoning' agents—those forming beliefs about others’ strategies from observations and best-responding—converge to Nash-like behavior on almost all realized play paths in the continuation game. Relax common-knowledge payoffs, allowing unknown stage payoffs and private realized stochastic payoffs while preserving on-path Nash convergence. Empirically validate via simulations across five games (e.g., repeated prisoner's dilemma, stylized marketing promotion games).
AI.result: Theoretical results show on-path convergence to Nash-like behavior in continuation games without post-training. Empirical simulations corroborate the theory, indicating that AI agents naturally exhibit reasoning patterns leading to stable equilibrium behaviors in repeated strategic interactions.
AI.conclusion: Findings suggest that off-the-shelf reasoning AI agents inherently tend toward Nash-like equilibria in many repeated strategic settings, reducing reliance on universal post-training alignment. This has practical implications for deploying diverse AI agents in economic environments, though success depends on assumptions about agents’ reasoning capabilities and observational access.

title: A Passive Elastic-Folding Mechanism for Stackable Airdrop Sensors
comment: 8 pages, 8 figures, The 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026)
summary: Air-dispersed sensor networks deployed from aerial robotic systems (e.g., UAVs) provide a low-cost approach to wide-area environmental monitoring. However, existing methods often rely on active actuators for mid-air shape or trajectory control, increasing both power consumption and system cost. Here, we introduce a passive elastic-folding hinge mechanism that transforms sensors from a flat, stackable form into a three-dimensional structure upon release. Hinges are fabricated by laminating commercial sheet materials with rigid printed circuit boards (PCBs) and programming fold angles through a single oven-heating step, enabling scalable production without specialized equipment. Our geometric model links laminate geometry, hinge mechanics, and resulting fold angle, providing a predictive design methodology for target configurations. Laboratory tests confirmed fold angles between 10 degrees and 100 degrees, with a standard deviation of 4 degrees and high repeatability. Field trials further demonstrated reliable data collection and LoRa transmission during dispersion, while the Horizontal Wind Model (HWM)-based trajectory simulations indicated strong potential for wide-area sensing exceeding 10 km.

AI.tldr: Passive elastic-folding hinge for air-dispersed sensors enabling flat-to-3D deployment via single-step oven lamination; predictive design model; lab and field validation; potential for wide-area sensing (>10 km) with LoRa.
AI.motivation: Reduce power consumption and system cost by removing active mid-air actuators for shape/trajectory control in UAV-dispersed sensor networks; enable scalable, wide-area environmental monitoring.
AI.method: Hinges produced by laminating commercial sheet materials with rigid PCBs; fold angles programmed through a single oven-heating step; geometric model links laminate geometry, hinge mechanics, and fold angle to enable predictive design; validated with lab tests (fold angles 10°–100°, stdev 4°); field trials demonstrated reliable data collection and LoRa transmission during dispersion; trajectory simulations using Horizontal Wind Model (HWM) to assess wide-area sensing potential.
AI.result: Achieved fold angles in the 10°–100° range with low spread (σ ≈ 4°) and good repeatability; field trials confirmed functional data collection and successful LoRa communication under dispersion; HWM-based simulations suggest feasibility of wide-area sensing beyond 10 km.
AI.conclusion: The work presents a scalable, low-cost, passive folding approach for air-dispersed sensors that eliminates the need for active actuators, enabling large-area environmental sensing. Practical deployment considerations include durability under field conditions, payload constraints, and robustness of the folding hinges; further optimization and extended field validation are suggested.

title: Recolour What Matters: Region-Aware Colour Editing via Token-Level Diffusion
summary: Colour is one of the most perceptually salient yet least controllable attributes in image generation. Although recent diffusion models can modify object colours from user instructions, their results often deviate from the intended hue, especially for fine-grained and local edits. Early text-driven methods rely on discrete language descriptions that cannot accurately represent continuous chromatic variations. To overcome this limitation, we propose ColourCrafter, a unified diffusion framework that transforms colour editing from global tone transfer into a structured, region-aware generation process. Unlike traditional colour driven methods, ColourCrafter performs token-level fusion of RGB colour tokens and image tokens in latent space, selectively propagating colour information to semantically relevant regions while preserving structural fidelity. A perceptual Lab-space Loss further enhances pixel-level precision by decoupling luminance and chrominance and constraining edits within masked areas. Additionally, we build ColourfulSet, a largescale dataset of high-quality image pairs with continuous and diverse colour variations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ColourCrafter achieves state-of-the-art colour accuracy, controllability and perceptual fidelity in fine-grained colour editing. Our project is available at https://yangyuqi317.github.io/ColourCrafter.github.io/.

AI.tldr: ColourCrafter provides a diffusion-based, region-aware color editing framework that fuses RGB colour tokens with image tokens in latent space, augmented by a Lab-space perceptual loss, and is backed by ColourfulSet for continuous color variations; claims state-of-the-art accuracy, controllability, and fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion methods struggle to realize precise, localized colour edits; global tone transfer and discrete language descriptions fail to capture continuous chromatic variations and fine-grained colour control while preserving structure.
AI.method: A unified diffusion framework performing token-level fusion of RGB colour tokens and image tokens in latent space to propagate colour information selectively to semantically relevant regions; introduces a Lab-space perceptual loss to decouple luminance and chrominance and constrain edits within masked areas; presents ColourfulSet, a large-scale paired dataset with continuous colour variations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments indicate ColourCrafter achieves state-of-the-art colour accuracy, controllability, and perceptual fidelity for fine-grained colour editing.
AI.conclusion: ColourCrafter effectively enables precise, region-aware colour edits with preserved structure, supported by a dedicated dataset; code and project page are provided.

title: ALIGN: Adversarial Learning for Generalizable Speech Neuroprosthesis
summary: Intracortical brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) can decode speech from neural activity with high accuracy when trained on data pooled across recording sessions. In realistic deployment, however, models must generalize to new sessions without labeled data, and performance often degrades due to cross-session nonstationarities (e.g., electrode shifts, neural turnover, and changes in user strategy). In this paper, we propose ALIGN, a session-invariant learning framework based on multi-domain adversarial neural networks for semi-supervised cross-session adaptation. ALIGN trains a feature encoder jointly with a phoneme classifier and a domain classifier operating on the latent representation. Through adversarial optimization, the encoder is encouraged to preserve task-relevant information while suppressing session-specific cues. We evaluate ALIGN on intracortical speech decoding and find that it generalizes consistently better to previously unseen sessions, improving both phoneme error rate and word error rate relative to baselines. These results indicate that adversarial domain alignment is an effective approach for mitigating session-level distribution shift and enabling robust longitudinal BCI decoding.

AI.tldr: ALIGN is a semi-supervised, session-invariant learning framework using multi-domain adversarial networks to adapt intracortical speech decoders across sessions without labeled data.
AI.motivation: Cross-session nonstationarities (electrode shifts, neural turnover, strategy changes) degrade BCI decoding; robust longitudinal performance requires adaptation without labeled data.
AI.method: Jointly train a feature encoder, a phoneme classifier, and a domain classifier on the latent space. Use adversarial optimization so the encoder preserves task-relevant information while removing session-specific cues. Operates in a semi-supervised, multi-domain setting across sessions.
AI.result: Generalizes better to unseen sessions, achieving improvements in phoneme error rate and word error rate compared to baselines; demonstrates effective mitigation of session-level distribution shifts.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial domain alignment is a viable strategy for robust cross-session BCI decoding and long-term stability in neural speech interfaces.

title: CAPSUL: A Comprehensive Human Protein Benchmark for Subcellular Localization
summary: Subcellular localization is a crucial biological task for drug target identification and function annotation. Although it has been biologically realized that subcellular localization is closely associated with protein structure, no existing dataset offers comprehensive 3D structural information with detailed subcellular localization annotations, thus severely hindering the application of promising structure-based models on this task. To address this gap, we introduce a new benchmark called $\mathbf{CAPSUL}$, a $\mathbf{C}$omprehensive hum$\mathbf{A}$n $\mathbf{P}$rotein benchmark for $\mathbf{SU}$bcellular $\mathbf{L}$ocalization. It features a dataset that integrates diverse 3D structural representations with fine-grained subcellular localization annotations carefully curated by domain experts. We evaluate this benchmark using a variety of state-of-the-art sequence-based and structure-based models, showcasing the importance of involving structural features in this task. Furthermore, we explore reweighting and single-label classification strategies to facilitate future investigation on structure-based methods for this task. Lastly, we showcase the powerful interpretability of structure-based methods through a case study on the Golgi apparatus, where we discover a decisive localization pattern $α$-helix from attention mechanisms, demonstrating the potential for bridging the gap with intuitive biological interpretability and paving the way for data-driven discoveries in cell biology.

AI.tldr: Introduces CAPSUL, a comprehensive 3D-structure–aware benchmark for subcellular localization, integrating diverse structural representations with fine-grained annotations; shows structure features improve predictions and offers reweighting, single-label strategies, and an interpretability case study (α-helix localization pattern in Golgi).
AI.motivation: Subcellular localization is closely linked to protein structure, but there is no dataset that jointly provides comprehensive 3D structural data with detailed localization annotations, hindering development of structure-based models.
AI.method: Construct CAPSUL by aggregating diverse 3D structural representations with expert-curated, fine-grained subcellular localization labels. Evaluate both sequence-based and structure-based models, explore reweighting and single-label classification strategies, and perform interpretability analysis via attention, including a Golgi case study to identify a decisive α-helix localization pattern.
AI.result: Demonstrates that incorporating structural features improves localization predictions, compares multiple models, shows utility of reweighting and single-label strategies, and provides an interpretable case study linking a specific α-helix pattern to Golgi localization.
AI.conclusion: CAPSUL provides a comprehensive, structure-aware benchmark for subcellular localization, enabling development of structure-based methods and fostering biologically interpretable, data-driven insights in cell biology.

title: Safety-Guaranteed Imitation Learning from Nonlinear Model Predictive Control for Spacecraft Close Proximity Operations
summary: This paper presents a safety-guaranteed, runtime-efficient imitation learning framework for spacecraft close proximity control. We leverage Control Barrier Functions (CBFs) for safety certificates and Control Lyapunov Functions (CLFs) for stability as unified design principles across data generation, training, and deployment. First, a nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) expert enforces CBF constraints to provide safe reference trajectories. Second, we train a neural policy with a novel CBF-CLF-informed loss and DAgger-like rollouts with curriculum weighting, promoting data-efficiency and reducing future safety filter interventions. Third, at deployment a lightweight one-step CBF-CLF quadratic program minimally adjusts the learned control input to satisfy hard safety constraints while encouraging stability. We validate the approach for ESA-compliant close proximity operations, including fly-around with a spherical keep-out zone and final approach inside a conical approach corridor, using the Basilisk high-fidelity simulator with nonlinear dynamics and perturbations. Numerical experiments indicate stable convergence to decision points and strict adherence to safety under the filter, with task performance comparable to the NMPC expert while significantly reducing online computation. A runtime analysis demonstrates real-time feasibility on a commercial off-the-shelf processor, supporting onboard deployment for safety-critical on-orbit servicing.

AI.tldr: Imitation learning with safety guarantees for spacecraft proximity control using CBFs and CLFs; combines NMPC-sourced safety, efficient policy training with a CBF-CLF loss and DAgger-like rollouts, and a one-step safety filter for real-time deployment.
AI.motivation: Achieve real-time, safety-critical autonomous spacecraft close-quarters operations with reduced online computation and provable safety/stability.
AI.method: Three-stage approach: (1) NMPC expert enforces CBF constraints to generate safe references; (2) train neural policy using a CBF-CLF–informed loss and DAgger-like curriculum for data efficiency and fewer safety filter interventions; (3) deploy with a lightweight one-step CBF-CLF quadratic program to minimally adjust the learned input to satisfy hard safety constraints while promoting stability. Validation in Basilisk simulator for ESA-like tasks (spherical keep-out, conical approach).
AI.result: Numerical experiments show convergence to decision points and strict safety adherence under the filter; task performance close to the NMPC expert while substantially reducing online computation; runtime analysis indicates real-time feasibility on a commodity processor, supporting onboard deployment for on-orbit servicing.
AI.conclusion: A practical, safety-guaranteed imitation learning pipeline for spacecraft proximity control that uses CBFs/CLFs as unified safety and stability certificates across data generation, training, and deployment, enabling safe, efficient autonomous operation with reduced compute.

title: Do Vision Language Models Understand Human Engagement in Games?
summary: Inferring human engagement from gameplay video is important for game design and player-experience research, yet it remains unclear whether vision--language models (VLMs) can infer such latent psychological states from visual cues alone. Using the GameVibe Few-Shot dataset across nine first-person shooter games, we evaluate three VLMs under six prompting strategies, including zero-shot prediction, theory-guided prompts grounded in Flow, GameFlow, Self-Determination Theory, and MDA, and retrieval-augmented prompting. We consider both pointwise engagement prediction and pairwise prediction of engagement change between consecutive windows. Results show that zero-shot VLM predictions are generally weak and often fail to outperform simple per-game majority-class baselines. Memory- or retrieval-augmented prompting improves pointwise prediction in some settings, whereas pairwise prediction remains consistently difficult across strategies. Theory-guided prompting alone does not reliably help and can instead reinforce surface-level shortcuts. These findings suggest a perception--understanding gap in current VLMs: although they can recognize visible gameplay cues, they still struggle to robustly infer human engagement across games.

AI.tldr: VLMs struggle to infer human engagement from gameplay visuals; zero-shot is weak; retrieval helps pointwise, but pairwise engagement changes are hard; theory-guided prompts are not reliably beneficial, indicating a perception–understanding gap.
AI.motivation: Assess whether vision-language models can infer latent psychological states (engagement) from visual gameplay cues using a cross-game FPS dataset.
AI.method: Evaluate three VLMs under six prompting strategies (zero-shot, theory-guided prompts anchored in Flow, GameFlow, Self-Determination Theory, and MDA; and retrieval-augmented prompting) on pointwise and pairwise engagement prediction across nine FPS games from the GameVibe Few-Shot dataset.
AI.result: Zero-shot predictions are generally weak and often do not outperform simple per-game baselines. Retrieval-augmented prompting improves pointwise prediction in some scenarios; pairwise prediction remains difficult across strategies.
AI.conclusion: Theory-guided prompting alone is not reliably beneficial and can reinforce superficial shortcuts. There is a perception–understanding gap in current VLMs: while they can recognize visible cues, they struggle to robustly infer engagement across games.

title: Interplay: Training Independent Simulators for Reference-Free Conversational Recommendation
summary: Training conversational recommender systems (CRS) requires extensive dialogue data, which is challenging to collect at scale. To address this, researchers have used simulated user-recommender conversations. Traditional simulation approaches often utilize a single large language model (LLM) that generates entire conversations with prior knowledge of the target items, leading to scripted and artificial dialogues. We propose a reference-free simulation framework that trains two independent LLMs, one as the user and one as the conversational recommender. These models interact in real-time without access to predetermined target items, but preference summaries and target attributes, enabling the recommender to genuinely infer user preferences through dialogue. This approach produces more realistic and diverse conversations that closely mirror authentic human-AI interactions. Our reference-free simulators match or exceed existing methods in quality, while offering a scalable solution for generating high-quality conversational recommendation data without constraining conversations to pre-defined target items. We conduct both quantitative and human evaluations to confirm the effectiveness of our reference-free approach.

AI.tldr: A reference-free two-LLM simulation framework for conversational recommender systems that creates realistic, diverse dialogues without fixed target items, matching or surpassing prior methods in quality and scalability.
AI.motivation: CRS requires large-scale dialogue data for effective training. Traditional simulations use a single LLM with knowledge of target items, yielding scripted conversations and high bias; there is a need for scalable, realistic data without predetermined targets.
AI.method: Train two independent LLMs as user and conversational recommender; they interact in real time without access to predetermined target items. They use preference summaries and target attributes to guide the recommender, enabling genuine inference of user preferences through dialogue. The framework is reference-free, avoiding reliance on predefined targets.
AI.result: The simulators produce more realistic and diverse conversations, matching or exceeding the quality of existing methods. Quantitative and human evaluations confirm effectiveness and scalability for generating high-quality CRS data.
AI.conclusion: Reference-free, dual-LLM simulation is effective for CRS data generation, delivering realistic interactions and scalable data without constraining conversations to pre-defined target items.

title: Lightweight Model Predictive Control for Spacecraft Rendezvous Attitude Synchronization
summary: This work introduces two lightweight model predictive control (MPC) approaches for attitude tracking with reaction wheels during spacecraft rendezvous synchronization. Both approaches are based on a novel attitude deviation formulation, which enables the use of inherently linear constraints on angular velocity. We develop a single-loop and a dual-loop MPC; the latter embeds a stabilizing feedback controller within the inner loop, yielding a linear time-invariant system. Both controllers are implemented with CasADi - including automatic code generation - evaluated across various solvers, and validated within the Basilisk astrodynamics simulation framework. The experimental results demonstrate improved tracking accuracy alongside reductions in computational effort and memory consumption. Finally, embedded delivery to an ARM Cortex-M7 - representative of commercial off-the-shelf devices used in New Space platforms - confirms the real-time feasibility of these approaches and highlights their suitability for onboard attitude control in resource-constrained spacecraft rendezvous missions.

AI.tldr: Two lightweight MPC schemes for spacecraft attitude tracking with reaction wheels, leveraging a novel attitude deviation formulation to enforce linear angular-velocity constraints; single-loop and dual-loop designs implemented with CasADi and validated in Basilisk; feasible in real-time on ARM Cortex-M7 for resource-constrained rendezvous missions.
AI.motivation: Attitude control during spacecraft rendezvous requires accurate tracking under tight resource constraints. Conventional MPC can be computationally heavy due to nonlinear dynamics and nonconvex constraints; therefore a lightweight, real-time capable strategy is needed.
AI.method: Introduce a novel attitude deviation formulation that yields linear angular-velocity constraints. Develop a single-loop MPC and a dual-loop MPC (inner loop includes a stabilizing feedback, yielding an LTI system). Implement with CasADi (automatic code generation), evaluate across solvers, validate in the Basilisk astrodynamics framework, and demonstrate onboard feasibility on an ARM Cortex-M7.
AI.result: Improved tracking accuracy with reduced computational load and memory usage. Real-time feasibility demonstrated on an ARM Cortex-M7, indicating suitability for onboard attitude control in resource-constrained rendezvous missions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight MPC approaches enable accurate, efficient onboard attitude control for spacecraft rendezvous, with a design that supports real-time operation on modest hardware through a linearized/decomposed control formulation and CasADi-based implementation.

title: T-QPM: Enabling Temporal Out-Of-Distribution Detection and Domain Generalization for Vision-Language Models in Open-World
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection remains a critical challenge in open-world learning, where models must adapt to evolving data distributions. While recent vision-language models (VLMS) like CLIP enable multimodal OOD detection through Dual-Pattern Matching (DPM), existing methods typically suffer from two major shortcomings: (1) They rely on fixed fusion rules and assume static environments, failing under temporal drift; and (2) they lack robustness against covariate shifted inputs. In this paper, we propose a novel two-step framework to enhance OOD detection and covariate distribution shift robustness in dynamic settings. We extend the dual-pattern regime into Temporal Quadruple-Pattern Matching (T-QPM). First, by pairing OOD images with text descriptions, we introduce cross-modal consistency patterns between ID and OOD signals, refining the decision boundary through joint image-text reasoning. Second, we address temporal distribution shifts by learning lightweight fusion weights to optimally combine semantic matching and visual typicality. To ensure stability, we enforce explicit regularization based on Average Thresholded Confidence (ATC), preventing performance degradation as distributions evolve. Experiments on temporally partitioned benchmarks demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms static baselines, offering a robust, temporally-consistent framework for multimodal OOD detection in non-stationary environments.

AI.tldr: Extends CLIP-like multimodal OOD detection to dynamic, non-stationary environments via Temporal Quadruple-Pattern Matching (T-QPM).
AI.motivation: OOD detection in open-world, non-stationary settings where fixed fusion and static assumptions fail under temporal drift and covariate shifts.
AI.method: A two-step framework: (1) cross-modal pairing of OOD images with text to enforce cross-modal consistency between ID and OOD signals, refining the decision boundary via joint image-text reasoning; (2) learn lightweight fusion weights to combine semantic matching and visual typicality to handle temporal distribution shifts; Regularization with Average Thresholded Confidence (ATC) for stability.
AI.result: On temporally partitioned benchmarks, the method significantly outperforms static baselines, delivering a robust, temporally-consistent multimodal OOD detector under non-stationary distributions.
AI.conclusion: Transforms Dual-Pattern Matching into Temporal Quadruple-Pattern Matching by incorporating cross-modal pairing, adaptive fusion, and ATC regularization, yielding improved robustness to temporal drift and covariate shifts in VLMS-based OOD detection.

title: Learning to Reason with Curriculum I: Provable Benefits of Autocurriculum
summary: Chain-of-thought reasoning, where language models expend additional computation by producing thinking tokens prior to final responses, has driven significant advances in model capabilities. However, training these reasoning models is extremely costly in terms of both data and compute, as it involves collecting long traces of reasoning behavior from humans or synthetic generators and further post-training the model via reinforcement learning. Are these costs fundamental, or can they be reduced through better algorithmic design? We show that autocurriculum, where the model uses its own performance to decide which problems to focus training on, provably improves upon standard training recipes for both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL). For SFT, we show that autocurriculum requires exponentially fewer reasoning demonstrations than non-adaptive fine-tuning, by focusing teacher supervision on prompts where the current model struggles. For RL fine-tuning, autocurriculum decouples the computational cost from the quality of the reference model, reducing the latter to a burn-in cost that is nearly independent of the target accuracy. These improvements arise purely from adaptive data selection, drawing on classical techniques from boosting and learning from counterexamples, and requiring no assumption on the distribution or difficulty of prompts.

AI.tldr: Autocurriculum improves training efficiency for chain-of-thought models via self-guided data selection; yields exponential data-efficiency gains in supervised fine-tuning and decouples RL cost from target accuracy, using boosting-like adaptive strategies without distributional assumptions.
AI.motivation: Reduce the substantial data and compute costs of training reasoning-enabled language models by adaptive data selection that focuses on the model's current weaknesses.
AI.method: Formulate an autocurriculum that uses the model’s own performance to determine which prompts to train on. For supervised fine-tuning (SFT), concentrate teacher supervision on prompts where the model struggles; for reinforcement learning (RL), decouple training cost from reference model quality by structuring training around a burn-in phase and adaptive data selection.
AI.result: The approach provably improves upon standard training recipes: in SFT, requires exponentially fewer reasoning demonstrations than non-adaptive fine-tuning; in RL, reduces the effective cost tied to target accuracy to a burn-in phase nearly independent of the final performance.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive data selection via autocurricula, rooted in boosting and counterexample strategies and free from distribution/difficulty assumptions, offers fundamental efficiency gains for both SFT and RL in reasoning-enabled models.

title: MedForge: Interpretable Medical Deepfake Detection via Forgery-aware Reasoning
summary: Text-guided image editors can now manipulate authentic medical scans with high fidelity, enabling lesion implantation/removal that threatens clinical trust and safety. Existing defenses are inadequate for healthcare. Medical detectors are largely black-box, while MLLM-based explainers are typically post-hoc, lack medical expertise, and may hallucinate evidence on ambiguous cases. We present MedForge, a data-and-method solution for pre-hoc, evidence-grounded medical forgery detection. We introduce MedForge-90K, a large-scale benchmark of realistic lesion edits across 19 pathologies with expert-guided reasoning supervision via doctor inspection guidelines and gold edit locations. Building on it, MedForge-Reasoner performs localize-then-analyze reasoning, predicting suspicious regions before producing a verdict, and is further aligned with Forgery-aware GSPO to strengthen grounding and reduce hallucinations. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art detection accuracy and trustworthy, expert-aligned explanations.

AI.tldr: MedForge offers a preemptive, evidence-grounded framework for detecting medical image forgery, anchored by the MedForge-90K dataset and MedForge-Reasoner with Forgery-aware GSPO, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and expert-aligned explanations.
AI.motivation: Medical scans are vulnerable to lesion implantation/removal via text-guided editors, threatening clinical trust. Existing defenses are inadequate (black-box detectors, post-hoc LLM explainers, risk of hallucinations). A pre-hoc, evidence-grounded detector with expert reasoning is needed.
AI.method: Introduce MedForge-90K, a large-scale benchmark with 19 pathologies and expert-guided supervision; develop MedForge-Reasoner to perform localize-then-analyze reasoning that identifies suspicious regions before verdict; integrate Forgery-aware GSPO to strengthen grounding and reduce hallucinations.
AI.result: Experiments show state-of-the-art detection accuracy and trustworthy, expert-aligned explanations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables pre-hoc, evidence-grounded detection of medical forgery with improved grounding and explanations, enhancing clinical trust and safety.

title: PRIOR: Perceptive Learning for Humanoid Locomotion with Reference Gait Priors
summary: Training perceptive humanoid locomotion policies that traverse complex terrains with natural gaits remains an open challenge, typically demanding multi-stage training pipelines, adversarial objectives, or extensive real-world calibration. We present PRIOR, an efficient and reproducible framework built on Isaac Lab that achieves robust terrain traversal with human-like gaits through a simple yet effective design: (i) a parametric gait generator that supplies stable reference trajectories derived from motion capture without adversarial training, (ii) a GRU-based state estimator that infers terrain geometry directly from egocentric depth images via self-supervised heightmap reconstruction, and (iii) terrain-adaptive footstep rewards that guide foot placement toward traversable regions. Through systematic analysis of depth image resolution trade-offs, we identify configurations that maximize terrain fidelity under real-time constraints, substantially reducing perceptual overhead without degrading traversal performance. Comprehensive experiments across terrains of varying difficulty-including stairs, boxes, and gaps-demonstrate that each component yields complementary and essential performance gains, with the full framework achieving a 100% traversal success rate. We will open-source the complete PRIOR framework, including the training pipeline, parametric gait generator, and evaluation benchmarks, to serve as a reproducible foundation for humanoid locomotion research on Isaac Lab.

AI.tldr: PRIOR presents a reproducible, perception-guided humanoid locomotion framework that uses a mocap-derived parametric gait, a GRU-based depth-to-heightmap estimator, and terrain-aware footstep rewards, achieving 100% traversal on stairs, boxes, and gaps without adversarial training; aims to be open-sourced on Isaac Lab.
AI.motivation: To overcome the challenges of training perceptive humanoid locomotion on complex terrains without multi-stage pipelines, adversarial objectives, or extensive real-world calibration.
AI.method: (i) Parametric gait generator from motion capture for stable reference trajectories; (ii) GRU-based state estimator that infers terrain geometry from egocentric depth via self-supervised heightmap reconstruction; (iii) terrain-adaptive footstep rewards guiding placement toward traversable regions. Depth-resolution trade-offs analyzed for real-time performance.
AI.result: Systematic analysis of depth image resolution trade-offs; full framework yields complementary gains across terrains (stairs, boxes, gaps) with reported 100% traversal success; real-time constraints and feasibility discussed; open-source plan for training pipeline, gait generator, and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PRIOR is a reproducible framework for humanoid locomotion research on Isaac Lab, expected to accelerate study of perceptive locomotion through a complete, open-source pipeline.

title: TexEditor: Structure-Preserving Text-Driven Texture Editing
summary: Text-guided texture editing aims to modify object appearance while preserving the underlying geometric structure. However, our empirical analysis reveals that even SOTA editing models frequently struggle to maintain structural consistency during texture editing, despite the intended changes being purely appearance-related. Motivated by this observation, we jointly enhance structure preservation from both data and training perspectives, and build TexEditor, a dedicated texture editing model based on Qwen-Image-Edit-2509. Firstly, we construct TexBlender, a high-quality SFT dataset generated with Blender, which provides strong structural priors for a cold start. Sec- ondly, we introduce StructureNFT, a RL-based approach that integrates structure-preserving losses to transfer the structural priors learned during SFT to real-world scenes. Moreover, due to the limited realism and evaluation coverage of existing benchmarks, we introduce TexBench, a general-purpose real-world benchmark for text-guided texture editing. Extensive experiments on existing Blender-based texture benchmarks and our TexBench show that TexEditor consistently outperforms strong baselines such as Nano Banana Pro. In addition, we assess TexEditor on the general purpose benchmark ImgEdit to validate its generalization. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/KlingAIResearch/TexEditor.

AI.tldr: TexEditor is a dedicated text-guided texture editing model that improves structure preservation during texture edits by using a synthetic structural prior (TexBlender) and an RL-based training approach (StructureNFT), and by introducing a real-world TexBench benchmark. It demonstrates state-of-the-art performance against baselines like Nano Banana Pro and generalizes to ImgEdit, with code and data released.
AI.motivation: Current text-guided texture editing methods often distort geometric structure when changing appearance. There is a need to jointly address data-driven priors and training-time losses to enforce structure preservation in real scenes.
AI.method: 1) TexEditor based on Qwen-Image-Edit-2509. 2) TexBlender: high-quality SFT dataset generated from Blender to inject strong structural priors. 3) StructureNFT: RL-based approach with structure-preserving losses to transfer learned priors to real-world scenes. 4) TexBench: real-world benchmark for evaluation. 5) Evaluation on Blender-based benchmarks, TexBench, and generalization test on ImgEdit. 6) Public code/data release.
AI.result: TexEditor consistently outperforms strong baselines such as Nano Banana Pro on Blender-based texture benchmarks and TexBench. It also demonstrates generalization to the ImgEdit benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The proposed TexEditor framework, grounded in synthetic structural priors and structure-preserving training, achieves better structure preservation in texture editing and generalizes across real-world scenarios, with public code and data available.

title: Escaping Offline Pessimism: Vector-Field Reward Shaping for Safe Frontier Exploration
summary: While offline reinforcement learning provides reliable policies for real-world deployment, its inherent pessimism severely restricts an agent's ability to explore and collect novel data online. Drawing inspiration from safe reinforcement learning, exploring near the boundary of regions well covered by the offline dataset and reliably modeled by the simulator allows an agent to take manageable risks--venturing into informative but moderate-uncertainty states while remaining close enough to familiar regions for safe recovery. However, naively rewarding this boundary-seeking behavior can lead to a degenerate parking behavior, where the agent simply stops once it reaches the frontier. To solve this, we propose a novel vector-field reward shaping paradigm designed to induce continuous, safe boundary exploration for non-adaptive deployed policies. Operating on an uncertainty oracle trained from offline data, our reward combines two complementary components: a gradient-alignment term that attracts the agent toward a target uncertainty level, and a rotational-flow term that promotes motion along the local tangent plane of the uncertainty manifold. Through theoretical analysis, we show that this reward structure naturally induces sustained exploratory behavior along the boundary while preventing degenerate solutions. Empirically, by integrating our proposed reward shaping with Soft Actor-Critic on a 2D continuous navigation task, we validate that agents successfully traverse uncertainty boundaries while balancing safe, informative data collection with primary task completion.

AI.tldr: Introduces a vector-field reward shaping strategy to enable continuous, safe boundary exploration in offline-to-online RL, using an uncertainty gradient-alignment term and a rotational-flow term to navigate near-data boundaries while avoiding degenerate stopping behavior; validated with SAC on a 2D navigation task both theoretically and empirically.
AI.motivation: Offline RL restricts online exploration due to pessimism, hindering data collection of informative states. Safe, boundary-focused exploration near the offline data manifold offers a path to improve policy while maintaining safety and recoverability.
AI.method: Train an uncertainty oracle from offline data; construct a reward as the sum of a gradient-alignment term toward a target uncertainty level and a rotational-flow term along the local tangent of the uncertainty manifold; integrate this vector-field reward shaping with Soft Actor-Critic to encourage continuous boundary exploration without degenerating into mere frontier parking; provide theoretical analysis of sustained exploration and prevent degenerate solutions.
AI.result: Theoretical results show the reward induces persistent exploration along the uncertainty boundary; empirically, SAC with the proposed shaping enables agents to traverse uncertainty boundaries and balance safe data collection with primary task performance in a 2D navigation task.
AI.conclusion: Vector-field reward shaping offers a principled mechanism for safe, continuous boundary exploration in offline-to-online RL, mitigating degenerate frontier behavior and enhancing data collection near the data-supported regions while preserving task objectives.

title: ZEBRAARENA: A Diagnostic Simulation Environment for Studying Reasoning-Action Coupling in Tool-Augmented LLMs
summary: Tool-augmented large language models (LLMs) must tightly couple multi-step reasoning with external actions, yet existing benchmarks often confound this interplay with complex environment dynamics, memorized knowledge or dataset contamination. In this paper, we introduce ZebraArena, a procedurally generated diagnostic environment for studying reasoning-action coupling in tool-augmented LLMs, with controllable difficulty and a knowledge-minimal design, which limits gains from memorization or dataset contamination. Each task in ZebraArena requires a set of critical information which is available only through targeted tool use, yielding an interpretable interface between external information acquisition and deductive reasoning. This design provides deterministic evaluation via unique solutions, and a theoretical optimal query count for measuring efficient tool use. We show that ZebraArena requires a combination of in-depth reasoning and accurate external tool calling, which remains a challenge as frontier reasoning models such as GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Pro only achieves 60% accuracy on the hard instances. We also observe a persistent gaps between theoretical optimality and practical tool usage. For example, GPT-5 uses 70-270% more tool calls than the theoretical optimum. We highlight the key findings in our evaluation, and hope ZebraArena stimulates further research on the interplay between internal reasoning and external action.

AI.tldr: ZebraArena offers a controllable, knowledge-minimal benchmark to study reasoning–action coupling in tool-augmented LLMs, with deterministic evaluation and a theoretical optimum for tool queries.
AI.motivation: To isolate and study the interaction between internal reasoning and external tool use, minimizing memorization and data contamination as confounds.
AI.method: Procedurally generated tasks where each problem requires information obtainable only via targeted tool use; unique solutions; metrics include accuracy on hard instances and deviation from a theoretical optimal tool-usage count.
AI.result: Frontier models (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro) reach about 60% accuracy on hard tasks; substantial inefficiency in tool use with 70–270% more tool calls than the theoretical optimum; persistent gap between theoretical optimality and practical usage.
AI.conclusion: ZebraArena reveals the challenge of efficient tool use in advanced LLMs and provides a platform for examining the coupling of reasoning with external actions; further work needed to close the gap between optimal planning and actual tool invocation.

title: MERGE: Guided Vision-Language Models for Multi-Actor Event Reasoning and Grounding in Human-Robot Interaction
summary: We introduce MERGE, a system for situational grounding of actors, objects, and events in dynamic human-robot group interactions. Effective collaboration in such settings requires consistent situational awareness, built on persistent representations of people and objects and an episodic abstraction of events. MERGE achieves this by uniquely identifying physical instances of actors (humans or robots) and objects and structuring them into actor-action-object relations, ensuring temporal consistency across interactions. Central to MERGE is the integration of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) guided with a perception pipeline: a lightweight streaming module continuously processes visual input to detect changes and selectively invokes the VLM only when necessary. This decoupled design preserves the reasoning power and zero-shot generalization of VLMs while improving efficiency, avoiding both the high monetary cost and the latency of frame-by-frame captioning that leads to fragmented and delayed outputs. To address the absence of suitable benchmarks for multi-actor collaboration, we introduce the GROUND dataset, which offers fine-grained situational annotations of multi-person and human-robot interactions. On this dataset, our approach improves the average grounding score by a factor of 2 compared to the performance of VLM-only baselines - including GPT-4o, GPT-5 and Gemini 2.5 Flash - while also reducing run-time by a factor of 4. The code and data are available at www.github.com/HRI-EU/merge.

AI.tldr: MERGE offers a persistent actor–object grounding framework with an episodic event abstraction, combining a lightweight perception pipeline with Vision-Language Models, and introduces the GROUND benchmark; it achieves 2x grounding accuracy and 4x speedups over VLM baselines.
AI.motivation: To enable consistent situational awareness in multi-actor human–robot teams, addressing fragmentation from frame-by-frame captioning and lack of suitable benchmarks.
AI.method: Represent physical instances as unique actors/objects, form actor–action–object relations with temporal consistency; integrate a streaming perception module that selectively invokes VLMs to maintain zero-shot capabilities while reducing latency; introduce the GROUND dataset with fine-grained annotations.
AI.result: On GROUND, 2x improvement in average grounding score vs VLM-only baselines (GPT-4o, GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Flash); 4x faster runtime; code and data available.
AI.conclusion: MERGE provides an efficient, scalable grounding framework for dynamic HRI, enabling persistent situational awareness and robust multi-actor grounding, supported by a new benchmark and release-ready code.

title: FILT3R: Latent State Adaptive Kalman Filter for Streaming 3D Reconstruction
summary: Streaming 3D reconstruction maintains a persistent latent state that is updated online from incoming frames, enabling constant-memory inference. A key failure mode is the state update rule: aggressive overwrites forget useful history, while conservative updates fail to track new evidence, and both behaviors become unstable beyond the training horizon. To address this challenge, we propose FILT3R, a training-free latent filtering layer that casts recurrent state updates as stochastic state estimation in token space. FILT3R maintains a per-token variance and computes a Kalman-style gain that adaptively balances memory retention against new observations. Process noise -- governing how much the latent state is expected to change between frames -- is estimated online from EMA-normalized temporal drift of candidate tokens. Using extensive experiments, we demonstrate that FILT3R yields an interpretable, plug-in update rule that generalizes common overwrite and gating policies as special cases. Specifically, we show that gains shrink in stable regimes as uncertainty contracts with accumulated evidence, and rise when genuine scene change increases process uncertainty, improving long-horizon stability for depth, pose, and 3D reconstruction, compared to the existing methods. Code will be released at https://github.com/jinotter3/FILT3R.
