AI.tldr: FILT3R is a training-free latent filtering layer for streaming 3D reconstruction that treats state updates as stochastic estimation in token space, using per-token variance and a Kalman-style gain to balance memory and new observations; online process-noise estimation from token drift yields improved long-horizon stability; acts as a general plug-in that subsumes overwrite and gating policies.
AI.motivation: Persistent latent states in streaming 3D reconstruction are prone to unstable updates when updating rules overwrite history too aggressively or fail to adapt to new evidence; a training-free, interpretable mechanism is needed to robustly update latent states online.
AI.method: Treat latent updates as stochastic state estimation in token space with per-token variance; compute Kalman-style gain to balance retention and new evidence; estimate process noise online from EMA-normalized temporal drift of candidate tokens; provide a plug-in update rule that generalizes existing update policies.
AI.result: FILT3R yields an interpretable update mechanism that generalizes common overwrite and gating policies; gains decrease when the system is stable and evidence accumulates, and increase with genuine scene changes; improves long-horizon stability for depth, pose, and 3D reconstruction in streaming scenarios; code to be released.
AI.conclusion: A training-free, Kalman-inspired latent filtering layer offers robust, interpretable, plug-in updates for streaming 3D reconstruction, capturing regime-dependent update behavior and enhancing long-term stability.

title: A Family of Adaptive Activation Functions for Mitigating Failure Modes in Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Physics-Informed Neural Networks(PINNs) are a powerful and flexible learning framework that has gained significant attention in recent years. It has demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of scientific and engineering problems. In parallel, wavelets have been extensively used as efficient computational tools due to their strong approximation capabilities. Motivated by the common failure modes observed in standard PINNs, this work introduces a novel family of adaptive wavelet-based activation functions. The proposed activation functions significantly improve training stability and expressive power by combining trainable wavelet functions with either trainable or fixed hyperbolic tangent and softplus functions. Five distinct activation functions are developed within the PINN framework and systematically evaluated across four representative classes of partial differential equations (PDEs). Comprehensive comparisons using bar plots demonstrate improved robustness and accuracy compared to traditional activation functions. Furthermore, the proposed approach is validated through direct comparisons with baseline PINNs, transformer-based architectures such as PINNsFormer, and other deep learning models, highlighting its effectiveness and generality.

AI.tldr: Adaptive wavelet-based activation functions in PINNs enhance training stability and expressivity, yielding higher accuracy across multiple PDEs and outperforming standard activations and transformer-based baselines.
AI.motivation: PINNs suffer from stability and expressivity limitations (failure modes). Wavelets offer strong local approximation capabilities; integrating trainable wavelet components with common activations (tanh/softplus) aims to improve robustness and learning performance.
AI.method: Develop five activation functions that combine trainable wavelet components with either trainable or fixed tanh and softplus. Integrate these activations into the PINN framework and evaluate on four representative PDE classes. Compare performance against traditional activations, baseline PINNs, PINNsFormer, and other DL models using bar plots.
AI.result: The proposed activations improve robustness and accuracy across the tested PDEs, outperforming traditional activations and showing competitive or superior results versus baselines like PINNsFormer and other DL models.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive wavelet-based activations provide a general, effective enhancement for PINNs, mitigating training instability and improving expressivity with strong empirical validation across diverse PDE problems.

title: Agentic Flow Steering and Parallel Rollout Search for Spatially Grounded Text-to-Image Generation
summary: Precise Text-to-Image (T2I) generation has achieved great success but is hindered by the limited relational reasoning of static text encoders and the error accumulation in open-loop sampling. Without real-time feedback, initial semantic ambiguities during the Ordinary Differential Equation trajectory inevitably escalate into stochastic deviations from spatial constraints. To bridge this gap, we introduce AFS-Search (Agentic Flow Steering and Parallel Rollout Search), a training-free closed-loop framework built upon FLUX.1-dev. AFS-Search incorporates a training-free closed-loop parallel rollout search and flow steering mechanism, which leverages a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a semantic critic to diagnose intermediate latents and dynamically steer the velocity field via precise spatial grounding. Complementarily, we formulate T2I generation as a sequential decision-making process, exploring multiple trajectories through lookahead simulations and selecting the optimal path based on VLM-guided rewards. Further, we provide AFS-Search-Pro for higher performance and AFS-Search-Fast for quicker generation. Experimental results show that our AFS-Search-Pro greatly boosts the performance of the original FLUX.1-dev, achieving state-of-the-art results across three different benchmarks. Meanwhile, AFS-Search-Fast also significantly enhances performance while maintaining fast generation speed.

AI.tldr: A training-free, closed-loop T2I framework (AFS-Search) that uses a Vision-Language Model as a semantic critic to steer parallel rollouts and velocity fields, enabling sequential decision-making with lookahead, yielding state-of-the-art results (Pro) and faster variants (Fast) across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome two main issues in precise T2I generation: (1) limited relational reasoning of static text encoders, and (2) error accumulation in open-loop sampling during ODE/diffusion trajectories, which cause semantic drift without real-time feedback.
AI.method: A training-free, closed-loop extension built on FLUX.1-dev. It introduces parallel rollout search and flow steering guided by a VLM to diagnose intermediate latents and adjust the velocity field for precise spatial grounding. Treats T2I as sequential decision-making with lookahead over multiple trajectories and VLM-based rewards. Provides AFS-Search-Pro (higher performance) and AFS-Search-Fast (faster generation).
AI.result: AFS-Search-Pro significantly boosts FLUX.1-dev, achieving state-of-the-art on three benchmarks; AFS-Search-Fast also yields notable improvements while preserving fast generation speed.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers a training-free closed-loop mechanism that improves semantic grounding and spatial constraint adherence in T2I, with scalable Pro and Fast variants enabling strong performance gains without additional training.

title: ATG-MoE: Autoregressive trajectory generation with mixture-of-experts for assembly skill learning
summary: Flexible manufacturing requires robot systems that can adapt to constantly changing tasks, objects, and environments. However, traditional robot programming is labor-intensive and inflexible, while existing learning-based assembly methods often suffer from weak positional generalization, complex multi-stage designs, and limited multi-skill integration capability. To address these issues, this paper proposes ATG-MoE, an end-to-end autoregressive trajectory generation method with mixture of experts for assembly skill learning from demonstration. The proposed method establishes a closed-loop mapping from multi-modal inputs, including RGB-D observations, natural language instructions, and robot proprioception to manipulation trajectories. It integrates multi-modal feature fusion for scene and task understanding, autoregressive sequence modeling for temporally coherent trajectory generation, and a mixture-of-experts architecture for unified multi-skill learning. In contrast to conventional methods that separate visual perception and control or train different skills independently, ATG-MoE directly incorporates visual information into trajectory generation and supports efficient multi-skill integration within a single model. We train and evaluate the proposed method on eight representative assembly skills from a pressure-reducing valve assembly task. Experimental results show that ATG-MoE achieves strong overall performance in simulation, with an average grasp success rate of 96.3% and an average overall success rate of 91.8%, while also demonstrating strong generalization and effective multi-skill integration. Real-world experiments further verify its practicality for multi-skill industrial assembly. The project page can be found at https://hwh23.github.io/ATG-MoE

AI.tldr: ATG-MoE presents an end-to-end autoregressive trajectory generator with a mixture-of-experts to learn and fuse multi-modal inputs for single-model, multi-skill robotic assembly, achieving strong simulation and real-world results on eight valve assembly skills; demonstrates improved generalization and integrated skill handling over traditional modular approaches.
AI.motivation: Address inflexibility and labor-intensive programming of traditional robotics and limitations of existing learning-based assembly methods, such as weak positional generalization, multi-stage designs, and poor multi-skill integration.
AI.method: End-to-end autoregressive trajectory generation with mixture of experts (MoE) that fuses RGB-D, language, and proprioception; multi-modal feature fusion for scene understanding; closed-loop mapping to manipulation trajectories; single model handling multiple assembly skills; trained and evaluated on eight pressure-reducing valve assembly skills; simulation and real-world validation.
AI.result: Simulation: average grasp success 96.3%; average overall success 91.8%; strong generalization and multi-skill integration. Real-world experiments confirm practicality for multi-skill industrial assembly; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: ATG-MoE demonstrates effective end-to-end learning for adaptive, multi-skill assembly with integrated perception and control, enabling efficient skill sharing within a single model and favorable generalization; suitable for industrial deployment.

title: Epistemic Generative Adversarial Networks
summary: Generative models, particularly Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), often suffer from a lack of output diversity, frequently generating similar samples rather than a wide range of variations. This paper introduces a novel generalization of the GAN loss function based on Dempster-Shafer theory of evidence, applied to both the generator and discriminator. Additionally, we propose an architectural enhancement to the generator that enables it to predict a mass function for each image pixel. This modification allows the model to quantify uncertainty in its outputs and leverage this uncertainty to produce more diverse and representative generations. Experimental evidence shows that our approach not only improves generation variability but also provides a principled framework for modeling and interpreting uncertainty in generative processes.

AI.tldr: DS-theory–based GAN loss with per-pixel mass-function outputs to quantify/drive diversity; joint generator-discriminator formulation.
AI.motivation: Mitigate mode collapse and lack of diversity in GANs by incorporating evidence-theoretic uncertainty modeling to guide generation and improve interpretability.
AI.method: Extend GAN loss using Dempster–Shafer theory for both generator and discriminator. Add a generator module predicting a mass function per image pixel, enabling pixel-level uncertainty quantification and using this uncertainty to promote diversity in outputs.
AI.result: Empirical evidence indicates improved generation variability and a principled framework for modeling and interpreting uncertainty in generative processes.
AI.conclusion: DS-based loss plus pixelwise mass-function modeling yields more diverse samples and provides a formal approach to uncertainty in GAN-based generation.

title: D-Mem: A Dual-Process Memory System for LLM Agents
summary: Driven by the development of persistent, self-adapting autonomous agents, equipping these systems with high-fidelity memory access for long-horizon reasoning has emerged as a critical requirement. However, prevalent retrieval-based memory frameworks often follow an incremental processing paradigm that continuously extracts and updates conversational memories into vector databases, relying on semantic retrieval when queried. While this approach is fast, it inherently relies on lossy abstraction, frequently missing contextually critical information and struggling to resolve queries that rely on fine-grained contextual understanding. To address this, we introduce D-Mem, a dual-process memory system. It retains lightweight vector retrieval for routine queries while establishing an exhaustive Full Deliberation module as a high-fidelity fallback. To achieve cognitive economy without sacrificing accuracy, D-Mem employs a Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy to dynamically bridge these two processes. Experiments on the LoCoMo and RealTalk benchmarks using GPT-4o-mini and Qwen3-235B-Instruct demonstrate the efficacy of our approach. Notably, our Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy achieves an F1 score of 53.5 on LoCoMo with GPT-4o-mini. This outperforms our static retrieval baseline, Mem0$^\ast$ (51.2), and recovers 96.7\% of the Full Deliberation's performance (55.3), while incurring significantly lower computational costs.

AI.tldr: D-Mem introduces a dual-process memory system for long-horizon reasoning: a lightweight vector retrieval path for routine queries and a high-fidelity Full Deliberation module as a fallback, governed by a Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy to balance accuracy and efficiency. Evaluated on LoCoMo and RealTalk with GPT-4o-mini and Qwen3-235B-Instruct, achieving 53.5 F1 on LoCoMo (GPT-4o-mini), surpassing Mem0* (51.2) and recovering 96.7% of Full Deliberation performance (55.3) at lower cost.
AI.motivation: Current retrieval-based memory systems in autonomous agents rely on incremental processing with vector-based, lossy abstractions that may miss fine-grained context and struggle with queries requiring detailed historical information. There is a need for high-fidelity, long-horizon memory access that preserves crucial context.
AI.method: Proposes D-Mem, a dual-process architecture with a lightweight vector retrieval path and a Full Deliberation module as a high-fidelity fallback. A Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy dynamically selects between the two processes to maintain cognitive efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on LoCoMo and RealTalk with GPT-4o-mini and Qwen3-235B-Instruct shows the gating policy achieves an F1 of 53.5 on LoCoMo (GPT-4o-mini), outperforming the static retrieval baseline Mem0* (51.2) and recovering 96.7% of the Full Deliberation’s performance (55.3) while reducing computational costs.
AI.conclusion: The Multi-dimensional Quality Gating policy enables efficient yet accurate long-horizon reasoning by adaptively bridging lightweight retrieval and exhaustive deliberation. D-Mem demonstrates strong potential to improve memory-based reasoning in persistent autonomous agents, offering a practical balance between speed and fidelity.

title: Fire as a Service: Augmenting Robot Simulators with Thermally and Visually Accurate Fire Dynamics
summary: Most existing robot simulators prioritize rigid-body dynamics and photorealistic rendering, but largely neglect the thermally and optically complex phenomena that characterize real-world fire environments. For robots envisioned as future firefighters, this limitation hinders both reliable capability evaluation and the generation of representative training data prior to deployment in hazardous scenarios. To address these challenges, we introduce Fire as a Service (FaaS), a novel, asynchronous co-simulation framework that augments existing robot simulators with high-fidelity and computationally efficient fire simulations. Our pipeline enables robots to experience accurate, multi-species thermodynamic heat transfer and visually consistent volumetric smoke without disrupting high-frequency rigid-body control loops. We demonstrate that our framework can be integrated with diverse robot simulators to generate physically accurate fire behavior, benchmark thermal hazards encountered by robotic platforms, and collect realistic multimodal perceptual data. Crucially, its real-time performance supports human-in-the-loop teleoperation, enabling the successful training of reactive, multimodal policies via Behavioral Cloning. By adding fire dynamics to robot simulations, FaaS provides a scalable pathway toward safer, more reliable deployment of robots in fire scenarios.

AI.tldr: Fire as a Service (FaaS) adds asynchronous, high-fidelity fire simulation to robot simulators to enable realistic thermodynamic and optical phenomena in real time for training and evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current robot simulators prioritize rigid-body dynamics and photorealism but neglect complex fire physics, limiting evaluation of robotic firefighters and data collection for training.
AI.method: An asynchronous co-simulation framework that couples existing robot simulators with high-fidelity fire models, capturing multi-species heat transfer and volumetric smoke while preserving high-frequency control loops; supports integration with diverse simulators.
AI.result: Demonstrates integration, generation of physically accurate fire behavior, benchmarking thermal hazards, and collection of realistic multimodal data; enables real-time teleoperation and training of reactive policies via Behavioral Cloning.
AI.conclusion: FaaS provides a scalable path toward safer, more reliable deployment of robots in fire scenarios by enriching simulation environments with fire dynamics.

title: Efficient Video Diffusion with Sparse Information Transmission for Video Compression
summary: Video compression aims to maximize reconstruction quality with minimal bitrates. Beyond standard distortion metrics, perceptual quality and temporal consistency are also critical. However, at ultra-low bitrates, traditional end-to-end compression models tend to produce blurry images of poor perceptual quality. Besides, existing generative compression methods often treat video frames independently and show limitations in time coherence and efficiency. To address these challenges, we propose the Efficient Video Diffusion with Sparse Information Transmission (Diff-SIT), which comprises the Sparse Temporal Encoding Module (STEM) and the One-Step Video Diffusion with Frame Type Embedder (ODFTE). The STEM sparsely encodes the original frame sequence into an information-rich intermediate sequence, achieving significant bitrate savings. Subsequently, the ODFTE processes this intermediate sequence as a whole, which exploits the temporal correlation. During this process, our proposed Frame Type Embedder (FTE) guides the diffusion model to perform adaptive reconstruction according to different frame types to optimize the overall quality. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that Diff-SIT establishes a new state-of-the-art in perceptual quality and temporal consistency, particularly in the challenging ultra-low-bitrate regime. Code is released at https://github.com/MingdeZhou/Diff-SIT.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based ultra-low bitrate video compression method (Diff-SIT) combining Sparse Temporal Encoding Module (STEM) and One-Step Video Diffusion with Frame Type Embedder (ODFTE) to achieve high perceptual quality and temporal consistency.
AI.motivation: Address blur and lack of temporal coherence at ultra-low bitrates; current end-to-end and per-frame generative methods struggle with time consistency and efficiency.
AI.method: STEM sparsely encodes the original frame sequence into an information-rich intermediate sequence. ODFTE processes this sequence as a whole, exploiting temporal correlations. Frame Type Embedder guides the diffusion model to adapt reconstruction to different frame types.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance in perceptual quality and temporal consistency at ultra-low bitrates across multiple datasets. Code released.
AI.conclusion: Diff-SIT effectively leverages sparse transmission and joint diffusion-based reconstruction to improve perceptual quality and temporal coherence under tight bitrate constraints, with demonstrated empirical gains and reproducible implementation.

title: Mathematical Foundations of Deep Learning
comment: Draft version. Final version is published in "Chapman & Hall/CRC Mathematics and Artificial Intelligence Series" by Taylor & Francis in 2026
summary: This draft book offers a comprehensive and rigorous treatment of the mathematical principles underlying modern deep learning. The book spans core theoretical topics, from the approximation capabilities of deep neural networks, the theory and algorithms of optimal control and reinforcement learning integrated with deep learning techniques, to contemporary generative models that drive today's advances in artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: A rigorous, unified mathematical treatment of modern deep learning foundations, covering neural network approximation, optimal control and reinforcement learning integration, and contemporary generative models.
AI.motivation: To provide solid theoretical grounding for deep learning by detailing core mathematical principles that underpin current algorithms and models.
AI.method: Draft book offering comprehensive, rigorous exposition across topics; synthesizes theory and algorithms in neural network approximation, control and reinforcement learning integrated with DL techniques, and generative modeling.
AI.result: Proposes a cohesive theoretical framework and reference work; no empirical results are reported in the abstract; aims to guide and unify future research.
AI.conclusion: If realized, it would advance the theoretical understanding of deep learning and inform the development of methods in approximation, control/RL integration, and generative modeling.

title: An Onto-Relational-Sophic Framework for Governing Synthetic Minds
summary: The rapid evolution of artificial intelligence, from task-specific systems to foundation models exhibiting broad, flexible competence across reasoning, creative synthesis, and social interaction, has outpaced the conceptual and governance frameworks designed to manage it. Current regulatory paradigms, anchored in a tool-centric worldview, address algorithmic bias and transparency but leave unanswered foundational questions about what increasingly capable synthetic minds are, how societies should relate to them, and the normative principles that should guide their development. Here we introduce the Onto-Relational-Sophic (ORS) framework, grounded in Cyberism philosophy, which offers integrated answers to these challenges through three pillars: (1) a Cyber-Physical-Social-Thinking (CPST) ontology that defines the mode of being for synthetic minds as irreducibly multi-dimensional rather than purely computational; (2) a graded spectrum of digital personhood providing a pragmatic relational taxonomy beyond binary person-or-tool classifications; and (3) Cybersophy, a wisdom-oriented axiology synthesizing virtue ethics, consequentialism, and relational approaches to guide governance. We apply the framework to emergent scenarios including autonomous research agents, AI-mediated healthcare, and agentic AI ecosystems, demonstrating its capacity to generate proportionate, adaptive governance recommendations. The ORS framework charts a path from narrow technical alignment toward comprehensive philosophical foundations for the synthetic minds already among us.

AI.tldr: Proposes ORS framework with CPST ontology, graded digital personhood, and Cybersophy to guide governance of synthetic minds; demonstrates applicability to autonomous agents, AI healthcare, and AI ecosystems.
AI.motivation: Addresses regulatory gaps and questions about the ontology, social relation, and normative governance of increasingly capable AI systems; current tool-centric frameworks insufficient.
AI.method: Develops a three-pillar framework grounded in Cyberism; defines CPST ontology as irreducibly multi-dimensional; introduces a graded spectrum of digital personhood; formulates Cybersophy as an integrated axiology; applies framework to emergent scenarios to derive governance guidance.
AI.result: Provides integrated theoretical foundations and practical governance recommendations; shows how ORS can move beyond narrow alignment toward philosophical foundations.
AI.conclusion: ORS offers a path to comprehensive, philosophy-grounded governance for synthetic minds and could influence policy and design of AI systems.

title: CAMO: A Conditional Neural Solver for the Multi-objective Multiple Traveling Salesman Problem
summary: Robotic systems often require a team of robots to collectively visit multiple targets while optimizing competing objectives, such as total travel cost and makespan. This setting can be formulated as the Multi-Objective Multiple Traveling Salesman Problem (MOMTSP). Although learning-based methods have shown strong performance on the single-agent TSP and multi-objective TSP variants, they rarely address the combined challenges of multi-agent coordination and multi-objective trade-offs, which introduce dual sources of complexity. To bridge this gap, we propose CAMO, a conditional neural solver for MOMTSP that generalizes across varying numbers of targets, agents, and preference vectors, and yields high-quality approximations to the Pareto front (PF). Specifically, CAMO consists of a conditional encoder to fuse preferences into instance representations, enabling explicit control over multi-objective trade-offs, and a collaborative decoder that coordinates all agents by alternating agent selection and node selection to construct multi-agent tours autoregressively. To further improve generalization, we train CAMO with a REINFORCE-based objective over a mixed distribution of problem sizes. Extensive experiments show that CAMO outperforms both neural and conventional heuristics, achieving a closer approximation of PFs. In addition, ablation results validate the contributions of CAMO's key components, and real-world tests on a mobile robot platform demonstrate its practical applicability.

AI.tldr: CAMO is a conditional neural solver for MOMTSP that generalizes across targets, agents, and preference vectors, producing high-quality Pareto-front approximations via a conditional encoder and collaborative autoregressive decoder trained with REINFORCE.
AI.motivation: Address the dual complexity of coordinating multiple agents and balancing multiple conflicting objectives in MOMTSP, which remains challenging for both learning-based and traditional methods.
AI.method: CAMO uses a conditional encoder to embed user preferences into instance representations and a collaborative, autoregressive decoder that alternates between agent selection and node selection to build multi-agent tours. It is trained with a REINFORCE objective over a mixed distribution of problem sizes to improve generalization.
AI.result: Outperforms neural and conventional heuristics, achieving closer approximation to the Pareto front. Ablation studies validate the key components. Real-world robotic experiments confirm practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: CAMO generalizes to varying numbers of targets, agents, and preference vectors, providing high-quality PF approximations and demonstrating practicality for real-world multi-robot systems.

title: HOMEY: Heuristic Object Masking with Enhanced YOLO for Property Insurance Risk Detection
summary: Automated property risk detection is a high-impact yet underexplored frontier in computer vision with direct implications for real estate, underwriting, and insurance operations. We introduce HOMEY (Heuristic Object Masking with Enhanced YOLO), a novel detection framework that combines YOLO with a domain-specific masking mechanism and a custom-designed loss function. HOMEY is trained to detect 17 risk-related property classes, including structural damages (e.g., cracked foundations, roof issues), maintenance neglect (e.g., dead yards, overgrown bushes), and liability hazards (e.g., falling gutters, garbage, hazard signs). Our approach introduces heuristic object masking to amplify weak signals in cluttered backgrounds and risk-aware loss calibration to balance class skew and severity weighting. Experiments on real-world property imagery demonstrate that HOMEY achieves superior detection accuracy and reliability compared to baseline YOLO models, while retaining fast inference. Beyond detection, HOMEY enables interpretable and cost-efficient risk analysis, laying the foundation for scalable AI-driven property insurance workflows.

AI.tldr: A YOLO-based framework named HOMEY that uses heuristic masking and a risk-aware loss to detect 17 risk-related property classes, yielding improved accuracy and fast inference for automated property risk analysis and scalable insurance workflows.
AI.motivation: Automated property risk detection is high-impact yet underexplored. Real estate, underwriting, and insurance workflows require reliable detection of structural damages, maintenance neglect, and hazards amid cluttered property imagery. Existing detectors struggle with weak signals, class imbalance, and domain-specific semantics.
AI.method: HOMEY augments YOLO with a domain-specific heuristic object masking module to amplify signals in cluttered scenes, and a risk-aware loss calibration to balance class skew and severity weighting across 17 risk-related classes (structural damages, maintenance neglect, liability hazards). Trained on real-world property imagery, the model supports fast inference and interpretable risk analysis.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate superior detection accuracy and reliability compared to baseline YOLO models, while maintaining fast inference. The approach yields interpretable, cost-efficient risk analysis and supports scalable AI-driven property insurance workflows in real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HOMEY framework advances automated property risk detection by combining masking-based signal amplification with a risk-aware objective, enabling accurate, scalable risk assessment for real estate, underwriting, and insurance operations.

title: RE-SAC: Disentangling aleatoric and epistemic risks in bus fleet control: A stable and robust ensemble DRL approach
summary: Bus holding control is challenging due to stochastic traffic and passenger demand. While deep reinforcement learning (DRL) shows promise, standard actor-critic algorithms suffer from Q-value instability in volatile environments. A key source of this instability is the conflation of two distinct uncertainties: aleatoric uncertainty (irreducible noise) and epistemic uncertainty (data insufficiency). Treating these as a single risk leads to value underestimation in noisy states, causing catastrophic policy collapse. We propose a robust ensemble soft actor-critic (RE-SAC) framework to explicitly disentangle these uncertainties. RE-SAC applies Integral Probability Metric (IPM)-based weight regularization to the critic network to hedge against aleatoric risk, providing a smooth analytical lower bound for the robust Bellman operator without expensive inner-loop perturbations. To address epistemic risk, a diversified Q-ensemble penalizes overconfident value estimates in sparsely covered regions. This dual mechanism prevents the ensemble variance from misidentifying noise as a data gap, a failure mode identified in our ablation study. Experiments in a realistic bidirectional bus corridor simulation demonstrate that RE-SAC achieves the highest cumulative reward (approx. -0.4e6) compared to vanilla SAC (-0.55e6). Mahalanobis rareness analysis confirms that RE-SAC reduces Oracle Q-value estimation error by up to 62% in rare out-of-distribution states (MAE of 1647 vs. 4343), demonstrating superior robustness under high traffic variability.

AI.tldr: Proposes RE-SAC to disentangle aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties in DRL for bus holding control, using IPM-based regularization and a diversified Q-ensemble; shows improved performance and robustness in a bus corridor simulation with reduced Oracle Q-value error.
AI.motivation: Q-value instability in actor-critic DRL under stochastic traffic and demand arises from conflating irreducible noise (aleatoric) with data-scarcity (epistemic); this leads to value underestimation and potential policy collapse.
AI.method: Apply Integral Probability Metric (IPM)-based weight regularization to the critic to hedge against aleatoric risk and derive a smooth lower bound for the robust Bellman operator without inner-loop perturbations; employ a diversified Q-ensemble to mitigate overconfident estimates in sparsely covered regions, together addressing both uncertainties.
AI.result: In a realistic bidirectional bus corridor simulation, RE-SAC achieves higher cumulative reward (~ -0.4e6) compared to vanilla SAC (~ -0.55e6). Mahalanobis rareness analysis shows up to a 62% reduction in Oracle Q-value estimation error in rare OOD states (MAE: 1647 vs 4343).
AI.conclusion: Explicitly disentangling aleatoric and epistemic risks via IPM-regularized critics and diverse Q-ensembles yields improved robustness and performance in stochastic bus-holding control, validated by ablation and rare-state analyses.

title: Balanced Thinking: Improving Chain of Thought Training in Vision Language Models
summary: Multimodal reasoning in vision-language models (VLMs) typically relies on a two-stage process: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL). In standard SFT, all tokens contribute equally to the loss, even though reasoning data are inherently token-imbalanced. Long <think> traces overshadow short but task-critical <answer> segments, leading to verbose reasoning and inaccurate answers. We propose SCALe (Scheduled Curriculum Adaptive Loss), which explicitly separates supervision over reasoning and answer segments using dynamic, length-independent weighting. Unlike vanilla SFT, which overweights the <think> segment, SCALe-SFT gradually shifts the focus from <think> to <answer> throughout training via a cosine scheduling policy, encouraging concise and well-grounded reasoning. We evaluate SCALe across diverse benchmarks and architectures. Results show that SCALe consistently improves accuracy over vanilla SFT and matches the performance of the full two-phase SFT + GRPO pipeline while requiring only about one-seventh of the training time, making it a lightweight yet effective alternative. When combined with GRPO, SCALe achieves the best overall performance, highlighting its value both as a standalone method and as a strong foundation for reinforcement refinement.

AI.tldr: SCALe introduces a dynamic, length-independent loss to separate supervision of reasoning and answering in vision-language model fine-tuning, shifting focus from think to answer with a cosine schedule; improves accuracy, reduces training time, and works well standalone or with GRPO.
AI.motivation: Standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) suffers from token-imbalance in reasoning data, where long 'think' segments dominate loss and lead to verbose reasoning and errors in concise answers. There is a need for a lightweight method that yields concise, well-grounded reasoning without expensive RL pipelines.
AI.method: SCALe (Scheduled Curriculum Adaptive Loss) applies a dynamic, length-independent weighting that explicitly separates supervision over reasoning (<think>) and answer (<answer>) segments. It uses a cosine scheduling policy to gradually shift emphasis from think to answer during training (SCALe-SFT). This approach can be used as a drop-in SFT alternative and can be combined with GRPO for reinforcement refinement.
AI.result: SCALe consistently improves accuracy over vanilla SFT and matches the performance of the full two-phase SFT + GRPO pipeline while requiring about one-seventh the training time. When combined with GRPO, SCALe achieves the best overall performance, indicating strong potential as both a standalone method and a foundation for reinforcement refinements.
AI.conclusion: SCALe provides a lightweight, effective alternative to heavy SFT+RL pipelines by rebalancing supervision between reasoning and answering; it delivers competitive performance and reduces training cost, and it serves as a robust platform for subsequent reinforcement-based refinements.

title: Articulated-Body Dynamics Network: Dynamics-Grounded Prior for Robot Learning
summary: Recent work in reinforcement learning has shown that incorporating structural priors for articulated robots, such as link connectivity, into policy networks improves learning efficiency. However, dynamics properties, despite their fundamental role in determining how forces and motion propagate through the body, remain largely underexplored as an inductive bias for policy learning. To address this gap, we present the Articulated-Body Dynamics Network (ABD-Net), a novel graph neural network architecture grounded in the computational structure of forward dynamics. Specifically, we adapt the inertia propagation mechanism from the Articulated Body Algorithm, systematically aggregating inertial quantities from child to parent links in a tree-structured manner, while replacing physical quantities with learnable parameters. Embedding ABD-NET into the policy actor enables dynamics-informed representations that capture how actions propagate through the body, leading to efficient and robust policy learning. Through experiments with simulated humanoid, quadruped, and hopper robots, our approach demonstrates increased sample efficiency and generalization to dynamics shifts compared to transformer-based and GNN baselines. We further validate the learned policy on real Unitree G1 and Go2 robots, state-of-the-art humanoid and quadruped platforms, generating dynamic, versatile and robust locomotion behaviors through sim-to-real transfer with real-time inference.

AI.tldr: A dynamics-informed GNN for articulated robots (ABD-Net) that uses an Articulated-Body-Algorithm–inspired inertia-propagation scheme with learnable parameters to propagate actions through the body, improving sample efficiency and sim-to-real transfer for locomotion in sim-to-real robotic platforms.
AI.motivation: Dynamics properties play a fundamental role in propagating forces and motion through an articulated body but have been underexplored as an inductive bias in policy learning; incorporating forward-dynamics priors can improve data efficiency and robustness.
AI.method: Introduce ABD-Net, a graph neural network aligned with the forward dynamics computation. It aggregates inertial quantities from child to parent links in a tree structure per the Articulated Body Algorithm, but replaces physical quantities with learnable parameters. Embeds this dynamics-informed representation into the policy actor to capture how actions propagate through the body.
AI.result: In simulated humanoid, quadruped, and hopper robots, ABD-Net improves sample efficiency and generalization to dynamics shifts relative to transformer-based and standard GNN baselines. Real-world validation on Unitree G1 and Go2 demonstrates sim-to-real transfer with real-time inference, achieving dynamic, versatile locomotion.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating dynamics priors via ABD-Net yields more efficient and robust policy learning for articulated robots and enhances sim-to-real transfer, highlighting the value of forward-dynamics-inspired inductive biases in policy networks.

title: From Snapshots to Symphonies: The Evolution of Protein Prediction from Static Structures to Generative Dynamics and Multimodal Interactions
summary: The protein folding problem has been fundamentally transformed by artificial intelligence, evolving from static structure prediction toward the modeling of dynamic conformational ensembles and complex biomolecular interactions. This review systematically examines the paradigm shift in AI driven protein science across five interconnected dimensions: unified multimodal representations that integrate sequences, geometries, and textual knowledge; refinement of static prediction through MSA free architectures and all atom complex modeling; generative frameworks, including diffusion models and flow matching, that capture conformational distributions consistent with thermodynamic ensembles; prediction of heterogeneous interactions spanning protein ligand, protein nucleic acid, and protein protein complexes; and functional inference of fitness landscapes, mutational effects, and text guided property prediction. We critically analyze current bottlenecks, including data distribution biases, limited mechanistic interpretability, and the disconnect between geometric metrics and biophysical reality, while identifying future directions toward physically consistent generative models, multimodal foundation architectures, and experimental closed loop systems. This methodological transformation marks artificial intelligence's transition from a structural analysis tool into a universal simulator capable of understanding and ultimately rewriting the dynamic language of life.

AI.tldr: AI-driven protein science evolves from static structure prediction to dynamic, multimodal generative modeling of conformational ensembles and interactions, outlined across five dimensions with identified bottlenecks and future directions.
AI.motivation: to synthesize and critique the current paradigm shift in AI-enabled protein science and to chart a coherent framework across representations, modeling strategies, interactions, and functional inference.
AI.method: systematic review and critical analysis of recent AI advances in protein science across five dimensions: unified multimodal representations, MSA-free static modeling, generative conformational modeling, heterogeneous interactions, and functional inference; evaluation of bottlenecks and forward-looking directions.
AI.result: identification of five interrelated dimensions driving the paradigm shift; documentation of bottlenecks (data biases, limited interpretability, misalignment between geometric metrics and biophysics); articulation of future directions toward physically consistent generative models, multimodal foundations, and closed-loop experimental integration.
AI.conclusion: AI is transitioning from a structural analysis tool to a universal simulator of dynamic biomolecular behavior.

title: FlowMS: Flow Matching for De Novo Structure Elucidation from Mass Spectra
summary: Mass spectrometry (MS) stands as a cornerstone analytical technique for molecular identification, yet de novo structure elucidation from spectra remains challenging due to the combinatorial complexity of chemical space and the inherent ambiguity of spectral fragmentation patterns. Recent deep learning approaches, including autoregressive sequence models, scaffold-based methods, and graph diffusion models, have made progress. However, diffusion-based generation for this task remains computationally demanding. Meanwhile, discrete flow matching, which has shown strong performance for graph generation, has not yet been explored for spectrum-conditioned structure elucidation. In this work, we introduce FlowMS, the first discrete flow matching framework for spectrum-conditioned de novo molecular generation. FlowMS generates molecular graphs through iterative refinement in probability space, enforcing chemical formula constraints while conditioning on spectral embeddings from a pretrained formula transformer encoder. Notably, it achieves state-of-the-art performance on 5 out of 6 metrics on the NPLIB1 benchmark: 9.15% top-1 accuracy (9.7% relative improvement over DiffMS) and 7.96 top-10 MCES (4.2% improvement over MS-BART). We also visualize the generated molecules, which further demonstrate that FlowMS produces structurally plausible candidates closely resembling ground truth structures. These results establish discrete flow matching as a promising paradigm for mass spectrometry-based structure elucidation in metabolomics and natural product discovery.

AI.tldr: Discrete flow matching (FlowMS) for spectrum-conditioned de novo molecular generation; achieves state-of-the-art on the NPLIB1 benchmark; uses spectral embeddings from a formula transformer encoder and a diffusion-free discrete flow process; outperforms previous methods.
AI.motivation: De novo structure elucidation from mass spectra is challenging due to the vast chemical space and spectral fragmentation ambiguity; a more efficient, accurate generative approach is needed.
AI.method: FlowMS adopts discrete flow matching to iteratively refine molecular graphs in probability space, enforces chemical formula constraints, and conditions generation on spectral embeddings from a pretrained formula transformer encoder.
AI.result: FlowMS achieves state-of-the-art on 5 of 6 metrics on NPLIB1, with top-1 accuracy 9.15% (9.7% relative improvement over DiffMS) and top-10 MCES 7.96 (4.2% improvement over MS-BART); qualitative visuals show structurally plausible candidates.
AI.conclusion: Discrete flow matching is a promising paradigm for MS-based structure elucidation, with FlowMS establishing a strong foundation for metabolomics and natural product discovery.

title: Thinking with Constructions: A Benchmark and Policy Optimization for Visual-Text Interleaved Geometric Reasoning
summary: Geometric reasoning inherently requires "thinking with constructions" -- the dynamic manipulation of visual aids to bridge the gap between problem conditions and solutions. However, existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are largely confined to passive inference with static diagrams, lacking the strategic knowledge of when and how to construct effective visual aids. To address this, we present a framework for Visual-Text Interleaved Chain-of-Thought. We first introduce GeoAux-Bench, the first benchmark comprising 4,334 geometry problems that aligns textual construction steps with ground-truth visual updates. Our pilot study reveals two critical insights: (1) interleaved visual-textual aids outperform single-modality counterparts, which cannot losslessly capture geometric synergy; and (2) valid constructions act as entropy reducers, strongly correlating with reduced reasoning perplexity. Building on these findings, we propose Action Applicability Policy Optimization (A2PO), a reinforcement learning paradigm for mastering strategic construction. A2PO employs Adaptive Reward Shaping to regulate the timing and quality of visual aids via counterfactual sampling to distinguish necessary from redundant constructions. Experiments demonstrate our approach enables MLLMs to leverage selective auxiliary constructions, yielding a 3.51% gain over strong baselines. Code and data are available on GitHub.

AI.tldr: Proposes a visual-text interleaved chain-of-thought framework for geometric reasoning in multimodal LLMs, introducing GeoAux-Bench and A2PO; shows interleaved aids outperform single modalities, with constructions reducing entropy and correlating with lower reasoning perplexity; RL-based optimization yields a 3.51% gain; code/data on GitHub.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of MLLMs relying on static diagrams by enabling strategic, interleaved construction of visual aids that bridge problem conditions and solutions in geometry.
AI.method: Introduce GeoAux-Bench, the first geometry benchmark with 4,334 problems aligned with ground-truth visual updates and textual construction steps; develop A2PO, an RL framework using Adaptive Reward Shaping and counterfactual sampling to regulate timing and quality of visual constructions.
AI.result: Interleaved visual-text reasoning outperforms single-modality baselines; valid constructions reduce entropy and correlate with lower reasoning perplexity; A2PO yields a 3.51% improvement over strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that selective auxiliary constructions enhance MLLM geometry reasoning, by providing a benchmark and a learning framework to master when and how to construct visual aids, with practical gains and publicly available code/data.

title: Tendon-Actuated Robots with a Tapered, Flexible Polymer Backbone: Design, Fabrication, and Modeling
summary: This paper presents the design, modeling, and fabrication of 3D-printed, tendon-actuated continuum robots featuring a flexible, tapered backbone constructed from thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU). Our scalable design incorporates an integrated electronics base housing that enables direct tendon tension control and sensing via actuators and compression load cells. Unlike many continuum robots that are single-purpose and costly, the proposed design prioritizes customizability, rapid assembly, and low cost while enabling high curvature and enhanced distal compliance through geometric tapering, thereby supporting a broad range of compliant robotic inspection and manipulation tasks. We develop a generalized forward kinetostatic model of the tapered backbone based on Cosserat rod theory using a Newtonian approach, extending existing tendon-actuated Cosserat rod formulations to explicitly account for spatially varying backbone cross-sectional geometry. The model captures the graded stiffness profile induced by the tapering and enables systematic exploration of the configuration space as a function of the geometric design parameters. Specifically, we analyze how the backbone taper angle influences the robot's configuration space and manipulability. The model is validated against motion capture data, achieving centimeter-level shape prediction accuracy after calibrating Young's modulus via a line search that minimizes modeling error. We further demonstrate teleoperated grasping using an endoscopic gripper routed along the continuum robot, mounted on a 6-DoF robotic arm. Parameterized iLogic/CAD scripts are provided for rapid geometry generation and scaling. The presented framework establishes a simple, rapid, and reproducible pathway from parametric design to controlled tendon actuation for tapered, tendon-driven continuum robots manufactured using fused deposition modeling 3D printers.

AI.tldr: A scalable, low-cost 3D-printed tendon-driven continuum robot with a tapered TPU backbone, featuring integrated electronics for direct tendon tension control and sensing; modeled via Cosserat rod theory to account for cross-sectional tapering, validated with motion capture, and demonstrated in teleoperated endoscopic grasping.
AI.motivation: To enable customizable, rapid-assembly, and low-cost continuum robots that can achieve high curvature and distal compliance for compliant inspection and manipulation tasks, addressing the limitations of single-purpose, expensive designs.
AI.method: Design and fabricate a 3D-printed, tendon-actuated continuum robot with a tapered flexible backbone; develop a generalized forward kinetostatic model based on Cosserat rod theory incorporating spatially varying cross-section; calibrate material properties via line search; validate against motion capture data; demonstrate teleoperation with an endoscopic gripper mounted on a 6-DoF arm; provide parametric iLogic/CAD scripts for scalable geometry generation.
AI.result: The model captures the graded stiffness due to tapering and maps back to the configuration space and manipulability as a function of design parameters; achieves centimeter-level shape prediction accuracy after Young’s modulus calibration; demonstrates successful teleoperated grasping; provides a reproducible workflow from parametric design to tendon actuation for tapered continuum robots using FDM 3D printing.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a simple, rapid, and reproducible pathway to design and control tapered, tendon-driven continuum robots manufactured via fused deposition modeling, enabling broad application in compliant inspection and manipulation tasks through customizable geometry and integrated sensing/control.

title: Foundations and Architectures of Artificial Intelligence for Motor Insurance
summary: This handbook presents a systematic treatment of the foundations and architectures of artificial intelligence for motor insurance, grounded in large-scale real-world deployment. It formalizes a vertically integrated AI paradigm that unifies perception, multimodal reasoning, and production infrastructure into a cohesive intelligence stack for automotive risk assessment and claims processing. At its core, the handbook develops domain-adapted transformer architectures for structured visual understanding, relational vehicle representation learning, and multimodal document intelligence, enabling end-to-end automation of vehicle damage analysis, claims evaluation, and underwriting workflows. These components are composed into a scalable pipeline operating under practical constraints observed in nationwide motor insurance systems in Thailand. Beyond model design, the handbook emphasizes the co-evolution of learning algorithms and MLOps practices, establishing a principled framework for translating modern artificial intelligence into reliable, production-grade systems in high-stakes industrial environments.

AI.tldr: A handbook proposing a vertically integrated AI stack for motor insurance, with domain-adapted transformers for perception, reasoning, and multimodal data; enables end-to-end automation of damage analysis, claims, and underwriting within a scalable pipeline, demonstrated under nationwide Thai deployment; emphasizes co-evolution of ML and MLOps for production-grade, high-stakes systems.
AI.motivation: To deliver reliable, scalable AI in high-stakes automotive insurance by unifying perception, reasoning, and production infrastructure; address real-world deployment constraints in nationwide systems (Thailand).
AI.method: Formalizes an end-to-end architecture (a vertically integrated AI paradigm) and develops domain-adapted transformer components for structured visual understanding, relational vehicle representation learning, and multimodal document intelligence; integrates these into a scalable claims/underwriting pipeline and aligns ML with MLOps practices for production deployment.
AI.result: Provides a cohesive architecture and component suite enabling end-to-end automation of vehicle damage analysis, claims evaluation, and underwriting within a scalable, nationwide workflow; demonstrates deployment considerations and the co-evolution of ML and MLOps in high-stakes environments.
AI.conclusion: The framework supports reliable, production-grade AI in motor insurance by combining advanced perception/reasoning with robust MLOps, suitable for nationwide deployment (Thailand) and similar high-stakes industrial contexts.

title: Self-Tuning Sparse Attention: Multi-Fidelity Hyperparameter Optimization for Transformer Acceleration
comment: Accepted to the International Conference on Machine Intelligence Theory and Applications (MiTA 2026)
summary: Sparse attention mechanisms promise to break the quadratic bottleneck of long-context transformers, yet production adoption remains limited by a critical usability gap: optimal hyperparameters vary substantially across layers and models, and current methods (e.g., SpargeAttn) rely on manual grid search to identify them. We propose AFBS-BO (Adaptive Fidelity Binary Search with Bayesian Optimization), a fully automated framework that discovers optimal layer- and head-specific hyperparameters without human intervention. Our hybrid algorithm combines Bayesian Optimization for global exploration with binary search for local refinement, leveraging multi-fidelity evaluation across sequence lengths to reduce tuning cost. On Llama-2-7B, AFBS-BO accelerates hyperparameter discovery by 3.4x with 8.8x fewer evaluations than grid search, and identifies high-sparsity configurations that outperform existing sparse attention baselines while closely matching dense attention quality. By transforming sparse attention from a manually tuned heuristic into a self-optimizing primitive, AFBS-BO enables plug-and-play acceleration across diverse transformer architectures and domains.

AI.tldr: AFBS-BO automatically discovers layer- and head-specific hyperparameters for sparse attention in transformers, using Bayesian Optimization with binary search and multi-fidelity testing to reduce tuning cost; it outperforms baselines and approaches dense attention quality.
AI.motivation: Current sparse-attention methods require manual, layer- and model-specific hyperparameter tuning, causing usability barriers; hyperparameters vary substantially across layers/models, and grid search is costly.
AI.method: Hybrid algorithm: Bayesian Optimization for global search + binary search for local refinement; employs multi-fidelity evaluations across different sequence lengths to cut costs.
AI.result: On Llama-2-7B, achieves 3.4x faster hyperparameter discovery with 8.8x fewer evaluations than grid search, finds high-sparsity configs that beat existing sparse baselines and closely match dense attention quality.
AI.conclusion: Treats sparse attention as a self-optimizing primitive; enables plug-and-play acceleration across transformer architectures and domains.

title: MANAR: Memory-augmented Attention with Navigational Abstract Conceptual Representation
summary: MANAR (Memory-augmented Attention with Navigational Abstract Conceptual Representation), contextualization layer generalizes standard multi-head attention (MHA) by instantiating the principles of Global Workspace Theory (GWT). While MHA enables unconstrained all-to-all communication, it lacks the functional bottleneck and global integration mechanisms hypothesized in cognitive models of consciousness. MANAR addresses this by implementing a central workspace through a trainable memory of abstract concepts and an Abstract Conceptual Representation (ACR). The architecture follows a two-stage logic that maps directly to GWT mechanics: (i) an integration phase, where retrieved memory concepts converge to form a collective "mental image" (the ACR) based on input stimuli; and (ii) a broadcasting phase, where this global state navigates and informs the contextualization of individual local tokens. We demonstrate that efficient linear-time scaling is a fundamental architectural byproduct of instantiating GWT functional bottleneck, as routing global information through a constant-sized ACR resolves the quadratic complexity inherent in standard attention. MANAR is a compatible re-parameterization of MHA with identical semantic roles for its projections, enabling knowledge transfer from pretrained transformers via weight-copy and thus overcoming the adoption barriers of structurally incompatible linear-time alternatives. MANAR enables non-convex contextualization, synthesizing representations that provably lie outside the convex hull of input tokens - a mathematical reflection of the creative synthesis described in GWT. Empirical evaluations confirm that MANAR matches or exceeds strong baselines across language (GLUE score of 85.1), vision (83.9% ImageNet-1K), and speech (2.7% WER on LibriSpeech), positioning it as an efficient and expressive alternative to quadratic attention.

AI.tldr: Manar introduces a memory-augmented attention mechanism inspired by Global Workspace Theory, using a constant-sized Abstract Conceptual Representation (ACR) as a bottleneck to achieve linear-time attention while enabling non-convex contextualization; claims strong cross-domain performance and easy integration with pretrained transformers via weight-copy.
AI.motivation: Address quadratic complexity of standard multi-head attention by imposing a central global workspace that enforces global integration and information routing through a fixed-size memory of abstract concepts.
AI.method: Two-stage process: (i) integration where retrieved memory concepts form the ACR from input stimuli, (ii) broadcasting where the ACR guides contextualization of local tokens. Reparameterizes MHA to use identical semantic roles for projections to allow weight-copy from pretrained transformers. The ACR operates as a constant-sized bottleneck, enabling linear-time scaling and non-convex contextualization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show competitive performance across domains: language (GLUE ~85.1), vision (ImageNet-1K ~83.9% top-1), and speech (LibriSpeech ~2.7% WER). The method is positioned as an efficient alternative to quadratic attention with potential for knowledge transfer via weight-copy.
AI.conclusion: MANAR provides a theoretically motivated, architecture-level bottleneck inspired by GWT that yields linear-time, expressive attention with cross-domain applicability and compatibility with pretrained transformers; it supports non-convex contextualization and matches or surpasses strong baselines across multiple modalities.

title: Introducing M: A Modular, Modifiable Social Robot
summary: We present M, an open-source, low-cost social robot platform designed to reduce platform friction that slows social robotics research by making robots easier to reproduce, modify, and deploy in real-world settings. M combines a modular mechanical design, multimodal sensing, and expressive yet mechanically simple actuation architecture with a ROS2-native software package that cleanly separates perception, expression control, and data management. The platform includes a simulation environment with interface equivalence to hardware to support rapid sim-to-real transfer of interaction behaviors. We demonstrate extensibility through additional sensing/actuation modules and provide example interaction templates for storytelling and two-way conversational coaching. Finally, we report real-world use in participatory design and week-long in-home deployments, showing how M can serve as a practical foundation for longitudinal, reproducible social robotics research.

AI.tldr: Open-source, low-cost modular social robot platform (M) to reduce research friction; supports reproducibility, rapid prototyping, sim-to-real transfer, and real-world deployments.
AI.motivation: Address bottlenecks in social robotics research due to platform heterogeneity, high cost, and limited reproducibility; enable rapid, longitudinal studies.
AI.method: Design of M with modular mechanics, multimodal sensing, simple actuators, ROS2-native software; hardware abstraction; a hardware-equivalent simulation environment; extensible sensing/actuation modules; example interaction templates for storytelling and conversational coaching; real-world deployments.
AI.result: Demonstrates extensibility and provides templates; reports participatory design and week-long in-home deployments; positions M as a practical foundation for longitudinal, reproducible social robotics research.
AI.conclusion: M offers a scalable, reusable platform that lowers barriers to entry and supports reproducible, real-world social robotics research; enables rapid prototyping and sim-to-real transfer.

title: OnlinePG: Online Open-Vocabulary Panoptic Mapping with 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Open-vocabulary scene understanding with online panoptic mapping is essential for embodied applications to perceive and interact with environments. However, existing methods are predominantly offline or lack instance-level understanding, limiting their applicability to real-world robotic tasks. In this paper, we propose OnlinePG, a novel and effective system that integrates geometric reconstruction and open-vocabulary perception using 3D Gaussian Splatting in an online setting. Technically, to achieve online panoptic mapping, we employ an efficient local-to-global paradigm with a sliding window. To build local consistency map, we construct a 3D segment clustering graph that jointly leverages geometric and semantic cues, fusing inconsistent segments within sliding window into complete instances. Subsequently, to update the global map, we construct explicit grids with spatial attributes for the local 3D Gaussian map and fuse them into the global map via robust bidirectional bipartite 3D Gaussian instance matching. Finally, we utilize the fused VLM features inside the 3D spatial attribute grids to achieve open-vocabulary scene understanding. Extensive experiments on widely used datasets demonstrate that our method achieves better performance among online approaches, while maintaining real-time efficiency.

AI.tldr: OnlinePG delivers online panoptic mapping with open-vocabulary perception by integrating 3D Gaussian Splatting using a local-to-global sliding window, a 3D segment clustering graph for local consistency, and robust fusion via bidirectional bipartite matching, achieving real-time open-vocabulary scene understanding.
AI.motivation: To enable online, instance-level, open-vocabulary perception for embodied robots, addressing the limitations of offline methods and the lack of real-time, integrated understanding.
AI.method: An online local-to-global framework with a sliding window. Within the window, construct a 3D segment clustering graph that fuses geometric and semantic cues to merge segments into complete instances. For the global map, embed a spatial grid on the local 3D Gaussian map and fuse into the global map using robust bidirectional bipartite 3D Gaussian instance matching. Open-vocabulary understanding is achieved by incorporating fused VLM features within the 3D spatial grids.
AI.result: Experiments on widely used datasets show the method achieves better performance among online approaches while maintaining real-time efficiency.
AI.conclusion: OnlinePG effectively combines geometric reconstruction with open-vocabulary perception in an online setting, delivering real-time, instance-level scene understanding for embodied tasks.

title: Towards Noise-Resilient Quantum Multi-Armed and Stochastic Linear Bandits
summary: Quantum multi-armed bandits (MAB) and stochastic linear bandits (SLB) have recently attracted significant attention, as their quantum counterparts can achieve quadratic speedups over classical MAB and SLB. However, most existing quantum MAB algorithms assume ideal quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) procedures on noise-free circuits, overlooking the impact of noise in current noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices. In this paper, we study a noise-robust QMC algorithm that improves estimation accuracy when querying quantum reward oracles. Building on this estimator, we propose noise-robust QMAB and QSLB algorithms that enhance performance in noisy environments while preserving the advantage over classical methods. Experiments show that our noise-robust approach improves QMAB estimation accuracy and reduces regret under several quantum noise models.

AI.tldr: A noise-robust quantum Monte Carlo estimator is developed for reward queries, enabling noise-tolerant QMAB and QSLB algorithms that maintain quantum speedups in noisy NISQ devices; empirical results show improved estimation accuracy and reduced regret under various noise models.
AI.motivation: Quantum MAB/SLB promise quadratic speedups, but practical performance is hindered by noise in NISQ devices. A robust estimation technique is needed to preserve advantage.
AI.method: Introduce a noise-robust quantum Monte Carlo estimator for reward oracles; build QMAB and QSLB algorithms on top of this estimator; analyze performance under multiple quantum noise models; provide empirical validation.
AI.result: Estimator yields improved reward estimation accuracy and lower regret in QMAB/QSLB under several noise models; experiments corroborate robustness and advantage preservation.
AI.conclusion: A noise-robust framework can sustain quantum advantages in MAB and SLB in practical, noisy quantum environments, with implications for near-term quantum learning tasks.

title: Accurate and Efficient Multi-Channel Time Series Forecasting via Sparse Attention Mechanism
summary:
The task of multi-channel time series forecasting is ubiquitous in numerous fields such as finance, supply chain management, and energy planning. It is critical to effectively capture complex dynamic dependencies within and between channels for accurate predictions. However, traditional method paid few attentions on learning the interaction among channels. This paper proposes Linear-Network (Li-Net), a novel architecture designed for multi-channel time series forecasting that captures the linear and non-linear dependencies among channels. Li-Net dynamically compresses representations across sequence and channel dimensions, processes the information through a configurable non-linear module and subsequently reconstructs the forecasts. Moreover, Li-Net integrates a sparse Top-K Softmax attention mechanism within a multi-scale projection framework to address these challenges. A core innovation is its ability to seamlessly incorporate and fuse multi-modal embeddings, guiding the sparse attention process to focus on the most informative time steps and feature channels. Through the experiment results on multiple real-world benchmark datasets demonstrate that Li-Net achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art baseline methods. Furthermore, Li-Net provides a superior balance between prediction accuracy and computational burden, exhibiting significantly lower memory usage and faster inference times. Detailed ablation studies and parameter sensitivity analyses validate the effectiveness of each key component in our proposed architecture.
  Keywords: Multivariate Time Series Forecasting, Sparse Attention Mechanism, Multimodal Information Fusion, Non-linear relationship

AI.tldr: Li-Net: a model for multichannel time series forecasting that captures linear and non-linear channel dependencies via dynamic sequence-channel compression, sparse Top-K Softmax attention with multi-scale projection, and multimodal embeddings; shows competitive accuracy with lower memory usage and faster inference, supported by ablations.
AI.motivation: To effectively model inter-channel dependencies in multivariate time series and address inefficiencies of existing methods that overlook channel interactions; emphasizes sparsity, scalability, and the integration of multimodal information.
AI.method: Li-Net compresses representations across sequence and channel dimensions, applies a configurable non-linear module, and reconstructs forecasts. It uses a sparse Top-K Softmax attention within a multi-scale projection framework, and incorporates multimodal embeddings to guide attention toward informative time steps and channels.
AI.result: Empirical results on real-world benchmarks show competitive performance against state-of-the-art baselines, with a superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off. Li-Net achieves lower memory usage and faster inference. Detailed ablations validate the effectiveness of each component.
AI.conclusion: Li-Net provides an effective, efficient framework for multichannel time series forecasting that explicitly models linear and non-linear channel dependencies, leveraging sparse attention and multimodal fusion. Future work could explore more fusion strategies and broader datasets.

title: Meanings and Measurements: Multi-Agent Probabilistic Grounding for Vision-Language Navigation
comment: Equal contribution: Swagat Padhan and Lakshya Jain, 9 pages, 6 figures, paper website: https://lakshya-asu.github.io/Meanings-Measurements-Multi-Agent-Probabilistic-Grounding/
summary: Robots collaborating with humans must convert natural language goals into actionable, physically grounded decisions. For example, executing a command such as "go two meters to the right of the fridge" requires grounding semantic references, spatial relations, and metric constraints within a 3D scene. While recent vision language models (VLMs) demonstrate strong semantic grounding capabilities, they are not explicitly designed to reason about metric constraints in physically defined spaces. In this work, we empirically demonstrate that state-of-the-art VLM-based grounding approaches struggle with complex metric-semantic language queries. To address this limitation, we propose MAPG (Multi-Agent Probabilistic Grounding), an agentic framework that decomposes language queries into structured subcomponents and queries a VLM to ground each component. MAPG then probabilistically composes these grounded outputs to produce metrically consistent, actionable decisions in 3D space. We evaluate MAPG on the HM-EQA benchmark and show consistent performance improvements over strong baselines. Furthermore, we introduce a new benchmark, MAPG-Bench, specifically designed to evaluate metric-semantic goal grounding, addressing a gap in existing language grounding evaluations. We also present a real-world robot demonstration showing that MAPG transfers beyond simulation when a structured scene representation is available.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent probabilistic grounding framework (MAPG) for metric-semantic robot grounding that decomposes language into subcomponents, grounds each with a vision-language model, and probabilistically fuses outputs to yield metrically consistent actions; shows improvement on HM-EQA, introduces MAPG-Bench, and demonstrates real-world transfer.
AI.motivation: Robots must satisfy metric constraints in 3D spaces, but state-of-the-art VLMs excel at semantic grounding yet struggle with metric grounding; bridging semantic understanding and metric reasoning enables actionable, physically-grounded robot behavior.
AI.method: An agentic framework (MAPG) that decomposes natural language queries into structured subcomponents (semantic + metric relations). Each component is grounded separately via a Vision-Language Model (VLM). The grounded outputs are then probabilistically composed to enforce metric consistency and produce actionable 3D-space decisions. Evaluation on HM-EQA shows improvements over strong baselines. A new benchmark, MAPG-Bench, targets metric-semantic grounding gaps. A real-world robot demonstration shows transfer with a structured scene representation.
AI.result: MAPG achieves consistent performance gains over baselines on HM-EQA; introduces MAPG-Bench to evaluate metric-semantic grounding; demonstrates real-world transfer when a structured scene representation is available.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing language into grounded subcomponents and probabilistically composing them enables metrically consistent, actionable robot decisions in 3D space. MAPG advances metric-semantic grounding and provides benchmarks (MAPG-Bench) and real-world demonstrations to support this direction.

title: CAFlow: Adaptive-Depth Single-Step Flow Matching for Efficient Histopathology Super-Resolution
summary: In digital pathology, whole-slide images routinely exceed gigapixel resolution, making computationally intensive generative super-resolution (SR) impractical for routine deployment. We introduce CAFlow, an adaptive-depth single-step flow-matching framework that routes each image tile to the shallowest network exit that preserves reconstruction quality. CAFlow performs flow matching in pixel-unshuffled rearranged space, reducing spatial computation by 16x while enabling direct inference. We show that dedicating half of training to exact t=0 samples is essential for single-step quality (-1.5 dB without it). The backbone, FlowResNet (1.90M parameters), mixes convolution and window self-attention blocks across four early exits spanning 3.1 to 13.3 GFLOPs. A lightweight exit classifier (~6K parameters) achieves 33% compute savings at only 0.12 dB cost. On multi-organ histopathology x4 SR, adaptive routing achieves 31.72 dB PSNR versus 31.84 dB at full depth, while the shallowest exit exceeds bicubic by +1.9 dB at 2.8x less compute than SwinIR-light. The method generalizes to held-out colon tissue with minimal quality loss (-0.02 dB), and at x8 upscaling it outperforms all comparable-compute baselines while remaining competitive with the much larger SwinIR-Medium model. Downstream nuclei segmentation confirms preservation of clinically relevant structure. The model trains in under 5 hours on a single GPU, and adaptive routing can reduce whole-slide inference from minutes to seconds.

AI.tldr: CAFlow is an adaptive-depth single-step flow-matching model for fast, high-quality super-resolution in digital pathology that routes tiles through shallow exits to save compute, achieving near full-depth performance with significant speedups and good generalization.
AI.motivation: Digital pathology whole-slide images are gigapixel-scale. Conventional generative SR is computationally prohibitive for routine deployment. The work aims to enable practical SR by reducing compute while preserving reconstruction quality and downstream clinical structure.
AI.method: A FlowResNet backbone with four early exits plus a lightweight exit classifier performs adaptive-depth flow matching in a pixel-unshuffled rearranged space. The model routes each image tile to the shallowest exit that preserves quality. Training emphasizes exact t=0 samples. The framework supports x4 and x8 SR, reporting PSNR and downstream nuclei segmentation, with inference-time gains.
AI.result: For x4 SR on multi-organ histopathology, adaptive routing yields 31.72 dB PSNR versus 31.84 dB at full depth; the shallowest exit outperforms bicubic by +1.9 dB and uses 2.8× less compute than SwinIR-light. Generalizes to held-out colon tissue with minimal loss (-0.02 dB). At x8, it outperforms all comparable-compute baselines and remains competitive with SwinIR-Medium. Nuclei segmentation downstream shows preserved structure. Training completes in under 5 hours on a single GPU, and adaptive routing reduces whole-slide inference from minutes to seconds.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive-depth flow matching in a pixel-rearranged space enables efficient, scalable super-resolution for digital pathology with minimal quality loss and preserved downstream clinical relevance, demonstrating strong generalization and substantial practical deployment benefits.

title: MLOW: Interpretable Low-Rank Frequency Magnitude Decomposition of Multiple Effects for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Separating multiple effects in time series is fundamental yet challenging for time-series forecasting (TSF). However, existing TSF models cannot effectively learn interpretable multi-effect decomposition by their smoothing-based temporal techniques. Here, a new interpretable frequency-based decomposition pipeline MLOW captures the insight: a time series can be represented as a magnitude spectrum multiplied by the corresponding phase-aware basis functions, and the magnitude spectrum distribution of a time series always exhibits observable patterns for different effects. MLOW learns a low-rank representation of the magnitude spectrum to capture dominant trending and seasonal effects. We explore low-rank methods, including PCA, NMF, and Semi-NMF, and find that none can simultaneously achieve interpretable, efficient and generalizable decomposition.
