Thus, we propose hyperplane-nonnegative matrix factorization (Hyperplane-NMF). Further, to address the frequency (spectral) leakage restricting high-quality low-rank decomposition, MLOW enables a flexible selection of input horizons and frequency levels via a mathematical mechanism. Visual analysis demonstrates that MLOW enables interpretable and hierarchical multiple-effect decomposition, robust to noises. It can also enable plug-and-play in existing TSF backbones with remarkable performance improvement but minimal architectural modifications.

AI.tldr: A frequency-based, interpretable low-rank decomposition for time-series forecasting (MLOW) that uses Hyperplane-NMF to extract dominant effects from the magnitude spectrum, addressing spectral leakage and enabling plug-and-play integration with TSF backbones.
AI.motivation: Separating multiple effects in time series is essential yet difficult; smoothing-based temporal methods struggle to produce interpretable, generalizable multi-effect decompositions. A magnitude-spectrum view promises observable patterns for different effects.
AI.method: Represent time series as magnitude spectrum scaled by phase-aware basis functions. Learn a low-rank representation of the magnitude spectrum. Compare PCA, NMF, and Semi-NMF; introduce Hyperplane-NMF to achieve interpretable, efficient, and generalizable decomposition. Address spectral leakage via flexible input horizons and frequency levels. Demonstrate visually interpretable, hierarchical decomposition and robustness to noise; enable plug-and-play improvements with minimal architectural changes in existing TSF backbones.
AI.result: PCA, NMF, and Semi-NMF fail to simultaneously achieve interpretability, efficiency, and generalizability for decomposition. Hyperplane-NMF within the MLOW framework yields interpretable, hierarchical multi-effect decomposition that is robust to noise and can be plugged into existing TSF backbones, delivering notable performance gains with minimal architectural changes.
AI.conclusion: MLOW provides an interpretable, frequency-based approach to decomposing multiple effects in time series. It addresses spectral leakage, supports flexible horizon/frequency settings, and offers a plug-and-play route to improve TSF performance with minimal architectural alterations.

title: MemMA: Coordinating the Memory Cycle through Multi-Agent Reasoning and In-Situ Self-Evolution
summary: Memory-augmented LLM agents maintain external memory banks to support long-horizon interaction, yet most existing systems treat construction, retrieval, and utilization as isolated subroutines. This creates two coupled challenges: strategic blindness on the forward path of the memory cycle, where construction and retrieval are driven by local heuristics rather than explicit strategic reasoning, and sparse, delayed supervision on the backward path, where downstream failures rarely translate into direct repairs of the memory bank. To address these challenges, we propose MemMA, a plug-and-play multi-agent framework that coordinates the memory cycle along both the forward and backward paths. On the forward path, a Meta-Thinker produces structured guidance that steers a Memory Manager during construction and directs a Query Reasoner during iterative retrieval. On the backward path, MemMA introduces in-situ self-evolving memory construction, which synthesizes probe QA pairs, verifies the current memory, and converts failures into repair actions before the memory is finalized. Extensive experiments on LoCoMo show that MemMA consistently outperforms existing baselines across multiple LLM backbones and improves three different storage backends in a plug-and-play manner. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/ventr1c/memma.

AI.tldr: MemMA is a plug-and-play framework that coordinates the full memory cycle in memory-augmented LLM agents via a Meta-Thinker for forward guidance and in-situ self-evolving memory for backward repair, improving performance across several backbones and storage backends on LoCoMo; code is released.
AI.motivation: Existing memory-augmented systems treat memory construction, retrieval, and utilization as isolated subroutines, causing strategic blind spots on forward memory operations and sparse supervision for repairs on the backward path. A unified, bidirectional memory cycle is needed to improve long-horizon reasoning.
AI.method: MemMA introduces a two-path coordination: (1) forward path with Meta-Thinker that provides structured guidance to a Memory Manager during construction and to a Query Reasoner during iterative retrieval; (2) backward path with in-situ self-evolving memory that synthesizes probe QA pairs, verifies memory, and converts failures into repair actions before finalization. It is plug-and-play and evaluated on LoCoMo with multiple LLM backbones and three storage backends.
AI.result: Empirical results on LoCoMo show MemMA consistently outperforms baselines across multiple LLM backbones and improves three storage backends; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: MemMA effectively coordinates forward and backward memory dynamics, enabling more strategic memory construction, retrieval, and repair, and demonstrates generality across backbones and storage backends. This points to a robust, extensible approach to memory-augmented agents and long-horizon reasoning.

title: ADMM-Based Distributed MPC with Control Barrier Functions for Safe Multi-Robot Quadrupedal Locomotion
summary: This paper proposes a fully decentralized model predictive control (MPC) framework with control barrier function (CBF) constraints for safety-critical trajectory planning in multi-robot legged systems. The incorporation of CBF constraints introduces explicit inter-agent coupling, which prevents direct decomposition of the resulting optimal control problems. To address this challenge, we reformulate the centralized safety-critical MPC problem using a structured distributed optimization framework based on the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM). By introducing a novel node-edge splitting formulation with consensus constraints, the proposed approach decomposes the global problem into independent node-local and edge-local quadratic programs that can be solved in parallel using only neighbor-to-neighbor communication. This enables fully decentralized trajectory optimization with symmetric computational load across agents while preserving safety and dynamic feasibility. The proposed framework is integrated into a hierarchical locomotion control architecture for quadrupedal robots, combining high-level distributed trajectory planning, mid-level nonlinear MPC enforcing single rigid body dynamics, and low-level whole-body control enforcing full-order robot dynamics. The effectiveness of the proposed approach is demonstrated through hardware experiments on two Unitree Go2 quadrupedal robots and numerical simulations involving up to four robots navigating uncertain environments with rough terrain and external disturbances. The results show that the proposed distributed formulation achieves performance comparable to centralized MPC while reducing the average per-cycle planning time by up to 51% in the four-agent case, enabling efficient real-time decentralized implementation.

AI.tldr: A decentralized MPC framework with CBF constraints for multi-robot legged systems, using ADMM-based node-edge splitting to decompose the centralized problem into parallel node-local and edge-local QPs; achieves near-central performance with substantial speedups, validated on quadrupeds.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical trajectory planning in multi-robot legged systems is hindered by inter-agent CBF coupling which prevents direct decentralization; need scalable real-time optimization.
AI.method: Reformulates centralized CBF-constrained MPC via ADMM with node-edge splitting and consensus constraints; solves independent node-local and edge-local QPs in parallel with neighbor communication; integrates into hierarchical locomotion stack (high-level distributed planning, mid-level single rigid body MPC, low-level full-order control).
AI.result: Hardware experiments with two Unitree Go2s; simulations with up to four robots in uncertain environments; distributed formulation achieves performance close to centralized MPC and reduces per-cycle planning time by up to 51% in a four-agent setup.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective decentralized trajectory optimization preserving safety and feasibility; scalable, real-time implementation enabled by the proposed ADMM-based decomposition and node-edge splitting for CBF-constrained MPC in multi-robot legged systems.

title: Counting Circuits: Mechanistic Interpretability of Visual Reasoning in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Counting serves as a simple but powerful test of a Large Vision-Language Model's (LVLM's) reasoning; it forces the model to identify each individual object and then add them all up. In this study, we investigate how LVLMs implement counting using controlled synthetic and real-world benchmarks, combined with mechanistic analyses. Our results show that LVLMs display a human-like counting behavior, with precise performance on small numerosities and noisy estimation for larger quantities. We introduce two novel interpretability methods, Visual Activation Patching and HeadLens, and use them to uncover a structured "counting circuit" that is largely shared across a variety of visual reasoning tasks. Building on these insights, we propose a lightweight intervention strategy that exploits simple and abundantly available synthetic images to fine-tune arbitrary pretrained LVLMs exclusively on counting. Despite the narrow scope of this fine-tuning, the intervention not only enhances counting accuracy on in-distribution synthetic data, but also yields an average improvement of +8.36% on out-of-distribution counting benchmarks and an average gain of +1.54% on complex, general visual reasoning tasks for Qwen2.5-VL. These findings highlight the central, influential role of counting in visual reasoning and suggest a potential pathway for improving overall visual reasoning capabilities through targeted enhancement of counting mechanisms.

AI.tldr: LVLMs count objects with human-like behavior (precise for small numbers, noisy for large). The paper reveals a shared counting circuit via two interpretability methods (Visual Activation Patching, HeadLens), and shows a counting-focused lightweight fine-tuning on synthetic data improves counting (in-distribution) and boosts out-of-distribution counting by 8.36% and general visual reasoning by 1.54% for Qwen2.5-VL.
AI.motivation: To understand how LVLMs perform counting, its underlying mechanics, and whether a targeted counting-centric fine-tuning can enhance broader visual reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Controlled synthetic and real-world benchmarks were used alongside mechanistic analyses. Two interpretability methods, Visual Activation Patching and HeadLens, were developed to uncover a structured counting circuit shared across visual reasoning tasks. A lightweight intervention fine-tunes pretrained LVLMs exclusively on counting using synthetic images.
AI.result: LVLMs exhibit human-like counting: exact for small numerosities, estimation-like for large numerosities. The interpretability methods uncover a shared counting circuit. The counting-focused fine-tuning yields +8.36% improvement on out-of-distribution counting benchmarks and +1.54% on complex visual reasoning tasks for Qwen2.5-VL.
AI.conclusion: Counting is central to visual reasoning in LVLMs; targeted enhancement of counting mechanisms via synthetic-data fine-tuning can meaningfully improve both counting accuracy and broader visual reasoning performance.

title: Discounted Beta--Bernoulli Reward Estimation for Sample-Efficient Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as an effective post-training paradigm for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models. However, existing group-based RLVR methods often suffer from severe sample inefficiency. This inefficiency stems from reliance on point estimation of rewards from a small number of rollouts, leading to high estimation variance, variance collapse, and ineffective utilization of generated responses. In this work, we reformulate RLVR from a statistical estimation perspective by modeling rewards as samples drawn from a policy-induced distribution and casting advantage computation as the problem of estimating the reward distribution from finite data. Building on this view, we propose Discounted Beta--Bernoulli (DBB) reward estimation, which leverages historical reward statistics for the non-stationary distribution. Although biased, the resulting estimator exhibits reduced and stable variance, theoretically avoids estimated variance collapse, and achieves lower mean squared error than standard point estimation. Extensive experiments across six in-distribution and three out-of-distribution reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that GRPO with DBB consistently outperforms naive GRPO, achieving average Acc@8 improvements of 3.22/2.42 points in-distribution and 12.49/6.92 points out-of-distribution on the 1.7B and 8B models, respectively, without additional computational cost or memory usage.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Discounted Beta--Bernoulli reward estimator for RLVR that treats rewards as samples from a policy-induced distribution, reducing variance and avoiding variance collapse to improve GRPO performance across multiple benchmarks and model scales.
AI.motivation: Group-based RLVR suffers from severe sample inefficiency due to reliance on point reward estimates from few rollouts, causing high variance and underutilization of generated responses.
AI.method: Model rewards as samples from a policy-induced distribution; recast advantage estimation as estimating the reward distribution from finite data; introduce Discounted Beta--Bernoulli (DBB) reward estimation that leverages historical reward statistics for a non-stationary distribution.
AI.result: The estimator is biased but has reduced and stable variance, avoids variance collapse, and achieves lower mean squared error than point estimation. GRPO with DBB improves Acc@8 by 3.22/2.42 points in-distribution and 12.49/6.92 points out-of-distribution on 1.7B/8B models, with no extra compute or memory cost.
AI.conclusion: DBB reward estimation provides a principled, variance-regularized reward estimate for RLVR, leading to consistent performance gains across diverse benchmarks and model sizes without additional resource overhead.

title: Analysis Of Linguistic Stereotypes in Single and Multi-Agent Generative AI Architectures
summary: Many works in the literature show that LLM outputs exhibit discriminatory behaviour, triggering stereotype-based inferences based on the dialect in which the inputs are written. This bias has been shown to be particularly pronounced when the same inputs are provided to LLMs in Standard American English (SAE) and African-American English (AAE). In this paper, we replicate existing analyses of dialect-sensitive stereotype generation in LLM outputs and investigate the effects of mitigation strategies, including prompt engineering (role-based and Chain-Of-Thought prompting) and multi-agent architectures composed of generate-critique-revise models. We define eight prompt templates to analyse different ways in which dialect bias can manifest, such as suggested names, jobs, and adjectives for SAE or AAE speakers. We use an LLM-as-judge approach to evaluate the bias in the results. Our results show that stereotype-bearing differences emerge between SAE- and AAE-related outputs across all template categories, with the strongest effects observed in adjective and job attribution. Baseline disparities vary substantially by model, with the largest SAE-AAE differential observed in Claude Haiku and the smallest in Phi-4 Mini. Chain-Of-Thought prompting proved to be an effective mitigation strategy for Claude Haiku, whereas the use of a multi-agent architecture ensured consistent mitigation across all the models. These findings suggest that for intersectionality-informed software engineering, fairness evaluation should include model-specific validation of mitigation strategies, and workflow-level controls (e.g., agentic architectures involving critique models) in high-impact LLM deployments. The current results are exploratory in nature and limited in scope, but can lead to extensions and replications by increasing the dataset size and applying the procedure to different languages or dialects.

AI.tldr: Dialect-sensitive stereotype biases persist in LLM outputs (SAE vs AAE); mitigation strategies like Chain-of-Thought prompting and multi-agent critique-revise architectures can reduce bias, with effectiveness varying by model.
AI.motivation: To quantify dialect-based stereotype generation in LLMs and assess mitigation strategies for fairness, informing intersectionality-aware software engineering.
AI.method: Eight prompt templates to elicit SAE/AAE-related stereotype cues; LLM-as-judge evaluation; compare role-based prompts and Chain-of-Thought prompting; explore multi-agent Generate-Critique-Revise architectures; assess multiple models (e.g., Claude Haiku, Phi-4 Mini).
AI.result: Stereotype-bearing differences emerged across all templates; strongest effects in adjective and job attribution; baseline disparities vary by model; Chain-of-Thought prompting mitigated bias for Claude Haiku; multi-agent architectures provided consistent mitigation across models.
AI.conclusion: Fairness evaluation should include model-specific validation of mitigation and workflow-level controls; results are exploratory but can be extended by larger datasets and applying the approach to other languages/dialects.

title: Sparse Autoencoders Reveal Interpretable and Steerable Features in VLA Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising approach for general-purpose robot manipulation. However, their generalization is inconsistent: while these models can perform impressively in some settings, fine-tuned variants often fail on novel objects, scenes, and instructions. We apply mechanistic interpretability techniques to better understand the inner workings of VLA models. To probe internal representations, we train Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) on hidden layer activations of the VLA. SAEs learn a sparse dictionary whose features act as a compact, interpretable basis for the model's computation. We find that the large majority of extracted SAE features correspond to memorized sequences from specific training demonstrations. However, some features correspond to interpretable, general, and steerable motion primitives and semantic properties, offering a promising glimpse toward VLA generalizability. We propose a metric to categorize features according to whether they represent generalizable transferable primitives or episode-specific memorization. We validate these findings through steering experiments on the LIBERO benchmark. We show that individual SAE features causally influence robot behavior. Steering general features induces behaviors consistent with their semantic meaning and can be applied across tasks and scenes. This work provides the first mechanistic evidence that VLAs can learn generalizable features across tasks and scenes. We observe that supervised fine-tuning on small robotics datasets disproportionately amplifies memorization. In contrast, training on larger, more diverse datasets (e.g., DROID) or using knowledge insulation promotes more general features. We provide an open-source codebase and user-friendly interface for activation collection, SAE training, and feature steering. Our project page is located at http://drvla.github.io

AI.tldr: VLAs exhibit inconsistent generalization; sparse autoencoders reveal mostly memorized demo sequences but also a subset of generalizable motion primitives and semantic properties; a metric differentiates transferable primitives from memorization; steering experiments on LIBERO validate causal influence of SAE features; larger, diverse training data and knowledge insulation reduce memorization and promote general features; codebase released.
AI.motivation: To understand the internal representations of Vision-Language-Action models for robot manipulation, diagnose the source of poor generalization (memorization vs. generalizable primitives), and identify factors that promote transferability across tasks and scenes.
AI.method: Train Sparse Autoencoders on hidden activations of VLA models to extract a sparse feature dictionary. Analyze features to categorize them as generalizable primitives or episode-specific memorization using a proposed metric. Validate causality by steering experiments with LIBERO benchmarks. Compare effects of fine-tuning on small robotics datasets versus training on large, diverse datasets (e.g., DROID) and apply knowledge insulation. Provide open-source code for activation collection, SAE training, and feature steering.
AI.result: SAE features predominantly capture memorized sequences from training demonstrations, while a subset corresponds to interpretable, general, steerable motion primitives and semantic properties. A metric can separate these categories. Steering experiments show that individual SAE features causally affect behavior, with general features producing task- and scene-transferable actions. Fine-tuning on small datasets amplifies memorization; larger, diverse data or knowledge insulation yields more general features.
AI.conclusion: This work provides mechanistic evidence that VLAs can learn transferable, generalizable features across tasks and scenes, though memorization is prevalent. To foster generalization, use diverse data and protective training strategies. An open-source toolkit is released to study activation, SAE training, and feature steering.

title: 3DreamBooth: High-Fidelity 3D Subject-Driven Video Generation Model
summary: Creating dynamic, view-consistent videos of customized subjects is highly sought after for a wide range of emerging applications, including immersive VR/AR, virtual production, and next-generation e-commerce. However, despite rapid progress in subject-driven video generation, existing methods predominantly treat subjects as 2D entities, focusing on transferring identity through single-view visual features or textual prompts. Because real-world subjects are inherently 3D, applying these 2D-centric approaches to 3D object customization reveals a fundamental limitation: they lack the comprehensive spatial priors necessary to reconstruct the 3D geometry. Consequently, when synthesizing novel views, they must rely on generating plausible but arbitrary details for unseen regions, rather than preserving the true 3D identity. Achieving genuine 3D-aware customization remains challenging due to the scarcity of multi-view video datasets. While one might attempt to fine-tune models on limited video sequences, this often leads to temporal overfitting. To resolve these issues, we introduce a novel framework for 3D-aware video customization, comprising 3DreamBooth and 3Dapter. 3DreamBooth decouples spatial geometry from temporal motion through a 1-frame optimization paradigm. By restricting updates to spatial representations, it effectively bakes a robust 3D prior into the model without the need for exhaustive video-based training. To enhance fine-grained textures and accelerate convergence, we incorporate 3Dapter, a visual conditioning module. Following single-view pre-training, 3Dapter undergoes multi-view joint optimization with the main generation branch via an asymmetrical conditioning strategy. This design allows the module to act as a dynamic selective router, querying view-specific geometric hints from a minimal reference set. Project page: https://ko-lani.github.io/3DreamBooth/

AI.tldr: A 3D-aware video customization framework (3DreamBooth + 3Dapter) that decouples spatial geometry from temporal motion using 1-frame optimization and a conditioning adapter to inject view-specific geometric priors, enabling robust, view-consistent 3D video synthesis with limited multi-view data.
AI.motivation: 2D-centric subject modeling fails to capture true 3D identity due to missing comprehensive 3D spatial priors; scarcity of multi-view video data makes 3D-aware customization challenging and prone to temporal overfitting when fine-tuning on limited footage.
AI.method: 3DreamBooth performs 1-frame optimization that updates only spatial representations to bake a robust 3D prior into the model, avoiding exhaustive video-based training. 3Dapter is a visual conditioning module trained after single-view pretraining; it then undergoes multi-view joint optimization with the main generation branch via an asymmetrical conditioning strategy, acting as a dynamic router that queries view-specific geometric hints from a small reference set.
AI.result: The abstract proposes a framework that decouples geometry and motion to enable 3D-aware video customization. It emphasizes robustness of the 3D prior and accelerated convergence through 3Dapter, with a project page provided for details; no quantitative results are stated in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The work targets the core challenges of 3D-aware video customization—data scarcity and temporal overfitting—by introducing 3DreamBooth to bake 3D priors with minimal video data and 3Dapter to refine textures and enable efficient, view-aware optimization.

title: Seeking Universal Shot Language Understanding Solutions
summary: Shot language understanding (SLU) is crucial for cinematic analysis but remains challenging due to its diverse cinematographic dimensions and subjective expert judgment. While vision-language models (VLMs) have shown strong ability in general visual understanding, recent studies reveal judgment discrepancies between VLMs and film experts on SLU tasks. To address this gap, we introduce SLU-SUITE, a comprehensive training and evaluation suite containing 490K human-annotated QA pairs across 33 tasks spanning six film-grounded dimensions. Using SLU-SUITE, we originally observe two insights into VLM-based SLU from: the model side, which diagnoses key bottlenecks of modules; the data side, which quantifies cross-dimensional influences among tasks. These findings motivate our universal SLU solutions from two complementary paradigms: UniShot, a balanced one-for-all generalist trained via dynamic-balanced data mixing, and AgentShots, a prompt-routed expert cluster that maximizes peak dimension performance. Extensive experiments show that our models outperform task-specific ensembles on in-domain tasks and surpass leading commercial VLMs by 22% on out-of-domain tasks.

AI.tldr: SLU-SUITE provides a large, diverse evaluation and training suite for shot language understanding (SLU) with 490k QA pairs across 33 tasks and six film-grounded dimensions, enabling diagnosis of model bottlenecks and cross-task influences; proposes UniShot and AgentShots as universal SLU solutions; shows improved in-domain performance and 22% gains on out-of-domain tasks versus leading VLMs.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between vision-language models (VLMs) and film experts in SLU due to diverse cinematographic dimensions and subjective judgment; create a unified benchmark and training framework to analyze model and data bottlenecks and to push toward universal SLU solutions.
AI.method: Construct SLU-SUITE, a comprehensive dataset with 490k human-annotated QA pairs across 33 tasks spanning six film-grounded dimensions. Analyze model bottlenecks on the VLM side and cross-dimensional influences on the data side. Propose two universal solutions: UniShot (a balanced one-for-all generalist trained via dynamic-balanced data mixing) and AgentShots (a prompt-routed expert cluster).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that UniShot and AgentShots outperform task-specific ensembles on in-domain SLU tasks and surpass leading commercial VLMs by 22% on out-of-domain tasks.
AI.conclusion: SLU-SUITE enables universal SLU development; the combination of a balanced generalist and a prompt-routed expert cluster yields robust SLU performance across diverse film-grounded tasks, narrowing the gap between VLMs and film experts.

title: Memento-Skills: Let Agents Design Agents
summary:
We introduce \emph{Memento-Skills}, a generalist, continually-learnable LLM agent system that functions as an \emph{agent-designing agent}: it autonomously constructs, adapts, and improves task-specific agents through experience. The system is built on a memory-based reinforcement learning framework with \emph{stateful prompts}, where reusable skills (stored as structured markdown files) serve as persistent, evolving memory. These skills encode both behaviour and context, enabling the agent to carry forward knowledge across interactions.
  Starting from simple elementary skills (like Web search and terminal operations), the agent continually improves via the \emph{Read--Write Reflective Learning} mechanism introduced in \emph{Memento~2}~\cite{wang2025memento2}. In the \emph{read} phase, a behaviour-trainable skill router selects the most relevant skill conditioned on the current stateful prompt; in the \emph{write} phase, the agent updates and expands its skill library based on new experience. This closed-loop design enables \emph{continual learning without updating LLM parameters}, as all adaptation is realised through the evolution of externalised skills and prompts.
  Unlike prior approaches that rely on human-designed agents, Memento-Skills enables a generalist agent to \emph{design agents end-to-end} for new tasks. Through iterative skill generation and refinement, the system progressively improves its own capabilities. Experiments on the \emph{General AI Assistants} benchmark and \emph{Humanity's Last Exam} demonstrate sustained gains, achieving 26.2\% and 116.2\% relative improvements in overall accuracy, respectively. Code is available at https://github.com/Memento-Teams/Memento-Skills.

AI.tldr: A generalist, continually-learnable LLM agent system (Memento-Skills) that designs, adapts, and improves task-specific agents through memory-based RL and stateful prompts; externalized skills evolve to enable continual learning without LLM updates; shows improvements on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the need for continual, architecture-agnostic agent learning by externalizing skills and prompts, enabling knowledge reuse and adaptation without updating LLM parameters.
AI.method: Memory-based reinforcement learning with stateful prompts. Skills are stored as structured markdown files and act as persistent memory. The Read phase uses a skill router to select relevant skills based on the current prompt; the Write phase updates and expands the skill library from new experiences. Starts from simple elementary skills and evolves a generalist agent that designs agents end-to-end for new tasks.
AI.result: Reported improvements on benchmarks: 26.2% relative improvement on the General AI Assistants benchmark and 116.2% relative improvement on Humanity's Last Exam. Code available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables continual learning without updating the LLM parameters by evolving external skills and prompts. It allows a generalist agent to design, instantiate, and refine task-specific agents end-to-end, achieving sustained performance gains.

title: FASTER: Rethinking Real-Time Flow VLAs
summary: Real-time execution is crucial for deploying Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in the physical world. Existing asynchronous inference methods primarily optimize trajectory smoothness, but neglect the critical latency in reacting to environmental changes. By rethinking the notion of reaction in action chunking policies, this paper presents a systematic analysis of the factors governing reaction time. We show that reaction time follows a uniform distribution determined jointly by the Time to First Action (TTFA) and the execution horizon. Moreover, we reveal that the standard practice of applying a constant schedule in flow-based VLAs can be inefficient and forces the system to complete all sampling steps before any movement can start, forming the bottleneck in reaction latency. To overcome this issue, we propose Fast Action Sampling for ImmediaTE Reaction (FASTER). By introducing a Horizon-Aware Schedule, FASTER adaptively prioritizes near-term actions during flow sampling, compressing the denoising of the immediate reaction by tenfold (e.g., in $π_{0.5}$ and X-VLA) into a single step, while preserving the quality of long-horizon trajectory. Coupled with a streaming client-server pipeline, FASTER substantially reduces the effective reaction latency on real robots, especially when deployed on consumer-grade GPUs. Real-world experiments, including a highly dynamic table tennis task, prove that FASTER unlocks unprecedented real-time responsiveness for generalist policies, enabling rapid generation of accurate and smooth trajectories.

AI.tldr: FASTER introduces a horizon-aware action sampling strategy to reduce reaction latency in flow-based Vision-Language-Action models. By prioritizing near-term actions and compressing immediate denoising to a single step, it achieves substantially faster reaction with preserved long-horizon quality, validated via a streaming client-server pipeline on real robots and dynamic tasks (e.g., table tennis).
AI.motivation: Real-time execution is essential for deploying VLA models in the physical world. Existing asynchronous inference focuses on trajectory smoothness but ignores reaction latency to environmental changes. The reaction time follows a uniform distribution determined by Time to First Action (TTFA) and the execution horizon, and constant flow schedules bottleneck early reactions.
AI.method: Propose Horizon-Aware Schedule within FASTER to adaptively prioritize near-term actions during flow sampling. Compress denoising of immediate reactions by about tenfold (e.g., in π0.5 and X-VLA) into a single step while preserving long-horizon trajectory quality. Combine this with a streaming client-server pipeline to enable real-time inference.
AI.result: Substantial reduction in effective reaction latency on real robots, particularly with consumer-grade GPUs. Real-world experiments including a highly dynamic table tennis task demonstrate unprecedented real-time responsiveness and the ability to generate accurate, smooth trajectories rapidly.
AI.conclusion: FASTER enables real-time responsiveness for generalist VLA policies by addressing the reaction bottleneck with horizon-aware scheduling and streaming inference, maintaining trajectory quality for long horizons.

title: Remedying Target-Domain Astigmatism for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection
summary: Cross-domain few-shot object detection (CD-FSOD) aims to adapt pretrained detectors from a source domain to target domains with limited annotations, suffering from severe domain shifts and data scarcity problems. In this work, we find a previously overlooked phenomenon: models exhibit dispersed and unfocused attention in target domains, leading to imprecise localization and redundant predictions, just like a human cannot focus on visual objects. Therefore, we call it the target-domain Astigmatism problem. Analysis on attention distances across transformer layers reveals that regular fine-tuning inherently shows a trend to remedy this problem, but results are still far from satisfactory, which we aim to enhance in this paper. Biologically inspired by the human fovea-style visual system, we enhance the fine-tuning's inherent trend through a center-periphery attention refinement framework, which contains (1) a Positive Pattern Refinement module to reshape attention toward semantic objects using class-specific prototypes, simulating the visual center region; (2) a Negative Context Modulation module to enhance boundary discrimination by modeling background context, simulating the visual periphery region; and (3) a Textual Semantic Alignment module to strengthen center-periphery distinction through cross-modal cues. Our bio-inspired approach transforms astigmatic attention into focused patterns, substantially improving adaptation to target domains. Experiments on six challenging CD-FSOD benchmarks consistently demonstrate improved detection accuracy and establish new state-of-the-art results.

AI.tldr: Center-periphery attention refinement for cross-domain few-shot object detection (CD-FSOD) to fix target-domain astigmatism by biasing attention toward object centers and reinforcing boundaries via prototypes, context modulation, and cross-modal alignment; yields state-of-the-art results on six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: CD-FSOD suffers from severe domain shifts and data scarcity. The authors identify a previously overlooked problem: target-domain astigmatism, where attention is dispersed and unfocused, causing imprecise localization and redundant predictions. The work aims to steer attention to semantic object regions to improve adaptation.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Positive Pattern Refinement using class-specific prototypes to bias attention toward semantic centers; (2) Negative Context Modulation to enhance boundary discrimination by modeling background context (periphery); (3) Textual Semantic Alignment to strengthen center-periphery distinction via cross-modal (textual-visual) cues.
AI.result: Experiments on six challenging CD-FSOD benchmarks show consistent accuracy gains and establish new state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: A biologically inspired center-periphery attention refinement framework effectively converts astigmatic attention into focused patterns, substantially improving cross-domain adaptation in few-shot object detection.

title: AcceRL: A Distributed Asynchronous Reinforcement Learning and World Model Framework for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) for large-scale Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models faces significant challenges in computational efficiency and data acquisition. We propose AcceRL, a fully asynchronous and decoupled RL framework designed to eliminate synchronization barriers by physically isolating training, inference, and rollouts. Crucially, AcceRL is the first to integrate a plug-and-play, trainable world model into a distributed asynchronous RL pipeline to generate virtual experiences. Experiments on the LIBERO benchmark demonstrate that AcceRL achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance. Systematically, it exhibits super-linear scaling in throughput and highly efficient hardware utilization. Algorithmically, the world-model-augmented variant delivers unprecedented sample efficiency and robust training stability in complex control tasks.

AI.tldr: Asynchronous, decoupled RL with a trainable world model for VLA tasks achieves SOTA on LIBERO with super-linear throughput and strong sample efficiency.
AI.motivation: Large-scale Vision-Language-Action (VLA) RL faces synchronization bottlenecks, high data demands, and inefficient hardware utilization. A scalable, data-efficient framework is needed.
AI.method: A fully asynchronous, decoupled RL architecture that isolates training, inference, and rollouts. Introduces a plug-and-play trainable world model to generate virtual experiences within a distributed RL pipeline.
AI.result: On the LIBERO benchmark, AcceRL attains state-of-the-art performance, exhibits super-linear throughput scaling, and efficiently utilizes hardware. The world-model–augmented variant achieves unprecedented sample efficiency and robust training stability in complex control tasks.
AI.conclusion: AcceRL demonstrates strong scalability and data efficiency for large-scale VLA RL by decoupling components and leveraging a trainable world model for virtual experiences. Potential limitations and future work likely involve model reliability, computation cost of the world model, and generalization to broader domains.

title: NeuroGame Transformer: Gibbs-Inspired Attention Driven by Game Theory and Statistical Physics
comment: This work has been submitted to IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics for possible publication
summary: Standard attention mechanisms in transformers are limited by their pairwise formulation, which hinders the modeling of higher-order dependencies among tokens. We introduce the NeuroGame Transformer (NGT) to overcome this by reconceptualizing attention through a dual perspective: tokens are treated simultaneously as players in a cooperative game and as interacting spins in a statistical physics system. Token importance is quantified using two complementary game-theoretic concepts -- Shapley values for global, permutation-based attribution and Banzhaf indices for local, coalition-level influence. These are combined via a learnable gating parameter to form an external magnetic field, while pairwise interaction potentials capture synergistic relationships. The system's energy follows an Ising Hamiltonian, with attention weights emerging as marginal probabilities under the Gibbs distribution, efficiently computed via mean-field equations. To ensure scalability despite the exponential coalition space, we develop importance-weighted Monte Carlo estimators with Gibbs-distributed weights. This approach avoids explicit exponential factors, ensuring numerical stability for long sequences. We provide theoretical convergence guarantees and characterize the fairness-sensitivity trade-off governed by the interpolation parameter. Experimental results demonstrate that the NeuroGame Transformer achieves strong performance across SNLI, and MNLI-matched, outperforming some major efficient transformer baselines. On SNLI, it attains a test accuracy of 86.4\% (with a peak validation accuracy of 86.6\%), surpassing ALBERT-Base and remaining highly competitive with RoBERTa-Base. Code is available at https://github.com/dbouchaffra/NeuroGame-Transformer.

AI.tldr: NeuroGame Transformer reframes attention as a joint cooperative game over tokens modeled by an Ising-like energy, using Shapley values and Banzhaf indices to compute global/local token importance; scalable inference via mean-field and Gibbs-based Monte Carlo; shows competitive results on SNLI/MNLI-matched, with code available.
AI.motivation: Standard attention in transformers is limited by its pairwise formulation, impeding higher-order token dependencies. A physics-inspired, scalable framework is proposed to capture complex interactions without prohibitive cost.
AI.method: Treat tokens as players in a cooperative game and as spins in an Ising-like energy system. Global token importance via Shapley values and local coalition influence via Banzhaf indices; combine via a learnable gate to form an external magnetic field. Model uses pairwise interaction potentials for synergy; attention weights are marginal probabilities under a Gibbs distribution, computed with mean-field equations. To scale to long sequences, importance-weighted Monte Carlo estimators with Gibbs-distributed weights are used to avoid explicit exponential factors. The approach includes theoretical convergence guarantees and analyzes a fairness-sensitivity trade-off controlled by an interpolation parameter.
AI.result: Empirical results on SNLI and MNLI-matched show strong performance, outperforming some major efficient transformer baselines. On SNLI, test accuracy is 86.4% (peak validation 86.6%), surpassing ALBERT-Base and remaining competitive with RoBERTa-Base. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: NGT demonstrates how higher-order token interactions can be captured via a physics-inspired attention mechanism with provable convergence and a tunable fairness-sensitivity trade-off. The framework yields competitive accuracy on standard NLI tasks while maintaining scalability through mean-field and Gibbs-weighted Monte Carlo inference.

title: OmniVTA: Visuo-Tactile World Modeling for Contact-Rich Robotic Manipulation
summary: Contact-rich manipulation tasks, such as wiping and assembly, require accurate perception of contact forces, friction changes, and state transitions that cannot be reliably inferred from vision alone. Despite growing interest in visuo-tactile manipulation, progress is constrained by two persistent limitations: existing datasets are small in scale and narrow in task coverage, and current methods treat tactile signals as passive observations rather than using them to model contact dynamics or enable closed-loop control explicitly. In this paper, we present \textbf{OmniViTac}, a large-scale visuo-tactile-action dataset comprising $21{,}000+$ trajectories across $86$ tasks and $100+$ objects, organized into six physics-grounded interaction patterns. Building on this dataset, we propose \textbf{OmniVTA}, a world-model-based visuo-tactile manipulation framework that integrates four tightly coupled modules: a self-supervised tactile encoder, a two-stream visuo-tactile world model for predicting short-horizon contact evolution, a contact-aware fusion policy for action generation, and a 60Hz reflexive controller that corrects deviations between predicted and observed tactile signals in a closed loop. Real-robot experiments across all six interaction categories show that OmniVTA outperforms existing methods and generalizes well to unseen objects and geometric configurations, confirming the value of combining predictive contact modeling with high-frequency tactile feedback for contact-rich manipulation. All data, models, and code will be made publicly available on the project website at https://mrsecant.github.io/OmniVTA.

AI.tldr: Introduces OmniViTac, a large-scale visuo-tactile-action dataset (21k+ trajectories, 86 tasks, 100+ objects, 6 interaction patterns) and OmniVTA, a world-model-based visuo-tactile manipulation framework with four modules (tactile encoder, two-stream visuo-tactile world model, contact-aware fusion policy, 60 Hz reflexive controller) for closed-loop control. Real-robot experiments show improved performance and generalization; dataset and code will be released publicly.
AI.motivation: Current visuo-tactile manipulation research suffers from small, narrow datasets and from treating tactile signals as passive observations rather than modeling contact dynamics for closed-loop control.
AI.method: Construct OmniViTac, a large-scale dataset of 21k+ trajectories across 86 tasks and 100+ objects organized into six physics-grounded interaction patterns. Develop OmniVTA, a modular, world-model-based framework: (1) self-supervised tactile encoder, (2) two-stream visuo-tactile world model predicting short-horizon contact evolution, (3) contact-aware fusion policy for action generation, (4) 60 Hz reflexive controller to correct predicted vs. observed tactile signals in closed loop. Real-robot evaluation across six interaction categories.
AI.result: OmniVTA outperforms existing methods and generalizes to unseen objects and geometries, validating the benefit of predictive contact modeling combined with high-frequency tactile feedback. Public release of data, models, and code is planned.
AI.conclusion: Integrating predictive contact dynamics with high-frequency tactile feedback enhances performance in contact-rich manipulation and provides a scalable, publicly available resource to advance visuo-tactile robotics.

title: CoDA: Exploring Chain-of-Distribution Attacks and Post-Hoc Token-Space Repair for Medical Vision-Language Models
summary: Medical vision--language models (MVLMs) are increasingly used as perceptual backbones in radiology pipelines and as the visual front end of multimodal assistants, yet their reliability under real clinical workflows remains underexplored. Prior robustness evaluations often assume clean, curated inputs or study isolated corruptions, overlooking routine acquisition, reconstruction, display, and delivery operations that preserve clinical readability while shifting image statistics. To address this gap, we propose CoDA, a chain-of-distribution framework that constructs clinically plausible pipeline shifts by composing acquisition-like shading, reconstruction and display remapping, and delivery and export degradations. Under masked structural-similarity constraints, CoDA jointly optimizes stage compositions and parameters to induce failures while preserving visual plausibility. Across brain MRI, chest X-ray, and abdominal CT, CoDA substantially degrades the zero-shot performance of CLIP-style MVLMs, with chained compositions consistently more damaging than any single stage. We also evaluate multimodal large language models (MLLMs) as technical-authenticity auditors of imaging realism and quality rather than pathology. Proprietary multimodal models show degraded auditing reliability and persistent high-confidence errors on CoDA-shifted samples, while the medical-specific MLLMs we test exhibit clear deficiencies in medical image quality auditing. Finally, we introduce a post-hoc repair strategy based on teacher-guided token-space adaptation with patch-level alignment, which improves accuracy on archived CoDA outputs. Overall, our findings characterize a clinically grounded threat surface for MVLM deployment and show that lightweight alignment improves robustness in deployment.

AI.tldr: CoDA introduces a chain-of-distribution framework to simulate clinically plausible imaging pipeline shifts (acquisition, reconstruction/display remapping, delivery/export degradations) and evaluates their impact on MVLMs and MLLMs; chained shifts are more damaging than single-stage shifts; proposes a post-hoc repair via teacher-guided token-space adaptation with patch-level alignment; results reveal a clinically grounded robustness threat and that lightweight alignment improves deployment robustness.
AI.motivation: Clinical deployment of medical vision--language models is threatened by realistic, multi-stage pipeline variations that are not captured by prior robustness benchmarks; there is a need to quantify this risk and develop mitigation.
AI.method: Construct CoDA by composing acquisition-like shading, reconstruction/display remapping, and delivery/export degradations under masked structural-similarity constraints; jointly optimize stage choices and parameters to induce failures while preserving visual plausibility; evaluate on brain MRI, chest X-ray, and abdominal CT for CLIP-style MVLMs; assess multimodal LLMs as realism auditors; implement post-hoc repair via teacher-guided token-space adaptation with patch-level alignment.
AI.result: CoDA degrades zero-shot MVLM performance; chained stage compositions consistently produce larger degradation than any single stage; proprietary MLLMs exhibit degraded auditing reliability with high-confidence errors on CoDA-shifted samples; medical-specific MLLMs show deficiencies in medical image quality auditing; patch-level alignment-based post-hoc repair improves accuracy on archived CoDA outputs.
AI.conclusion: There exists a clinically grounded threat surface for MVLM deployment; lightweight alignment (post-hoc token-space adaptation with patch-level alignment) can improve robustness; results underscore the need for pipeline-aware robustness evaluation and mitigation strategies in medical imaging ML deployments.

title: AIMER: Calibration-Free Task-Agnostic MoE Pruning
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models increase parameter capacity without proportional per-token compute, but the deployment still requires storing all experts, making expert pruning important for reducing memory and serving overhead. Existing task-agnostic expert pruning methods are typically calibration-dependent: they estimate expert importance from routing or activation statistics on a calibration set, which makes pruning outcomes sensitive to the choice of calibration set and adds substantial preprocessing cost. We introduce AIMER (\textbf{A}bsolute mean over root mean square \textbf{IM}portance for \textbf{E}xpert \textbf{R}anking), a simple calibration-free criterion that yields clear within-layer score separation and distinct expert stratification. Across 7B to 30B MoE language models at 25\% and 50\% pruning ratios over 16 benchmarks, AIMER consistently delivers competitive or stronger overall performance against state-of-the-art calibration-based expert pruning baselines with only 0.22--1.27 seconds for scoring the experts.

AI.tldr: A calibration-free method (AIMER) for pruning MoE experts that matches or beats calibration-based baselines with fast, calibration-free scoring.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory and serving overhead for Mixture-of-Experts models by pruning experts without calibration data or heavy preprocessing.
AI.method: Introduce AIMER (Absolute mean over root mean square Importance for Expert Ranking). Compute an importance score per expert as the ratio of the absolute mean to the root-mean-square magnitude across data, rank within each layer, and prune at 25% or 50% budgets across 7B–30B MoE LMs evaluated on 16 benchmarks.
AI.result: AIMER delivers competitive or superior performance compared with state-of-the-art calibration-based pruning baselines. It achieves clear within-layer score separation and distinct expert stratification, with fast scoring times of 0.22–1.27 seconds across model sizes and pruning ratios.
AI.conclusion: A simple, calibration-free criterion for MoE expert pruning that reduces dependence on calibration data while maintaining strong performance and enabling efficient pruning at scale.

title: A Concept is More Than a Word: Diversified Unlearning in Text-to-Image Diffusion Models
summary: Concept unlearning has emerged as a promising direction for reducing the risks of harmful content generation in text-to-image diffusion models by selectively erasing undesirable concepts from a model's parameters. Existing approaches typically rely on keywords to identify the target concept to be unlearned. However, we show that this keyword-based formulation is inherently limited: a visual concept is multi-dimensional, can be expressed in diverse textual forms, and often overlap with related concepts in the latent space, making keyword-only unlearning, which imprecisely indicate the target concept is brittle and prone to over-forgetting. This occurs because a single keyword represents only a narrow point estimate of the concept, failing to cover its full semantic distribution and entangled variations in the latent space. To address this limitation, we propose Diversified Unlearning, a distributional framework that represents a concept through a set of contextually diverse prompts rather than a single keyword. This richer representation enables more precise and robust unlearning. Through extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and state-of-the-art baselines, we demonstrate that integrating Diversified Unlearning as an add-on component into existing unlearning pipelines consistently achieves stronger erasure, better retention of unrelated concepts, and improved robustness against adversarial recovery attacks.

AI.tldr: Diversified Unlearning represents a concept with multiple context-rich prompts instead of a single keyword, enabling more precise and robust erasure in diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Keyword-based concept unlearning is brittle due to a concept's multi-dimensional, overlapping latent representations and diverse textual realizations; aiming for reliable erasure without harming unrelated concepts.
AI.method: Replace single-keyword targeting with a set of contextually diverse prompts to define a concept. Integrate this distributional concept representation as an add-on to existing unlearning pipelines, guiding erasure across the latent space and mitigating overlap with related concepts.
AI.result: Empirically yields stronger concept erasure, better retention of unrelated concepts, and greater robustness against adversarial recovery across multiple benchmarks and baselines.
AI.conclusion: A distributional, prompt-diversified concept representation improves unlearning effectiveness and robustness, and can be adopted as a practical add-on to current unlearning methods.

title: NavTrust: Benchmarking Trustworthiness for Embodied Navigation
summary: There are two major categories of embodied navigation: Vision-Language Navigation (VLN), where agents navigate by following natural language instructions; and Object-Goal Navigation (OGN), where agents navigate to a specified target object. However, existing work primarily evaluates model performance under nominal conditions, overlooking the potential corruptions that arise in real-world settings. To address this gap, we present NavTrust, a unified benchmark that systematically corrupts input modalities, including RGB, depth, and instructions, in realistic scenarios and evaluates their impact on navigation performance. To our best knowledge, NavTrust is the first benchmark that exposes embodied navigation agents to diverse RGB-Depth corruptions and instruction variations in a unified framework. Our extensive evaluation of seven state-of-the-art approaches reveals substantial performance degradation under realistic corruptions, which highlights critical robustness gaps and provides a roadmap toward more trustworthy embodied navigation systems. Furthermore, we systematically evaluate four distinct mitigation strategies to enhance robustness against RGB-Depth and instructions corruptions. Our base models include Uni-NaVid and ETPNav. We deployed them on a real mobile robot and observed improved robustness to corruptions. The project website is: https://navtrust.github.io.

AI.tldr: NavTrust introduces a unified, realistic corruption benchmark for embodied navigation (VLN and OGN), reveals robustness gaps across seven SOTA methods, proposes four mitigation strategies, and validates improvements on a real mobile robot.
AI.motivation: Real-world perception and instruction inputs are prone to corruption, yet existing benchmarks largely ignore such degradations. A unified, realistic benchmark is needed to quantify robustness and guide improvements for trustworthy embodied navigation.
AI.method: Design NavTrust to systematically corrupt input modalities (RGB, depth, and instructions) under realistic scenarios in a unified framework. Evaluate seven SOTA embodied navigation methods (including Uni-NaVid and ETPNav), test four mitigation strategies, and validate results on a real mobile robot. A project website is provided for replication.
AI.result: Seven SOTA approaches suffer substantial performance degradation under realistic corruptions, revealing important robustness gaps. Four mitigation strategies moderately to substantially improve robustness to RGB-Depth and instruction corruptions. Base models Uni-NaVid and ETPNav were deployed on a real robot with observed improved robustness.
AI.conclusion: NavTrust fills a key gap by providing a unified, realistic corruption benchmark for embodied navigation, exposing robustness weaknesses and offering practical mitigation pathways, with real-world validation and a clear roadmap toward more trustworthy navigation systems.

title: HiMu: Hierarchical Multimodal Frame Selection for Long Video Question Answering
summary: Long-form video question answering requires reasoning over extended temporal contexts, making frame selection critical for large vision-language models (LVLMs) bound by finite context windows. Existing methods face a sharp trade-off: similarity-based selectors are fast but collapse compositional queries into a single dense vector, losing sub-event ordering and cross-modal bindings; agent-based methods recover this structure through iterative LVLM inference, but at prohibitive cost. We introduce HiMu, a training-free framework that bridges this gap. A single text-only LLM call decomposes the query into a hierarchical logic tree whose leaves are atomic predicates, each routed to a lightweight expert spanning vision (CLIP, open-vocabulary detection, OCR) and audio (ASR, CLAP). The resulting signals are normalized, temporally smoothed to align different modalities, and composed bottom-up through fuzzy-logic operators that enforce temporal sequencing and adjacency, producing a continuous satisfaction curve. Evaluations on Video-MME, LongVideoBench and HERBench-Lite show that HiMu advances the efficiency-accuracy Pareto front: at 16 frames with Qwen3-VL 8B it outperforms all competing selectors, and with GPT-4o it surpasses agentic systems operating at 32-512 frames while requiring roughly 10x fewer FLOPs.

AI.tldr: HiMu is a training-free multi-modality frame selector for long-form VQA that uses an LLM-driven hierarchical logic decomposition and lightweight vision/audio experts to produce a continuous satisfaction curve, improving efficiency-accuracy trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Long-form video QA requires reasoning over extended temporal contexts; traditional similarity-based selectors compress queries and lose sub-event ordering, while agent-based methods are accurate but computationally expensive. A scalable, training-free approach is needed to bridge this gap.
AI.method: A single text-only LLM call decomposes the query into a hierarchical logic tree. Leaves are atomic predicates routed to lightweight experts spanning vision (CLIP, open-vocabulary detection, OCR) and audio (ASR, CLAP). Signals are normalized, temporally smoothed, and composed bottom-up via fuzzy-logic operators enforcing temporal sequencing and adjacency to yield a continuous satisfaction curve.
AI.result: On Video-MME, LongVideoBench, and HERBench-Lite, HiMu advances the efficiency-accuracy Pareto front: at 16 frames with Qwen3-VL 8B it outperforms all competing selectors; with GPT-4o it surpasses agentic systems operating at 32–512 frames while requiring roughly 10x fewer FLOPs.
AI.conclusion: HiMu effectively bridges the gap between fast similarity-based selectors and costly agent-based reasoning, delivering accurate long-form VQA with improved efficiency and scalability and a strong empirical Pareto improvement across benchmarks.

title: Balancing the Reasoning Load: Difficulty-Differentiated Policy Optimization with Length Redistribution for Efficient and Robust Reinforcement Learning
summary:
Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have shown exceptional reasoning capabilities, but they also suffer from the issue of overthinking, often generating excessively long and redundant answers.
  For problems that exceed the model's capabilities, LRMs tend to exhibit the overconfidence phenomenon, generating overly short but incorrect answers, which may contribute to suboptimal performance.
  To address these issues, we propose Difficulty-Differentiated Policy Optimization (DDPO), an efficient reinforcement learning algorithm that optimizes simple and complex tasks separately based on the overconfidence phenomenon.
  Specifically, it reduces the output length for simple tasks without compromising accuracy, while for complex tasks, it expands the exploration space to improve performance. We further derive the theoretical conditions for maximizing expected accuracy, which require the length distribution to closely approximate the optimal length and be as concentrated as possible. Based on these conditions, we propose using the difficulty-level average as a well-founded reference for length optimization.
  Extensive experiments on both in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks validate the superiority and effectiveness of DDPO. Compared to GRPO, DDPO reduces the average answer length by 12% while improving accuracy by 1.85% across multiple benchmarks, achieving a better trade-off between accuracy and length. The code is available at https://github.com/Yinan-Xia/DDPO.

AI.tldr: DDPO introduces a difficulty-differentiated policy optimization strategy for large reasoning models, shortening simple-task outputs while expanding exploration for complex tasks. It provides theoretical conditions for optimal length distribution, uses the difficulty-average as a reference, and shows improved accuracy with shorter outputs (−12% length, +1.85% accuracy) vs GRPO across in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks. Code available at GitHub.
AI.motivation: LRMs suffer from overthinking (excessively long answers) and overconfidence on hard problems (short but incorrect answers). A policy that differentiates tasks by difficulty could optimize length-accuracy trade-offs more effectively.
AI.method: DDPO optimizes simple and complex tasks separately within a reinforcement learning framework. It aims to reduce output length for simple tasks without losing accuracy, while expanding exploration for complex tasks. Theoretical conditions for maximizing expected accuracy are derived, requiring the length distribution to be near-optimal and concentrated. The difficulty-level average is proposed as a reference for length optimization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on both in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks show DDPO outperforms GRPO, reducing average answer length by 12% and increasing accuracy by 1.85% across multiple benchmarks, indicating a better accuracy-length trade-off. Code: GitHub link provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: DDPO effectively balances brevity and correctness by differentiating task difficulty, supported by theoretical length-distribution conditions and empirical gains. The approach offers a principled path to mitigate overthinking and overconfidence in LRMs.

title: Proceedings of the 2nd Workshop on Advancing Artificial Intelligence through Theory of Mind
comment: workshop proceedings. contains arXiv:2601.03389, arXiv:2511.15895, arXiv:2512.23482, arXiv:2601.01599
summary: This volume includes a selection of papers presented at the 2nd Workshop on Advancing Artificial Intelligence through Theory of Mind held at AAAI 2026 in Singapore on 26th January 2026. The purpose of this volume is to provide an open access and curated anthology for the ToM and AI research community.

AI.tldr: Open-access, curated anthology of ToM-and-AI workshop papers from AAAI 2026.
AI.motivation: Advance the study of theory of mind in AI and provide accessible dissemination for the ToM-AI research community.
AI.method: Editorial selection and compilation of papers presented at the 2nd Workshop on Advancing Artificial Intelligence through Theory of Mind at AAAI 2026; open-access volume.
AI.result: A published open-access collection that curates workshop papers, serving as a resource for researchers.
AI.conclusion: The volume supports ongoing ToM-AI research and community building by providing an accessible, curated anthology.

title: Not All Features Are Created Equal: A Mechanistic Study of Vision-Language-Action Models
comment: Accepted to Multimodal Intelligence Workshop @ ICLR
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models combine perception, language, and motor control in a single architecture, yet how they translate multimodal inputs into actions remains poorly understood. We apply activation injection, sparse autoencoders (SAEs), and linear probes to six models spanning 80M--7B parameters across 394,000+ rollout episodes on four benchmarks. The visual pathway dominates action generation across all architectures: injecting baseline activations into null-prompt episodes recovers near-identical behavior, while cross-task injection steers robots toward source-task positions (99.8\% of X-VLA episodes align with the source trajectory), exposing spatially bound motor programs tied to scene coordinates rather than abstract task representations. Language sensitivity depends on task structure, not model design: when visual context uniquely specifies the task, language is ignored; when multiple goals share a scene, language becomes essential (X-VLA \texttt{libero\_goal}: 94\%$\to$10\% under wrong prompts vs.\ \texttt{libero\_object}: 60--100\% regardless). In all three multi-pathway architectures (\pizhalf{}, SmolVLA, GR00T), expert pathways encode motor programs while VLM pathways encode goal semantics ($2\times$ greater behavioral displacement from expert injection), and subspace injection confirms these occupy separable activation subspaces. Per-token SAE processing is essential for action fidelity on most architectures, though mean-pooling improves fidelity on X-VLA. Contrastive identification recovers 82+ manipulation concepts, and causal ablation reveals sensitivity spanning 28--92\% zero-effect rates independent of representation width. We release \textbf{Action Atlas} (https://action-atlas.com) for interactive exploration of VLA representations across all six models.

AI.tldr: Visual pathways dominate action in Vision-Language-Action models; activation injections and sparse autoencoders reveal separable motor-program vs goal-semantic representations across six models (80M–7B) with 394k+ rollouts; language usage hinges on task structure; Action Atlas released.
AI.motivation: To elucidate how multimodal inputs are transformed into motor actions in VLA systems and how different architectural pathways contribute to action vs semantic goals.
AI.method: Activation injection, sparse autoencoders, and linear probes applied to six VLA models across 394k+ rollouts on four benchmarks; cross-task injections; per-token SAE processing; mean-pooling; contrastive identification; causal ablation; evaluation across three multi-pathway architectures (pizhalf, SmolVLA, GR00T).
AI.result: Visual pathway dominates action generation; injecting baseline activations recovers near-identical behavior; cross-task injections align with source trajectories; language sensitivity linked to task structure; expert motor pathways encode motor programs while VLM pathways encode goal semantics; per-token SAE processing essential; mean-pooling helps in X-VLA; contrastive identification recovers 82+ manipulation concepts; causal ablation yields 28–92% zero-effect rates; subspaces for motor vs semantic representations are separable; Action Atlas released.
AI.conclusion: Findings indicate separable activation subspaces for motor programs and goal semantics across diverse VLA architectures; motor control is scene-bound and largely driven by visual inputs, while language use depends on task structure; results support targeted probing/injection methods and provide an interactive resource (Action Atlas) for exploring VLA representations.

title: CausalVAD: De-confounding End-to-End Autonomous Driving via Causal Intervention
summary: Planning-oriented end-to-end driving models show great promise, yet they fundamentally learn statistical correlations instead of true causal relationships. This vulnerability leads to causal confusion, where models exploit dataset biases as shortcuts, critically harming their reliability and safety in complex scenarios. To address this, we introduce CausalVAD, a de-confounding training framework that leverages causal intervention. At its core, we design the sparse causal intervention scheme (SCIS), a lightweight, plug-and-play module to instantiate the backdoor adjustment theory in neural networks. SCIS constructs a dictionary of prototypes representing latent driving contexts. It then uses this dictionary to intervene on the model's sparse vectorized queries. This step actively eliminates spurious associations induced by confounders, thereby eliminating spurious factors from the representations for downstream tasks. Extensive experiments on benchmarks like nuScenes show CausalVAD achieves state-of-the-art planning accuracy and safety. Furthermore, our method demonstrates superior robustness against both data bias and noisy scenarios configured to induce causal confusion.

AI.tldr: Introduces CausalVAD to de-confound planning-oriented driving models using a plug-and-play SCIS module that implements backdoor adjustment with a prototype dictionary to intervene on latent representations, achieving state-of-the-art planning performance and robustness against causal confounding.
AI.motivation: Addresses causal confounding in planning-based end-to-end driving by moving beyond correlation-based learning to remove spurious associations caused by confounders, improving reliability and safety.
AI.method: CausalVAD framework; SCIS, a sparse intervention module, builds a dictionary of latent-context prototypes; applies backdoor-adjustment to intervene on the model’s sparse queries to purge confounding factors; designed as a plug-and-play component for neural planners.
AI.result: On benchmarks such as nuScenes, CausalVAD achieves state-of-the-art planning accuracy and safety, with superior robustness against data bias and noisy scenarios intended to induce causal confusion.
AI.conclusion: CausalVAD with SCIS effectively mitigates causal confounding in planning models for autonomous driving, improving reliability and safety, and showing potential generalization to other planning tasks that rely on latent context representations.

title: Data-efficient pre-training by scaling synthetic megadocs
summary: Synthetic data augmentation has emerged as a promising solution when pre-training is constrained by data rather than compute. We study how to design synthetic data algorithms that achieve better loss scaling: not only lowering loss at finite compute but especially as compute approaches infinity. We first show that pre-training on web data mixed with synthetically generated rephrases improves i.i.d. validation loss on the web data, despite the synthetic data coming from an entirely different distribution. With optimal mixing and epoching, loss and benchmark accuracy improve without overfitting as the number of synthetic generations grows, plateauing near $1.48\times$ data efficiency at 32 rephrases per document. We find even better loss scaling under a new perspective: synthetic generations from the same document can form a single substantially longer megadocument instead of many short documents. We show two ways to construct megadocs: stitching synthetic rephrases from the same web document or stretching a document by inserting rationales. Both methods improve i.i.d. loss, downstream benchmarks, and especially long-context loss relative to simple rephrasing, increasing data efficiency from $1.48\times$ to $1.80\times$ at $32$ generations per document. Importantly, the improvement of megadocs over simple rephrasing widens as more synthetic data is generated. Our results show how to design synthetic data algorithms that benefit more from increasing compute when data-constrained.

AI.tldr: Synthetic data augmentation with rephrases and megadocs improves loss scaling and data efficiency, especially under data-constrained pre-training, with megadocs outperforming simple rephrasing as compute/data grows.
AI.motivation: To maximize pre-training effectiveness when data is the limiting resource and compute is abundant; investigate how synthetic data design influences loss scaling and downstream performance.
AI.method: Systematically mix web data with synthetically generated rephrases; optimize mixing/epoching; introduce megadocs by stitching rephrases or inserting rationales to stretch documents; evaluate on i.i.d. loss and benchmarks, including long-context loss.
AI.result: Loss and benchmark accuracy improve with synthetic data; no overfitting as generations increase; data efficiency improves from 1.48x (32 generations) to 1.80x with megadocs; improvements magnify with more synthetic data; synthetic data from same document to megadoc leads to superior loss scaling, especially for long-context tasks.
AI.conclusion: Careful synthetic data design, especially megadoc constructions, can yield better loss scaling and data efficiency, enabling stronger gains when data is scarce and compute grows.

title: dTRPO: Trajectory Reduction in Policy Optimization of Diffusion Large Language Models
summary: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) introduce a new paradigm for language generation, which in turn presents new challenges for aligning them with human preferences. In this work, we aim to improve the policy optimization for dLLMs by reducing the cost of the trajectory probability calculation, thereby enabling scaled-up offline policy training. We prove that: (i) under reference policy regularization, the probability ratio of the newly unmasked tokens is an unbiased estimate of that of intermediate diffusion states, and (ii) the probability of the full trajectory can be effectively estimated with a single forward pass of a re-masked final state. By integrating these two trajectory reduction strategies into a policy optimization objective, we propose Trajectory Reduction Policy Optimization (dTRPO). We evaluate dTRPO on 7B dLLMs across instruction-following and reasoning benchmarks. Results show that it substantially improves the core performance of state-of-the-art dLLMs, achieving gains of up to 9.6% on STEM tasks, up to 4.3% on coding tasks, and up to 3.0% on instruction-following tasks. Moreover, dTRPO exhibits strong training efficiency due to its offline, single-forward nature, and achieves improved generation efficiency through high-quality outputs.

AI.tldr: Introduces dTRPO, an offline policy optimization method for diffusion LLMs that reduces trajectory probability computations via two reductions, yielding notable performance gains and improved efficiency.
AI.motivation: Diffusion LLMs pose alignment challenges and high computation in trajectory probability; offline training scalability requires reducing trajectory calculations.
AI.method: Proposes Trajectory Reduction Policy Optimization (dTRPO) by integrating two trajectory-reduction strategies: (i) under reference policy regularization, the new-token probability ratio unbiased for intermediate diffusion states; (ii) full-trajectory probability estimated by a single forward pass of a re-masked final state; combined into a policy optimization objective.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 7B dLLMs across STEM, coding, and instruction-following benchmarks shows gains up to 9.6% (STEM), 4.3% (coding), 3.0% (instruction-following). Offline, single-forward training improves efficiency and generation quality.
AI.conclusion: dTRPO substantially improves core performance of state-of-the-art dLLMs and enables scalable offline policy training with efficient generation.

title: HAViT: Historical Attention Vision Transformer
summary: Vision Transformers have excelled in computer vision but their attention mechanisms operate independently across layers, limiting information flow and feature learning. We propose an effective cross-layer attention propagation method that preserves and integrates historical attention matrices across encoder layers, offering a principled refinement of inter-layer information flow in Vision Transformers. This approach enables progressive refinement of attention patterns throughout the transformer hierarchy, enhancing feature acquisition and optimization dynamics. The method requires minimal architectural changes, adding only attention matrix storage and blending operations. Comprehensive experiments on CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet demonstrate consistent accuracy improvements, with ViT performance increasing from 75.74% to 77.07% on CIFAR-100 (+1.33%) and from 57.82% to 59.07% on TinyImageNet (+1.25%). Cross-architecture validation shows similar gains across transformer variants, with CaiT showing 1.01% enhancement. Systematic analysis identifies the blending hyperparameter of historical attention (alpha = 0.45) as optimal across all configurations, providing the ideal balance between current and historical attention information.
