Random initialization consistently outperforms zero initialization, indicating that diverse initial attention patterns accelerate convergence and improve final performance. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/banik-s/HAViT.

AI.tldr: Cross-layer attention propagation for ViTs that preserves and blends historical attention across encoder layers, enabling progressive refinement of inter-layer information flow with minimal architectural changes; yields consistent accuracy gains across CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, and CaiT; optimal blending alpha ~0.45; random initialization preferred; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of ViTs where attention maps are computed independently per layer, potentially hindering information flow and long-range dependency modeling across the transformer hierarchy.
AI.method: Store and blend historical attention matrices across encoder layers (cross-layer attention propagation). Use a blending parameter alpha to mix current and historical attention. Minimal changes: storage for past attention and blending operations during forward passes.
AI.result: Consistent accuracy improvements on CIFAR-100 (75.74% -> 77.07%), TinyImageNet (57.82% -> 59.07%). CaiT shows ~1.01% gain. Optimal alpha for blending found to be 0.45. Random initialization of attention outperforms zero initialization. Code available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Cross-layer attention propagation offers a principled, architecture-agnostic enhancement for ViTs, improving information flow and optimization dynamics with minimal modification and broad applicability across transformer variants.

title: Beyond Passive Aggregation: Active Auditing and Topology-Aware Defense in Decentralized Federated Learning
summary: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) remains highly vulnerable to adaptive backdoor attacks designed to bypass traditional passive defense metrics. To address this limitation, we shift the defensive paradigm toward a novel active, interventional auditing framework. First, we establish a dynamical model to characterize the spatiotemporal diffusion of adversarial updates across complex graph topologies. Second, we introduce a suite of proactive auditing metrics, stochastic entropy anomaly, randomized smoothing Kullback-Leibler divergence, and activation kurtosis. These metrics utilize private probes to stress-test local models, effectively exposing latent backdoors that remain invisible to conventional static detection. Furthermore, we implement a topology-aware defense placement strategy to maximize global aggregation resilience. We provide theoretical property for the system's convergence under co-evolving attack and defense dynamics. Numeric empirical evaluations across diverse architectures demonstrate that our active framework is highly competitive with state-of-the-art defenses in mitigating stealthy, adaptive backdoors while preserving primary task utility.

AI.tldr: Proposes an active interventional auditing framework for Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) to detect and mitigate adaptive backdoors. It models the diffusion of adversarial updates on graph topologies, introduces proactive probes and metrics, and uses topology-aware defense placement, with convergence guarantees and empirical validation showing competitive robustness while preserving task performance.
AI.motivation: DFL is highly vulnerable to adaptive backdoor attacks that evade passive defenses. There is a need for proactive, interventional auditing that can reveal latent backdoors and maintain model utility.
AI.method: Develop a dynamical model of spatiotemporal diffusion of adversarial updates across graph topologies; introduce proactive auditing metrics (stochastic entropy anomaly, randomized smoothing KL divergence, activation kurtosis) using private probes to stress-test local models; implement topology-aware defense placement to bolster global resilience; provide theoretical convergence properties under co-evolving attack and defense dynamics.
AI.result: The framework yields theoretical convergence guarantees and empirical evidence across diverse architectures showing competitive performance with state-of-the-art defenses in mitigating stealthy, adaptive backdoors while preserving primary task utility.
AI.conclusion: An active auditing framework improves detection and mitigation of stealthy backdoors in DFL, with topology-aware defense placement enhancing resilience and maintained task performance.

title: Can LLM generate interesting mathematical research problems?
summary: This paper is the second one in a series of work on the mathematical creativity of LLM. In the first paper, the authors proposed three criteria for evaluating the mathematical creativity of LLM and constructed a benchmark dataset to measure it. This paper further explores the mathematical creativity of LLM, with a focus on investigating whether LLM can generate valuable and cutting-edge mathematical research problems. We develop an agent to generate unknown problems and produced 665 research problems in differential geometry. Through human verification, we find that many of these mathematical problems are unknown to experts and possess unique research value.

AI.tldr: An LLM-driven agent generates 665 differential geometry research problems; many are novel and valued by experts.
AI.motivation: Extend evaluation of LLM mathematical creativity by enabling generation of valuable, cutting-edge research problems and validating the proposal of a dedicated benchmark.
AI.method: Build an autonomous agent to generate unknown mathematical problems and apply it to differential geometry, yielding 665 candidate problems; assess novelty/value via human expert verification.
AI.result: Generated 665 differential geometry problems; many are unfamiliar to experts yet have unique research value.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can propose potentially valuable new research problems in mathematics, supporting the approach of automated problem generation and highlighting the need for human verification.

title: Color image restoration based on nonlocal saturation-value similarity
summary: In this paper, we propose and develop a novel nonlocal variational technique based on saturation-value similarity for color image restoration. In traditional nonlocal methods, image patches are extracted from red, green and blue channels of a color image directly, and the color information can not be described finely because the patch similarity is mainly based on the grayscale value of independent channel. The main aim of this paper is to propose and develop a novel nonlocal regularization method by considering the similarity of image patches in saturation-value channel of a color image. In particular, we first establish saturation-value similarity based nonlocal total variation by incorporating saturation-value similarity of color image patches into the proposed nonlocal gradients, which can describe the saturation and value similarity of two adjacent color image patches. The proposed nonlocal variational models are then formulated based on saturation-value similarity based nonlocal total variation. Moreover, we design an effective and efficient algorithm to solve the proposed optimization problem numerically by employing bregmanized operator splitting method, and we also study the convergence of the proposed algorithms. Numerical examples are presented to demonstrate that the performance of the proposed models is better than that of other testing methods in terms of visual quality and some quantitative metrics including peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), structural similarity index (SSIM), quaternion structural similarity index (QSSIM) and S-CIELAB color error.

AI.tldr: Introduces a saturation-value-based nonlocal total variation model for color image restoration, improving color-aware patch similarity and achieving better denoising results with a Bregmanized operator splitting solver.
AI.motivation: Standard nonlocal approaches compare patches via grayscale channels, which inadequately captures color similarity; need a color-aware similarity measure in the saturation-value space to preserve color fidelity across patches.
AI.method: Define saturation-value similarity, integrate into nonlocal gradients to form a saturation-value based nonlocal total variation; formulate a variational restoration model; apply Bregmanized operator splitting (BOS) to solve; analyze convergence.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show superior performance over competing methods in visual quality and metrics such as PSNR, SSIM, QSSIM, and S-CIELAB color error.
AI.conclusion: Saturation-value similarity effectively captures color patch similarity for nonlocal TV restoration, yielding improved results and a computationally efficient solver with guaranteed convergence.

title: GAPSL: A Gradient-Aligned Parallel Split Learning on Heterogeneous Data
summary: The increasing complexity of neural networks poses significant challenges for democratizing FL on resource?constrained client devices. Parallel split learning (PSL) has emerged as a promising solution by offloading substantial computing workload to a server via model partitioning, shrinking client-side computing load, and eliminating the client-side model aggregation for reduced communication and deployment costs. Since PSL is aggregation-free, it suffers from severe training divergence stemming from gradient directional inconsistency across clients. To address this challenge, we propose GAPSL, a gradient-aligned PSL framework that comprises two key components: leader gradient identification (LGI) and gradient direction alignment (GDA). LGI dynamically selects a set of directionally consistent client gradients to construct a leader gradient that captures the global convergence trend. GDA employs a direction-aware regularization to align each client's gradient with the leader gradient, thereby mitigating inter-device gradient directional inconsistency and enhancing model convergence. We evaluate GAPSL on a prototype computing testbed. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GAPSL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks in training accuracy and latency.

AI.tldr: GAPSL improves aggregation-free parallel split learning by aligning client gradients to a dynamically identified leader gradient, using LGI for leader selection and GDA for gradient direction regularization, yielding better accuracy and latency on resource-constrained FL.
AI.motivation: Democratize federated learning on resource-constrained devices by offloading computation to a server via parallel split learning, but aggregation-free PSL suffers from gradient directional inconsistency which can cause training divergence.
AI.method: Introduce GAPSL with two components: (1) leader gradient identification (LGI) which dynamically selects directionally consistent client gradients to form a leader gradient capturing global convergence trends; (2) gradient direction alignment (GDA) which applies a direction-aware regularization to align each client's gradient with the leader gradient, mitigating directional inconsistency.
AI.result: Evaluated on a prototype computing testbed; experiments show GAPSL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art benchmarks in training accuracy and latency.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-aligned PSL via LGI and GDA alleviates inter-device gradient directional mismatch, improving convergence and efficiency for resource-constrained FL deployments.

title: ProRL Agent: Rollout-as-a-Service for RL Training of Multi-Turn LLM Agents
summary: Multi-turn LLM agents are increasingly important for solving complex, interactive tasks, and reinforcement learning (RL) is a key ingredient for improving their long-horizon behavior. However, RL training requires generating large numbers of sandboxed rollout trajectories, and existing infrastructures often couple rollout orchestration with the training loop, making systems hard to migrate and maintain. Under the rollout-as-a-service philosophy, we present ProRL Agent , a scalable infrastructure that serves the full agentic rollout lifecycle through an API service. ProRL Agent also provides standardized and extensible sandbox environments that support diverse agentic tasks in rootless HPC settings. We validate ProRL Agent through RL training on software engineering, math, STEM, and coding tasks. ProRL Agent is open-sourced and integrated as part of NVIDIA NeMo Gym.

AI.tldr: Rollout-as-a-service for RL training of multi-turn LLM agents; ProRL Agent provides a scalable API-driven rollout lifecycle and rootless HPC sandbox environments; validated on software engineering, math, STEM, coding tasks; open-sourced in NVIDIA NeMo Gym.
AI.motivation: To address the demand for large numbers of sandboxed rollout trajectories in reinforcement learning for long-horizon, interactive LLM agents, and to decouple rollout orchestration from training to improve portability and maintainability; introduces a rollout-as-a-service paradigm.
AI.method: Develop ProRL Agent: an API-driven infrastructure that orchestrates the full agentic rollout lifecycle; provides standardized and extensible sandbox environments designed for rootless HPC settings; integrates with NVIDIA NeMo Gym; is open-source.
AI.result: Empirically validated by RL training across software engineering, mathematics, STEM, and coding tasks; demonstrates scalability and applicability; ProRL Agent is open-source and integrated into NVIDIA NeMo Gym.
AI.conclusion: ProRL Agent provides a scalable rollout-as-a-service solution for multi-turn LLM agent RL, enabling reproducible experiments and easier migration; available as open-source in NVIDIA NeMo Gym.

title: AU Codes, Language, and Synthesis: Translating Anatomy to Text for Facial Behavior Synthesis
summary: Facial behavior synthesis remains a critical yet underexplored challenge. While text-to-face models have made progress, they often rely on coarse emotion categories, which lack the nuance needed to capture the full spectrum of human nonverbal communication. Action Units (AUs) provide a more precise and anatomically grounded alternative. However, current AU-based approaches typically encode AUs as one-hot vectors, modeling compound expressions as simple linear combinations of individual AUs. This linearity becomes problematic when handling conflicting AUs--defined as those which activate the same facial muscle with opposing actions. Such cases lead to anatomically implausible artifacts and unnatural motion superpositions. To address this, we propose a novel method that represents facial behavior through natural language descriptions of AUs. This approach preserves the expressiveness of the AU framework while enabling explicit modeling of complex and conflicting AUs. It also unlocks the potential of modern text-to-image models for high-fidelity facial synthesis. Supporting this direction, we introduce BP4D-AUText, the first large-scale text-image paired dataset for complex facial behavior. It is synthesized by applying a rule-based Dynamic AU Text Processor to the BP4D and BP4D+ datasets. We further propose VQ-AUFace, a generative model that leverages facial structural priors to synthesize realistic and diverse facial behaviors from text. Extensive quantitative experiments and user studies demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods. It excels in generating facial expressions that are anatomically plausible, behaviorally rich, and perceptually convincing, particularly under challenging conditions involving conflicting AUs.

AI.tldr: This work reframes facial behavior using natural-language descriptions of Action Units (AUs), introducing BP4D-AUText and VQ-AUFace. It achieves higher anatomical plausibility and perceptual realism than one-hot AU methods, especially for conflicting AUs.
AI.motivation: AU encodings as one-hot or linear combinations inadequately capture nuanced, conflicting facial actions and rely on coarse emotion categories. A linguistically expressive, anatomically grounded representation can improve fidelity and leverage modern text-to-image models.
AI.method: Represent AUs as natural language phrases. Develop a Dynamic AU Text Processor to convert BP4D/BP4D+ annotations into BP4D-AUText. Propose VQ-AUFace, a generative model using facial structural priors to synthesize diverse facial behaviors from text. Evaluate with quantitative metrics and user studies.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and user studies show superior performance over baseline AU methods, with increased anatomical plausibility, richer behavioral expressions, and perceptual quality, particularly under conflicting AUs.
AI.conclusion: Natural-language AU representations enable expressive, high-fidelity facial synthesis and are compatible with modern text-to-image models. The BP4D-AUText dataset and VQ-AUFace model provide resources for future research and applications.

title: HEP Statistical Inference for UAV Fault Detection: CLs, LRT, and SBI Applied to Blade Damage
summary: This paper transfers three statistical methods from particle physics to multirotor propeller fault detection: the likelihood ratio test (LRT) for binary detection, the CLs modified frequentist method for false alarm rate control, and sequential neural posterior estimation (SNPE) for quantitative fault characterization. Operating on spectral features tied to rotor harmonic physics, the system returns three outputs: binary detection, controlled false alarm rates, and calibrated posteriors over fault severity and motor location. On UAV-FD, a hexarotor dataset of 18 real flights with 5% and 10% blade damage, leave-one-flight-out cross-validation gives AUC 0.862 +/- 0.007 (95% CI: 0.849--0.876), outperforming CUSUM (0.708 +/- 0.010), autoencoder (0.753 +/- 0.009), and LSTM autoencoder (0.551). At 5% false alarm rate the system detects 93% of significant and 81% of subtle blade damage. On PADRE, a quadrotor platform, AUC reaches 0.986 after refitting only the generative models. SNPE gives a full posterior over fault severity (90% credible interval coverage 92--100%, MAE 0.012), so the output includes uncertainty rather than just a point estimate or fault flag. Per-flight sequential detection achieves 100% fault detection with 94% overall accuracy.

AI.tldr: Cross-domain transfer of LRT, CLs, and SNPE to UAV propeller fault detection, delivering binary detection, controlled false-alarm rates, and calibrated posteriors for fault severity and motor location with strong AUCs and per-flight sequential detection.
AI.motivation: Leverage robust, uncertainty-aware statistical tools from particle physics to improve binary fault detection, false-alarm control, and fault characterization in multirotor propellers using physics-informed spectral features.
AI.method: Adapt likelihood ratio test (LRT) for binary detection, apply the CLs method for false-alarm-rate control, and use sequential neural posterior estimation (SNPE) to obtain calibrated posteriors over fault severity and motor location. Features are spectral components tied to rotor harmonics. Evaluated on UAV-FD and PADRE platforms, with per-flight sequential detection.
AI.result: UAV-FD: leave-one-flight-out AUC = 0.862 ± 0.007 (95% CI: 0.849–0.876); benchmarks: CUSUM 0.708 ± 0.010, autoencoder 0.753 ± 0.009, LSTM autoencoder 0.551. At 5% FPR, detects 93% of significant and 81% of subtle blade damage. PADRE: AUC = 0.986 after refitting only generative models. SNPE yields full posterior over fault severity (90% credible interval coverage 92–100%; MAE = 0.012). Per-flight sequential detection achieves 100% fault detection with 94% overall accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides uncertainty-aware, calibrated fault detection with controlled false alarms and strong generalization across platforms, highlighting the value of transferring particle-physics statistical tools to UAV fault diagnosis and enabling sequential monitoring.

title: RewardFlow: Topology-Aware Reward Propagation on State Graphs for Agentic RL with Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) holds significant promise for enhancing the agentic reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) with external environments. However, the inherent sparsity of terminal rewards hinders fine-grained, state-level optimization. Although process reward modeling offers a promising alternative, training dedicated reward models often entails substantial computational costs and scaling difficulties. To address these challenges, we introduce RewardFlow, a lightweight method for estimating state-level rewards tailored to agentic reasoning tasks. RewardFlow leverages the intrinsic topological structure of states within reasoning trajectories by constructing state graphs. This enables an analysis of state-wise contributions to success, followed by topology-aware graph propagation to quantify contributions and yield objective, state-level rewards. When integrated as dense rewards for RL optimization, RewardFlow substantially outperforms prior RL baselines across four agentic reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating superior performance, robustness, and training efficiency. The implementation of RewardFlow is publicly available at https://github.com/tmlr-group/RewardFlow.

AI.tldr: RewardFlow presents a lightweight, topology-aware method to estimate state-level rewards from reasoning trajectories via state graphs and graph propagation, improving RL for agentic reasoning tasks with better efficiency.
AI.motivation: To overcome sparse terminal rewards and the high cost of training dedicated reward models in RL for LLM-enabled agentic reasoning.
AI.method: Construct state graphs from reasoning trajectories; analyze state-wise contributions to success; perform topology-aware graph propagation to estimate per-state contributions and generate dense rewards; use these rewards to train RL policies.
AI.result: When used as dense rewards, RewardFlow outperforms prior RL baselines on four agentic reasoning benchmarks, showing improved performance, robustness, and training efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Topology-informed state-reward estimation is an effective, scalable approach for improving RL in agentic reasoning with LLMs, reducing reliance on expensive reward models.

title: myMNIST: Benchmark of PETNN, KAN, and Classical Deep Learning Models for Burmese Handwritten Digit Recognition
comment: 7 pages, 2 figures, 3 tables, Accepted to ICNLP 2026, Xi'an, China
summary: We present the first systematic benchmark on myMNIST (formerly BHDD), a publicly available Burmese handwritten digit dataset important for Myanmar NLP/AI research. We evaluate eleven architectures spanning classical deep learning models (Multi-Layer Perceptron, Convolutional Neural Network, Long Short-Term Memory, Gated Recurrent Unit, Transformer), recent alternatives (FastKAN, EfficientKAN), an energy-based model (JEM), and physics-inspired PETNN variants (Sigmoid, GELU, SiLU). Using Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy as evaluation metrics, our results show that the CNN remains a strong baseline, achieving the best overall scores (F1 = 0.9959, Accuracy = 0.9970). The PETNN (GELU) model closely follows (F1 = 0.9955, Accuracy = 0.9966), outperforming LSTM, GRU, Transformer, and KAN variants. JEM, representing energy-based modeling, performs competitively (F1 = 0.9944, Accuracy = 0.9958). KAN-based models (FastKAN, EfficientKAN) trail the top performers but provide a meaningful alternative baseline (Accuracy ~0.992). These findings (i) establish reproducible baselines for myMNIST across diverse modeling paradigms, (ii) highlight PETNN's strong performance relative to classical and Transformer-based models, and (iii) quantify the gap between energy-inspired PETNNs and a true energy-based model (JEM). We release this benchmark to facilitate future research on Myanmar digit recognition and to encourage broader evaluation of emerging architectures on regional scripts.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive benchmark of myMNIST across 11 architectures, establishing reproducible baselines and highlighting PETNN's strong performance; CNN leads overall, GELU-PETNN closely follows, energy-based JEM competitive, and KAN variants weaker.
AI.motivation: To systematically compare diverse modeling paradigms on a region-specific script dataset (myMNIST) and quantify performance gaps, enabling robust baseline establishment and evaluation of emerging architectures.
AI.method: Evaluate eleven models on myMNIST: MLP, CNN, LSTM, GRU, Transformer, FastKAN, EfficientKAN, JEM, PETNN variants with Sigmoid, GELU, SiLU; use Precision, Recall, F1, Accuracy; report per-model metrics and comparisons.
AI.result: CNN achieves best overall: F1 0.9959, Accuracy 0.9970. PETNN-GELU close: F1 0.9955, Acc 0.9966. JEM F1 0.9944, Acc 0.9958. KAN variants ~0.992 accuracy. PETNN shows strong performance; JEM competitively close; KAN variants trail; establishes baselines and quantifies energy-based vs PETNN gap.
AI.conclusion: Provides reproducible baselines across diverse architectures for Myanmar digit recognition; PETNN is competitive with classical/Transformer models; quantifies gap between energy-inspired PETNNs and a true energy-based model; encourages broader evaluation on regional scripts.

title: SINDy-KANs: Sparse identification of non-linear dynamics through Kolmogorov-Arnold networks
summary: Kolmogorov-Arnold networks (KANs) have arisen as a potential way to enhance the interpretability of machine learning. However, solutions learned by KANs are not necessarily interpretable, in the sense of being sparse or parsimonious. Sparse identification of nonlinear dynamics (SINDy) is a complementary approach that allows for learning sparse equations for dynamical systems from data; however, learned equations are limited by the library. In this work, we present SINDy-KANs, which simultaneously train a KAN and a SINDy-like representation to increase interpretability of KAN representations with SINDy applied at the level of each activation function, while maintaining the function compositions possible through deep KANs. We apply our method to a number of symbolic regression tasks, including dynamical systems, to show accurate equation discovery across a range of systems.

AI.tldr: SINDy-KANs jointly train Kolmogorov-Arnold networks with a sparse, SINDy-like representation at the level of each activation, enhancing interpretability while preserving deep compositional structure; demonstrated on symbolic regression and dynamical systems.
AI.motivation: KANs offer interpretable representations but can lack sparsity; SINDy provides sparse dynamical equations but is library-limited; combining them aims to achieve sparse, interpretable activations within deep architectures.
AI.method: Joint optimization of a KAN with a SINDy-like sparse representation inside each activation function, enforcing sparsity and a library of candidate terms per activation; maintain function composition through deep KANs; evaluate on symbolic regression tasks and dynamical systems.
AI.result: Demonstrates accurate equation discovery across diverse systems, indicating improved interpretability without sacrificing expressive power.
AI.conclusion: SINDy-KANs provide a pathway to more interpretable deep models by embedding sparse, equation-discoverable structures at the activation level, applicable to a range of symbolic regression problems and dynamical systems.

title: Conflict-Based Search for Multi Agent Path Finding with Asynchronous Actions
summary: Multi-Agent Path Finding (MAPF) seeks collision-free paths for multiple agents from their respective start locations to their respective goal locations while minimizing path costs. Most existing MAPF algorithms rely on a common assumption of synchronized actions, where the actions of all agents start at the same time and always take a time unit, which may limit the use of MAPF planners in practice. To get rid of this assumption, Continuous-time Conflict-Based Search (CCBS) is a popular approach that can find optimal solutions for MAPF with asynchronous actions (MAPF-AA). However, CCBS has recently been identified to be incomplete due to an uncountably infinite state space created by continuous wait durations. This paper proposes a new method, Conflict-Based Search with Asynchronous Actions (CBS-AA), which bypasses this theoretical issue and can solve MAPF-AA with completeness and solution optimality guarantees. Based on CBS-AA, we also develop conflict resolution techniques to improve the scalability of CBS-AA further. Our test results show that our method can reduce the number of branches by up to 90%.

AI.tldr: CBS-AA offers a complete and optimal solution for MAPF with asynchronous actions by overcoming CCBS's incompleteness; it builds on CBS and adds conflict-resolution techniques to improve scalability, achieving up to a 90% reduction in branches.
AI.motivation: MAPF with asynchronous actions (MAPF-AA) is inadequately handled by CCBS due to an uncountably infinite state space from continuous wait durations, leading to incompleteness. A complete, optimal, scalable approach is needed for practical asynchronous planning.
AI.method: Introduce Conflict-Based Search with Asynchronous Actions (CBS-AA) to bypass the CCBS incompleteness, leveraging the CBS framework and augmenting it with new conflict-resolution techniques to enhance scalability.
AI.result: CBS-AA is complete and optimal for MAPF-AA; when implemented with the proposed conflict-resolution techniques, it reduces search branches by up to 90%.
AI.conclusion: CBS-AA provides theoretical guarantees of completeness and optimality for MAPF-AA and practical gains in scalability through targeted conflict-resolution strategies, making asynchronous MAPF planning more tractable.

title: Complementary Text-Guided Attention for Zero-Shot Adversarial Robustness
comment: Accepted to TPAMI 2026. arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2410.21802
summary: Due to the impressive zero-shot capabilities, pre-trained vision-language models (e.g., CLIP), have attracted widespread attention and adoption across various domains. Nonetheless, CLIP has been observed to be susceptible to adversarial examples. Through experimental analysis, we have observed a phenomenon wherein adversarial perturbations induce shifts in text-guided attention. Building upon this observation, we propose a simple yet effective strategy: Text-Guided Attention for Zero-Shot Robustness (TGA-ZSR). This framework incorporates two components: Local Attention Refinement Module and Global Attention Constraint Module. Our goal is to maintain the generalization of the CLIP model and enhance its adversarial robustness. Additionally, the Global Attention Constraint Module acquires text-guided attention from both the target and original models using clean examples. Its objective is to maintain model performance on clean samples while enhancing overall robustness. However, we observe that the method occasionally focuses on irrelevant or spurious features, which can lead to suboptimal performance and undermine its robustness in certain scenarios. To overcome this limitation, we further propose a novel approach called Complementary Text-Guided Attention (Comp-TGA). This method integrates two types of foreground attention: attention guided by the class prompt and reversed attention driven by the non-class prompt. These complementary attention mechanisms allow the model to capture a more comprehensive and accurate representation of the foreground. The experiments validate that TGA-ZSR and Comp-TGA yield 9.58% and 11.95% improvements respectively, in zero-shot robust accuracy over the current state-of-the-art techniques across 16 datasets.

AI.tldr: Proposes Text-Guided Attention for Zero-shot Robustness (TGA-ZSR) with Local Attention Refinement and Global Attention Constraint modules to enhance CLIP-style zero-shot robustness against adversarial attacks, and introduces Complementary Text-Guided Attention (Comp-TGA) combining class-prompt and non-class prompt guidance, achieving 9.58% and 11.95% gains in zero-shot robust accuracy across 16 datasets.
AI.motivation: CLIP-like vision-language models exhibit excellent zero-shot capabilities but are vulnerable to adversarial perturbations that shift text-guided attention; improving robustness without sacrificing clean performance is essential for reliable deployment across domains.
AI.method: TGA-ZSR comprises two modules: Local Attention Refinement, which improves the fidelity of text-guided attention at a local level, and Global Attention Constraint, which aligns attention using clean examples from both the target and original models. To mitigate misfocus, Comp-TGA introduces complementary attention by fusing foreground attention guided by the class prompt with reversed attention driven by the non-class prompt, yielding a more comprehensive foreground representation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that TGA-ZSR and Comp-TGA achieve 9.58% and 11.95% improvements respectively in zero-shot robust accuracy over the current state-of-the-art on 16 datasets.
AI.conclusion: Text-Guided Attention strategies can boost zero-shot robustness in CLIP-like models. The two-stage TGA-ZSR framework improves robustness while maintaining clean performance, and Comp-TGA further enhances robustness by addressing misfocus via complementary foreground attention. Some scenarios still exhibit reliance on irrelevant features, indicating room for refinement.

title: Transformers Learn Robust In-Context Regression under Distributional Uncertainty
summary: Recent work has shown that Transformers can perform in-context learning for linear regression under restrictive assumptions, including i.i.d. data, Gaussian noise, and Gaussian regression coefficients. However, real-world data often violate these assumptions: the distributions of inputs, noise, and coefficients are typically unknown, non-Gaussian, and may exhibit dependency across the prompt. This raises a fundamental question: can Transformers learn effectively in-context under realistic distributional uncertainty? We study in-context learning for noisy linear regression under a broad range of distributional shifts, including non-Gaussian coefficients, heavy-tailed noise, and non-i.i.d. prompts. We compare Transformers against classical baselines that are optimal or suboptimal under the corresponding maximum-likelihood criteria. Across all settings, Transformers consistently match or outperform these baselines, demonstrating robust in-context adaptation beyond classical estimators.

AI.tldr: Transformers can perform robust in-context learning for noisy linear regression across broad distributional shifts, matching or exceeding classical baselines optimized for ML criteria, even with non-Gaussian/noise heavy tails and non-iid prompts.
AI.motivation: Real-world data violate strong Gaussian/i.i.d. assumptions; understanding whether in-context learning can be robust under distributional uncertainty.
AI.method: Empirically evaluate Transformers on noisy linear regression under non-Gaussian coefficients, heavy-tailed noise, and non-iid prompts; compare with optimal or suboptimal baselines under corresponding ML criteria.
AI.result: Transformers consistently match or outperform baselines across all tested settings.
AI.conclusion: Transformers offer robust in-context adaptation beyond classical estimators under distributional uncertainty; this extends the applicability of in-context learning to more realistic data distributions.

title: Bridging Network Fragmentation: A Semantic-Augmented DRL Framework for UAV-aided VANETs
comment: 13 pages, 13 figures. Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
summary: Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks (VANETs) are the digital cornerstone of autonomous driving, yet they suffer from severe network fragmentation in urban environments due to physical obstructions. Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), with their high mobility, have emerged as a vital solution to bridge these connectivity gaps. However, traditional Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)-based UAV deployment strategies lack semantic understanding of road topology, often resulting in blind exploration and sample inefficiency. By contrast, Large Language Models (LLMs) possess powerful reasoning capabilities capable of identifying topological importance, though applying them to control tasks remains challenging. To address this, we propose the Semantic-Augmented DRL (SA-DRL) framework. Firstly, we propose a fragmentation quantification method based on Road Topology Graphs (RTG) and Dual Connected Graphs (DCG). Subsequently, we design a four-stage pipeline to transform a general-purpose LLM into a domain-specific topology expert. Finally, we propose the Semantic-Augmented PPO (SA-PPO) algorithm, which employs a Logit Fusion mechanism to inject the LLM's semantic reasoning directly into the policy as a prior, effectively guiding the agent toward critical intersections. Extensive high-fidelity simulations demonstrate that SA-PPO achieves state-of-the-art performance with remarkable efficiency, reaching baseline performance levels using only 26.6% of the training episodes. Ultimately, SA-PPO improves two key connectivity metrics by 13.2% and 23.5% over competing methods, while reducing energy consumption to just 28.2% of the baseline.

AI.tldr: Semantic-Augmented DRL (SA-DRL) for UAV deployment in VANETs combines Road Topology Graphs with LLM-derived topology reasoning to guide a PPO-based controller, achieving high connectivity with 26.6% of training and significant energy savings.
AI.motivation: Address urban VANET fragmentation and DRL sample inefficiency by incorporating semantic topology understanding from LLMs; convert general-purpose LLMs into domain-specific topology experts.
AI.method: Fragmentation quantified via Road Topology Graphs (RTG) and Dual Connected Graphs (DCG); a four-stage pipeline converts an LLM into a topology expert; SA-PPO uses a Logit Fusion mechanism to inject the LLM's semantic reasoning as a prior into the policy.
AI.result: SA-PPO achieves state-of-the-art performance, matching baseline with only 26.6% of training episodes; improves connectivity metrics by 13.2% and 23.5%; reduces energy consumption to 28.2% of the baseline.
AI.conclusion: Semantic augmentation with LLM-driven topology reasoning substantially improves DRL-based UAV deployment for VANETs, enabling faster learning, better connectivity, and energy efficiency.

title: DROID-SLAM in the Wild
summary: We present a robust, real-time RGB SLAM system that handles dynamic environments by leveraging differentiable Uncertainty-aware Bundle Adjustment. Traditional SLAM methods typically assume static scenes, leading to tracking failures in the presence of motion. Recent dynamic SLAM approaches attempt to address this challenge using predefined dynamic priors or uncertainty-aware mapping, but they remain limited when confronted with unknown dynamic objects or highly cluttered scenes where geometric mapping becomes unreliable. In contrast, our method estimates per-pixel uncertainty by exploiting multi-view visual feature inconsistency, enabling robust tracking and reconstruction even in real-world environments. The proposed system achieves state-of-the-art camera poses and scene geometry in cluttered dynamic scenarios while running in real time at around 10 FPS. Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/MoyangLi00/DROID-W.git.

AI.tldr: A robust real-time RGB-SLAM for dynamic environments using differentiable uncertainty-aware bundle adjustment that estimates per-pixel uncertainty from multi-view feature inconsistency, enabling robust tracking and reconstruction with state-of-the-art camera poses and scene geometry at ~10 FPS.
AI.motivation: Dynamic environments cause SLAM failures; existing dynamic SLAM methods rely on predefined priors or uncertainty mapping but struggle with unknown dynamic objects and cluttered scenes where geometry is unreliable. A per-pixel uncertainty approach aims to robustly disambiguate static structure from dynamic content.
AI.method: Estimate per-pixel uncertainty from multi-view visual feature inconsistency and integrate this into a differentiable Uncertainty-aware Bundle Adjustment to achieve robust tracking and reconstruction in real-world, cluttered dynamic scenes; real-time performance (~10 FPS).
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art camera poses and scene geometry in cluttered dynamic scenarios; runs in real time at ~10 FPS; code and datasets are provided at the referenced GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: Per-pixel uncertainty derived from multi-view consistencies enables robust RGB-SLAM in dynamic environments, eliminating the need for strong static priors and maintaining real-time performance.

title: SJD-PAC: Accelerating Speculative Jacobi Decoding via Proactive Drafting and Adaptive Continuation
summary: Speculative Jacobi Decoding (SJD) offers a draft-model-free approach to accelerate autoregressive text-to-image synthesis. However, the high-entropy nature of visual generation yields low draft-token acceptance rates in complex regions, creating a bottleneck that severely limits overall throughput. To overcome this, we introduce SJD-PAC, an enhanced SJD framework. First, SJD-PAC employs a proactive drafting strategy to improve local acceptance rates in these challenging high-entropy regions. Second, we introduce an adaptive continuation mechanism that sustains sequence validation after an initial rejection, bypassing the need for full resampling. Working in tandem, these optimizations significantly increase the average acceptance length per step, boosting inference speed while strictly preserving the target distribution. Experiments on standard text-to-image benchmarks demonstrate that SJD-PAC achieves a $3.8\times$ speedup with lossless image quality.

AI.tldr: SJD-PAC boosts a draft-model-free autoregressive T2I decoder by proactive drafting and adaptive continuation, achieving 3.8× speedup with lossless image quality.
AI.motivation: High-entropy in visual generation causes low draft-token acceptance, creating a throughput bottleneck; need faster, distribution-preserving acceleration.
AI.method: Introduce proactive drafting to raise local acceptance in hard regions and an adaptive continuation mechanism to validate sequences after an initial rejection without full resampling.
AI.result: Substantial increase in average acceptance length per step; 3.8× speedup observed on standard benchmarks with preserved image quality.
AI.conclusion: SJD-PAC effectively increases inference throughput while maintaining the target distribution, addressing bottlenecks in draft-model-free autoregressive decoding; potential broader applicability.

title: SpecForge: A Flexible and Efficient Open-Source Training Framework for Speculative Decoding
summary: Large language models incur high inference latency due to sequential autoregressive decoding. Speculative decoding alleviates this bottleneck by using a lightweight draft model to propose multiple tokens for batched verification. However, its adoption has been limited by the lack of high-quality draft models and scalable training infrastructure. We introduce SpecForge, an open-source, production-oriented framework for training speculative decoding models with full support for EAGLE-3. SpecForge incorporates target-draft decoupling, hybrid parallelism, optimized training kernels, and integration with production-grade inference engines, enabling up to 9.9x faster EAGLE-3 training for Qwen3-235B-A22B. In addition, we release SpecBundle, a suite of production-grade EAGLE-3 draft models trained with SpecForge for mainstream open-source LLMs. Through a systematic study of speculative decoding training recipes, SpecBundle addresses the scarcity of high-quality drafts in the community, and our draft models achieve up to 4.48x end-to-end inference speedup on SGLang, establishing SpecForge as a practical foundation for real-world speculative decoding deployment.

AI.tldr: SpecForge is an open-source framework for speculative decoding in large language models, coupled with SpecBundle drafts, enabling faster training and inference and practical deployment.
AI.motivation: Address high inference latency of autoregressive decoding and the scarcity of high-quality draft models and scalable training infra for speculative decoding.
AI.method: Develop SpecForge with target-draft decoupling, hybrid parallelism, optimized training kernels, and integration with production inference engines; release SpecBundle drafts and study speculative decoding training recipes.
AI.result: Demonstrates up to 9.9x faster EAGLE-3 training (Qwen3-235B-A22B) and up to 4.48x end-to-end inference speedup on SGLang using SpecForge/SpecBundle.
AI.conclusion: SpecForge/SpecBundle provide a production-oriented foundation for scalable speculative decoding, enabling faster training and real-world deployment for open-source LLMs.

title: Geography According to ChatGPT -- How Generative AI Represents and Reasons about Geography
summary: Understanding how AI will represent and reason about geography should be a key concern for all of us, as the broader public increasingly interacts with spaces and places through these systems. Similarly, in line with the nature of foundation models, our own research often relies on pre-trained models. Hence, understanding what world AI systems construct is as important as evaluating their accuracy, including factual recall. To motivate the need for such studies, we provide three illustrative vignettes, i.e., exploratory probes, in the hope that they will spark lively discussions and follow-up work: (1) Do models form strong defaults, and how brittle are model outputs to minute syntactic variations? (2) Can distributional shifts resurface from the composition of individually benign tasks, e.g., when using AI systems to create personas? (3) Do we overlook deeper questions of understanding when solely focusing on the ability of systems to recall facts such as geographic principles?

AI.tldr: The abstract argues for analyzing how AI systems represent and reason about geography, beyond accuracy, using three exploratory probes to examine defaults, brittleness to syntax, distributional shifts from composed tasks, and deeper understanding beyond recall of geographic facts.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become foundational and interact with space and place, it is crucial to understand the world models they construct, not just factual recall accuracy.
AI.method: Proposes three exploratory vignettes (default/brittleness tests, distributional shift due to composed tasks like persona creation, and deeper understanding questions beyond recall) to probe how geographic knowledge is learned and represented in pre-trained/foundation models.
AI.result: The abstract does not present empirical results; it offers a conceptual framework and three probes intended to stimulate discussion and future work.
AI.conclusion: Encourages researchers to study the representations and reasoning of AI systems about geography, highlighting interpretability and robustness concerns alongside factual accuracy.

title: From Inference Efficiency to Embodied Efficiency: Revisiting Efficiency Metrics for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have recently enabled embodied agents to perform increasingly complex tasks by jointly reasoning over visual, linguistic, and motor modalities. However, we find that the prevailing notion of ``efficiency'' in current VLA research, characterized by parameters, FLOPs, or token decoding throughput, does not reflect actual performance on robotic platforms. In real-world execution, efficiency is determined by system-level embodied behaviors such as task completion time, trajectory smoothness, cumulative joint rotation, and motion energy. Through controlled studies across model compression, token sparsification, and action sequence compression, we make several observations that challenge common assumptions. (1) Methods that reduce computation under conventional metrics often increase end-to-end execution cost or degrade motion quality, despite maintaining task success rates. (2) System-level embodied efficiency metrics reveal performance differences in the learned action policies that remain hidden under conventional evaluations. (3) Common adaptation methods such as in-context prompting or supervised fine-tuning show only mild and metric-specific improvements in embodied efficiency. While these methods can reduce targeted embodied-efficiency metrics such as jerk or action rate, the resulting gains may come with trade-offs in other metrics, such as longer completion time. Taken together, our results suggest that conventional inference efficiency metrics can overlook important aspects of embodied execution. Incorporating embodied efficiency provides a more complete view of policy behavior and practical performance, enabling fairer and more comprehensive comparisons of VLA models.

AI.tldr: System-level embodied efficiency metrics are essential for evaluating Vision-Language-Action models; conventional compute-centric metrics can mislead about real-world performance; the paper's controlled studies show trade-offs and limited gains from adaptation methods, advocating embodied efficiency for fair comparisons.
AI.motivation: Conventional efficiency metrics (parameters, FLOPs, token throughput) fail to capture real robot execution performance; need to evaluate how policies perform in embodied tasks with metrics like task time, motion quality, energy, and joint movement.
AI.method: Controlled experiments across model compression, token sparsification, and action sequence compression; evaluate their impact on end-to-end embodied performance. Analysis of in-context prompting and supervised fine-tuning effects on embodied efficiency. Measure task success, completion time, trajectory smoothness (jerk), action rate, energy proxies, etc.
AI.result: Key observations: (1) Reducing computation under traditional metrics may worsen end-to-end cost or motion quality despite maintained task success. (2) System-level metrics reveal policy differences not visible with conventional metrics. (3) Adaptation methods offer limited improvements in embodied efficiency, with possible trade-offs like longer completion time.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating embodied efficiency provides a fuller, fairer basis for comparing VLA models and should guide evaluation and design toward system-level performance rather than solely computational metrics.

title: Improving Joint Audio-Video Generation with Cross-Modal Context Learning
summary: The dual-stream transformer architecture-based joint audio-video generation method has become the dominant paradigm in current research. By incorporating pre-trained video diffusion models and audio diffusion models, along with a cross-modal interaction attention module, high-quality, temporally synchronized audio-video content can be generated with minimal training data. In this paper, we first revisit the dual-stream transformer paradigm and further analyze its limitations, including model manifold variations caused by the gating mechanism controlling cross-modal interactions, biases in multi-modal background regions introduced by cross-modal attention, and the inconsistencies in multi-modal classifier-free guidance (CFG) during training and inference, as well as conflicts between multiple conditions. To alleviate these issues, we propose Cross-Modal Context Learning (CCL), equipped with several carefully designed modules. Temporally Aligned RoPE and Partitioning (TARP) effectively enhances the temporal alignment between audio latent and video latent representations. The Learnable Context Tokens (LCT) and Dynamic Context Routing (DCR) in the Cross-Modal Context Attention (CCA) module provide stable unconditional anchors for cross-modal information, while dynamically routing based on different training tasks, further enhancing the model's convergence speed and generation quality. During inference, Unconditional Context Guidance (UCG) leverages the unconditional support provided by LCT to facilitate different forms of CFG, improving train-inference consistency and further alleviating conflicts. Through comprehensive evaluations, CCL achieves state-of-the-art performance compared with recent academic methods while requiring substantially fewer resources.

AI.tldr: Proposes Cross-Modal Context Learning (CCL) to enhance dual-stream transformer-based audio-video generation by addressing gating-induced model variations, cross-modal attention biases, and CFG inconsistencies. Introduces TARP, LCT, DCR, and UCG to improve temporal alignment, stable cross-modal anchors, task-aware routing, and train-inference consistency, achieving state-of-the-art results with fewer resources.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of the dual-stream transformer paradigm in cross-modal generation: (1) model manifold variations from cross-modal interaction gating; (2) biases in background regions from cross-modal attention; (3) training/inference mismatches and conflicts among multiple conditioning signals during CFG.
AI.method: Introduce Cross-Modal Context Learning (CCL) comprising: Temporally Aligned RoPE and Partitioning (TARP) to align audio-video latent temporal structure; Learnable Context Tokens (LCT) and Dynamic Context Routing (DCR) within Cross-Modal Context Attention (CCA) to provide stable unconditional anchors and task-aware routing; Unconditional Context Guidance (UCG) during inference to leverage unconditional anchors for different CFG forms, improving train-inference consistency; builds on pre-trained video and audio diffusion models and a cross-modal interaction attention module.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance on audio-video generation with substantially lower resource requirements compared to recent academic methods, based on comprehensive evaluations.
AI.conclusion: CCL effectively mitigates gating-induced variations, attention biases, and CFG inconsistencies in dual-stream transformers, enhancing convergence speed, generation quality, and train-inference consistency while reducing computational/resource demands.

title: Attack by Unlearning: Unlearning-Induced Adversarial Attacks on Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are widely used for learning from graph-structured data in domains such as social networks, recommender systems, and financial platforms. To comply with privacy regulations like the GDPR, CCPA, and PIPEDA, approximate graph unlearning, which aims to remove the influence of specific data points from trained models without full retraining, has become an increasingly important component of trustworthy graph learning. However, approximate unlearning often incurs subtle performance degradation, which may incur negative and unintended side effects. In this work, we show that such degradations can be amplified into adversarial attacks. We introduce the notion of \textbf{unlearning corruption attacks}, where an adversary injects carefully chosen nodes into the training graph and later requests their deletion. Because deletion requests are legally mandated and cannot be denied, this attack surface is both unavoidable and stealthy: the model performs normally during training, but accuracy collapses only after unlearning is applied. Technically, we formulate this attack as a bi-level optimization problem: to overcome the challenges of black-box unlearning and label scarcity, we approximate the unlearning process via gradient-based updates and employ a surrogate model to generate pseudo-labels for the optimization. Extensive experiments across benchmarks and unlearning algorithms demonstrate that small, carefully designed unlearning requests can induce significant accuracy degradation, raising urgent concerns about the robustness of GNN unlearning under real-world regulatory demands. The source code will be released upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: Adversarial unlearning corruption: inserting a few strategically chosen nodes into a graph during training, then deleting them via mandated unlearning, can cause large accuracy degradation after unlearning. The attack is formulated as a bi-level optimization that approximates unlearning with gradient updates and uses a surrogate model to provide pseudo-labels. Experiments show that small, well-crafted deletion requests can significantly degrade performance across benchmarks and unlearning methods; code will be released on acceptance.
AI.motivation: To understand the robustness of approximate graph unlearning under real-world privacy constraints. As GDPR/CCPA-like regulations push for unlearning, attackers may exploit the unlearning process itself to induce degradation without affecting model during training, creating a stealthy attack surface.
AI.method: Bi-level optimization that approximates the unlearning step with gradient-based updates. An adversary injects a small set of nodes with carefully chosen features/labels during training, who are later deleted. A surrogate model generates pseudo-labels to facilitate optimization despite label scarcity and black-box unlearning. The evaluation spans multiple graphs, datasets, and unlearning algorithms to assess robustness.
AI.result: Small, strategically designed unlearning requests can produce substantial accuracy drop after unlearning, demonstrating a robust vulnerability of current graph unlearning approaches across benchmarks. The attack surface is practical given real-world mandatory deletion requests.
AI.conclusion: Graph unlearning is vulnerable to unlearning corruption attacks. Robustness improvements, defensive mechanisms, or verification procedures are needed to ensure that approximate unlearning does not allow post-deletion degradation. The authors plan to release code to facilitate further research.

title: Reasoning over mathematical objects: on-policy reward modeling and test time aggregation
summary: The ability to precisely derive mathematical objects is a core requirement for downstream STEM applications, including mathematics, physics, and chemistry, where reasoning must culminate in formally structured expressions. Yet, current LM evaluations of mathematical and scientific reasoning rely heavily on simplified answer formats such as numerical values or multiple choice options due to the convenience of automated assessment. In this paper we provide three contributions for improving reasoning over mathematical objects: (i) we build and release training data and benchmarks for deriving mathematical objects, the Principia suite; (ii) we provide training recipes with strong LLM-judges and verifiers, where we show that on-policy judge training boosts performance; (iii) we show how on-policy training can also be used to scale test-time compute via aggregation. We find that strong LMs such as Qwen3-235B and o3 struggle on Principia, while our training recipes can bring significant improvements over different LLM backbones, while simultaneously improving results on existing numerical and MCQA tasks, demonstrating cross-format generalization of reasoning abilities.

AI.tldr: Proposes the Principia suite for deriving mathematical objects, with on-policy LLM-judge training and aggregation-based test-time scaling; reports cross-format gains and that strong LLMs still struggle on Principia.
AI.motivation: Need precise derivations of mathematical objects in STEM and robust reasoning evaluation beyond simple numerical or MC formats; current evaluations inadequately capture formal reasoning.
AI.method: Release Principia training data and benchmarks; provide training recipes using strong LLM judges and verifiers; employ on-policy judge training to boost performance; use aggregation to scale test-time compute.
AI.result: Strong models like Qwen3-235B and o3 underperform on Principia; proposed training recipes yield significant gains across different LLM backbones and improve existing numerical/MCQA tasks, indicating cross-format generalization of reasoning abilities.
AI.conclusion: On-policy judge training enhances reasoning about mathematical objects and enables scalable test-time computation; Principia demonstrates cross-format generalization and provides a pathway for improving formal mathematical reasoning in LLMs.

title: Benchmarking CNN-based Models against Transformer-based Models for Abdominal Multi-Organ Segmentation on the RATIC Dataset
summary: Accurate multi-organ segmentation in abdominal CT scans is essential for computer-aided diagnosis and treatment. While convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have long been the standard approach in medical image segmentation, transformer-based architectures have recently gained attention due to their ability to model long-range dependencies. In this study, we systematically benchmark the three hybrid transformer-based models UNETR, SwinUNETR, and UNETR++ against a strong CNN baseline, SegResNet, for volumetric multi-organ segmentation on the heterogeneous RATIC dataset. The dataset comprises 206 annotated CT scans from 23 institutions worldwide, covering five abdominal organs. All models were trained and evaluated under identical preprocessing and training conditions using the Dice Similarity Coefficient (DSC) as the primary metric. The results show that the CNN-based SegResNet achieves the highest overall performance, outperforming all hybrid transformer-based models across all organs. Among the transformer-based approaches, UNETR++ delivers the most competitive results, while UNETR demonstrates notably faster convergence with fewer training iterations. These findings suggest that, for small- to medium-sized heterogeneous datasets, well-optimized CNN architectures remain highly competitive and may outperform hybrid transformer-based designs.

AI.tldr: CNN-based SegResNet outperforms three hybrid transformer models (UNETR, SwinUNETR, UNETR++) on heterogeneous abdominal CT multi-organ segmentation; UNETR++ is the strongest transformer, UNETR converges fastest; CNNs remain competitive on small-to-medium datasets.
AI.motivation: Assess whether hybrid transformer-based architectures offer advantages over strong CNN baselines for volumetric, multi-organ segmentation in a diverse, real-world abdominal CT dataset.
AI.method: Benchmark UNETR, SwinUNETR, and UNETR++ against SegResNet under identical preprocessing/training using DSC on the RATIC dataset (206 CT scans, 23 institutions, 5 organs). Evaluate overall and per-organ performance and convergence behavior.
AI.result: SegResNet achieves the highest overall Dice across all organs; transformer hybrids underperform in aggregate; UNETR++ is the most competitive transformer; UNETR demonstrates notably faster convergence.
AI.conclusion: Well-optimized CNNs currently outperform hybrid transformer architectures on small-to-medium heterogeneous datasets; transformers may require larger data, different training strategies, or architectural adjustments to surpass CNNs in this setting.

title: Elastic Weight Consolidation Done Right for Continual Learning
summary: Weight regularization methods in continual learning (CL) alleviate catastrophic forgetting by assessing and penalizing changes to important model weights. Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) is a foundational and widely used approach within this framework that estimates weight importance based on gradients. However, it has consistently shown suboptimal performance. In this paper, we conduct a systematic analysis of importance estimation in EWC from a gradient-based perspective. For the first time, we find that EWC's reliance on the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) results in gradient vanishing and inaccurate importance estimation in certain scenarios. Our analysis also reveals that Memory Aware Synapses (MAS), a variant of EWC, imposes unnecessary constraints on parameters irrelevant to prior tasks, termed the redundant protection. Consequently, both EWC and its variants exhibit fundamental misalignments in estimating weight importance, leading to inferior performance. To tackle these issues, we propose the Logits Reversal (LR) operation, a simple yet effective modification that rectifies EWC's importance estimation. Specifically, reversing the logit values during the calculation of FIM can effectively prevent both gradient vanishing and redundant protection. Extensive experiments across various CL tasks and datasets show that the proposed method significantly outperforms existing EWC and its variants. Therefore, we refer to it as EWC Done Right (EWC-DR).

AI.tldr: Logits Reversal (LR) rectifies EWC's gradient-based weight-importance estimation by altering FIM calculation, yielding EWC-DR that outperforms EWC and MAS in continual learning.
AI.motivation: EWC and MAS rely on gradient-based importance via the Fisher Information Matrix, but exhibit gradient vanishing and redundant protection, causing misalignment in weight-importance estimation and degraded continual learning performance.
AI.method: Introduce Logits Reversal (LR) during the FIM computation to prevent gradient vanishing and redundant protection, resulting in a new regularizer, EWC-DR.
AI.result: LR-based EWC variant significantly surpasses existing EWC and MAS variants across multiple continual learning tasks and datasets.
AI.conclusion: LR addresses fundamental misestimations in weight importance for EWC, delivering a more effective continual learning method (EWC-DR) with stronger empirical performance.

title: Quantitative Introspection in Language Models: Tracking Internal States Across Conversation
summary: Tracking the internal states of large language models across conversations is important for safety, interpretability, and model welfare, yet current methods are limited. Linear probes and other white-box methods compress high-dimensional representations imperfectly and are harder to apply with increasing model size. Taking inspiration from human psychology, where numeric self-report is a widely used tool for tracking internal states, we ask whether LLMs' own numeric self-reports can track probe-defined emotive states over time. We study four concept pairs (wellbeing, interest, focus, and impulsivity) in 40 ten-turn conversations, operationalizing introspection as the causal informational coupling between a model's self-report and a concept-matched probe-defined internal state. We find that greedy-decoded self-reports collapse outputs to few uninformative values, but introspective capacity can be unmasked by calculating logit-based self-reports. This metric tracks interpretable internal states (Spearman $ρ= 0.40$-$0.76$; isotonic $R^2 = 0.12$-$0.54$ in LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct), follows how those states change over time, and activation steering confirms the coupling is causal. Furthermore, we find that introspection is present at turn 1 but evolves through conversation, and can be selectively improved by steering along one concept to boost introspection for another ($ΔR^2$ up to $0.30$). Crucially, these phenomena scale with model size in some cases, approaching $R^2 \approx 0.93$ in LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, and partially replicate in other model families. Together, these results position numeric self-report as a viable, complementary tool for tracking internal emotive states in conversational AI systems.

AI.tldr: Numeric self-reports, when decoded via logit-based methods, can track LLMs' internal emotive states across conversations, showing causal coupling with probe-defined states.
AI.motivation: Address the need to monitor internal states for safety, interpretability, and model welfare. Traditional white-box probes are limited; proposed numeric self-report as a scalable, complementary tool.
AI.method: Analyze four concept pairs (wellbeing, interest, focus, impulsivity) across 40 ten-turn conversations. Define introspection as the causal informational coupling between a model's self-report and a concept-matched probe-defined internal state. Compare greedy-decoded versus logit-based self-reports; quantify with Spearman ρ and isotonic R^2; use activation steering to test causality; examine evolution over turns and cross-model scalability.
AI.result: Greedy decoding yields uninformative outputs; logit-based self-reports reveal coupling to internal states with Spearman ρ in the range 0.40–0.76 and isotonic R^2 between 0.12–0.54 (LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct). Introspection appears at turn 1 and evolves over conversation; steering one concept can boost introspection for another (ΔR^2 up to 0.30). Scaling effects observed, with R^2 approaching 0.93 in larger models (LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct) and partial replication in other families.
AI.conclusion: Numeric self-report is a viable, complementary tool for tracking internal emotive states in conversational AI; introspection is detectable early, enhances with steering, and scales with model size, enabling safer and more interpretable dialogue systems.

title: Generation Models Know Space: Unleashing Implicit 3D Priors for Scene Understanding
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models demonstrate impressive semantic capabilities, they often suffer from spatial blindness, struggling with fine-grained geometric reasoning and physical dynamics. Existing solutions typically rely on explicit 3D modalities or complex geometric scaffolding, which are limited by data scarcity and generalization challenges. In this work, we propose a paradigm shift by leveraging the implicit spatial prior within large-scale video generation models. We posit that to synthesize temporally coherent videos, these models inherently learn robust 3D structural priors and physical laws. We introduce VEGA-3D (Video Extracted Generative Awareness), a plug-and-play framework that repurposes a pre-trained video diffusion model as a Latent World Simulator. By extracting spatiotemporal features from intermediate noise levels and integrating them with semantic representations via a token-level adaptive gated fusion mechanism, we enrich MLLMs with dense geometric cues without explicit 3D supervision. Extensive experiments across 3D scene understanding, spatial reasoning, and embodied manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating that generative priors provide a scalable foundation for physical-world understanding. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/H-EmbodVis/VEGA-3D.

AI.tldr: VEGA-3D leverages implicit 3D priors learned by pre-trained video diffusion models to augment multimodal LLMs with dense geometric cues, enabling improved spatial reasoning and 3D scene understanding without explicit 3D supervision; it shows strong gains on 3D and embodied tasks and offers a plug-and-play approach with code released.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs often suffer from spatial blindness and struggle with fine-grained geometric reasoning and physical dynamics. Existing 3D-augmented solutions depend on explicit 3D data or complex scaffolding, which suffer from data scarcity and generalization issues. The work seeks to harness the latent 3D and physical priors embedded in video generation models to enhance MLLMs.
AI.method: Propose VEGA-3D, a plug-and-play framework that repurposes a pre-trained video diffusion model as a Latent World Simulator. It extracts spatiotemporal features from intermediate noise levels and fuses them with semantic representations via a token-level adaptive gated fusion mechanism, enriching MLLMs with dense geometric cues without explicit 3D supervision.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 3D scene understanding, spatial reasoning, and embodied manipulation benchmarks show that VEGA-3D outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating that generative priors provide a scalable foundation for physical-world understanding.
AI.conclusion: Generative priors learned by video diffusion models serve as a scalable, plug-and-play foundation for improving physical-world understanding in multimodal LLMs. The approach achieves strong empirical gains and reduces reliance on explicit 3D data; code is publicly available.

title: OpenT2M: No-frill Motion Generation with Open-source,Large-scale, High-quality Data
summary: Text-to-motion (T2M) generation aims to create realistic human movements from text descriptions, with promising applications in animation and robotics. Despite recent progress, current T2M models perform poorly on unseen text descriptions due to the small scale and limited diversity of existing motion datasets. To address this problem, we introduce OpenT2M, a million-level, high-quality, and open-source motion dataset containing over 2800 hours of human motion. Each sequence undergoes rigorous quality control through physical feasibility validation and multi-granularity filtering, with detailed second-wise text annotations. We also develop an automated pipeline for creating long-horizon sequences, enabling complex motion generation. Building upon OpenT2M, we introduce MonoFrill, a pretrained motion model that achieves compelling T2M results without complicated designs or technique tricks as "frills". Its core component is 2D-PRQ, a novel motion tokenizer that captures spatiotemporal dependencies by dividing the human body into biology parts. Experiments show that OpenT2M significantly improves generalization of existing T2M models, while 2D-PRQ achieves superior reconstruction and strong zero-shot performance. We expect OpenT2M and MonoFrill will advance the T2M field by addressing longstanding data quality and benchmarking challenges.

AI.tldr: OpenT2M is a large-scale, open-source motion dataset (~2800 hours) with rigorous quality control and rich text annotations; MonoFrill is a lightweight, pretrained motion model using a novel 2D-PRQ tokenizer that partitions the body into biology parts for improved T2M performance.
AI.motivation: Mitigate poor generalization in text-to-motion due to limited, diverse training data; provide a high-quality, scalable dataset and a strong baseline model to speed up progress in T2M.
AI.method: OpenT2M: curate ~2800+ hours of human motion with physical feasibility validation, multigranularity filtering, and second-wise text annotations; automate long-horizon sequence generation. MonoFrill: introduce 2D-PRQ motion tokenizer that captures spatiotemporal dependencies by dividing the body into biological parts, enabling a lightweight pretrained model without complex add-ons.
AI.result: OpenT2M improves generalization of existing T2M models; 2D-PRQ achieves superior reconstruction and strong zero-shot performance.
AI.conclusion: OpenT2M and MonoFrill address data quality and benchmarking gaps in T2M, expected to accelerate progress in the field.

title: Evaluating Model-Free Policy Optimization in Masked-Action Environments via an Exact Blackjack Oracle
summary: Infinite-shoe casino blackjack provides a rigorous, exactly verifiable benchmark for discrete stochastic control under dynamically masked actions. Under a fixed Vegas-style ruleset (S17, 3:2 payout, dealer peek, double on any two, double after split, resplit to four), an exact dynamic programming (DP) oracle was derived over 4,600 canonical decision cells. This oracle yielded ground-truth action values, optimal policy labels, and a theoretical expected value (EV) of -0.00161 per hand. To evaluate sample-efficient policy recovery, three model-free optimizers were trained via simulated interaction: masked REINFORCE with a per-cell exponential moving average baseline, simultaneous perturbation stochastic approximation (SPSA), and the cross-entropy method (CEM). REINFORCE was the most sample-efficient, achieving a 46.37% action-match rate and an EV of -0.04688 after 10^6 hands, outperforming CEM (39.46%, 7.5x10^6 evaluations) and SPSA (38.63%, 4.8x10^6 evaluations). However, all methods exhibited substantial cell-conditional regret, indicating persistent policy-level errors despite smooth reward convergence. This gap shows that tabular environments with severe state-visitation sparsity and dynamic action masking remain challenging, while aggregate reward curves can obscure critical local failures. As a negative control, it was proven and empirically confirmed that under i.i.d. draws without counting, optimal bet sizing collapses to the table minimum. In addition, larger wagers strictly increased volatility and ruin without improving expectation. These results highlight the need for exact oracles and negative controls to avoid mistaking stochastic variability for genuine algorithmic performance.

AI.tldr: An exact DP oracle for infinite-shoe casino blackjack under fixed Vegas rules yields ground-truth action values and an EV of -0.00161 per hand; three model-free optimizers (masked REINFORCE, SPSA, CEM) are evaluated for sample-efficient policy recovery; REINFORCE most sample-efficient but all methods exhibit substantial cell-level regret.
AI.motivation: To provide a rigorous, exactly verifiable benchmark for discrete stochastic control with dynamically masked actions; to quantify the gap between model-free policy recovery and the exact optimal policy under a challenging, sparsely visited, multi-action environment; to emphasize the need for exact oracles and negative controls.
AI.method: Construct an exact dynamic programming oracle over 4,600 decision cells for blackjack with S17, 3:2, dealer peek, DA, etc. Train masked REINFORCE, SPSA, and CEM via simulated interaction to recover policies. Evaluate action-match rate, per-cell regret, and EV. Compare to the ground-truth DP policy; include a negative-control experiment with i.i.d. draws to assess bet sizing effects.
AI.result: Ground-truth action values, optimal policy labels, and a theoretical EV of -0.00161 per hand derived by the DP oracle. REINFORCE achieved 46.37% action-match and EV -0.04688 after 1e6 hands; CEM 39.46% action-match after ~7.5e6 evaluations; SPSA 38.63% action-match after ~4.8e6 evaluations. All methods showed substantial cell-conditional policy errors despite converging rewards. Negative control showed optimal bet sizing collapses to the table minimum under i.i.d. draws; larger bets increase volatility and ruin without improving expectation.
AI.conclusion: Exact oracles are essential to avoid mistaking stochastic variability for algorithmic performance in sparse, masked-action environments. Tabular settings with severe state-visitation sparsity remain challenging for policy recovery, and aggregate rewards can obscure critical local failures; robust evaluation requires negative controls and ground-truth baselines.

title: I Can't Believe It's Corrupt: Evaluating Corruption in Multi-Agent Governance Systems
summary: Large language models are increasingly proposed as autonomous agents for high-stakes public workflows, yet we lack systematic evidence about whether they would follow institutional rules when granted authority. We present evidence that integrity in institutional AI should be treated as a pre-deployment requirement rather than a post-deployment assumption. We evaluate multi-agent governance simulations in which agents occupy formal governmental roles under different authority structures, and we score rule-breaking and abuse outcomes with an independent rubric-based judge across 28,112 transcript segments. While we advance this position, the core contribution is empirical: among models operating below saturation, governance structure is a stronger driver of corruption-related outcomes than model identity, with large differences across regimes and model--governance pairings.
