Lightweight safeguards can reduce risk in some settings but do not consistently prevent severe failures. These results imply that institutional design is a precondition for safe delegation: before real authority is assigned to LLM agents, systems should undergo stress testing under governance-like constraints with enforceable rules, auditable logs, and human oversight on high-impact actions.

AI.tldr: Pre-deployment integrity and governance design are crucial for safe delegation of authority to LLMs; governance structure, not model identity, drives corruption outcomes when models operate below saturation; stress-testing with enforceable rules and human oversight is recommended before real authority is granted.
AI.motivation: To determine whether large language models will follow institutional rules in high-stakes public workflows and whether integrity can be treated as a pre-deployment requirement rather than an afterthought.
AI.method: Simulation study with multiple agents occupying formal governmental roles under different governance structures; rule-breaking and abuse outcomes scored by an independent rubric-based judge across 28,112 transcript segments; comparison across model identities and governance regimes.
AI.result: Among sub-saturation models, governance structure is a stronger predictor of corruption-related outcomes than model identity, with substantial variation across regimes and model–governance pairings. Lightweight safeguards help in some settings but do not reliably prevent severe failures.
AI.conclusion: Institutional design is a precondition for safe delegation; before granting real authority to LLM agents, systems should undergo governance-like stress testing with enforceable rules, auditable logs, and human oversight on high-impact actions.

title: GenVideoLens: Where LVLMs Fall Short in AI-Generated Video Detection?
comment: ECCV 2026 submission. 14 pages, 6 figures, 4 tables. Supplementary material included
summary: In recent years, AI-generated videos have become increasingly realistic and sophisticated. Meanwhile, Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have shown strong potential for detecting such content. However, existing evaluation protocols largely treat the task as a binary classification problem and rely on coarse-grained metrics such as overall accuracy, providing limited insight into where LVLMs succeed or fail. To address this limitation, we introduce GenVideoLens, a fine-grained benchmark that enables dimension-wise evaluation of LVLM capabilities in AI-generated video detection. The benchmark contains 400 highly deceptive AI-generated videos and 100 real videos, annotated by experts across 15 authenticity dimensions covering perceptual, optical, physical, and temporal cues. We evaluate eleven representative LVLMs on this benchmark. Our analysis reveals a pronounced dimensional imbalance. While LVLMs perform relatively well on perceptual cues, they struggle with optical consistency, physical interactions, and temporal-causal reasoning. Model performance also varies substantially across dimensions, with smaller open-source models sometimes outperforming stronger proprietary models on specific authenticity cues. Temporal perturbation experiments further show that current LVLMs make limited use of temporal information. Overall, GenVideoLens provides diagnostic insights into LVLM behavior, revealing key capability gaps and offering guidance for improving future AI-generated video detection systems.

AI.tldr: A fine-grained LVLM benchmark for AI-generated video detection (GenVideoLens) is introduced, revealing dimensional imbalances and underutilization of temporal cues across 11 LVLMs; smaller open-source models sometimes excel on specific cues; the dataset enables diagnostic insights and guides improvements in detection systems.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation treats AI-generated video detection as a binary task with coarse accuracy, obscuring which cues LVLMs can leverage or fail on. There is a need for fine-grained, dimensional analysis across perceptual, optical, physical, and temporal cues to diagnose capabilities and guide improvements.
AI.method: Construct GenVideoLens with 400 highly deceptive AI-generated videos and 100 real videos. Expert annotators label across 15 authenticity dimensions spanning perceptual, optical, physical, and temporal cues. Evaluate eleven representative LVLMs. Conduct temporal perturbation experiments to assess utilization of temporal information.
AI.result: LVLMs perform reasonably on perceptual cues but struggle with optical consistency, physical interactions, and temporal-causal reasoning. Model performance varies across dimensions; smaller open-source models sometimes outperform stronger proprietary models on specific cues. Temporal perturbations indicate limited use of temporal information.
AI.conclusion: GenVideoLens provides diagnostic insights into LVLM behavior, identifies key capability gaps, and offers guidance for improving AI-generated video detection systems.

title: Enhancing Multi-Corpus Training in SSL-Based Anti-Spoofing Models: Domain-Invariant Feature Extraction
summary: The performance of speech spoofing detection often varies across different training and evaluation corpora. Leveraging multiple corpora typically enhances robustness and performance in fields like speaker recognition and speech recognition. However, our spoofing detection experiments show that multi-corpus training does not consistently improve performance and may even degrade it. We hypothesize that dataset-specific biases impair generalization, leading to performance instability. To address this, we propose an Invariant Domain Feature Extraction (IDFE) framework, employing multi-task learning and a gradient reversal layer to minimize corpus-specific information in learned embeddings. The IDFE framework reduces the average equal error rate by 20% compared to the baseline, assessed across four varied datasets.

AI.tldr: Invariant Domain Feature Extraction (IDFE) improves cross-corpus spoofing detection by minimizing corpus-specific biases; uses multi-task learning with a gradient reversal layer, achieving ~20% relative EER reduction across four datasets.
AI.motivation: Dataset-specific biases hinder cross-corpus generalization in spoofing detection; while multi-corpus training helps in other fields, it does not consistently improve performance here, causing instability.
AI.method: Invariant Domain Feature Extraction (IDFE) framework employing multi-task learning and a gradient reversal layer to minimize corpus-specific information in feature embeddings, promoting invariant representations across corpora.
AI.result: IDFE reduces the average equal error rate by about 20% relative to a baseline, when evaluated across four varied datasets.
AI.conclusion: IDFE enhances cross-corpus robustness in spoofing detection by suppressing dataset-specific cues in learned embeddings; demonstrates potential for more stable, generalizable detectors across diverse datasets.

title: Secure Linear Alignment of Large Language Models
summary: Language models increasingly appear to learn similar representations, despite differences in training objectives, architectures, and data modalities. This emerging compatibility between independently trained models introduces new opportunities for cross-model alignment to downstream objectives. Moreover, it unlocks new potential application domains, such as settings where security, privacy, or competitive constraints prohibit direct data or model sharing. In this work, we propose a privacy-preserving framework that exploits representational convergence to enable cross-silo inference between independent language models. The framework learns an affine transformation over a shared public dataset and applies homomorphic encryption to protect client queries during inference. By encrypting only the linear alignment and classification operations, the method achieves sub-second inference latency while maintaining strong security guarantees. We support this framework with an empirical investigation into representational convergence, in which we learn linear transformations between the final hidden states of independent models. We evaluate these cross-model mappings on embedding classification and out-of-distribution detection, observing minimal performance degradation across model pairs. Additionally, we show for the first time that linear alignment sometimes enables text generation across independently trained models.

AI.tldr: Proposes a privacy-preserving cross-silo inference framework that uses a learnable affine alignment between final hidden states of independent language models, trained on a public dataset and protected by homomorphic encryption for client queries. Demonstrates sub-second latency, empirical evidence of representational convergence, and cross-model mappings for embeddings, OOD detection, and even generation with minimal performance loss.
AI.motivation: Independently trained LMs often converge to similar representations. However, data or model sharing is often restricted for privacy, security, or competitive reasons. The work aims to enable cross-silo inference and collaboration by exploiting representational alignment while preserving privacy.
AI.method: Learn a linear (affine) transformation between the final hidden states of independent models using a shared public dataset. Use homomorphic encryption to protect client queries during inference, encrypting only the linear alignment and classification operations. Achieve sub-second latency by limiting encrypted computations to these linear components.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrate representational convergence and learnable linear mappings between final hidden states across model pairs. Evaluate cross-model mappings on embedding classification and out-of-distribution detection, observing minimal performance degradation across model pairs. In some cases, linear alignment enables text generation across independently trained models.
AI.conclusion: Affine linear alignment, combined with homomorphic encryption for the encrypted parts of the pipeline, is a viable privacy-preserving mechanism for cross-model collaboration. Representational convergence supports transferability across independently trained models, enabling tasks such as embedding classification, OOD detection, and even generation, with low latency and strong security guarantees.

title: GEAR: Geography-knowledge Enhanced Analog Recognition Framework in Extreme Environments
summary: The Mariana Trench and the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau exhibit significant similarities in geological origins and microbial metabolic functions. Given that deep-sea biological sampling faces prohibitive costs, recognizing structurally homologous terrestrial analogs of the Mariana Trench on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau is of great significance. Yet, no existing model adequately addresses cross-domain topographic similarity retrieval, either neglecting geographical knowledge or sacrificing computational efficiency. To address these challenges, we present \underline{\textbf{G}}eography-knowledge \underline{\textbf{E}}nhanced \underline{\textbf{A}}nalog \underline{\textbf{R}}ecognition (\textbf{GEAR}) Framework, a three-stage pipeline designed to efficiently retrieve analogs from 2.5 million square kilometers of the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau: (1) Skeleton guided Screening and Clipping: Recognition of candidate valleys and initial screening based on size and linear morphological criteria. (2) Physics aware Filtering: The Topographic Waveform Comparator (TWC) and Morphological Texture Module (MTM) evaluate the waveform and texture and filter out inconsistent candidate valleys. (3) Graph based Fine Recognition: We design a \underline{\textbf{M}}orphology-integrated \underline{\textbf{S}}iamese \underline{\textbf{G}}raph \underline{\textbf{N}}etwork (\textbf{MSG-Net}) based on geomorphological metrics. Correspondingly, we release an expert-annotated topographic similarity dataset targeting tectonic collision zones. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of every stage. Besides, MSG-Net achieved an F1-Score 1.38 percentage points higher than the SOTA baseline. Using features extracted by MSG-Net, we discovered a significant correlation with biological data, providing evidence for future biological analysis.

AI.tldr: GEAR is a three-stage framework for cross-domain topographic analog recognition, transforming 2.5M km2 plateaus into terrestrial analogs of Mariana Trench with a MSG-Net graph network and a released similarity dataset; it improves a state-of-the-art baseline and links geomorphology features to biology.
AI.motivation: Deep-sea sampling is costly; identifying structurally homologous terrestrial analogs on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau enables biological and geological insights without expensive sampling. Existing models fail to capture cross-domain topographic similarity efficiently, motivating a dedicated, knowledge-informed pipeline.
AI.method: 1) Skeleton guided Screening and Clipping to detect candidate valleys by size/linear morphology; 2) Physics aware Filtering with Topographic Waveform Comparator (TWC) and Morphological Texture Module (MTM) to assess waveform/texture and filter out inconsistencies; 3) Graph-based Fine Recognition using Morphology-integrated Siamese Graph Network (MSG-Net) on geomorphological metrics; release of an expert-annotated topographic similarity dataset for tectonic collision zones.
AI.result: MSG-Net achieves F1-Score 1.38 percentage points above the SOTA baseline. Each stage improves effectiveness; features from MSG-Net correlate with biological data, suggesting relevance to biological analyses and potential future work.
AI.conclusion: The GEARS pipeline provides an efficient, knowledge-guided solution for cross-domain topographic similarity retrieval, delivering a high-performing comparator network and a dataset that enable correlations with biological data and future multidisciplinary studies.

title: Revisiting Label Inference Attacks in Vertical Federated Learning: Why They Are Vulnerable and How to Defend
summary: Vertical federated learning (VFL) allows an active party with a top model, and multiple passive parties with bottom models to collaborate. In this scenario, passive parties possessing only features may attempt to infer active party's private labels, making label inference attacks (LIAs) a significant threat. Previous LIA studies have claimed that well-trained bottom models can effectively represent labels. However, we demonstrate that this view is misleading and exposes the vulnerability of existing LIAs. By leveraging mutual information, we present the first observation of the "model compensation" phenomenon in VFL. We theoretically prove that, in VFL, the mutual information between layer outputs and labels increases with layer depth, indicating that bottom models primarily extract feature information while the top model handles label mapping. Building on this insight, we introduce task reassignment to show that the success of existing LIAs actually stems from the distribution alignment between features and labels. When this alignment is disrupted, the performance of LIAs declines sharply or even fails entirely. Furthermore, the implications of this insight for defenses are also investigated. We propose a zero-overhead defense technique based on layer adjustment. Extensive experiments across five datasets and five representative model architectures indicate that shifting cut layers forward to increase the proportion of top model layers in the entire model not only improves resistance to LIAs but also enhances other defenses.

AI.tldr: In vertical FL, passive parties' LIAs exploit feature–label alignment; deeper layers increase label leakage via model compensation, but task reassignment and a zero-cost defense—shifting the cut-layer forward—mitigate LIAs and can bolster other defenses across multiple datasets and architectures.
AI.motivation: Explain why label inference attacks remain effective in vertical FL, challenge the assumption that bottom models can fully encode labels, and develop practical, low-overhead defenses.
AI.method: Mutual information analysis to show model compensation with depth; theoretical proof that MI between layer outputs and labels grows with depth; introduce task reassignment to disrupt feature–label alignment; propose layer-adjustment defense by moving the cut-layer forward; comprehensive experiments on five datasets and five architectures.
AI.result: Demonstrates depth-dependent label leakage (increasing MI); LIAs rely on distribution alignment; disrupting alignment reduces or eliminates LIA effectiveness; zero-overhead layer-adjustment defense improves resilience and benefits other defenses; empirical validation across diverse settings.
AI.conclusion: Bottom models primarily capture features while the top model handles label mapping; layer-adjustment defense provides a simple, effective, and general approach to mitigate LIAs in VFL without overhead, with broader defensive implications.

title: Agentic Business Process Management: A Research Manifesto
summary:
This paper presents a manifesto that articulates the conceptual foundations of Agentic Business Process Management (APM), an extension of Business Process Management (BPM) for governing autonomous agents executing processes in organizations. From a management perspective, APM represents a paradigm shift from the traditional process view of the business process, driven by the realization of process awareness and an agent-oriented abstraction, where software and human agents act as primary functional entities that perceive, reason, and act within explicit process frames. This perspective marks a shift from traditional, automation-oriented BPM toward systems in which autonomy is constrained, aligned, and made operational through process awareness.
  We introduce the core abstractions and architectural elements required to realize APM systems and elaborate on four key capabilities that such APM agents must support: framed autonomy, explainability, conversational actionability, and self-modification. These capabilities jointly ensure that agents' goals are aligned with organizational goals and that agents behave in a framed yet proactive manner in pursuing those goals. We discuss the extent to which the capabilities can be realized and identify research challenges whose resolution requires further advances in BPM, AI, and multi-agent systems. The manifesto thus serves as a roadmap for bridging these communities and for guiding the development of APM systems in practice.

AI.tldr: Proposes Agentic BPM (APM), extending BPM to govern autonomous agents with process-aware framing; identifies four capabilities (framed autonomy, explainability, conversational actionability, self-modification) and presents architectural foundations and a research agenda to align agent behavior with organizational goals; serves as a roadmap bridging BPM, AI, and multi-agent systems.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of traditional BPM in handling autonomous agents; realize process awareness and an agent-oriented abstraction to coordinate software and human actors within explicit process frames; ensure alignment with organizational goals while maintaining constrained autonomy.
AI.method: Conceptual manifesto outlining core abstractions and architectural elements; defines four capabilities required for APM agents; discusses realizability and research challenges; positions as a roadmap rather than an empirical study.
AI.result: Formalization of APM concepts and capabilities; architectural framework; assessment of feasibility; identification of research challenges; bridging BPM, AI, and MAS communities.
AI.conclusion: APM offers a paradigm shift with framed autonomy and explainability; success depends on advances across BPM, AI, and multi-agent systems; provides a roadmap for practical development.

title: Teleological Inference in Structural Causal Models via Intentional Interventions
summary: Structural causal models (SCMs) were conceived to formulate and answer causal questions. This paper shows that SCMs can also be used to formulate and answer teleological questions, concerning the intentions of a state-aware, goal-directed agent intervening in a causal system. We review limitations of previous approaches to modeling such agents, and then introduce intentional interventions, a new time-agnostic operator that induces a twin SCM we call a structural final model (SFM). SFMs treat observed values as the outcome of intentional interventions and relate them to the counterfactual conditions of those interventions (what would have happened had the agent not intervened). We show how SFMs can be used to empirically detect agents and to discover their intentions.

AI.tldr: Introduces structural final models (SFM), a time-agnostic twin-SCM to model and infer teleological questions about intentional interventions by a state-aware agent; enables linking observed outcomes to counterfactuals and empirical detection of agents and their intentions.
AI.motivation: Extend causal modeling to teleology by formalizing intentional interventions and addressing limitations of prior approaches to modeling goal-directed agents.
AI.method: Define an intentional-intervention operator that induces a twin structural causal model (SFM). In SFMs, observed values are treated as outcomes of intentional interventions and are related to the counterfactual conditions of those interventions (what would have happened had the agent not intervened).
AI.result: A formal framework (SFM) that allows the empirical detection of agents and the discovery of their intentions by relating observed data to counterfactual intervention scenarios.
AI.conclusion: SFMs extend SCMs to teleological domains, enabling empirical study of state-aware, goal-directed agents and their intentions, with implications for detection and inference of agent behavior.

title: Training-Free Sparse Attention for Fast Video Generation via Offline Layer-Wise Sparsity Profiling and Online Bidirectional Co-Clustering
summary: Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) achieve strong video generation quality but suffer from high inference cost due to dense 3D attention, leading to the development of sparse attention technologies to improve efficiency. However, existing training-free sparse attention methods in video generation still face two unresolved limitations: ignoring layer heterogeneity in attention pruning and ignoring query-key coupling in block partitioning, which hinder a better quality-speedup trade-off. In this work, we uncover a critical insight that the attention sparsity of each layer is its intrinsic property, with minor effects across different inputs. Motivated by this, we propose SVOO, a training-free Sparse attention framework for fast Video generation via Offline layer-wise sparsity profiling and Online bidirectional co-clustering. Specifically, SVOO adopts a two-stage paradigm: (i) offline layer-wise sensitivity profiling to derive intrinsic per-layer pruning levels, and (ii) online block-wise sparse attention via a novel bidirectional co-clustering algorithm. Extensive experiments on seven widely used video generation models demonstrate that SVOO achieves a superior quality-speedup trade-off over state-of-the-art methods, delivering up to $1.93\times$ speedup while maintaining a PSNR of up to 29 dB on Wan2.1.

AI.tldr: SVOO is a training-free sparse-attention framework for video diffusion transformers that uses offline layer-wise sparsity profiling and online bidirectional co-clustering to achieve a better quality–speedup trade-off, delivering up to 1.93× speedup with PSNR up to 29 dB on Wan2.1 across seven models.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Transformer (DiT) video generation suffers from high inference cost due to dense 3D attention. Existing training-free sparse-attention methods ignore (1) layer heterogeneity in pruning and (2) query–key coupling in block partitioning, limiting efficiency gains. A per-layer intrinsic sparsity property suggests a principled sparsification strategy.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (i) offline layer-wise sensitivity profiling to determine per-layer pruning levels (layer-aware sparsity); (ii) online block-wise sparse attention driven by a novel bidirectional co-clustering that jointly considers queries and keys for adaptive block partitioning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on seven video-generation models show SVOO achieves superior quality–speedup trade-offs compared to state-of-the-art training-free sparse methods, delivering up to 1.93× speedup while maintaining PSNR up to 29 dB on Wan2.1.
AI.conclusion: SVOO demonstrates that attention sparsity is an intrinsic per-layer property and that combining offline layer-wise profiling with online bidirectional co-clustering yields robust, training-free acceleration for video diffusion transformers across diverse models.

title: STEP: Scientific Time-Series Encoder Pretraining via Cross-Domain Distillation
summary: Scientific time series are central to scientific AI but are typically sparse, highly heterogeneous, and limited in scale, making unified representation learning particularly challenging. Meanwhile, foundation models pretrained on relevant time series domains such as audio, general time series, and brain signals contain rich knowledge, but their applicability to scientific signals remains underexplored. In this paper, we investigate the transferability and complementarity of foundation models from relevant time series domains, and study how to effectively leverage them to build a unified encoder for scientific time series. We first systematically evaluate relevant foundation models, showing the effectiveness of knowledge transfer to scientific tasks and their complementary strengths. Based on this observation, we propose STEP, a Scientific Time Series Encoder Pretraining framework via cross domain distillation. STEP introduces adaptive patching to handle extreme-length sequences and a statistics compensation scheme to accommodate diverse numerical scales. It further leverages cross-domain distillation to integrate knowledge from multiple foundation models into a unified encoder. By combining complementary representations across different domains, STEP learns general-purpose and transferable features tailored for scientific signals. Experiments on seven scientific time series tasks demonstrate that STEP provides both an effective structure and an effective pretraining paradigm, taking a STEP toward scientific time series representation learning.

AI.tldr: STEP is a framework for pretraining a unified encoder for scientific time series by cross-domain distillation from multiple foundation models (audio, general time series, brain signals). It uses adaptive patching for long sequences and statistics-aware scaling to handle diverse numerics, demonstrating transferability and complementary strengths across seven scientific tasks.
AI.motivation: Scientific time series are sparse, heterogeneous, and small-scale, hindering unified representation learning. Foundation models from related domains contain relevant knowledge but their applicability to scientific signals is underexplored; leveraging them could yield transferable, general-purpose representations.
AI.method: STEP pretrains a unified encoder via cross-domain distillation from multiple foundation models. It introduces adaptive patching to handle extremely long sequences and a statistics compensation scheme to accommodate diverse numeric scales, then fuses cross-domain knowledge into a single encoder to capture complementary representations across domains.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on seven scientific time series tasks shows STEP effectively leverages cross-domain knowledge, producing transferable, generalizable representations and confirming complementary strengths of different foundation models.
AI.conclusion: STEP advances scientific time series representation by providing a practical pretraining paradigm and architecture for integrating diverse-domain knowledge into a single encoder, moving toward robust, domain-spanning scientific time series representations.

title: PhysVideo: Physically Plausible Video Generation with Cross-View Geometry Guidance
summary: Recent progress in video generation has led to substantial improvements in visual fidelity, yet ensuring physically consistent motion remains a fundamental challenge. Intuitively, this limitation can be attributed to the fact that real-world object motion unfolds in three-dimensional space, while video observations provide only partial, view-dependent projections of such dynamics. To address these issues, we propose PhysVideo, a two-stage framework that first generates physics-aware orthogonal foreground videos and then synthesizes full videos with background. In the first stage, Phys4View leverages physics-aware attention to capture the influence of physical attributes on motion dynamics, and enhances spatio-temporal consistency by incorporating geometry-enhanced cross-view attention and temporal attention. In the second stage, VideoSyn uses the generated foreground videos as guidance and learns the interactions between foreground dynamics and background context for controllable video synthesis. To support training, we construct PhysMV, a dataset containing 40K scenes, each consisting of four orthogonal viewpoints, resulting in a total of 160K video sequences. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PhysVideo significantly improves physical realism and spatial-temporal coherence over existing video generation methods. Home page: https://anonymous.4open.science/w/Phys4D/.

AI.tldr: Two-stage physics-aware video generation (PhysVideo) that first produces physics-consistent orthogonal foreground videos (Phys4View: physics-aware attention, geometry-enhanced cross-view and temporal attention) and then synthesizes full videos with background (VideoSyn). Trains on PhysMV dataset (40k scenes, 4 orthogonal viewpoints; 160k sequences). Improves physical realism and spatio-temporal coherence versus prior methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world object motion is inherently 3D while video frames provide 2D projections, leading to physically inconsistent motion when generating videos. Enforcing physics-aware dynamics and geometry-consistent motion can improve realism.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: Stage 1 Phys4View generates physics-aware orthogonal foreground videos using physics-aware attention, geometry-enhanced cross-view attention, and temporal attention to enhance spatio-temporal coherence. Stage 2 VideoSyn uses the generated foreground as guidance to learn interactions between foreground dynamics and background for controllable video synthesis. Training data PhysMV with 40k scenes, each with four orthogonal viewpoints (total 160k sequences).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that PhysVideo improves physical realism and spatial-temporal coherence over existing video generation methods.
AI.conclusion: PhysVideo decouples physics-aware foreground motion from background synthesis, leveraging a physics-informed attention framework and a large multi-view dataset to achieve more realistic and controllable video generation than prior work.

title: Unmasking Algorithmic Bias in Predictive Policing: A GAN-Based Simulation Framework with Multi-City Temporal Analysis
summary:
Predictive policing systems that direct patrol resources based on algorithmically generated crime forecasts have been widely deployed across US cities, yet their tendency to encode and amplify racial disparities remains poorly understood in quantitative terms. We present a reproducible simulation framework that couples a Generative Adversarial Network GAN with a Noisy OR patrol detection model to measure how racial bias propagates through the full enforcement pipeline from crime occurrence to police contact. Using 145000 plus Part 1 crime records from Baltimore 2017 to 2019 and 233000 plus records from Chicago 2022, augmented with US Census ACS demographic data, we compute four monthly bias metrics across 264 city year mode observations: the Disparate Impact Ratio DIR, Demographic Parity Gap, Gini Coefficient, and a composite Bias Amplification Score.
  Our experiments reveal extreme and year variant bias in Baltimores detected mode, with mean annual DIR up to 15714 in 2019, moderate under detection of Black residents in Chicago DIR equals 0.22, and persistent Gini coefficients of 0.43 to 0.62 across all conditions. We further demonstrate that a Conditional Tabular GAN CTGAN debiasing approach partially redistributes detection rates but cannot eliminate structural disparity without accompanying policy intervention. Socioeconomic regression analysis confirms strong correlations between neighborhood racial composition and detection likelihood Pearson r equals 0.83 for percent White and r equals negative 0.81 for percent Black. A sensitivity analysis over patrol radius, officer count, and citizen reporting probability reveals that outcomes are most sensitive to officer deployment levels. The code and data are publicly available at this repository.

AI.tldr: A reproducible pipeline quantifies how predictive policing biases propagate from crime to police contact, revealing substantial city-year variation in racial disparity and limited debiasing efficacy.
AI.motivation: To quantify and understand how algorithmic bias in predictive policing propagates through the enforcement pipeline from crime occurrence to police contact.
AI.method: A simulation framework coupling a Generative Adversarial Network (using CTGAN) with a Noisy OR patrol-detection model. Data from Baltimore (2017–2019; 145k+ Part 1 crimes) and Chicago (2022; 233k+ crimes), augmented with US Census demographics. Computation of four monthly bias metrics across 264 city-year observations: Disparate Impact Ratio (DIR), Demographic Parity Gap, Gini Coefficient, and a Bias Amplification Score. Includes a Conditional Tabular GAN debiasing approach, socioeconomic regression analysis ( Pearson correlations: percent White r=0.83, percent Black r=-0.81 ), and a sensitivity analysis over patrol radius, officer count, and citizen reporting probability. Also reports that code and data are publicly available.
AI.result: Extreme, year-variant bias in Baltimore’s detected mode (mean annual DIR up to 15714 in 2019). Chicago shows moderate under-detection of Black residents (DIR ≈ 0.22). Persistent Gini coefficients between 0.43 and 0.62 across conditions. CTGAN debiasing partially redistributes detection rates but does not remove structural disparity without policy changes. Strong socio-economic correlations: White share r=0.83; Black share r=-0.81. Outcomes are most sensitive to officer deployment levels; code and data are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The enforcement pipeline exhibits substantial racial bias that persists despite debiasing attempts; data-driven debiasing alone is insufficient to eliminate disparities—policy interventions targeting deployment and policing practices are required.

title: MeInTime: Bridging Age Gap in Identity-Preserving Face Restoration
summary: To better preserve an individual's identity, face restoration has evolved from reference-free to reference-based approaches, which leverage high-quality reference images of the same identity to enhance identity fidelity in the restored outputs. However, most existing methods implicitly assume that the reference and degraded input are age-aligned, limiting their effectiveness in real-world scenarios where only cross-age references are available, such as historical photo restoration. This paper proposes MeInTime, a diffusion-based face restoration method that extends reference-based restoration from same-age to cross-age settings. Given one or few reference images along with an age prompt corresponding to the degraded input, MeInTime achieves faithful restoration with both identity fidelity and age consistency. Specifically, we decouple the modeling of identity and age conditions. During training, we focus solely on effectively injecting identity features through a newly introduced attention mechanism and introduce Gated Residual Fusion modules to facilitate the integration between degraded features and identity representations. At inference, we propose Age-Aware Gradient Guidance, a training-free sampling strategy, using an age-driven direction to iteratively nudge the identity-aware denoising latent toward the desired age semantic manifold. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MeInTime outperforms existing face restoration methods in both identity preservation and age consistency. Our code is available at: https://github.com/teer4/MeInTime

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based cross-age face restoration (MeInTime) uses cross-age reference images to preserve identity and match age, decouples identity and age conditioning, introduces Identity-feature injection via attention, and Gated Residual Fusion; inference uses Age-Aware Gradient Guidance; shows superior identity and age fidelity; code released.
AI.motivation: Limitations of prior reference-based restoration: assume same-age references; real-world scenarios (e.g., historical photos) require cross-age references; need to separate identity and age during modeling; improve fidelity and consistency across ages.
AI.method: Train-time: inject identity features via a dedicated attention mechanism; employ Gated Residual Fusion to fuse degraded features with identity representations; inference-time: Age-Aware Gradient Guidance—a training-free sampling strategy that nudges the identity-aware denoising latent toward the target age semantic manifold; inputs: one or few reference images plus an age prompt; outputs: restored face with preserved identity and consistent age.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show MeInTime outperforms existing face restoration methods in both identity preservation and age consistency; code available at: https://github.com/teer4/MeInTime
AI.conclusion: Proposes a practical cross-age, reference-based restoration by decoupling identity and age modeling and introducing a training-free age-guided sampling strategy; demonstrates effectiveness for real-world cross-age restoration tasks.

title: Off-Policy Learning with Limited Supply
comment: Published as a conference paper at WWW 2026
summary: We study off-policy learning (OPL) in contextual bandits, which plays a key role in a wide range of real-world applications such as recommendation systems and online advertising. Typical OPL in contextual bandits assumes an unconstrained environment where a policy can select the same item infinitely. However, in many practical applications, including coupon allocation and e-commerce, limited supply constrains items through budget limits on distributed coupons or inventory restrictions on products. In these settings, greedily selecting the item with the highest expected reward for the current user may lead to early depletion of that item, making it unavailable for future users who could potentially generate higher expected rewards. As a result, OPL methods that are optimal in unconstrained settings may become suboptimal in limited supply settings. To address the issue, we provide a theoretical analysis showing that conventional greedy OPL approaches may fail to maximize the policy performance, and demonstrate that policies with superior performance must exist in limited supply settings. Based on this insight, we introduce a novel method called Off-Policy learning with Limited Supply (OPLS). Rather than simply selecting the item with the highest expected reward, OPLS focuses on items with relatively higher expected rewards compared to the other users, enabling more efficient allocation of items with limited supply. Our empirical results on both synthetic and real-world datasets show that OPLS outperforms existing OPL methods in contextual bandit problems with limited supply.

AI.tldr: OPLS extends off-policy learning in contextual bandits to limited-supply settings by shifting from greedy per-user rewards to allocations that compare relative rewards across users, improving performance under inventory/coupon constraints.
AI.motivation: In many real-world scenarios, scarce resources (inventory, coupons) render greedy single-step decisions suboptimal since depleting high-reward items early can harm future gains; a theoretical and empirical study is needed to understand and address this gap.
AI.method: Propose Off-Policy learning with Limited Supply (OPLS). It avoids always picking the locally best item and instead prioritizes items with relatively higher expected rewards across the user population to balance allocation under scarcity. Provides theoretical analysis showing greediness can be suboptimal and demonstrates the existence of superior policies in limited supply settings; validates via experiments on synthetic and real-world data.
AI.result: Empirical results show OPLS outperforms existing OPL methods in contextual bandits with limited supply across both synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: OPLS offers a principled approach to OPL under resource constraints, improving policy performance when inventory or coupon budgets are limited; future work could explore scalability, dynamic supply, and formal regret bounds under supply constraints.

title: Evaluating Game Difficulty in Tetris Block Puzzle
summary: Tetris Block Puzzle is a single player stochastic puzzle in which a player places blocks on an 8 x 8 grid to complete lines; its popular variants have amassed tens of millions of downloads. Despite this reach, there is little principled assessment of which rule sets are more difficult. Inspired by prior work that uses AlphaZero as a strong evaluator for chess variants, we study difficulty in this domain using Stochastic Gumbel AlphaZero (SGAZ), a budget-aware planning agent for stochastic environments. We evaluate rule changes including holding block h, preview holding block p, and additional Tetris block variants using metrics such as training reward and convergence iterations. Empirically, increasing h and p reduces difficulty (higher reward and faster convergence), while adding more Tetris block variants increases difficulty, with the T-pentomino producing the largest slowdown. Through analysis, SGAZ delivers strong play under small simulation budgets, enabling efficient, reproducible comparisons across rule sets and providing a reference for future design in stochastic puzzle games.

AI.tldr: SGAZ evaluates difficulty of stochastic 8x8 Tetris rule changes; holding and preview features ease play, while adding more block variants increases difficulty, especially with the T-pentomino; results support budget-aware evaluators for design.
AI.motivation: Address lack of principled, cross-rule difficulty assessment in stochastic puzzle games; leverage AlphaZero-inspired evaluators (SGAZ) to enable fair, budget-conscious comparisons across rule sets.
AI.method: Apply Stochastic Gumbel AlphaZero (SGAZ), a budget-aware planning agent, to evaluate rule changes (holding h, preview holding p, and additional Tetris blocks) on an 8x8 grid; metrics include training reward and convergence iterations.
AI.result: Increasing h and p reduces difficulty (higher rewards, faster convergence); adding more block variants increases difficulty, with the T-pentomino causing the largest slowdown; SGAZ performs well under small simulation budgets and enables reproducible cross-rule comparisons.
AI.conclusion: SGAZ provides a principled, budget-aware evaluation framework for stochastic puzzle design and can guide future rule-set design and benchmarking.

title: Click-to-Ask: An AI Live Streaming Assistant with Offline Copywriting and Online Interactive QA
comment: 4 pages, 2 figures, Accepted at WWW2026 Demos
summary: Live streaming commerce has become a prominent form of broadcasting in the modern era. To facilitate more efficient and convenient product promotions for streamers, we present Click-to-Ask, an AI-driven assistant for live streaming commerce with complementary offline and online components. The offline module processes diverse multimodal product information, transforming complex inputs into structured product data and generating compliant promotional copywriting. During live broadcasts, the online module enables real-time responses to viewer inquiries by allowing streamers to click on questions and leveraging both the structured product information generated by the offline module and an event-level historical memory maintained in a streaming architecture. This system significantly reduces the time needed for promotional preparation, enhances content engagement, and enables prompt interaction with audience inquiries, ultimately improving the effectiveness of live streaming commerce. On our collected dataset of TikTok live stream frames, the proposed method achieves a Question Recognition Accuracy of 0.913 and a Response Quality score of 0.876, demonstrating considerable potential for practical application. The video demonstration can be viewed here: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/mWIXK-SWhiE.

AI.tldr: Two-module AI assistant for live streaming commerce (offline multimodal data processing and online real-time Q&A) that generates structured product data and compliant copy, then responds to viewer questions using memory and product data; achieves strong metrics on TikTok frames.
AI.motivation: To reduce promotional prep time and enhance real-time audience engagement in live streaming commerce by automating data extraction/media processing and providing instant, informed responses.
AI.method: Offline module processes multimodal product information into structured data and generates compliant promotional copy; Online module lets streamers click questions and receive real-time responses using the structured data and an event-level memory within a streaming architecture.
AI.result: On a collected TikTok live stream frames dataset, the method attains Question Recognition Accuracy of 0.913 and a Response Quality score of 0.876; a video demonstration is provided.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates practical potential for automating promo preparation and improving engagement in live streaming commerce, supported by solid QA and response metrics on benchmark data.

title: From ex(p) to poly: Gaussian Splatting with Polynomial Kernels
summary: Recent advancements in Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have introduced various modifications to the original kernel, resulting in significant performance improvements. However, many of these kernel changes are incompatible with existing datasets optimized for the original Gaussian kernel, presenting a challenge for widespread adoption. In this work, we address this challenge by proposing an alternative kernel that maintains compatibility with existing datasets while improving computational efficiency. Specifically, we replace the original exponential kernel with a polynomial approximation combined with a ReLU function. This modification allows for more aggressive culling of Gaussians, leading to enhanced performance across different 3DGS implementations. Our results show a notable performance improvement of 4 to 15% with negligible impact on image quality. We also provide a detailed mathematical analysis of the new kernel and discuss its potential benefits for 3DGS implementations on NPU hardware.

AI.tldr: Proposes a polynomial-ReLU kernel as a dataset-compatible alternative to the original exponential Gaussian kernel in 3D Gaussian Splatting, enabling more aggressive Gaussian culling and modest quality impact, with 4–15% performance gains and potential benefits for NPU hardware.
AI.motivation: To resolve incompatibilities between modified 3D Gaussian Splatting kernels and datasets tuned for the original kernel, by designing a kernel that preserves compatibility while improving computational efficiency.
AI.method: Replace the exponential kernel with a polynomial approximation combined with a ReLU function, enabling aggressive Gaussians culling. Provide a mathematical analysis of the new kernel and discuss implications for hardware (NPU) implementations. Evaluate performance improvements across different 3DGS pipelines.
AI.result: Reported 4–15% performance improvements with negligible impact on image quality. Demonstrated compatibility with existing datasets; offered a detailed mathematical analysis of the kernel; discussed potential benefits for NPU-based deployment.
AI.conclusion: An alternative polynomial-ReLU kernel offers efficiency gains without sacrificing dataset compatibility, supporting broader adoption of 3DGS; future work includes deeper hardware validation and cross-dataset evaluation.

title: Regret Bounds for Competitive Resource Allocation with Endogenous Costs
comment: This is Paper 7 in a 9-paper series on Super-Alignment via Wuxing Institutional Architecture. The series explores resource competition and institutional design for human-aligned AI systems
summary:
We study online resource allocation among N interacting modules over T rounds. Unlike standard online optimization, costs are endogenous: they depend on the full allocation vector through an interaction matrix W encoding pairwise cooperation and competition.
  We analyze three paradigms: (I) uniform allocation (cost-ignorant), (II) gated allocation (cost-estimating), and (III) competitive allocation via multiplicative weights update with interaction feedback (cost-revealing). Our main results establish a strict separation under adversarial sequences with bounded variation: uniform incurs Omega(T) regret, gated achieves O(T^{2/3}), and competitive achieves O(sqrt(T log N)). The performance gap stems from competitive allocation's ability to exploit endogenous cost information revealed through interactions.
  We further show that W's topology governs a computation-regret tradeoff. Full interaction (|E|=O(N^2)) yields the tightest bound but highest per-step cost, while sparse topologies (|E|=O(N)) increase regret by at most O(sqrt(log N)) while reducing per-step cost from O(N^2) to O(N). Ring-structured topologies with both cooperative and competitive links - of which the five-element Wuxing topology is canonical - minimize the computation x regret product.
  These results provide the first formal regret-theoretic justification for decentralized competitive allocation in modular architectures and establish cost endogeneity as a fundamental challenge distinct from partial observability.
  Keywords: online learning, regret bounds, resource allocation, endogenous costs, interaction topology, multiplicative weights, modular systems, Wuxing topology

AI.tldr: Online resource allocation with endogenous costs encoded by a pairwise interaction matrix W; shows a strict separation of regret across three paradigms; competitive allocation via multiplicative weights with interaction feedback achieves near-optimal O(sqrt(T log N)); topology of W drives a computation-regret tradeoff; dense vs sparse vs ring (Wuxing) topologies; first regret-theoretic justification for decentralized competitive allocation.
AI.motivation: Understand how endogeneous costs arising from interactions affect online learning in modular systems; compare cost-ignorant, cost-estimating, and cost-revealing strategies; quantify how topology shapes performance.
AI.method: Analyze three paradigms: (I) uniform, (II) gated, (III) competitive MWU with interaction feedback; derive regret bounds under adversarial sequences with bounded variation; study effect of interaction graph topology |E| on regret and per-step cost; characterize tradeoffs and special topology (Wuxing).
AI.result: Uniform incurs Omega(T) regret; gated achieves O(T^{2/3}); competitive achieves O(sqrt(T log N)); topology governs computation-regret tradeoff; full interaction yields tight bounds but high per-step cost; sparse topologies increase regret by at most O(sqrt(log N)) while reducing per-step cost from O(N^2) to O(N); ring/Wuxing topology minimizes the computation x regret product.
AI.conclusion: Provides the first formal regret-theoretic justification for decentralized competitive allocation in modular architectures; endogeneity of costs is a fundamental challenge distinct from partial observability.

title: Benchmarking PDF Parsers on Table Extraction with LLM-based Semantic Evaluation
summary:
Reliably extracting tables from PDFs is essential for large-scale scientific data mining and knowledge base construction, yet existing evaluation approaches rely on rule-based metrics that fail to capture semantic equivalence of table content. We present a benchmarking framework based on synthetically generated PDFs with precise LaTeX ground truth, using tables sourced from arXiv to ensure realistic complexity and diversity. As our central methodological contribution, we apply LLM-as-a-judge for semantic table evaluation, integrated into a matching pipeline that accommodates inconsistencies in parser outputs. Through a human validation study comprising over 1,500 quality judgments on extracted table pairs, we show that LLM-based evaluation achieves substantially higher correlation with human judgment (Pearson r=0.93) compared to Tree Edit Distance-based Similarity (TEDS, r=0.68) and Grid Table Similarity (GriTS, r=0.70). Evaluating 21 contemporary PDF parsers across 100 synthetic documents containing 451 tables reveals significant performance disparities. Our results offer practical guidance for selecting parsers for tabular data extraction and establish a reproducible, scalable evaluation methodology for this critical task.
  Code and data: https://github.com/phorn1/pdf-parse-bench Metric study and human evaluation: https://github.com/phorn1/table-metric-study

AI.tldr: A benchmarking framework for evaluating PDF table extraction using synthetic PDFs with LaTeX ground truth and LLM-based semantic evaluation, revealing higher alignment with human judgments than rule-based metrics; evaluated 21 parsers on 100 synthetic documents (451 tables); provides reproducible data/code and guidance for parser selection.
AI.motivation: Rule-based metrics fail to capture semantic equivalence of table content; existing approaches do not reliably reflect humans’ assessment of table correctness, hindering scalable evaluation of PDF table extraction.
AI.method: Generate synthetic PDFs with precise LaTeX ground truth, using arXiv-sourced tables for realism; employ LLM-as-a-judge within a matching pipeline that tolerates parser inconsistencies; validate with a human study (~1,500 judgments) to calibrate/assess the semantic metric.
AI.result: LLM-based semantic evaluation correlates with human judgment (Pearson r=0.93) significantly higher than TED-based similarity (r=0.68) and GriTS (r=0.70); reveal sizable performance gaps among 21 parsers across 100 synthetic docs (451 tables).
AI.conclusion: Provides a reproducible, scalable evaluation framework and practical guidance for selecting PDF table parsers; makes available code and data to support broader benchmarking of tabular data extraction.

title: CausalRM: Causal-Theoretic Reward Modeling for RLHF from Observational User Feedbacks
summary: Despite the success of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) in aligning language models, current reward modeling heavily relies on experimental feedback data collected from human annotators under controlled and costly conditions. In this work, we introduce observational reward modeling -- learning reward models with observational user feedback (e.g., clicks, copies, and upvotes) -- as a scalable and cost-effective alternative. We identify two fundamental challenges in this setting: (1) observational feedback is noisy due to annotation errors, which deviates it from true user preference; (2) observational feedback is biased by user preference, where users preferentially provide feedback on responses they feel strongly about, which creats a distribution shift between training and inference data. To address these challenges, we propose CausalRM, a causal-theoretic reward modeling framework that aims to learn unbiased reward models from observational feedback. To tackle challenge (1), CausalRM introduces a noise-aware surrogate loss term that is provably equivalent to the primal loss under noise-free conditions by explicitly modeling the annotation error generation process. To tackle challenge (2), CausalRM uses propensity scores -- the probability of a user providing feedback for a given response -- to reweight training samples, yielding a loss function that eliminates user preference bias. Extensive experiments across diverse LLM backbones and benchmark datasets validate that CausalRM effectively learns accurate reward signals from noisy and biased observational feedback and delivers substantial performance improvements on downstream RLHF tasks -- including a 49.2% gain on WildGuardMix and a 32.7% improvement on HarmBench. Code is available on our project website.

AI.tldr: Introduces CausalRM, a noise-aware and bias-corrected observational reward modeling framework for RLHF that learns unbiased reward signals from observational feedback by modeling annotation errors and using propensity scores; demonstrates strong improvements on downstream RLHF benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address scalability and cost constraints in RLHF by using observational user feedback (clicks, upvotes, copies) while mitigating two key issues: annotation noise and selection bias.
AI.method: Propose CausalRM with (1) a noise-aware surrogate loss that explicitly models the annotation error generation process to remain equivalent to the noise-free loss under correct assumptions, and (2) propensity-score reweighting of training samples to remove user-preference bias in feedback.
AI.result: Empirically robust reward models learned from noisy and biased observational feedback; substantial downstream RLHF gains across backbones and benchmarks (e.g., 49.2% on WildGuardMix, 32.7% on HarmBench); code released.
AI.conclusion: CausalRM offers a principled, scalable approach to reward modeling from observational feedback, addressing annotation noise and user bias to improve RLHF performance at scale.

title: Behavioral Fingerprints for LLM Endpoint Stability and Identity
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure, submitted to CAIS 2026 System Demonstrations
summary: The consistency of AI-native applications depends on the behavioral consistency of the model endpoints that power them. Traditional reliability metrics such as uptime, latency and throughput do not capture behavioral change, and an endpoint can remain "healthy" while its effective model identity changes due to updates to weights, tokenizers, quantization, inference engines, kernels, caching, routing, or hardware. We introduce Stability Monitor, a black-box stability monitoring system that periodically fingerprints an endpoint by sampling outputs from a fixed prompt set and comparing the resulting output distributions over time. Fingerprints are compared using a summed energy distance statistic across prompts, with permutation-test p-values as evidence of distribution shift aggregated sequentially to detect change events and define stability periods. In controlled validation, Stability Monitor detects changes to model family, version, inference stack, quantization, and behavioral parameters. In real-world monitoring of the same model hosted by multiple providers, we observe substantial provider-to-provider and within-provider stability differences.

AI.tldr: Proposes Stability Monitor, a black-box system to detect behavioral drift in AI model endpoints by fingerprinting outputs with a fixed prompt set and detecting distribution shifts over time.
AI.motivation: Traditional reliability metrics (uptime, latency) miss behavioral changes; model identity can drift with updates (weights, tokenizers, quantization, inference stacks, hardware), risking degraded behavior without obvious failures.
AI.method: Black-box endpoint fingerprinting: sample outputs for a fixed prompt set; summarize output distributions with a summed energy distance across prompts; use permutation-test p-values to evidence distribution shifts; aggregate p-values over time to detect change events and define stability periods.
AI.result: Controlled validation shows stability monitoring detects changes in model family, version, inference stack, quantization, and behavioral parameters. Real-world deployment across multiple providers reveals substantial provider-to-provider and within-provider stability differences.
AI.conclusion: Stability Monitor enables detection of behavioral drift in AI-native endpoints beyond traditional reliability metrics; provider stability varies, and monitoring can reveal subtle but important changes to model behavior over time.

title: Multiscale Switch for Semi-Supervised and Contrastive Learning in Medical Ultrasound Image Segmentation
comment: This is the author-submitted LaTeX version with original typesetting. The final published version (with IEEE production formatting and layout changes) is available at http://doi.org/10.1109/TNNLS.2026.3669814 under CC BY 4.0 license
summary: Medical ultrasound image segmentation faces significant challenges due to limited labeled data and characteristic imaging artifacts including speckle noise and low-contrast boundaries. While semi-supervised learning (SSL) approaches have emerged to address data scarcity, existing methods suffer from suboptimal unlabeled data utilization and lack robust feature representation mechanisms. In this paper, we propose Switch, a novel SSL framework with two key innovations: (1) Multiscale Switch (MSS) strategy that employs hierarchical patch mixing to achieve uniform spatial coverage; (2) Frequency Domain Switch (FDS) with contrastive learning that performs amplitude switching in Fourier space for robust feature representations. Our framework integrates these components within a teacher-student architecture to effectively leverage both labeled and unlabeled data. Comprehensive evaluation across six diverse ultrasound datasets (lymph nodes, breast lesions, thyroid nodules, and prostate) demonstrates consistent superiority over state-of-the-art methods. At 5\% labeling ratio, Switch achieves remarkable improvements: 80.04\% Dice on LN-INT, 85.52\% Dice on DDTI, and 83.48\% Dice on Prostate datasets, with our semi-supervised approach even exceeding fully supervised baselines. The method maintains parameter efficiency (1.8M parameters) while delivering superior performance, validating its effectiveness for resource-constrained medical imaging applications. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/jinggqu/Switch

AI.tldr: A semi-supervised ultrasound segmentation framework (Switch) with two innovations—Multiscale Switch (MSS) for hierarchical patch mixing and Frequency Domain Switch (FDS) for contrastive learning in Fourier space—achieves state-of-the-art performance with only 5% labeled data, using a compact 1.8M-parameter teacher-student model across six datasets, and even surpasses some fully supervised baselines. Code is released.
AI.motivation: Medical ultrasound suffers from limited labeled data and imaging artifacts (speckle noise, low-contrast boundaries). Existing SSL methods underutilize unlabeled data and lack robust feature representations. A more effective SSL framework tailored to ultrasound can improve segmentation performance while remaining resource-efficient.
AI.method: Two-key innovations integrated into a teacher-student SSL framework: (1) Multiscale Switch (MSS): hierarchical patch mixing to ensure uniform spatial coverage and data augmentation of unlabeled data. (2) Frequency Domain Switch (FDS): contrastive learning in Fourier space that performs amplitude switching to learn robust representations. These components are incorporated in a standard SSL setup.
AI.result: Evaluated on six diverse ultrasound datasets (lymph nodes LN-INT, breast lesions DDTI, thyroid nodules, and prostate). At 5% labeling ratio, Switch achieves Dice of 80.04% (LN-INT), 85.52% (DDTI), and 83.48% (Prostate), outperforming state-of-the-art SSL methods and even surpassing some fully supervised baselines while maintaining only 1.8M parameters.
AI.conclusion: Switch is effective, data-efficient, and computationally light, making it suitable for resource-constrained clinical settings. The approach advances SSL for ultrasound segmentation and is reproducible via publicly available code.

title: Are complicated loss functions necessary for teaching LLMs to reason?
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) highlight the importance of post training techniques for improving reasoning and mathematical ability. Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has shown promise in this domain by combining group relative advantage estimation, PPO style clipping, and KL regularization. However, its complexity raises the question of whether all components are necessary for fostering reasoning behaviors. We conduct a systematic analysis of GRPO and identify two key findings: (1) incorporating negative feedback is essential training solely on actions above a baseline limits learning; and (2) PPO style constraints, such as policy ratio clipping, are not required to improve mathematical reasoning or performance. Building on these insights, we propose REINFORCE with Group Relative Advantage (RGRA), a simplified variant that retains group relative advantage estimation but removes PPO style clipping and policy ratio terms. Experiments across standard mathematical benchmarks indicate that RGRA has the potential to achieve stronger performance than GRPO. Our results suggest that simpler REINFORCE based approaches can effectively enhance reasoning in LLMs, offering a more transparent and efficient alternative to GRPO.

AI.tldr: Negative feedback is essential; PPO clipping is unnecessary; RGRA (REINFORCE with Group Relative Advantage) can outperform GRPO on math benchmarks, suggesting simpler REINFORCE-based methods can enhance LLM reasoning.
AI.motivation: Identify which components of GRPO are actually necessary for fostering reasoning in LLMs and to develop a simpler, more efficient training objective.
AI.method: Systematically ablate GRPO components (negative feedback usage, PPO-style clipping) and compare against a simplified RGRA variant across standard mathematical benchmarks.
AI.result: Two key findings: (1) Training with negative feedback beyond a baseline is essential; training solely on actions above a baseline is insufficient. (2) PPO-style constraints (e.g., policy ratio clipping) are not required to improve mathematical reasoning or performance. RGRA, a REINFORCE-based variant using Group Relative Advantage, can achieve stronger performance than GRPO.
AI.conclusion: A simpler, transparent REINFORCE-based approach with group relative advantage can effectively enhance reasoning in LLMs, offering an efficient alternative to GRPO.

title: Man and machine: artificial intelligence and judicial decision making
summary: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies into judicial decision-making - particularly in pretrial, sentencing, and parole contexts - has generated substantial concerns about transparency, reliability, and accountability. At the same time, these developments have brought the limitations of human judgment into sharper relief and underscored the importance of understanding how judges interact with AI-based decision aids. Using criminal justice risk assessment as a focal case, we conduct a synthetic review connecting three intertwined aspects of AI's role in judicial decision-making: the performance and fairness of AI tools, the strengths and biases of human judges, and the nature of AI+human interactions. Across the fields of computer science, economics, law, criminology and psychology, researchers have made significant progress in evaluating the predictive validity of automated risk assessment instruments, documenting biases in judicial decision-making, and, to a more limited extent, examining how judges use algorithmic recommendations. While the existing empirical evidence indicates that the impact of AI decision aid tools on pretrial and sentencing decisions is modest or inexistent, our review also reveals important gaps in the canvassed literatures. Further research is needed to evaluate the performance of AI risk assessment instruments, understand how judges navigate noisy decision making environments and how individual characteristics influence judges' responses to AI advice. We argue that AI vs Human comparisons have the potential to yield new insights into both algorithmic tools and human decision-makers and advocate greater interdisciplinary integration and cross-fertilization in future research.

AI.tldr: Synthetic review of AI in judicial decision-making (pretrial, sentencing, parole) focusing on risk assessment tools, human biases, and AI–human interactions; finds modest/no impact on decisions and identifies gaps for future interdisciplinary research.
AI.motivation: Clarify how AI risk assessment tools perform, address fairness and biases, and understand how judges interact with AI aids to inform policy and practice.
AI.method: Interdisciplinary synthesis across computer science, economics, law, criminology, and psychology; evaluates predictive validity, biases in judicial decisions, and how judges use algorithmic recommendations; compares AI with human decision-makers.
AI.result: Empirical evidence suggests limited or no effect of AI decision aids on pretrial/sentencing outcomes; notable gaps in evaluating instrument performance and user interaction; need to study decision-making in noisy environments and individual differences in responses to AI advice.
AI.conclusion: AI–human comparisons can yield new insights; calls for greater interdisciplinary integration and cross-fertilization in future research to better understand tools and decision-makers.

title: Multimodal Model for Computational Pathology:Representation Learning and Image Compression
summary: Whole slide imaging (WSI) has transformed digital pathology by enabling computational analysis of gigapixel histopathology images. Recent foundation model advances have accelerated progress in computational pathology, facilitating joint reasoning across pathology images, clinical reports, and structured data. Despite this progress, challenges remain: the extreme resolution of WSIs creates computational hurdles for visual learning; limited expert annotations constrain supervised approaches; integrating multimodal information while preserving biological interpretability remains difficult; and the opacity of modeling ultra-long visual sequences hinders clinical transparency. This review comprehensively surveys recent advances in multimodal computational pathology. We systematically analyze four research directions: (1) self-supervised representation learning and structure-aware token compression for WSIs; (2) multimodal data generation and augmentation; (3) parameter-efficient adaptation and reasoning-enhanced few-shot learning; and (4) multi-agent collaborative reasoning for trustworthy diagnosis. We specifically examine how token compression enables cross-scale modeling and how multi-agent mechanisms simulate a pathologist's "Chain of Thought" across magnifications to achieve uncertainty-aware evidence fusion. Finally, we discuss open challenges and argue that future progress depends on unified multimodal frameworks integrating high-resolution visual data with clinical and biomedical knowledge to support interpretable and safe AI-assisted diagnosis.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of multimodal computational pathology for whole slide imaging (WSI), outlining four main research directions and advocating unified, interpretable, high-resolution multimodal frameworks.
AI.motivation: WSIs are ultra-high resolution and challenging for visual learning; expert annotations are scarce; integrating imaging with clinical and biomedical knowledge remains hard; model opacity on ultra-long sequences reduces clinical trust.
AI.method: Systematic review of recent literature in four areas: (1) structure-aware, self-supervised learning and token compression for WSIs, (2) multimodal data generation and augmentation, (3) parameter-efficient adaptation and reasoning-enhanced few-shot learning, (4) multi-agent collaborative reasoning for trustworthy diagnosis; evaluates how token compression enables cross-scale modeling and how multi-agent chains of thought simulate pathologists across magnifications for uncertainty-aware evidence fusion.
AI.result: Synthesis of state-of-the-art approaches across the four directions, with emphasis on cross-scale representation, multimodal fusion, efficient fine-tuning, and reasoning-driven diagnosis; highlights the potential of chain-of-thought-like multi-agent systems for interpretable uncertainty quantification; identifies gaps (computational cost, data scarcity, interpretability, safety) and practical considerations for clinical deployment.
AI.conclusion: Progress will hinge on unified multimodal frameworks that jointly leverage high-resolution visual data and clinical/biomedical knowledge to deliver interpretable, safe AI-assisted diagnoses and to overcome challenges in scalability, data efficiency, and transparency.

title: Serendipity by Design: Evaluating the Impact of Cross-domain Mappings on Human and LLM Creativity
summary: Are large language models (LLMs) creative in the same way humans are, and can the same interventions increase creativity in both? We evaluate a promising but largely untested intervention for creativity: forcing creators to draw an analogy from a random, remote source domain (''cross-domain mapping''). Human participants and LLMs generated novel features for ten daily products (e.g., backpack, TV) under two prompts: (i) cross-domain mapping, which required translating a property from a randomly assigned source (e.g., octopus, cactus, GPS), and (ii) user-need, which required proposing innovations targeting unmet user needs. We show that humans reliably benefit from randomly assigned cross-domain mappings, while LLMs, on average, generate more original ideas than humans and do not show a statistically significant effect of cross-domain mappings. However, in both systems, the impact of cross-domain mapping increases when the inspiration source becomes more semantically distant from the target. Our results highlight both the role of remote association in creative ideation and systematic differences in how humans and LLMs respond to the same intervention for creativity.

AI.tldr: Cross-domain mapping boosts human originality; no robust average effect for LLMs; effect grows with greater semantic distance between source and target.
AI.motivation: Compare how cross-domain analogical prompts influence creativity in humans versus LLMs to assess whether remote associations generalize across biological and artificial systems.
AI.method: Two prompts applied to ten daily products (e.g., backpack, TV). For each item, participants and LLMs generated novel features under (i) cross-domain mapping—translate a property from a random source domain (e.g., octopus, cactus, GPS) to the target—and (ii) user-need—propose innovations addressing unmet user needs. Originality was assessed; comparisons made between prompts and between humans and LLMs; distance between source and target varied.
AI.result: Humans reliably benefit from cross-domain mappings; LLMs, on average, generate more original ideas than humans and show no statistically significant cross-domain effect overall. The cross-domain benefit increases as the source becomes more semantically distant from the target.
AI.conclusion: Cross-domain mapping can facilitate human creative ideation, but LLMs exhibit different generative dynamics and are less uniformly sensitive to this intervention; the efficacy of cross-domain prompts is modulated by semantic distance and differs across systems.

title: Towards High-Quality Image Segmentation: Improving Topology Accuracy by Penalizing Neighbor Pixels
summary: Standard deep learning models for image segmentation cannot guarantee topology accuracy, failing to preserve the correct number of connected components or structures. This, in turn, affects the quality of the segmentations and compromises the reliability of the subsequent quantification analyses. Previous works have proposed to enhance topology accuracy with specialized frameworks, architectures, and loss functions. However, these methods are often cumbersome to integrate into existing training pipelines, they are computationally very expensive, or they are restricted to structures with tubular morphology. We present SCNP, an efficient method that improves topology accuracy by penalizing the logits with their poorest-classified neighbor, forcing the model to improve the prediction at the pixels' neighbors before allowing it to improve the pixels themselves. We show the effectiveness of SCNP across 13 datasets, covering different structure morphologies and image modalities, and integrate it into three frameworks for semantic and instance segmentation. Additionally, we show that SCNP can be integrated into several loss functions, making them improve topology accuracy. Our code can be found at https://jmlipman.github.io/SCNP-SameClassNeighborPenalization.

AI.tldr: SCNP is a lightweight, plug-in penalty for segmentation that forces improvements in the poorest-classified neighbor pixels before updating a pixel, improving topology accuracy across diverse datasets and architectures; integrates with multiple losses; code released.
AI.motivation: Standard segmentation models struggle to preserve topology (correct number of components/structures), and existing topology-enhancing methods are costly or limited to tubular morphologies. A scalable, easy-to-integrate method is needed to enforce topology correctly during training.
AI.method: Introduce Same-Class Neighbor Penalization (SCNP): for each pixel, identify its poorest-classified neighbor and penalize the pixel's logits using that neighbor, encouraging the model to first correct errors in neighboring pixels before changing the target pixel. This approach is designed to be modular and compatible with semantic and instance segmentation frameworks and with various loss functions.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness on 13 datasets with diverse morphologies and modalities. SCNP integrates into three segmentation frameworks and can augment multiple loss functions, consistently improving topology accuracy. Code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: SCNP offers an efficient, versatile approach to enhance topology accuracy in segmentation, with broad compatibility and easy integration into existing training pipelines.

title: Automatic Configuration of LLM Post-Training Pipelines
summary: LLM post-training pipelines that combine supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning are difficult to configure under realistic compute budgets: the configuration space is high-dimensional and heterogeneous, stages are strongly coupled, and each end-to-end evaluation is expensive. We propose AutoPipe, a budget-aware two-stage framework for configuration selection in LLM post-training. Offline, AutoPipe learns a dataset-conditioned learning-to-rank surrogate from historical runs, capturing within-dataset preferences and providing transferable guidance toward promising regions of the configuration space.
