Online, for a new dataset, AutoPipe uses the offline guidance to steer Bayesian optimization and models dataset-specific deviations with a Gaussian-process residual surrogate. To reduce evaluation cost, each trial is early-stopped and scored by a learned predictor that maps early training signals to a low-cost proxy for final post-training performance. Experiments on biomedical reasoning tasks show that AutoPipe consistently outperforms offline-only baselines and achieves comparable performance with the strongest online HPO baselines while using less than 10\% of their computational cost.

AI.tldr: AutoPipe is a budget-aware, two-stage configuration method for LLM post-training pipelines, combining offline learning-to-rank guidance with online Bayesian optimization augmented by a Gaussian-process residual and cheap proxy-based early-stopping; it achieves strong performance with under 10% compute on biomedical reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Configuring LLM post-training pipelines (supervised fine-tuning plus reinforcement learning) is difficult due to high-dimensional, heterogeneous configuration spaces, strong stage coupling, and expensive end-to-end evaluations; a budget-aware, data-driven approach is needed to efficiently navigate configurations.
AI.method: Stage 1 offline: learn a dataset-conditioned learning-to-rank surrogate from historical runs to capture within-dataset preferences and provide transferable guidance. Stage 2 online: for a new dataset, use offline guidance to steer Bayesian optimization and model dataset-specific deviations with a Gaussian-process residual surrogate. To cut cost, each trial is early-stopped and scored by a learned predictor that maps early training signals to a cheap proxy for final performance.
AI.result: Experiments on biomedical reasoning tasks show AutoPipe consistently outperforms offline-only baselines and achieves performance comparable to the strongest online HPO baselines while using less than 10% of their computational cost.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage, budget-aware configuration framework with transferable offline guidance and online adaptation enables efficient and competitive LLM post-training pipelines, reducing compute substantially while maintaining high performance. The approach appears effective in biomedical reasoning settings and suggests potential generalization to other domains.

title: LuMamba: Latent Unified Mamba for Electrode Topology-Invariant and Efficient EEG Modeling
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) enables non-invasive monitoring of brain activity across clinical and neurotechnology applications, yet building foundation models for EEG remains challenging due to \emph{differing electrode topologies} and \emph{computational scalability}, as Transformer architectures incur quadratic sequence complexity. As a joint solution, we propose \textbf{LuMamba} (\textbf{L}atent \textbf{U}nified \textbf{Mamba}), a self-supervised framework combining topology-invariant encodings with linear-complexity state-space modeling, using LUNA's learned-query cross-attention mechanism for channel unification~\cite{luna}, and FEMBA's bidirectional Mamba blocks for efficient temporal modeling~\cite{femba}. Within this architecture, we provide the first systematic investigation of the Latent-Euclidean Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (LeJEPA) for biosignal learning. Pre-trained on over 21,000 hours of unlabeled EEG from the TUEG corpus, LuMamba is evaluated on five downstream tasks spanning abnormality detection, artifact recognition, and mental condition classification across electrode configurations ranging from 16 to 26 channels. In the pre-training objective, masked reconstruction alone yields structured but less generalizable representations, while LeJEPA alone produces diffuse embeddings; combining both objectives achieves the most robust performance. With only 4.6M parameters, LuMamba attains 80.99\% balanced accuracy on TUAB and achieves state-of-art performance on Alzheimer's detection (0.97 AUPR), while requiring \textbf{377$\times$ fewer FLOPS} than state-of-art models at equivalent sequence lengths and scaling to \textbf{12$\times$ longer sequences} before reaching typical GPU memory limits. Code is available at https://github.com/pulp-bio/biofoundation

AI.tldr: LuMamba: a lightweight, topology-aware EEG foundation model that blends topology-invariant encodings with linear-time state-space temporal modeling, achieving strong downstream performance with far lower FLOPS and longer sequence support.
AI.motivation: Address two core challenges in EEG foundation modeling: (1) differing electrode topologies across datasets and devices, and (2) computational scalability given quadratic Transformer complexity.
AI.method: Integrates LUNA's learned-query cross-attention for channel unification with FEMBA's bidirectional Mamba blocks for efficient temporal modeling. Systematically investigates Latent-Euclidean Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (LeJEPA) for biosignals. Pretrained on 21k hours of unlabeled EEG (TUEG). Evaluated on five downstream tasks across 16–26 channel configurations. Pretraining objectives include masked reconstruction and LeJEPA, with a final joint objective.
AI.result: 4.6M parameters; 80.99% balanced accuracy on TUAB; Alzheimer’s detection 0.97 AUPR; 377x fewer FLOPS than state-of-the-art at equivalent sequence lengths; scales to 12x longer sequences before GPU memory limits. Code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing topology-invariant encoding and linear-complexity temporal modeling yields robust, scalable EEG foundation representations, achieving state-of-the-art performance on select tasks while dramatically reducing computational requirements.

title: Ontology-Guided Diffusion for Zero-Shot Visual Sim2Real Transfer
summary: Bridging the simulation-to-reality (sim2real) gap remains challenging as labelled real-world data is scarce. Existing diffusion-based approaches rely on unstructured prompts or statistical alignment, which do not capture the structured factors that make images look real. We introduce Ontology- Guided Diffusion (OGD), a neuro-symbolic zero-shot sim2real image translation framework that represents realism as structured knowledge. OGD decomposes realism into an ontology of interpretable traits -- such as lighting and material properties -- and encodes their relationships in a knowledge graph. From a synthetic image, OGD infers trait activations and uses a graph neural network to produce a global embedding. In parallel, a symbolic planner uses the ontology traits to compute a consistent sequence of visual edits needed to narrow the realism gap. The graph embedding conditions a pretrained instruction-guided diffusion model via cross-attention, while the planned edits are converted into a structured instruction prompt. Across benchmarks, our graph-based embeddings better distinguish real from synthetic imagery than baselines, and OGD outperforms state-of-the-art diffusion methods in sim2real image translations. Overall, OGD shows that explicitly encoding realism structure enables interpretable, data-efficient, and generalisable zero-shot sim2real transfer.

AI.tldr: Ontology-Guided Diffusion (OGD) uses a structured realism ontology and graph reasoning to steer diffusion-based sim2real image translation in a zero-shot, data-efficient manner, improving realism and interpretability.
AI.motivation: To bridge the sim2real gap with scarce real-labelled data by capturing realism as structured knowledge rather than unstructured prompts or statistics.
AI.method: Construct an ontology of realism traits (lighting, material properties, etc.) and a knowledge graph; use a graph neural network to extract a global embedding from a synthetic image; employ a symbolic planner to compute a sequence of visual edits; condition a pretrained instruction-guided diffusion model via cross-attention with the graph embedding and translate planned edits into structured prompts.
AI.result: Graph embeddings more effectively distinguish real vs. synthetic images than baselines; OGD achieves state-of-the-art performance in sim2real image translation across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly encoding realism structure enables interpretable, data-efficient, and generalisable zero-shot sim2real transfer.

title: Signals of Success and Struggle: Early Prediction and Physiological Signatures of Human Performance across Task Complexity
summary: User performance is crucial in interactive systems, capturing how effectively users engage with task execution. Prospectively predicting performance enables the timely identification of users struggling with task demands. While ocular and cardiac signals are widely used to characterise performance-relevant visual behaviour and physiological activation, their potential for early prediction and for revealing the physiological mechanisms underlying performance differences remains underexplored. We conducted a within-subject experiment in a game environment with naturally unfolding complexity, using early ocular and cardiac signals to predict later performance and to examine physiological and self-reported group differences. Results show that the ocular-cardiac fusion model achieves a balanced accuracy of 0.86, and the ocular-only model shows comparable predictive power. High performers exhibited targeted gaze and adjusted visual sampling, and sustained more stable cardiac activation as demands intensified, with a more positive affective experience. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of cross-session prediction from early physiology, providing interpretable insights into performance variation and facilitating future proactive intervention.

AI.tldr: Early ocular and cardiac signals can predict later task performance; a fusion model of ocular and cardiac data achieves high predictive power (balanced accuracy ~0.86), with ocular data alone performing comparably.
AI.motivation: To enable early identification of performance difficulties and to uncover physiological mechanisms behind performance differences in interactive tasks; existing work has not extensively explored early physiology for prediction.
AI.method: Within-subject experiment in a game environment with naturally unfolding complexity; collect early ocular and cardiac data; build predictive models (ocular-only and ocular–cardiac fusion); evaluate cross-session prediction and analyze group differences via self-reports.
AI.result: The ocular–cardiac fusion model achieves balanced accuracy of 0.86; ocular-only model shows comparable predictive power; high performers exhibit targeted gaze, adaptive sampling, and more stable cardiac activation under increasing demands, with more positive affect.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of cross-session performance prediction from early physiology; provides interpretable insights into performance variation and supports potential proactive interventions in interactive systems.

title: How Uncertainty Estimation Scales with Sampling in Reasoning Models
summary:
Uncertainty estimation is critical for deploying reasoning language models, yet remains poorly understood under extended chain-of-thought reasoning. We study parallel sampling as a fully black-box approach using verbalized confidence and self-consistency. Across three reasoning models and 17 tasks spanning mathematics, STEM, and humanities, we characterize how these signals scale.
  Both self-consistency and verbalized confidence scale in reasoning models, but self-consistency exhibits lower initial discrimination and lags behind verbalized confidence under moderate sampling. Most uncertainty gains, however, arise from signal combination: with just two samples, a hybrid estimator improves AUROC by up to $+12$ on average and already outperforms either signal alone even when scaled to much larger budgets, after which returns diminish. These effects are domain-dependent: in mathematics, the native domain of RLVR-style post-training, reasoning models achieve higher uncertainty quality and exhibit both stronger complementarity and faster scaling than in STEM or humanities.

AI.tldr: A two-signal hybrid estimator combining verbalized confidence and self-consistency provides substantial uncertainty gains for reasoning LMs; two samples yield large AUROC improvements and outperform single signals, with diminishing returns; gains are domain-dependent, strongest in mathematics.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty estimation is essential for safe deployment of reasoning LMs with extended chain-of-thought. The study investigates black-box, parallel sampling using verbalized confidence and self-consistency to understand scaling of uncertainty signals.
AI.method: Evaluation across three reasoning models and 17 tasks spanning mathematics, STEM, and humanities. Compares verbalized confidence and self-consistency as black-box signals under parallel sampling. Introduces a hybrid estimator combining the two signals. Measures AUROC as uncertainty quality, examines performance at small budgets (two samples) and larger budgets, and analyzes domain dependence.
AI.result: Both signals scale with sampling, but self-consistency has lower initial discrimination and lags behind verbalized confidence under moderate sampling. Most gains arise from combining signals: with two samples, the hybrid estimator improves AUROC by up to +12 on average and outperforms either signal alone at larger budgets, though returns diminish beyond a point. Effects are domain-dependent: mathematics shows higher uncertainty quality and stronger complementarity and faster scaling than STEM or humanities.
AI.conclusion: Uncertainty gains are driven by complementarity between signals; combining verbalized confidence and self-consistency yields the strongest performance, especially with small sample budgets. Domain matters, with mathematics offering the greatest benefits due to its native RLVR-style post-training. Practical guidance favors early, multi-signal fusion for robust uncertainty estimation in reasoning LMs.

title: EdgeCrafter: Compact ViTs for Edge Dense Prediction via Task-Specialized Distillation
summary: Deploying high-performance dense prediction models on resource-constrained edge devices remains challenging due to strict limits on computation and memory. In practice, lightweight systems for object detection, instance segmentation, and pose estimation are still dominated by CNN-based architectures such as YOLO, while compact Vision Transformers (ViTs) often struggle to achieve similarly strong accuracy efficiency tradeoff, even with large scale pretraining. We argue that this gap is largely due to insufficient task specific representation learning in small scale ViTs, rather than an inherent mismatch between ViTs and edge dense prediction. To address this issue, we introduce EdgeCrafter, a unified compact ViT framework for edge dense prediction centered on ECDet, a detection model built from a distilled compact backbone and an edge-friendly encoder decoder design. On the COCO dataset, ECDet-S achieves 51.7 AP with fewer than 10M parameters using only COCO annotations. For instance segmentation, ECInsSeg achieves performance comparable to RF-DETR while using substantially fewer parameters. For pose estimation, ECPose-X reaches 74.8 AP, significantly outperforming YOLO26Pose-X (71.6 AP) despite the latter's reliance on extensive Objects365 pretraining. These results show that compact ViTs, when paired with task-specialized distillation and edge-aware design, can be a practical and competitive option for edge dense prediction. Code is available at: https://intellindust-ai-lab.github.io/projects/EdgeCrafter/

AI.tldr: EdgeCrafter presents a compact ViT framework for edge dense prediction (ECDet/ECInsSeg/ECPose-X) that matches or surpasses CNNs and larger ViTs on COCO with under 10M parameters, using task-specific distillation and edge-aware encoder-decoder design.
AI.motivation: Gaps in accuracy-efficiency for edge dense prediction when using compact ViTs; belief that insufficient task-specific representation learning—not inherent ViT limitation—limits performance.
AI.method: Introduce EdgeCrafter, centered on ECDet; employs distilled compact backbone and edge-friendly encoder-decoder; task-specific distillation; evaluate on COCO for object detection, instance segmentation, and pose estimation; compare with CNN baselines and pretraining regimes.
AI.result: ECDet-S achieves 51.7 AP with <10M parameters on COCO; ECInsSeg comparable to RF-DETR with far fewer params; ECPose-X 74.8 AP beats YOLO26Pose-X (71.6 AP) even without extensive pretraining; code released.
AI.conclusion: Compact ViTs, when augmented with task-specific distillation and edge-aware design, offer a practical and competitive option for edge dense prediction; EdgeCrafter demonstrates viability and encourages further development.

title: Seasoning Generative Models for a Generalization Aftertaste
summary: The use of discriminators to train or fine-tune generative models has proven to be a rather successful framework. A notable example is Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) that minimize a loss incurred by training discriminators along with other paradigms that boost generative models via discriminators that satisfy weak learner constraints. More recently, even diffusion models have shown advantages with some kind of discriminator guidance. In this work, we extend a strong-duality result related to $f$-divergences which gives rise to a discriminator-guided recipe that allows us to \textit{refine} any generative model. We then show that the refined generative models provably improve generalization, compared to its non-refined counterpart. In particular, our analysis reveals that the gap in generalization is improved based on the Rademacher complexity of the discriminator set used for refinement. Our recipe subsumes a recently introduced score-based diffusion approach (Kim et al., 2022) that has shown great empirical success, however allows us to shed light on the generalization guarantees of this method by virtue of our analysis. Thus, our work provides a theoretical validation for existing work, suggests avenues for new algorithms, and contributes to our understanding of generalization in generative models at large.

AI.tldr: Discriminator-guided refinement of generative models via an f-divergence strong-duality framework leads to provable generalization improvements; the method generalizes existing score-based diffusion approaches and links refinement quality to the discriminator set's Rademacher complexity.
AI.motivation: Although discriminators are central to GANs and diffusion-model guidance, a rigorous, general theory tying discriminator-based refinement to generalization is lacking. This work provides a strong-duality, f-divergence-based recipe to refine any generator and analyzes its impact on generalization.
AI.method: Extend a strong-duality result for f-divergences to derive a discriminator-guided refinement recipe applicable to arbitrary generative models. Show that refined models have improved generalization bounds, with the improvement tied to the Rademacher complexity of the discriminator family. Demonstrate that the framework subsumes a score-based diffusion approach (Kim et al., 2022).
AI.result: Refined generative models provably exhibit better generalization than their non-refined counterparts, with the generalization gap reduced in proportion to the discriminator set's Rademacher complexity. The approach also provides theoretical understanding for diffusion-guided methods.
AI.conclusion: The work provides theoretical validation for discriminator-guided refinement, offers guidance for new algorithms, and deepens understanding of generalization in generative modeling.

title: Implicit Patterns in LLM-Based Binary Analysis
summary: Binary vulnerability analysis is increasingly performed by LLM-based agents in an iterative, multi-pass manner, with the model as the core decision-maker. However, how such systems organize exploration over hundreds of reasoning steps remains poorly understood, due to limited context windows and implicit token-level behaviors. We present the first large-scale, trace-level study showing that multi-pass LLM reasoning gives rise to structured, token-level implicit patterns. Analyzing 521 binaries with 99,563 reasoning steps, we identify four dominant patterns: early pruning, path-dependent lock-in, targeted backtracking, and knowledge-guided prioritization that emerge implicitly from reasoning traces. These token-level implicit patterns serve as an abstraction of LLM reasoning: instead of explicit control-flow or predefined heuristics, exploration is organized through implicit decisions regulating path selection, commitment, and revision. Our analysis shows these patterns form a stable, structured system with distinct temporal roles and measurable characteristics. Our results provide the first systematic characterization of LLM-driven binary analysis and a foundation for more reliable analysis systems.

AI.tldr: A large-scale trace-level study of LLM-driven binary vulnerability analysis reveals four token-level implicit patterns—early pruning, path-dependent lock-in, targeted backtracking, and knowledge-guided prioritization—demonstrating structured exploration and enabling more reliable systems.
AI.motivation: Understanding how multi-pass LLM reasoning organizes exploration across hundreds of steps in binary analysis and how context-window limits and implicit token-level decisions shape performance.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of 521 binaries across 99,563 reasoning steps, extracting and characterizing token-level patterns from reasoning traces; identify temporal roles and measurable characteristics.
AI.result: Four dominant patterns identified: early pruning, path-dependent lock-in, targeted backtracking, and knowledge-guided prioritization; patterns emerge implicitly and form a stable, structured system guiding path selection, commitment, and revision.
AI.conclusion: First systematic characterization of LLM-driven binary analysis; provides foundation for more reliable and robust analysis systems.

title: 6Bit-Diffusion: Inference-Time Mixed-Precision Quantization for Video Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion transformers have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in generating videos. However, their practical deployment is severely constrained by high memory usage and computational cost. Post-Training Quantization provides a practical way to reduce memory usage and boost computation speed. Existing quantization methods typically apply a static bit-width allocation, overlooking the quantization difficulty of activations across diffusion timesteps, leading to a suboptimal trade-off between efficiency and quality. In this paper, we propose a inference time NVFP4/INT8 Mixed-Precision Quantization framework. We find a strong linear correlation between a block's input-output difference and the quantization sensitivity of its internal linear layers. Based on this insight, we design a lightweight predictor that dynamically allocates NVFP4 to temporally stable layers to maximize memory compression, while selectively preserving INT8 for volatile layers to ensure robustness. This adaptive precision strategy enables aggressive quantization without compromising generation quality. Beside this, we observe that the residual between the input and output of a Transformer block exhibits high temporal consistency across timesteps. Leveraging this temporal redundancy, we introduce Temporal Delta Cache (TDC) to skip computations for these invariant blocks, further reducing the computational cost. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves 1.92$\times$ end-to-end acceleration and 3.32$\times$ memory reduction, setting a new baseline for efficient inference in Video DiTs.

AI.tldr: Adaptive mixed-precision quantization (NVFP4/INT8) with a Temporal Delta Cache for diffusion-based video transformers, achieving near 2x speedup and over 3x memory reduction.
AI.motivation: Diffusion transformers for video generation incur high memory and computation; static quantization harms quality; need adaptive, temporally-aware quantization to balance efficiency and fidelity.
AI.method: A lightweight predictor dynamically assigns NVFP4 to temporally stable layers and preserves INT8 for volatile layers based on a found linear correlation between a block's input–output difference and internal quantization sensitivity. Introduces Temporal Delta Cache (TDC) to skip computations on temporally invariant transformer blocks by exploiting temporal redundancy.
AI.result: End-to-end acceleration of 1.92x and memory reduction of 3.32x on Video Diffusion Transformers (Video DiTs). Baseline for efficient inference in diffusion-based video generation.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive mixed-precision quantization combined with temporal computation reuse can aggressively compress memory and speed without sacrificing generation quality, establishing a new efficiency baseline for diffusion-based video models.

title: A Model Ensemble-Based Post-Processing Framework for Fairness-Aware Prediction
summary: Striking an optimal balance between predictive performance and fairness continues to be a fundamental challenge in machine learning. In this work, we propose a post-processing framework that facilitates fairness-aware prediction by leveraging model ensembling. Designed to operate independently of any specific model internals, our approach is widely applicable across various learning tasks, model architectures, and fairness definitions. Through extensive experiments spanning classification, regression, and survival analysis, we demonstrate that the framework effectively enhances fairness while maintaining, or only minimally affecting, predictive accuracy.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic post-processing ensembling framework for fairness that improves fairness across tasks with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Fairness vs. predictive performance is a core ML challenge; existing methods often rely on specific models or internals, limiting generality. A generic, post-processing, ensemble-based approach could be applied broadly across tasks and definitions of fairness.
AI.method: A post-processing framework that leverages model ensembling and operates independently of any model internals, enabling applicability across various learning tasks, architectures, and fairness definitions.
AI.result: Empirical studies across classification, regression, and survival analysis show the framework enhances fairness while preserving predictive accuracy, achieving fairness gains with little to no degradation in performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework is widely applicable and effective for fairness-aware prediction, enabling broad applicability across tasks, models, and fairness notions without requiring changes to underlying models.

title: D5P4: Partition Determinantal Point Process for Diversity in Parallel Discrete Diffusion Decoding
summary: Discrete diffusion models are promising alternatives to autoregressive approaches for text generation, yet their decoding methods remain under-studied. Standard decoding methods for autoregressive models, such as beam search, do not directly apply to iterative denoising, and existing diffusion decoding techniques provide limited control over in-batch diversity. To bridge this gap, we introduce a generalized beam-search framework for discrete diffusion that generates candidates in parallel and supports modular beam-selection objectives. As a diversity-focused instantiation, we propose D5P4, which formulates the selection step as MAP inference over a Determinantal Point Process. Leveraging a scalable greedy solver, D5P4 maintains multi-GPU compatibility and enables an explicit trade-off between model probability and target diversity with near-zero compute overhead. Experiments on free-form generation and question answering demonstrate that D5P4 improves diversity over strong baselines while maintaining competitive generation quality.

AI.tldr: A generalized beam-search for discrete diffusion enables parallel candidate generation and modular beam-selection, with a diversity-focused instantiation D5P4 using Determinantal Point Process (DPP) for MAP-based selection; achieves improved diversity with near-zero compute overhead and competitive quality.
AI.motivation: Discrete diffusion decoding lacks the parallelized, diversity-controllable decoding framework of autoregressive models. Existing diffusion decoding methods offer limited in-batch diversity control and do not easily scale to multi-GPU settings.
AI.method: Introduce a generalized beam-search framework for discrete diffusion that generates candidates in parallel and supports modular beam-selection objectives. Propose D5P4, a diversity-focused instantiation that formulates the selection step as MAP inference over a DETerminantal Point Process (DPP) and solves it with a scalable greedy algorithm. The approach is designed to be multi-GPU compatible and to allow an explicit trade-off between model probability and diversity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on free-form generation and question answering shows that D5P4 increases diversity relative to strong baselines while maintaining competitive generation quality, with near-zero additional compute overhead.
AI.conclusion: D5P4 provides scalable, controllable diversity for discrete diffusion decoding and demonstrates that a DPP-based selection mechanism can be integrated into a parallel beam-search framework without sacrificing efficiency.

title: WeNLEX: Weakly Supervised Natural Language Explanations for Multilabel Chest X-ray Classification
summary: Natural language explanations provide an inherently human-understandable way to explain black-box models, closely reflecting how radiologists convey their diagnoses in textual reports. Most works explicitly supervise the explanation generation process using datasets annotated with explanations. Thus, though plausible, the generated explanations are not faithful to the model's reasoning. In this work, we propose WeNLEX, a weakly supervised model for the generation of natural language explanations for multilabel chest X-ray classification. Faithfulness is ensured by matching images generated from their corresponding natural language explanations with original images, in the black-box model's feature space. Plausibility is maintained via distribution alignment with a small database of clinician-annotated explanations. We empirically demonstrate, through extensive validation on multiple metrics to assess faithfulness, simulatability, diversity, and plausibility, that WeNLEX is able to produce faithful and plausible explanations, using as little as 5 ground-truth explanations per diagnosis. Furthermore, WeNLEX can operate in both post-hoc and in-model settings. In the latter, i.e., when the multilabel classifier is trained together with the rest of the network, WeNLEX improves the classification AUC of the standalone classifier by 2.21%, thus showing that adding interpretability to the training process can actually increase the downstream task performance. Additionally, simply by changing the database, WeNLEX explanations are adaptable to any target audience, and we showcase this flexibility by training a layman version of WeNLEX, where explanations are simplified for non-medical users.

AI.tldr: WeNLEX is a weakly supervised framework that generates faithful and plausible natural language explanations for multilabel chest X-ray classification, using image-generation fidelity and clinician-explanation distribution alignment; it works post-hoc or end-to-end and can improve AUC.
AI.motivation: Existing explanations rely on annotated explanations, risking misalignment with the model's reasoning; need faithful, human-understandable explanations in radiology.
AI.method: Propose WeNLEX, a weakly supervised model for NL explanations; faithfulness via image reconstruction from explanations compared to original images in the model's feature space; plausibility via distribution alignment with a small clinician-annotated explanation database; supports post-hoc and in-model training; domain adaptation to layperson explanations via database switching.
AI.result: Empirical validation across metrics for faithfulness, simulability, diversity, plausibility; effective with as few as 5 ground-truth explanations per diagnosis; in-model training yields 2.21% AUC improvement over standalone classifier.
AI.conclusion: WeNLEX can produce faithful and plausible explanations with limited supervision, is adaptable to audiences by database choice, and can enhance downstream task performance when integrated into the classifier.

title: DriftGuard: Mitigating Asynchronous Data Drift in Federated Learning
summary: In real-world Federated Learning (FL) deployments, data distributions on devices that participate in training evolve over time. This leads to asynchronous data drift, where different devices shift at different times and toward different distributions. Mitigating such drift is challenging: frequent retraining incurs high computational cost on resource-constrained devices, while infrequent retraining degrades performance on drifting devices. We propose DriftGuard, a federated continual learning framework that efficiently adapts to asynchronous data drift. DriftGuard adopts a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) inspired architecture that separates shared parameters, which capture globally transferable knowledge, from local parameters that adapt to group-specific distributions. This design enables two complementary retraining strategies: (i) global retraining, which updates the shared parameters when system-wide drift is identified, and (ii) group retraining, which selectively updates local parameters for clusters of devices identified via MoE gating patterns, without sharing raw data. Experiments across multiple datasets and models show that DriftGuard matches or exceeds state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing total retraining cost by up to 83%. As a result, it achieves the highest accuracy per unit retraining cost, improving over the strongest baseline by up to 2.3x. DriftGuard is available for download from https://github.com/blessonvar/DriftGuard.

AI.tldr: DriftGuard introduces a mixture-of-experts–inspired FL model to handle asynchronous data drift by coupling global shared parameters with local, cluster-specific parameters, enabling two retraining modes and reducing retraining cost while maintaining high accuracy.
AI.motivation: In real-world FL, client data drift evolves unevenly over time, causing either frequent costly retraining or degraded performance if retraining is infrequent.
AI.method: Adopts a Mixture-of-Experts–inspired architecture separating shared/global parameters from local/group-specific parameters. Employs two retraining strategies: (i) global retraining to update shared parameters upon system-wide drift, and (ii) group retraining to selectively update local parameters for device clusters identified via MoE gating, without sharing raw data.
AI.result: Empirical results show accuracy comparable to or surpassing state-of-the-art, with retraining cost reduced by up to 83%; achieves the highest accuracy per retraining cost, up to 2.3× improvement over the strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: DriftGuard provides an efficient, adaptable FL framework for asynchronous data drift using MoE-based parameter separation and dual retraining strategies; code is available at the provided GitHub repository.

title: cuGenOpt: A GPU-Accelerated General-Purpose Metaheuristic Framework for Combinatorial Optimization
comment: 28 pages, 9 figures. Code available at https://github.com/L-yang-yang/cugenopt
summary:
Combinatorial optimization problems arise in logistics, scheduling, and resource allocation, yet existing approaches face a fundamental trade-off among generality, performance, and usability. We present cuGenOpt, a GPU-accelerated general-purpose metaheuristic framework that addresses all three dimensions simultaneously.
  At the engine level, cuGenOpt adopts a "one block evolves one solution" CUDA architecture with a unified encoding abstraction (permutation, binary, integer), a two-level adaptive operator selection mechanism, and hardware-aware resource management. At the extensibility level, a user-defined operator registration interface allows domain experts to inject problem-specific CUDA search operators. At the usability level, a JIT compilation pipeline exposes the framework as a pure-Python API, and an LLM-based modeling assistant converts natural-language problem descriptions into executable solver code.
  Experiments across five thematic suites on three GPU architectures (T4, V100, A800) show that cuGenOpt outperforms general MIP solvers by orders of magnitude, achieves competitive quality against specialized solvers on instances up to n=150, and attains 4.73% gap on TSP-442 within 30s. Twelve problem types spanning five encoding variants are solved to optimality. Framework-level optimizations cumulatively reduce pcb442 gap from 36% to 4.73% and boost VRPTW throughput by 75-81%.
  Code: https://github.com/L-yang-yang/cugenopt

AI.tldr: cuGenOpt is a GPU-accelerated general-purpose metaheuristic framework that unifies encoding, adaptive operators, and GPU resource management to deliver high generality, performance, and usability; it supports user-defined CUDA operators, JIT Python API, and LLM-assisted problem modeling.
AI.motivation: To address the persistent trade-off among generality, performance, and usability in combinatorial optimization by providing an extensible, GPU-accelerated framework with accessible interfaces and problem-specific operator customization.
AI.method: Engine: 'one block evolves one solution' CUDA design; unified encoding (permutation, binary, integer); two-level adaptive operator selection; hardware-aware resource management. Extensibility: user-defined operator registration for problem-specific CUDA operators. Usability: JIT compilation exposing a pure-Python API; LLM-based modeling assistant converts natural-language problem descriptions into executable solver code. Evaluation across five thematic suites on three GPU architectures (T4, V100, A800); comparisons to general MIP solvers and specialized solvers; 12 problem types solved to optimality; notable improvements in pcb442 gap and VRPTW throughput.
AI.result: Outperforms general MIP solvers by orders of magnitude; competitive quality against specialized solvers on instances up to n=150; achieves 4.73% gap on TSP-442 within 30 seconds; twelve problem types solved to optimality; pcb442 gap reduced from 36% to 4.73%; VRPTW throughput increased by 75–81%; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: cuGenOpt demonstrates a versatile, efficient, and user-friendly GPU-based metaheuristic framework with broad applicability across encoding types and problem domains; the architecture and tooling address the three core dimensions (generality, performance, usability) with demonstrated empirical gains.

title: Authority-Level Priors: An Under-Specified Constraint in Hierarchical Predictive Processing
summary: Hierarchical predictive processing explains adaptive behaviour through precision-weighted inference. Explicit belief revision often fails to produce corresponding changes in stress reactivity or autonomic regulation. This asymmetry suggests the framework leaves under-specified a governance-level constraint concerning which identity-level hypotheses regulate autonomic and behavioural control under uncertainty. We introduce Authority-Level Priors (ALPs) as meta-structural constraints defining a regulatory-admissible subset (Hauth, a subset of H) of identity-level hypotheses. ALPs are not additional representational states nor hyperpriors over precision; they constrain which hypotheses are admissible for regulatory control. Precision determines influence conditional on admissibility; ALPs determine admissibility itself. This explains why explicit belief updating modifies representational beliefs while autonomic threat responses remain stable. A computational formalisation restricts policy optimisation to policies generated by authorised hypotheses, yielding testable predictions concerning stress-reactivity dynamics, recovery time constants, compensatory control engagement, and behavioural persistence. Neurobiologically, ALPs manifest through distributed prefrontal arbitration and control networks. The proposal is compatible with variational active inference and introduces no additional inferential operators, instead formalising a boundary condition required for determinate identity-regulation mapping. The model generates falsifiable predictions: governance shifts should produce measurable changes in stress-reactivity curves, recovery dynamics, compensatory cognitive effort, and behavioural change durability. ALPs are advanced as an architectural hypothesis to be evaluated through computational modelling and longitudinal stress-induction paradigms.

AI.tldr: ALPs are meta-structural authority constraints that govern which identity-level hypotheses can regulate autonomic/behavioral control, explaining why explicit belief updates do not necessarily shift stress responses; they constrain admissible hypotheses, shape regulatory dynamics, and yield testable predictions with neurobiological substrates.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between explicit belief revision and autonomic regulation under uncertainty by introducing governance constraints on identity-level inference; provide a computational and neurobiological framework and generate falsifiable predictions for stress dynamics.
AI.method: Introduce Authority-Level Priors (ALPs) as meta-structural constraints within hierarchical predictive processing; they are not new representations nor hyperpriors, but boundary conditions that restrict policy optimization to authorised hypotheses; map ALPs to distributed prefrontal control networks within variational active inference.
AI.result: Derives testable predictions: changes in stress-reactivity curves, recovery time constants, compensatory cognitive effort, and behavioral persistence; provides a neurobiological mapping to prefrontal networks; no empirical data yet; framework remains compatible with variational active inference.
AI.conclusion: ALPs constitute an architectural hypothesis for governing identity-level regulation. They can be evaluated via computational modelling and longitudinal stress-induction paradigms, with falsifiable predictions guiding future experiments.

title: Box Maze: A Process-Control Architecture for Reliable LLM Reasoning
comment: 10 pages, 5 tables, 0 figures. Conceptual architecture with preliminary simulation-based validation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong generative capabilities but remain vulnerable to hallucination and unreliable reasoning under adversarial prompting. Existing safety approaches -- such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and output filtering -- primarily operate at the behavioral level and may lack explicit architectural mechanisms for enforcing reasoning process integrity.
  This paper proposes the Box Maze framework, a conceptual process-control architecture that decomposes LLM reasoning into three explicit layers: memory grounding, structured inference, and boundary enforcement. We introduce preliminary simulation-based evaluation involving progressive boundary erosion scenarios across multiple heterogeneous LLM systems (DeepSeek-V3, Doubao, Qwen). Results from n=50 adversarial scenarios suggest that explicit cognitive control layers may improve consistency in boundary maintenance, with architectural constraints reducing boundary failure rates from approximately 40% (baseline RLHF) to below 1% under adversarial conditions.
  While current validation is simulation-based, these preliminary results indicate that process-level control may offer a promising direction for improving reliability in large language model reasoning.

AI.tldr: Box Maze introduces a 3-layer cognitive-control architecture for LLM reasoning (memory grounding, structured inference, boundary enforcement) and shows via simulation that explicit control can dramatically reduce boundary failures under adversarial prompting.
AI.motivation: Addresses hallucination and unreliable reasoning under adversarial prompts; existing safety methods (RLHF, output filtering) operate at the behavioral level and lack explicit architectural enforcement of reasoning integrity.
AI.method: Proposes the Box Maze framework with three layers (memory grounding, structured inference, boundary enforcement). Conducts preliminary simulation-based evaluation across heterogeneous LLMs (DeepSeek-V3, Doubao, Qwen) with n=50 adversarial scenarios, including progressive boundary erosion, comparing against baseline RLHF.
AI.result: Explicit cognitive-control layers improved boundary maintenance; architectural constraints reduced boundary failure rates from ~40% (baseline RLHF) to below 1% under adversarial conditions in simulations.
AI.conclusion: Validation is simulation-based, but results suggest process-level control may be a promising direction for increasing reliability of LLM reasoning.

title: ProCal: Probability Calibration for Neighborhood-Guided Source-Free Domain Adaptation
summary: Source-Free Domain Adaptation (SFDA) adapts pre-trained models to unlabeled target domains without requiring access to source data. Although state-of-the-art methods leveraging local neighborhood structures show promise for SFDA, they tend to over-rely on prediction similarity among neighbors. This over-reliance accelerates the forgetting of source knowledge and increases susceptibility to local noise overfitting. To address these issues, we introduce ProCal, a probability calibration method that dynamically calibrates neighborhood-based predictions through a dual-model collaborative prediction mechanism. ProCal integrates the source model's initial predictions with the current model's online outputs to effectively calibrate neighbor probabilities. This strategy not only mitigates the interference of local noise but also preserves the discriminative information from the source model, thereby achieving a balance between knowledge retention and domain adaptation. Furthermore, we design a joint optimization objective that combines a soft supervision loss with a diversity loss to guide the target model. Our theoretical analysis shows that ProCal converges to an equilibrium where source knowledge and target information are effectively fused, reducing both knowledge forgetting and overfitting. We validate the effectiveness of our approach through extensive experiments on 31 cross-domain tasks across four public datasets. Our code is available at: https://github.com/zhengyinghit/ProCal.

AI.tldr: ProCal introduces probability calibration for Source-Free Domain Adaptation (SFDA) using a dual-model collaboration to balance source knowledge with target adaptation; it combines source initial predictions with the current model's outputs, and uses a soft supervision plus diversity loss; proven to converge and effective across 31 cross-domain tasks; code available.
AI.motivation: SFDA methods relying on neighborhood-based predictions tend to overemphasize prediction similarity among neighbors, leading to rapid forgetting of source knowledge and sensitivity to local noise overfitting. A calibration mechanism is needed to fuse source information with target signals and stabilize learning.
AI.method: ProCal employs a dual-model collaborative prediction mechanism that blends the source model's initial predictions with the current online predictions to calibrate neighborhood probabilities. It introduces a joint optimization objective consisting of a soft supervision loss and a diversity loss, and provides theoretical analysis showing convergence to an equilibrium that fuses source knowledge with target information.
AI.result: Extensive empirical validation on 31 cross-domain tasks across four public datasets demonstrates the effectiveness of ProCal in balancing knowledge retention and domain adaptation. The approach reduces forgetting and overfitting while improving adaptation.
AI.conclusion: ProCal achieves a stable fusion of source and target information, mitigating knowledge forgetting and local-noise overfitting in SFDA, with strong empirical support and theoretical convergence guarantees.

title: Uniform a priori bounds and error analysis for the Adam stochastic gradient descent optimization method
summary: The adaptive moment estimation (Adam) optimizer proposed by Kingma & Ba (2014) is presumably the most popular stochastic gradient descent (SGD) optimization method for the training of deep neural networks (DNNs) in artificial intelligence (AI) systems. Despite its groundbreaking success in the training of AI systems, it still remains an open research problem to provide a complete error analysis of Adam, not only for optimizing DNNs but even when applied to strongly convex stochastic optimization problems (SOPs). Previous error analysis results for strongly convex SOPs in the literature provide conditional convergence analyses that rely on the assumption that Adam does not diverge to infinity but remains uniformly bounded. It is the key contribution of this work to establish uniform a priori bounds for Adam and, thereby, to provide -- for the first time -- an unconditional error analysis for Adam for a large class of strongly convex SOPs.

AI.tldr: This work provides unconditional convergence analysis for Adam in strongly convex stochastic optimization by proving uniform a priori bounds on the iterates, removing the usual boundedness assumption.
AI.motivation: Adam is widely used in training neural networks, but prior analyses require the iterates to remain uniformly bounded. This paper aims to attain unconditional error guarantees, filling a key theoretical gap.
AI.method: Derives uniform (a priori) bounds for Adam's parameter and gradient-like estimates under standard smoothness and strong convexity assumptions, then uses these bounds to establish unconditional error/convergence results for a broad class of strongly convex SOPs.
AI.result: First unconditional error bounds and convergence guarantees for Adam applicable to a large class of strongly convex stochastic optimization problems.
AI.conclusion: Eliminates the need for a bounded-iterate assumption in Adam analysis, strengthening theoretical understanding and reliability of Adam in convex settings; opens avenues for extending unconditional analyses to other adaptive optimizers.

title: OS-Themis: A Scalable Critic Framework for Generalist GUI Rewards
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has the potential to improve the robustness of GUI agents in stochastic environments, yet training is highly sensitive to the quality of the reward function. Existing reward approaches struggle to achieve both scalability and performance. To address this, we propose OS-Themis, a scalable and accurate multi-agent critic framework. Unlike a single judge, OS-Themis decomposes trajectories into verifiable milestones to isolate critical evidence for decision making and employs a review mechanism to strictly audit the evidence chain before making the final verdict. To facilitate evaluation, we further introduce OmniGUIRewardBench (OGRBench), a holistic cross-platform benchmark for GUI outcome rewards, where all evaluated models achieve their best performance under OS-Themis. Extensive experiments on AndroidWorld show that OS-Themis yields a 10.3% improvement when used to support online RL training, and a 6.9% gain when used for trajectory validation and filtering in the self-training loop, highlighting its potential to drive agent evolution.

AI.tldr: OS-Themis introduces a scalable multi-agent critic for GUI RL by decomposing trajectories into verifiable milestones and auditing evidence chains, paired with a cross-platform GUI outcome reward benchmark (OGRBench). It claims improvements in RL performance and trajectory filtering on AndroidWorld.
AI.motivation: Reward quality critically affects robustness in RL for GUI agents in stochastic environments; existing reward methods struggle with scalability and performance. A scalable, accurate reward framework is needed to reliably guide learning and evaluation across platforms.
AI.method: OS-Themis uses a multi-agent critic to replace a single judge, decomposing trajectories into milestones to isolate key evidence for decisions, and employs a review mechanism to audit the evidence chain before final verdicts. OGRBench provides holistic GUI outcome rewards across platforms for evaluation. Experiments on AndroidWorld evaluate OS-Themis in online RL training and in self-training loops (trajectory validation and filtering).
AI.result: OS-Themis yields a 10.3% improvement in online RL training performance and a 6.9% gain in trajectory validation/filtering within the self-training loop on AndroidWorld.
AI.conclusion: OS-Themis and OGRBench offer a scalable, accurate reward framework and evaluation benchmark that improve GUI RL outcomes and support agent evolution across platforms.

title: SEAR: Simple and Efficient Adaptation of Visual Geometric Transformers for RGB+Thermal 3D Reconstruction
summary: Foundational feed-forward visual geometry models enable accurate and efficient camera pose estimation and scene reconstruction by learning strong scene priors from massive RGB datasets. However, their effectiveness drops when applied to mixed sensing modalities, such as RGB-thermal (RGB-T) images. We observe that while a visual geometry grounded transformer pretrained on RGB data generalizes well to thermal-only reconstruction, it struggles to align RGB and thermal modalities when processed jointly. To address this, we propose SEAR, a simple yet efficient fine-tuning strategy that adapts a pretrained geometry transformer to multimodal RGB-T inputs. Despite being trained on a relatively small RGB-T dataset, our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods for 3D reconstruction and camera pose estimation, achieving significant improvements over all metrics (e.g., over 29\% in AUC@30) and delivering higher detail and consistency between modalities with negligible overhead in inference time compared to the original pretrained model. Notably, SEAR enables reliable multimodal pose estimation and reconstruction even under challenging conditions, such as low lighting and dense smoke. We validate our architecture through extensive ablation studies, demonstrating how the model aligns both modalities. Additionally, we introduce a new dataset featuring RGB and thermal sequences captured at different times, viewpoints, and illumination conditions, providing a robust benchmark for future work in multimodal 3D scene reconstruction. Code and models are publicly available at https://www.github.com/Schindler-EPFL-Lab/SEAR.

AI.tldr: SEAR is a lightweight fine-tuning strategy that adapts a pretrained visual geometry transformer to RGB-T inputs, enabling accurate multimodal 3D reconstruction and pose estimation with substantial gains and minimal inference overhead, plus a new RGB-T dataset for benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Pretrained geometry transformers on RGB data underperform on multimodal RGB-T tasks due to misalignment between modalities; a simple adaptation is needed to preserve geometry priors while enabling joint RGB-T reasoning.
AI.method: Fine-tune a pretrained geometry transformer for multimodal RGB-T inputs using SEAR, a simple yet efficient strategy designed to align the RGB and thermal modalities and preserve performance at inference time; validate on a small RGB-T dataset with extensive ablations; release code and a new dataset.
AI.result: Significant improvements over state-of-the-art for 3D reconstruction and camera pose estimation, including >29% improvement in AUC@30; improved detail and cross-modal consistency with negligible inference overhead; demonstrated robustness in low light and smoky conditions.
AI.conclusion: SEAR provides reliable multimodal pose estimation and reconstruction by effectively aligning RGB and thermal features with minimal computational cost, and the work contributes a new multimodal RGB-T dataset and open-source code for future research.

title: Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flow for Transition Probability Density Functions of Diffusion Models
summary: We propose a new Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flow framework to approximate the transition probability density function of a diffusion process by solving the corresponding Fokker-Planck equation with an atomic initial distribution, parametrically with respect to the location of the initial mass. By using Normalizing Flows, we look for the solution as a transformation of the transition probability density function of a reference stochastic process, ensuring that our approximation is structure-preserving and automatically satisfies positivity and mass conservation constraints. By extending Neural Galerkin schemes to the context of Normalizing Flows, we derive a system of ODEs for the time evolution of the Normalizing Flow's parameters. Adaptive sampling routines are used to evaluate the Fokker-Planck residual in meaningful locations, which is of vital importance to address high-dimensional PDEs. Numerical results show that this strategy captures key features of the true solution and enforces the causal relationship between the initial datum and the density function at subsequent times. After completing an offline training phase, online evaluation becomes significantly more cost-effective than solving the PDE from scratch. The proposed method serves as a promising surrogate model, which could be deployed in many-query problems associated with stochastic differential equations, like Bayesian inference, simulation, and diffusion bridge generation.

AI.tldr: Neural Galerkin Normalizing Flow to solve Fokker-Planck for diffusion with atomic initial data, yielding a structure-preserving, trainable surrogate that evolves NF parameters via ODEs; adaptive residual sampling enables high-dimensional PDEs; offline training accelerates online inference for many-query SDE problems.
AI.motivation: Address the computational challenge of solving high-dimensional Fokker-Planck equations while preserving positivity and mass conservation, and provide an efficient surrogate for many-query problems in stochastic modeling (e.g., Bayesian inference, diffusion bridges).
AI.method: Represent the evolving density as a Normalizing Flow applied to a reference process; extend Neural Galerkin discretization to the flow framework to derive ODEs for the flow parameters; use adaptive sampling to evaluate the Fokker-Planck residual; initialize with an atomic distribution; perform offline training to obtain a surrogate, enabling fast online evaluations.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show the method captures key features of the true density, preserves causality between initial data and evolved density, and yields substantial online efficiency after offline training.
AI.conclusion: The approach is a promising surrogate model for SDE-related PDEs, with potential applications in Bayesian inference, diffusion bridge generation, and other many-query settings; extensions to broader SPDEs and more complex initial data are feasible.

title: Points-to-3D: Structure-Aware 3D Generation with Point Cloud Priors
summary: Recent progress in 3D generation has been driven largely by models conditioned on images or text, while readily available 3D priors are still underused. In many real-world scenarios, the visible-region point cloud are easy to obtain from active sensors such as LiDAR or from feed-forward predictors like VGGT, offering explicit geometric constraints that current methods fail to exploit. In this work, we introduce Points-to-3D, a diffusion-based framework that leverages point cloud priors for geometry-controllable 3D asset and scene generation. Built on a latent 3D diffusion model TRELLIS, Points-to-3D first replaces pure-noise sparse structure latent initialization with a point cloud priors tailored input formulation.A structure inpainting network, trained within the TRELLIS framework on task-specific data designed to learn global structural inpainting, is then used for inference with a staged sampling strategy (structural inpainting followed by boundary refinement), completing the global geometry while preserving the visible regions of the input priors.In practice, Points-to-3D can take either accurate point-cloud priors or VGGT-estimated point clouds from single images as input. Experiments on both objects and scene scenarios consistently demonstrate superior performance over state-of-the-art baselines in terms of rendering quality and geometric fidelity, highlighting the effectiveness of explicitly embedding point-cloud priors for achieving more accurate and structurally controllable 3D generation.

AI.tldr: Presents Points-to-3D, a diffusion-based framework that uses point-cloud priors to enable geometry-controllable 3D generation, integrating with the TRELLIS latent 3D diffusion model and a structure inpainting module; shows improved rendering quality and geometric fidelity over SOTA baselines for objects and scenes, using either accurate point clouds or VGGT-estimated priors from single images.
AI.motivation: Current 3D generation largely relies on image or text conditioning and does not fully exploit readily available 3D priors like visible-region point clouds, which provide explicit geometric constraints crucial for fidelity and controllability.
AI.method: Extend TRELLIS latent 3D diffusion model by replacing the pure-noise latent initialization with a point cloud priors tailored input; introduce a structure inpainting network trained within TRELLIS for global structural inpainting; apply a staged sampling strategy: first structural inpainting, then boundary refinement; enable inference with either accurate priors or VGGT-estimated point clouds derived from single images.
AI.result: Empirical results on both object and scene datasets show superior performance relative to state-of-the-art baselines in rendering quality and geometric fidelity, demonstrating that explicitly embedding point-cloud priors improves accuracy and structural controllability of 3D generation.
AI.conclusion: Point-cloud priors can be effectively integrated into diffusion-based 3D generation to achieve geometry-controllable assets and scenes, with inputs from either precise priors or VGGT estimates from images; the approach yields improved geometry and rendering fidelity and broadens controllable 3D synthesis.

title: An Optimised Greedy-Weighted Ensemble Framework for Financial Loan Default Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of loan defaults is a central challenge in credit risk management, particularly in modern financial datasets characterised by nonlinear relationships, class imbalance, and evolving borrower behaviour. Traditional statistical models and static ensemble methods often struggle to maintain reliable performance under such conditions. This study proposes an Optimised Greedy-Weighted Ensemble framework for loan default prediction that dynamically allocates model weights based on empirical predictive performance. The framework integrates multiple machine learning classifiers, with their hyperparameters first optimised using Particle Swarm Optimisation. Model predictions are then combined via a regularised greedy weighting mechanism. At the same time, a neural-network-based meta-learner is employed within stacked-ensemble to capture higher-order relationships among model outputs. Experiments conducted on the Lending Club dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework improves predictive performance compared with individual classifiers. The BlendNet ensemble achieved the strongest results with an AUC of 0.80, a macro-average F1-score of 0.73, and a default recall of 0.81. Calibration analysis further shows that tree-based ensembles such as Extra Trees and Gradient Boosting provide the most reliable probability estimates, while the stacked ensemble offers superior ranking capability. Feature analysis using Recursive Feature Elimination identifies revolving utilisation, annual income, and debt-to-income ratio as the most influential predictors of loan default. These findings demonstrate that performance-driven ensemble weighting can improve both predictive accuracy and interpretability in credit risk modelling. The proposed framework provides a scalable data-driven approach to support institutional credit assessment, risk monitoring, and financial decision-making.

AI.tldr: Optimised Greedy-Weighted Ensemble (BlendNet) for loan default prediction using PSO-tuned classifiers, regularised greedy weighting, and a neural meta-learner; on Lending Club data, achieves AUC 0.80, macro-F1 0.73, recall 0.81, with calibration analyses favoring tree ensembles and feature importance from RFE.
AI.motivation: Credit risk modeling under nonlinear relationships, class imbalance, and evolving borrower behavior; traditional statistical models and static ensembles struggle to maintain reliable performance in such conditions; need a dynamic, interpretable, and scalable solution.
AI.method: Hyperparameter optimization for multiple classifiers via Particle Swarm Optimization; predictions fused with a regularised greedy weighting mechanism; stacked ensemble with a neural-network meta-learner to capture higher-order relationships among model outputs; calibration analysis; Recursive Feature Elimination for feature importance; evaluation on Lending Club dataset; named BlendNet ensemble.
AI.result: BlendNet achieves AUC 0.80, macro-F1 0.73, recall 0.81; calibration indicates tree-based ensembles (Extra Trees, Gradient Boosting) provide reliable probability estimates, while the stacked ensemble offers superior ranking; Revolving Utilisation, Annual Income, and Debt-to-Income ratio identified as top predictors via RFE; overall improved predictive performance over individual classifiers; scalable data-driven approach for credit risk modeling.
AI.conclusion: Performance-driven ensemble weighting improves accuracy and interpretability in credit risk modelling; framework supports scalable credit assessment, risk monitoring, and informed financial decision-making.

title: Rethinking Uncertainty Quantification and Entanglement in Image Segmentation
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is crucial in safety-critical applications such as medical image segmentation. Total uncertainty is typically decomposed into data-related aleatoric uncertainty (AU) and model-related epistemic uncertainty (EU). Many methods exist for modeling AU (such as Probabilistic UNet, Diffusion) and EU (such as ensembles, MC Dropout), but it is unclear how they interact when combined. Additionally, recent work has revealed substantial entanglement between AU and EU, undermining the interpretability and practical usefulness of the decomposition. We present a comprehensive empirical study covering a broad range of AU-EU model combinations, propose a metric to quantify uncertainty entanglement, and evaluate both across downstream UQ tasks. For out-of-distribution detection, ensembles exhibit consistently lower entanglement and superior performance. For ambiguity modeling and calibration the best models are dataset-dependent, with softmax/SSN-based methods performing well and Probabilistic UNets being less entangled. A softmax ensemble fares remarkably well on all tasks. Finally, we analyze potential sources of uncertainty entanglement and outline directions for mitigating this effect.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive empirical study on combining aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in medical image segmentation; introduces a metric for uncertainty entanglement; finds ensembles reduce entanglement for OOD tasks; results are dataset-dependent for ambiguity modeling and calibration; softmax ensembles perform strongly across tasks; outlines sources and mitigations of entanglement.
AI.motivation: Clarify how data (AU) and model (EU) uncertainties interact when combined in practical UQ pipelines, and address the entanglement that undermines interpretability and usefulness of the AU/EU decomposition.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation across a broad set of AU-EU model combinations; propose a metric to quantify uncertainty entanglement; assess performance on downstream tasks: out-of-distribution detection, ambiguity modeling, and calibration; compare ensembles, MC Dropout, Probabilistic UNet, diffusion-based AU models, and softmax/SSN-based approaches; include a softmax ensemble as a strong baseline.
AI.result: Ensembles consistently yield lower entanglement and better OOD performance. Ambiguity modeling and calibration results are dataset-dependent; softmax/SSN-based methods perform well in many cases, while Probabilistic UNets tend to be less entangled. A softmax ensemble demonstrates strong performance across all tasks.
AI.conclusion: Identify potential sources of AU-EU entanglement and propose directions to mitigate it. Emphasize ensemble-based strategies (notably softmax ensembles) as robust baselines, while recognizing dataset-specific best choices for AU/EU models; outline methodological and practical steps for reducing entanglement in future work.

title: Context Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) suffers from exploration inefficiency, where models struggle to generate successful rollouts, resulting in minimal learning signal. This challenge is particularly severe for tasks that require the acquisition of novel reasoning patterns or domain-specific knowledge. To address this, we propose Context Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning (CBRL), which augments RLVR training by stochastically prepending few-shot demonstrations to training prompts. The injection probability follows a curriculum that starts high to bootstrap early exploration, then anneals to zero so the model must ultimately succeed without assistance. This forces the policy to internalize reasoning patterns from the demonstrations rather than relying on them at test time. We validate CBRL across two model families and five Reasoning Gym tasks. Our results demonstrate that CBRL consistently improves success rate, provides better exploration efficiency, and is algorithm-agnostic. We further demonstrate CBRL's practical applicability on Q, a domain-specific programming language that diverges significantly from mainstream language conventions.

AI.tldr: Proposes Context Bootstrapped Reinforcement Learning (CBRL) to boost exploration in RLVR by stochastically prepending few-shot demonstrations during training with a curriculum; improves success rate and exploration efficiency across models and tasks, including a domain-specific language Q.
AI.motivation: RLVR struggles with exploration efficiency, especially for tasks needing novel reasoning or domain knowledge; reliance on demonstrations at test time is undesirable; need methods that bootstrap learning yet allow autonomous performance.
AI.method: Injects few-shot demonstrations at training prompts with a curriculum controlling injection probability, starting high and annealing to zero; demonstrated across two model families and five Reasoning Gym tasks; also shows applicability to Q language.
AI.result: CBRL consistently improves success rates and exploration efficiency; appears algorithm-agnostic; gains validated on multiple tasks and models, including domain-specific language.
AI.conclusion: CBRL is an effective, general strategy to bootstrap exploration in RLVR by internalizing reasoning patterns from demonstrations, reducing reliance at test time and enabling cross-domain applicability.

title: Perceptio: Perception Enhanced Vision Language Models via Spatial Token Generation
summary: Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) excel at semantic understanding but struggle with fine grained spatial grounding, as the model must implicitly infer complex geometry without ever producing a spatial interpretation. We present Perceptio, a perception enhanced LVLM with 2D and 3D spatial reasoning abilities, enabled via explicit semantic segmentation tokens and depth tokens generated directly within the autoregressive sequence. Concretely, we (i) distill a VQVAE depth codebook from a strong monocular teacher to tokenize dense depth into compact sequences, and (ii) integrate SAM2 based semantic segmentation tokens and VQ-VAE depth tokens inside the LLM so the model first emits spatial tokens and then answers. To stabilize depth token generation, we introduce novel composite depth-token objectives (marker, token, and count losses) and a soft-merging technique for differentiable reconstruction. We adopt a multi-task co-training strategy across diverse datasets, letting the model learn perception tokens to tackle multiple downstream tasks. Building on InternVL, Perceptio achieves state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks: improving referring expression segmentation by +0.8/+1.4/+1.1 cIoU on RefCOCO/+/g HardBLINK spatial understanding accuracy by 10.3%, and MMBench accuracy by 1.0%, demonstrating that explicit spatial chain-of-thought materially strengthens spatial grounding in LVLMs.

AI.tldr: Perceptio endows LVLMs with explicit 2D/3D spatial reasoning by injecting segmentation and depth tokens into the autoregressive stream, enabling spatial chain-of-thought and improved grounding.
AI.motivation: LVLMs excel at semantics but lack fine-grained spatial grounding; explicit spatial interpretations are hard to induce implicitly, limiting tasks requiring geometry and localization.
AI.method: 1) Distill VQVAE depth codebook from a strong monocular teacher to tokenize dense depth. 2) Integrate SAM2-based semantic segmentation tokens and VQ-VAE depth tokens inside the LLM’s sequence so spatial tokens are emitted before answers. 3) Stabilize depth tokens with composite depth-token objectives (marker, token, count losses) and differentiable soft-merging for reconstruction. 4) Multi-task co-training across diverse datasets to learn robust perception tokens for multiple downstream tasks.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on several benchmarks: RefCOCO/+/g: +0.8/+1.4/+1.1 cIoU improvements in referring expression segmentation; HardBLINK: 10.3% spatial understanding accuracy gain; MMBench: 1.0% accuracy gain.
AI.conclusion: Explicit spatial tokens and a perception-centric training regime substantially strengthen spatial grounding in LVLMs, with Perceptio outperforming prior methods across multiple spatial understanding tasks.

title: VesselTok: Tokenizing Vessel-like 3D Biomedical Graph Representations for Reconstruction and Generation
summary: Spatial graphs provide a lightweight and elegant representation of curvilinear anatomical structures such as blood vessels, lung airways, and neuronal networks. Accurately modeling these graphs is crucial in clinical and (bio-)medical research. However, the high spatial resolution of large networks drastically increases their complexity, resulting in significant computational challenges. In this work, we aim to tackle these challenges by proposing VesselTok, a framework that approaches spatially dense graphs from a parametric shape perspective to learn latent representations (tokens). VesselTok leverages centerline points with a pseudo radius to effectively encode tubular geometry. Specifically, we learn a novel latent representation conditioned on centerline points to encode neural implicit representations of vessel-like, tubular structures. We demonstrate VesselTok's performance across diverse anatomies, including lung airways, lung vessels, and brain vessels, highlighting its ability to robustly encode complex topologies. To prove the effectiveness of VesselTok's learnt latent representations, we show that they (i) generalize to unseen anatomies, (ii) support generative modeling of plausible anatomical graphs, and (iii) transfer effectively to downstream inverse problems, such as link prediction.

AI.tldr: VesselTok develops a parametric-shape latent token framework to encode dense tubular anatomical graphs using centerline-based representations with a pseudo radius, enabling generalization, generative modeling, and transfer to downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Dense, high-resolution vascular, airway, and neuronal graphs are computationally expensive to analyze. A compact, shape-aware representation can reduce complexity while preserving topology and geometry.
AI.method: Learn latent tokens conditioned on centerline points to encode neural implicit representations of vessel-like tubular structures. Uses centerline + pseudo radius to capture tubular geometry and supports generative modeling and transfer to downstream inverse problems (e.g., link prediction). Demonstrated on lung airways/vessels and brain vessels.
AI.result: The approach shows robust encoding across diverse anatomies, latent representations generalize to unseen anatomies, enable generation of plausible anatomical graphs, and transfer effectively to tasks like link prediction.
AI.conclusion: A parametric-shape latent-token framework (VesselTok) can compactly represent and reason about tubular spatial graphs, enabling generalization, generative modeling, and transfer to downstream analyses across multiple organ systems.

title: BVSIMC: Bayesian Variable Selection-Guided Inductive Matrix Completion for Improved and Interpretable Drug Discovery
summary: Recent advances in drug discovery have demonstrated that incorporating side information (e.g., chemical properties about drugs and genomic information about diseases) often greatly improves prediction performance. However, these side features can vary widely in relevance and are often noisy and high-dimensional. We propose Bayesian Variable Selection-Guided Inductive Matrix Completion (BVSIMC), a new Bayesian model that enables variable selection from side features in drug discovery. By learning sparse latent embeddings, BVSIMC improves both predictive accuracy and interpretability. We validate our method through simulation studies and two drug discovery applications: 1) prediction of drug resistance in Mycobacterium tuberculosis, and 2) prediction of new drug-disease associations in computational drug repositioning. On both synthetic and real data, BVSIMC outperforms several other state-of-the-art methods in terms of prediction. In our two real examples, BVSIMC further reveals the most clinically meaningful side features.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian variable-selection guided inductive matrix completion (BVSIMC) that learns sparse latent embeddings from side information to improve prediction and interpretability in drug discovery; validated on drug resistance in Mycobacterium tuberculosis and drug–disease repositioning.
AI.motivation: Integrating heterogeneous, high-dimensional, and noisy side information (chemical properties, genomic data) improves prediction but requires identifying relevant features and maintaining interpretability.
AI.method: Introduce BVSIMC, a Bayesian model that performs variable selection on side features within an inductive matrix completion framework to learn sparse latent embeddings.
AI.result: BVSIMC outperforms several state-of-the-art methods on both synthetic and real data; real-data applications highlight clinically meaningful side features; improves predictive accuracy and interpretability.
