AI.conclusion: BVSIMC provides an effective framework for incorporating side information via sparse embeddings and Bayesian variable selection, with potential applicability to other domains with high-dimensional side information.

title: Statistical Characteristic-Guided Denoising for Rapid High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy Imaging
summary: High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) enables atomic-scale observation of nucleation dynamics, which boosts the studies of advanced solid materials. Nonetheless, due to the millisecond-scale rapid change of nucleation, it requires short-exposure rapid imaging, leading to severe noise that obscures atomic positions. In this work, we propose a statistical characteristic-guided denoising network, which utilizes statistical characteristics to guide the denoising process in both spatial and frequency domains. In the spatial domain, we present spatial deviation-guided weighting to select appropriate convolution operations for each spatial position based on deviation characteristic. In the frequency domain, we present frequency band-guided weighting to enhance signals and suppress noise based on band characteristics. We also develop an HRTEM-specific noise calibration method and generate a dataset with disordered structures and realistic HRTEM image noises. It can ensure the denoising performance of models on real images for nucleation observation. Experiments on synthetic and real data show our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in HRTEM image denoising, with effectiveness in the localization downstream task. Code will be available at https://github.com/HeasonLee/SCGN.

AI.tldr: A stat-guided denoising network (SCGN) for HRTEM images that uses spatial deviation-guided and frequency band-guided weighting, plus noise calibration and a realistic synthetic-real dataset, achieving state-of-the-art denoising and better downstream localization.
AI.motivation: HRTEM nucleation dynamics are obscured by heavy noise due to short exposure, so robust denoising that preserves atomic positions is essential for reliable observation and downstream analysis.
AI.method: 1) Develop SCGN with spatial deviation-guided weighting to adapt convolutions per spatial location; 2) apply frequency band-guided weighting to emphasize signal and suppress noise in the frequency domain; 3) include an HRTEM-specific noise calibration method; 4) construct a dataset with disordered structures and realistic HRTEM noise for training and evaluation.
AI.result: SCGN outperforms state-of-the-art denoising methods on both synthetic and real HRTEM data and improves accuracy in a downstream localization task.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SCGN effectively leverages spatial and frequency statistics to denoise HRTEM images while preserving atomic features, enabling improved nucleation observation; the authors also provide a dataset with calibrated noise and will release code.

title: Maximum-Entropy Exploration with Future State-Action Visitation Measures
comment: arXiv admin note: substantial text overlap with arXiv:2412.06655
summary: Maximum entropy reinforcement learning motivates agents to explore states and actions to maximize the entropy of some distribution, typically by providing additional intrinsic rewards proportional to that entropy function. In this paper, we study intrinsic rewards proportional to the entropy of the discounted distribution of state-action features visited during future time steps. This approach is motivated by two results. First, we show that the expected sum of these intrinsic rewards is a lower bound on the entropy of the discounted distribution of state-action features visited in trajectories starting from the initial states, which we relate to an alternative maximum entropy objective. Second, we show that the distribution used in the intrinsic reward definition is the fixed point of a contraction operator and can therefore be estimated off-policy. Experiments highlight that the new objective leads to improved visitation of features within individual trajectories, in exchange for slightly reduced visitation of features in expectation over different trajectories, as suggested by the lower bound. It also leads to improved convergence speed for learning exploration-only agents. Control performance remains similar across most methods on the considered benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Entropy-based intrinsic rewards over the discounted future feature distribution; proven to bound trajectory entropy and be off-policy estimable via a contraction fixed point; yields faster exploration convergence with comparable control performance.
AI.motivation: Promote exploration by maximizing the entropy of the discounted distribution of future state-action features, addressing sparse rewards and providing a principled objective related to maximum entropy RL.
AI.method: Define intrinsic rewards proportional to the entropy of the discounted distribution of features visited in future time steps. Show that the expected sum of these intrinsic rewards lower-bounds the entropy of the discounted trajectory feature distribution (linking to a maximum entropy objective). Establish that the feature distribution is a contraction fixed point, enabling off-policy estimation. Validate empirically that the objective increases intra-trajectory feature visitation and accelerates learning for exploration-only agents, with similar control performance.
AI.result: Experiments indicate improved intra-trajectory feature visitation at the expense of slightly reduced expected feature visitation across trajectories (consistent with the lower bound). Learning speed for exploration-focused agents improves, while control performance remains similar across methods on benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: An entropy-based intrinsic reward framework provides a tractable, off-policy estimable objective that enhances local exploration by biasing toward diverse features, without degrading control performance.

title: Towards Interpretable Foundation Models for Retinal Fundus Images
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables, submitted to MICCAI 2026
summary: Foundation models are used to extract transferable representations from large amounts of unlabeled data, typically via self-supervised learning (SSL). However, many of these models rely on architectures that offer limited interpretability, which is a critical issue in high-stakes domains such as medical imaging. We propose Dual-IFM, a foundation model that is interpretable-by-design in two ways: First, it provides local interpretability for individual images through class evidence maps that are faithful to the decision-making process. Second, it provides global interpretability for entire datasets through a 2D projection layer that allows for direct visualization of the model's representation space. We trained our model on over 800,000 color fundus photography from various sources to learn generalizable, interpretable representations for different downstream tasks. Our results show that our model reaches a performance range similar to that of state-of-the-art foundation models with up to $16\times$ the number of parameters, while providing interpretable predictions on out-of-distribution data. Our results suggest that large-scale SSL pretraining paired with inherent interpretability can lead to robust representations for retinal imaging.

AI.tldr: Interpretable-by-design Dual-IFM for retinal imaging: matches state-of-the-art performance with up to 16× more parameters, offering local class evidence maps and a global 2D projection for dataset-level interpretability, trained via self-supervised learning on 800k fundus images.
AI.motivation: High-stakes domains like medical imaging require model interpretability. Foundation models often lack transparency; this work proposes a model that is interpretable by design at both local and global levels.
AI.method: Dual-IFM incorporates (1) local interpretability via faithful class evidence maps that explain individual predictions, and (2) global interpretability via a 2D projection layer to visualize the representation space. The model is pretrained with self-supervised learning on a large, diverse fundus image dataset (~800,000 images).
AI.result: The model achieves performance in the range of state-of-the-art foundation models, potentially with up to 16× more parameters. It provides interpretable predictions on out-of-distribution data, suggesting robustness of the learned representations for retinal imaging.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale SSL with inherent interpretable design can yield robust, interpretable representations for retinal imaging, enabling trustworthy deployment in clinical settings.

title: Best-of-Both-Worlds Multi-Dueling Bandits: Unified Algorithms for Stochastic and Adversarial Preferences under Condorcet and Borda Objectives
summary: Multi-dueling bandits, where a learner selects $m \geq 2$ arms per round and observes only the winner, arise naturally in many applications including ranking and recommendation systems, yet a fundamental question has remained open: can a single algorithm perform optimally in both stochastic and adversarial environments, without knowing which regime it faces? We answer this affirmatively, providing the first best-of-both-worlds algorithms for multi-dueling bandits under both Condorcet and Borda objectives. For the Condorcet setting, we propose \texttt{MetaDueling}, a black-box reduction that converts any dueling bandit algorithm into a multi-dueling bandit algorithm by transforming multi-way winner feedback into an unbiased pairwise signal. Instantiating our reduction with \texttt{Versatile-DB} yields the first best-of-both-worlds algorithm for multi-dueling bandits: it achieves $O(\sqrt{KT})$ pseudo-regret against adversarial preferences and the instance-optimal $O\!\left(\sum_{i \neq a^\star} \frac{\log T}{Δ_i}\right)$ pseudo-regret under stochastic preferences, both simultaneously and without prior knowledge of the regime. For the Borda setting, we propose \AlgBorda, a stochastic-and-adversarial algorithm that achieves $O\left(K^2 \log KT + K \log^2 T + \sum_{i: Δ_i^{\mathrm{B}} > 0} \frac{K\log KT}{(Δ_i^{\mathrm{B}})^2}\right)$ regret in stochastic environments and $O\left(K \sqrt{T \log KT} + K^{1/3} T^{2/3} (\log K)^{1/3}\right)$ regret against adversaries, again without prior knowledge of the regime. We complement our upper bounds with matching lower bounds for the Condorcet setting. For the Borda setting, our upper bounds are near-optimal with respect to the lower bounds (within a factor of $K$) and match the best-known results in the literature.

AI.tldr: Introduces best-of-both-worlds algorithms for multi-dueling bandits in Condorcet and Borda settings. A black-box MetaDueling reduction converts multi-way winner feedback into unbiased pairwise signals and, with Versatile-DB, achieves the first best-of-both-worlds results: adversarial regret O(√KT) and stochastic instance-optimal regret O(∑_{i≠a*} log T / Δ_i) for Condorcet. For Borda, AlgBorda yields stochastic regret O(K^2 log KT + K log^2 T + ∑_{i: Δ_i^B>0} (K log KT)/(Δ_i^B)^2) and adversarial regret O(K√(T log KT) + K^{1/3} T^{2/3} (log K)^{1/3}); with matching lower bounds for Condorcet and near-optimal bounds for Borda.
AI.motivation: Address the open question whether a single algorithm can perform optimally in both stochastic and adversarial environments for multi-dueling bandits, without prior regime knowledge, across Condorcet and Borda objectives.
AI.method: Condorcet: MetaDueling, a black-box reduction that turns any dueling-bandit algorithm into a multi-dueling-bandit algorithm by producing unbiased pairwise signals from multi-way winner feedback. Instantiating with Versatile-DB yields a best-of-both-worlds algorithm. Borda: propose AlgBorda, a stochastic-and-adversarial algorithm.
AI.result: Condorcet: O(√KT) pseudo-regret against adversaries; instance-optimal O(∑_{i≠a*} log T / Δ_i) in stochastic; both without regime knowledge. Borda: stochastic regret O(K^2 log KT + K log^2 T + ∑_{i:Δ_i^B>0} (K log KT)/(Δ_i^B)^2); adversarial regret O(K√(T log KT) + K^{1/3} T^{2/3} (log K)^{1/3}); lower bounds provided for Condorcet; bounds for Borda near-optimal (within factor K) compared to lower bounds and match best-known results.
AI.conclusion: First to present best-of-both-worlds algorithms for multi-dueling bandits under both Condorcet and Borda; establishes matching (Condorcet) and near-optimal (Borda) bounds, providing a unified, regime-agnostic approach and highlighting the effectiveness of the MetaDueling reduction and AlgBorda.

title: HORNet: Task-Guided Frame Selection for Video Question Answering with Vision-Language Models
summary: Video question answering (VQA) with vision-language models (VLMs) depends critically on which frames are selected from the input video, yet most systems rely on uniform or heuristic sampling that cannot be optimized for downstream answering quality. We introduce \textbf{HORNet}, a lightweight frame selection policy trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to learn which frames a frozen VLM needs to answer questions correctly. With fewer than 1M trainable parameters, HORNet reduces input frames by up to 99\% and VLM processing time by up to 93\%, while improving answer quality on short-form benchmarks (+1.7\% F1 on MSVD-QA) and achieving strong performance on temporal reasoning tasks (+7.3 points over uniform sampling on NExT-QA). We formalize this as Select Any Frames (SAF), a task that decouples visual input curation from VLM reasoning, and show that GRPO-trained selection generalizes better out-of-distribution than supervised and PPO alternatives. HORNet's policy further transfers across VLM answerers without retraining, yielding an additional 8.5\% relative gain when paired with a stronger model. Evaluated across six benchmarks spanning 341,877 QA pairs and 114.2 hours of video, our results demonstrate that optimizing \emph{what} a VLM sees is a practical and complementary alternative to optimizing what it generates while improving efficiency. Code is available at https://github.com/ostadabbas/HORNet.

AI.tldr: HORNet trains a GRPO-based frame selector to pick frames for VQA using a frozen VLM, achieving major efficiency gains (up to 99% fewer frames, 93% faster) while improving answer quality; introduces Select Any Frames (SAF) and cross-VLM transfer; code released.
AI.motivation: VQA with vision-language models hinges on which video frames are used. Uniform or heuristic sampling is suboptimal for downstream QA quality and efficiency; a learnable frame-selection policy is needed.
AI.method: Propose HORNet, a lightweight frame-selection policy (<1M parameters) trained with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to select frames from video input for a frozen VLM. Formalizes Select Any Frames (SAF) to decouple input curation from reasoning. Demonstrates cross-VLM transferability of the policy across different answerers.
AI.result: HORNet reduces input frames by up to 99% and VLM processing time by up to 93%; improves QA accuracy (+1.7% F1 on MSVD-QA) and supports temporal reasoning (+7.3 points over uniform sampling on NExT-QA). Achieves strong generalization on six benchmarks (341,877 QA pairs, 114.2 hours of video). The policy transfers across VLMs with an additional 8.5% relative gain when paired with a stronger model.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing what a VLM sees (frame selection) is a practical and complementary approach to optimizing what it generates. The GRPO-trained HORNet generalizes across VLMs, supports cross-model transfer, and offers substantial efficiency gains. Code is available.

title: Book your room in the Turing Hotel! A symmetric and distributed Turing Test with multiple AIs and humans
summary: In this paper, we report our experience with ``TuringHotel'', a novel extension of the Turing Test based on interactions within mixed communities of Large Language Models (LLMs) and human participants. The classical one-to-one interaction of the Turing Test is reinterpreted in a group setting, where both human and artificial agents engage in time-bounded discussions and, interestingly, are both judges and respondents. This community is instantiated in the novel platform UNaIVERSE (https://unaiverse.io), creating a ``World'' which defines the roles and interaction dynamics, facilitated by the platform's built-in programming tools. All communication occurs over an authenticated peer-to-peer network, ensuring that no third parties can access the exchange. The platform also provides a unified interface for humans, accessible via both mobile devices and laptops, that was a key component of the experience in this paper. Results of our experimentation involving 17 human participants and 19 LLMs revealed that current models are still sometimes confused as humans. Interestingly, there are several unexpected mistakes, suggesting that human fingerprints are still identifiable but not fully unambiguous, despite the high-quality language skills of artificial participants. We argue that this is the first experiment conducted in such a distributed setting, and that similar initiatives could be of national interest to support ongoing experiments and competitions aimed at monitoring the evolution of large language models over time.

AI.tldr: Summary generation failed
AI.motivation: Motivation analysis unavailable

title: Motion-o: Trajectory-Grounded Video Reasoning
summary: Recent research has made substantial progress on video reasoning, with many models leveraging spatio-temporal evidence chains to strengthen their inference capabilities. At the same time, a growing set of datasets and benchmarks now provides structured annotations designed to support and evaluate such reasoning. However, little attention has been paid to reasoning about \emph{how} objects move between observations: no prior work has articulated the motion patterns by connecting successive observations, leaving trajectory understanding implicit and difficult to verify. We formalize this missing capability as Spatial-Temporal-Trajectory (STT) reasoning and introduce \textbf{Motion-o}, a motion-centric video understanding extension to visual language models that makes trajectories explicit and verifiable. To enable motion reasoning, we also introduce a trajectory-grounding dataset artifact that expands sparse keyframe supervision via augmentation to yield denser bounding box tracks and a stronger trajectory-level training signal. Finally, we introduce Motion Chain of Thought (MCoT), a structured reasoning pathway that makes object trajectories through discrete \texttt{<motion/>} tag summarizing per-object direction, speed, and scale (of velocity) change to explicitly connect grounded observations into trajectories. To train Motion-o, we design a reward function that compels the model to reason directly over visual evidence, all while requiring no architectural modifications. Empirical results demonstrate that Motion-o improves spatial-temporal grounding and trajectory prediction while remaining fully compatible with existing frameworks, establishing motion reasoning as a critical extension for evidence-based video understanding. Code is available at https://github.com/ostadabbas/Motion-o.

AI.tldr: Motion-o is a motion-centric extension to visual-language models that explicitly reasons about object trajectories across video observations. It introduces a trajectory-grounding data augmentation and Motion Chain of Thought (MCoT) with <motion/> annotations, plus a reward-based training signal that requires no architectural changes, improving spatial-temporal grounding and trajectory prediction.
AI.motivation: Current video-language models leverage spatio-temporal cues but do not explicitly model how objects move between observations. Trajectory understanding remains implicit and hard to verify, hindering robust motion reasoning and verifiability.
AI.method: Propose Motion-o, a motion-centric video understanding extension to visual-language models. Introduce a trajectory-grounding dataset artifact that densifies sparse keyframe annotations into continuous bounding-box tracks to strengthen trajectory supervision. Develop Motion Chain of Thought (MCoT), a structured reasoning pathway using <motion/> tags that summarize per-object direction, speed, and scale-change to connect grounded observations into trajectories. Train with a reward function that incentivizes reasoning over visual evidence without changing model architecture, maintaining compatibility with existing frameworks.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved spatial-temporal grounding and trajectory prediction. Motion-o remains fully compatible with existing VLM frameworks. Code is publicly available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Explicit motion reasoning is a critical extension for evidence-based video understanding. Motion-o demonstrates that trajectories can be grounded, verified, and reasoned about, via dense trajectory supervision, MCoT, and a reward-based training signal without architectural changes.

title: Foundations of Schrödinger Bridges for Generative Modeling
summary: At the core of modern generative modeling frameworks, including diffusion models, score-based models, and flow matching, is the task of transforming a simple prior distribution into a complex target distribution through stochastic paths in probability space. Schrödinger bridges provide a unifying principle underlying these approaches, framing the problem as determining an optimal stochastic bridge between marginal distribution constraints with minimal-entropy deviations from a pre-defined reference process. This guide develops the mathematical foundations of the Schrödinger bridge problem, drawing on optimal transport, stochastic control, and path-space optimization, and focuses on its dynamic formulation with direct connections to modern generative modeling. We build a comprehensive toolkit for constructing Schrödinger bridges from first principles, and show how these constructions give rise to generalized and task-specific computational methods.

AI.tldr: Unifies diffusion-, score-based-, and flow-matching generative modeling under Schrödinger bridges, formulating a dynamic, entropy-regularized transport problem and deriving a first-principles toolkit and task-specific computational methods.
AI.motivation: Provide a principled, unifying framework for transforming a simple prior into a complex target via stochastic paths, by casting generative modeling as a Schrödinger bridge problem with minimal-entropy deviation from a reference process.
AI.method: Develop the dynamic Schrödinger bridge formulation from optimal transport, stochastic control, and path-space optimization; construct bridges from first principles; connect bridge constructions to generalized and task-specific computational methods for modern generative models.
AI.result: Establishes a mathematical foundation and toolkit for Schrödinger bridges and shows how constructions yield broad, task-specific computational methods applicable to diffusion-, score-based-, and flow-matching-type models.
AI.conclusion: Schrödinger bridges offer a unifying, principled framework for dynamic generative modeling, enabling principled construction of stochastic bridges and the development of generalized and task-specific algorithms across modern generative modeling paradigms.

title: PromptHub: Enhancing Multi-Prompt Visual In-Context Learning with Locality-Aware Fusion, Concentration and Alignment
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2026. 17 pages, 11 figures, and 9 tables
summary: Visual In-Context Learning (VICL) aims to complete vision tasks by imitating pixel demonstrations. Recent work pioneered prompt fusion that combines the advantages of various demonstrations, which shows a promising way to extend VICL. Unfortunately, the patch-wise fusion framework and model-agnostic supervision hinder the exploitation of informative cues, thereby limiting performance gains. To overcome this deficiency, we introduce PromptHub, a framework that holistically strengthens multi-prompting through locality-aware fusion, concentration and alignment. PromptHub exploits spatial priors to capture richer contextual information, employs complementary concentration, alignment, and prediction objectives to mutually guide training, and incorporates data augmentation to further reinforce supervision. Extensive experiments on three fundamental vision tasks demonstrate the superiority of PromptHub. Moreover, we validate its universality, transferability, and robustness across out-of-distribution settings, and various retrieval scenarios. This work establishes a reliable locality-aware paradigm for prompt fusion, moving beyond prior patch-wise approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/luotc-why/ICLR26-PromptHub.

AI.tldr: PromptHub presents a locality-aware, multi-prompt fusion framework for Visual In-Context Learning that uses spatial priors, concentration/alignment/prediction objectives, and data augmentation to outperform patch-wise methods across three vision tasks and show strong OOD robustness; code at provided link.
AI.motivation: Patch-wise fusion and model-agnostic supervision in VICL fail to exploit informative cues; a holistic, locality-aware approach is needed to better leverage spatial context and cues.
AI.method: Introduce PromptHub: a locality-aware fusion framework for multi-prompting; leverages spatial priors to capture richer context; combines concentration, alignment, and prediction objectives to mutually guide training; uses data augmentation to reinforce supervision; argues universality across prompts and retrieval scenarios.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three fundamental vision tasks show superior performance; demonstrates universality, transferability, and robustness in out-of-distribution settings and across retrieval scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a reliable locality-aware paradigm for prompt fusion that surpasses patch-wise methods; code is available at the provided repository.

title: AgentDS Technical Report: Benchmarking the Future of Human-AI Collaboration in Domain-Specific Data Science
summary: Data science plays a critical role in transforming complex data into actionable insights across numerous domains. Recent developments in large language models (LLMs) and artificial intelligence (AI) agents have significantly automated data science workflow. However, it remains unclear to what extent AI agents can match the performance of human experts on domain-specific data science tasks, and in which aspects human expertise continues to provide advantages. We introduce AgentDS, a benchmark and competition designed to evaluate both AI agents and human-AI collaboration performance in domain-specific data science. AgentDS consists of 17 challenges across six industries: commerce, food production, healthcare, insurance, manufacturing, and retail banking. We conducted an open competition involving 29 teams and 80 participants, enabling systematic comparison between human-AI collaborative approaches and AI-only baselines. Our results show that current AI agents struggle with domain-specific reasoning. AI-only baselines perform near or below the median of competition participants, while the strongest solutions arise from human-AI collaboration. These findings challenge the narrative of complete automation by AI and underscore the enduring importance of human expertise in data science, while illuminating directions for the next generation of AI. Visit the AgentDS website here: https://agentds.org/ and open source datasets here: https://huggingface.co/datasets/lainmn/AgentDS .

AI.tldr: AgentDS benchmarks AI agents against human-AI collaboration in 17 domain-specific data science tasks across six industries; results show AI alone underperforms and human-AI collaboration yields the strongest performance, challenging full automation narratives.
AI.motivation: Quantify how well AI agents perform on domain-specific data science tasks compared to human experts, to identify remaining gaps and guide future AI development.
AI.method: AgentDS is a benchmark with 17 challenges spanning commerce, food production, healthcare, insurance, manufacturing, and retail banking. An open competition included 29 teams and 80 participants, comparing AI-only baselines to human-AI collaborative solutions across tasks.
AI.result: Current AI agents struggle with domain-specific reasoning. AI-only baselines perform near or below the competition median, while the strongest results arise from human-AI collaboration.
AI.conclusion: Automation by AI is not yet complete for domain-specific data science; human expertise remains essential. The work highlights directions for advancing AI to better support or augment domain experts.

title: MultihopSpatial: Multi-hop Compositional Spatial Reasoning Benchmark for Vision-Language Model
summary: Spatial reasoning is foundational for Vision-Language Models (VLMs), particularly when deployed as Vision-Language-Action (VLA) agents in physical environments. However, existing benchmarks predominantly focus on elementary, single-hop relations, neglecting the multi-hop compositional reasoning and precise visual grounding essential for real-world scenarios. To address this, we introduce MultihopSpatial, offering three key contributions: (1) A comprehensive benchmark designed for multi-hop and compositional spatial reasoning, featuring 1- to 3-hop complex queries across diverse spatial perspectives. (2) Acc@50IoU, a complementary metric that simultaneously evaluates reasoning and visual grounding by requiring both answer selection and precise bounding box prediction - capabilities vital for robust VLA deployment. (3) MultihopSpatial-Train, a dedicated large-scale training corpus to foster spatial intelligence. Extensive evaluation of 37 state-of-the-art VLMs yields eight key insights, revealing that compositional spatial reasoning remains a formidable challenge. Finally, we demonstrate that reinforcement learning post-training on our corpus enhances both intrinsic VLM spatial reasoning and downstream embodied manipulation performance.

AI.tldr: Introduces MultihopSpatial: a multi-hop spatial reasoning benchmark for Vision-Language-Action, plus Acc@50IoU and a large-scale training corpus; comprehensive evaluation shows multi-hop spatial reasoning remains challenging, with RL post-training improving intrinsic reasoning and embodied manipulation.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks emphasize single-hop spatial relations and lack precise visual grounding required for real-world VLA; a dedicated multi-hop, grounding-focused benchmark and training data are needed.
AI.method: Three contributions: (1) MultihopSpatial benchmark with 1–3-hop complex queries across diverse spatial perspectives; (2) Acc@50IoU metric that combines answer selection with precise bounding-box prediction; (3) MultihopSpatial-Train large-scale corpus for training spatial intelligence.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation of 37 state-of-the-art VLMs yields eight insights, indicating compositional spatial reasoning remains challenging; reinforcement learning post-training on the corpus enhances both intrinsic VLM spatial reasoning and downstream embodied manipulation performance.
AI.conclusion: MultihopSpatial provides a principled benchmark, metric, and training data to advance robust spatial reasoning in VLA; RL on the corpus is a viable route to improve both reasoning and manipulation.

title: When Differential Privacy Meets Wireless Federated Learning: An Improved Analysis for Privacy and Convergence
summary: Differentially private wireless federated learning (DPWFL) is a promising framework for protecting sensitive user data. However, foundational questions on how to precisely characterize privacy loss remain open, and existing work is further limited by convergence analyses that rely on restrictive convexity assumptions or ignore the effect of gradient clipping. To overcome these issues, we present a comprehensive analysis of privacy and convergence for DPWFL with general smooth non-convex loss objectives. Our analysis explicitly incorporates both device selection and mini-batch sampling, and shows that the privacy loss can converge to a constant rather than diverge with the number of iterations. Moreover, we establish convergence guarantees with gradient clipping and derive an explicit privacy-utility trade-off. Numerical results validate our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive analysis of privacy and convergence for differentially private wireless federated learning (DPWFL) with non-convex objectives, explicitly incorporating device selection and mini-batch sampling; shows that privacy loss can converge to a constant with iterations, establishes convergence under gradient clipping, and provides an explicit privacy-utility trade-off validated by numerical results.
AI.motivation: Existing DPWFL analyses rely on restrictive convexity assumptions or ignore gradient clipping, leaving precise privacy loss characterization and practical convergence guarantees for non-convex settings unresolved.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis that models DPWFL with general smooth non-convex loss objectives, incorporating device selection and mini-batch sampling; derives bounds showing privacy loss converges to a constant; proves convergence guarantees under gradient clipping; and characterizes the privacy-utility trade-off. Numerical experiments validate the theory.
AI.result: Privacy loss can converge to a constant rather than diverge with the number of iterations; convergence guarantees hold when gradient clipping is used; explicit privacy-utility trade-off derived; numerical results corroborate the theoretical findings.
AI.conclusion: DPWFL with non-convex objectives and practical mechanisms (device sampling, mini-batch updates, gradient clipping) yields reliable privacy guarantees and convergent learning, with a clear trade-off between privacy level and utility that is supported by empirical validation.

title: Translating MRI to PET through Conditional Diffusion Models with Enhanced Pathology Awareness
summary: Positron emission tomography (PET) is a widely recognized technique for diagnosing neurodegenerative diseases, offering critical functional insights. However, its high costs and radiation exposure hinder its widespread use. In contrast, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) does not involve such limitations. While MRI also detects neurodegenerative changes, it is less sensitive for diagnosis compared to PET. To overcome such limitations, one approach is to generate synthetic PET from MRI. Recent advances in generative models have paved the way for cross-modality medical image translation; however, existing methods largely emphasize structural preservation while neglecting the critical need for pathology awareness. To address this gap, we propose PASTA, a novel image translation framework built on conditional diffusion models with enhanced pathology awareness. PASTA surpasses state-of-the-art methods by preserving both structural and pathological details through its highly interactive dual-arm architecture and multi-modal condition integration. Additionally, we introduce a novel cycle exchange consistency and volumetric generation strategy that significantly enhances PASTA's ability to produce high-quality 3D PET images. Our qualitative and quantitative results demonstrate the high quality and pathology awareness of the synthesized PET scans. For Alzheimer's diagnosis, the performance of these synthesized scans improves over MRI by 4%, almost reaching the performance of actual PET. Our code is available at https://github.com/ai-med/PASTA.

AI.tldr: PASTA is a conditional diffusion-based framework that translates MRI to synthetically generate PET with enhanced pathology awareness, featuring a dual-arm architecture and cycle exchange, enabling high-quality 3D PET and improved Alzheimer's diagnostic performance.
AI.motivation: PET offers functional insights but is costly and involves radiation; MRI is safer but less sensitive for PET-level pathology. There is a need for pathology-aware cross-modality translation to reduce PET reliance.
AI.method: PASTA uses conditional diffusion models with a dual-arm architecture and multi-modal conditioning to translate MRI to PET. It introduces cycle exchange consistency and a volumetric generation strategy to produce high-quality 3D PET images while preserving pathology.
AI.result: PASTA outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both structural and pathological preservation. Qualitative and quantitative analyses show high-quality PET synthesis, with Alzheimer's diagnosis performance improved by ~4% over MRI and nearing true PET performance. Code is available online.
AI.conclusion: PASTA delivers pathology-aware, high-fidelity MRI-to-PET synthesis, including effective 3D generation, closing the gap between MRI and PET for neurodegenerative diagnosis and enabling practical clinical translation.

title: Communication-Efficient and Robust Multi-Modal Federated Learning via Latent-Space Consensus
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
summary: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed devices without sharing raw data, but applying FL to multi-modal settings introduces significant challenges. Clients typically possess heterogeneous modalities and model architectures, making it difficult to align feature spaces efficiently while preserving privacy and minimizing communication costs. To address this, we introduce CoMFed, a Communication-Efficient Multi-Modal Federated Learning framework that uses learnable projection matrices to generate compressed latent representations. A latent-space regularizer aligns these representations across clients, improving cross-modal consistency and robustness to outliers. Experiments on human activity recognition benchmarks show that CoMFed achieves competitive accuracy with minimal overhead.

AI.tldr: CoMFed is a communication-efficient multi-modal federated learning framework that uses learnable projection matrices to compress latent representations and a latent-space regularizer to align them across clients, achieving competitive HAR accuracy with low overhead.
AI.motivation: Multi-modal FL faces heterogeneity in modalities and architectures across clients, making feature-space alignment difficult while preserving privacy and minimizing communication.
AI.method: Introduce learnable projection matrices to map local features into compressed latent representations; employ a latent-space regularizer to align these latent representations across clients, enforcing cross-modal consistency and robustness to outliers.
AI.result: Empirical validation on human activity recognition benchmarks shows competitive accuracy with minimal communication and computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: CoMFed demonstrates effective cross-modal alignment in FL with reduced communication cost and robustness, suggesting practical viability for edge-based multi-modal learning.

title: GHOST: Fast Category-agnostic Hand-Object Interaction Reconstruction from RGB Videos using Gaussian Splatting
summary: Understanding realistic hand-object interactions from monocular RGB videos is essential for AR/VR, robotics, and embodied AI. Existing methods rely on category-specific templates or heavy computation, yet still produce physically inconsistent hand-object alignment in 3D. We introduce GHOST (Gaussian Hand-Object Splatting), a fast, category-agnostic framework for reconstructing dynamic hand-object interactions using 2D Gaussian Splatting. GHOST represents both hands and objects as dense, view-consistent Gaussian discs and introduces three key innovations: (1) a geometric-prior retrieval and consistency loss that completes occluded object regions, (2) a grasp-aware alignment that refines hand translations and object scale to ensure realistic contact, and (3) a hand-aware background loss that prevents penalizing hand-occluded object regions. GHOST achieves complete, physically consistent, and animatable reconstructions from a single RGB video while running an order of magnitude faster than prior category-agnostic methods. Extensive experiments on ARCTIC, HO3D, and in-the-wild datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art accuracy in 3D reconstruction and 2D rendering quality, establishing GHOST as an efficient and robust solution for realistic hand-object interaction modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/ATAboukhadra/GHOST.

AI.tldr: GHOST provides fast, category-agnostic 3D hand–object reconstruction from a single RGB video using 2D Gaussian Splatting. It introduces geometric-prior retrieval with consistency loss, grasp-aware alignment, and a hand-aware background loss to ensure realistic contact, achieving state-of-the-art results and faster speed than prior category-agnostic methods.
AI.motivation: Existing monocular hand–object reconstruction methods rely on category-specific templates or heavy computation and still yield physically inconsistent 3D alignments. A fast, category-agnostic, physically coherent method is needed for AR/VR, robotics, and embodied AI.
AI.method: Represent hands and objects as dense, view-consistent Gaussian discs via 2D Gaussian Splatting. Introduce (1) geometric-prior retrieval and a consistency loss to complete occluded regions, (2) grasp-aware alignment to refine hand translations and object scale for realistic contact, (3) a hand-aware background loss to avoid penalizing hand-occluded regions. The approach enables complete, animatable reconstructions from a single RGB video and is designed to be much faster than previous category-agnostic methods.
AI.result: GHOST achieves complete, physically consistent, and animatable hand–object reconstructions from monocular video and runs an order of magnitude faster than prior category-agnostic methods. Experiments on ARCTIC, HO3D, and in-the-wild datasets report state-of-the-art 3D reconstruction quality and 2D rendering fidelity.
AI.conclusion: GHOST is an efficient, robust solution for realistic hand–object interaction modeling from monocular RGB data; code is released, enabling practical deployment.

title: Position: Spectral GNNs Are Neither Spectral Nor Superior for Node Classification
summary: Spectral Graph Neural Networks (Spectral GNNs) for node classification promise frequency-domain filtering on graphs, yet rest on flawed foundations. Recent work shows that graph Laplacian eigenvectors do not in general have the key properties of a true Fourier basis, but leaves the empirical success of Spectral GNNs unexplained. We identify two theoretical glitches: (1) commonly used "graph Fourier bases" are not classical Fourier bases for graph signals; (2) (n-1)-degree polynomials (n = number of nodes) can exactly interpolate any spectral response via a Vandermonde system, so the usual "polynomial approximation" narrative is not theoretically justified. The effectiveness of GCN is commonly attributed to spectral low-pass filtering, yet we prove that low- and high-pass behaviors arise solely from message-passing dynamics rather than Graph Fourier Transform-based spectral formulations. We then analyze two representative directed spectral models, MagNet and HoloNet. Their reported effectiveness is not spectral: it arises from implementation issues that reduce them to powerful MPNNs. When implemented consistently with the claimed spectral algorithms, performance becomes weak. This position paper argues that: for node classification, Spectral GNNs neither meaningfully capture the graph spectrum nor reliably improve performance; competitive results are better explained by their equivalence to MPNNs, sometimes aided by implementations inconsistent with their intended design.

AI.tldr: Spectral GNNs do not meaningfully utilize graph spectra for node classification; two theoretical issues undermine the foundations: graph Fourier bases are not true Fourier bases for graph signals, and any spectral response can be exactly interpolated by degree-(n-1) polynomials, invalidating the common polynomial-approximation reasoning. Empirical success of spectral models stems from their equivalence to standard MPNNs or from implementation choices that mimic spatial methods rather than genuine spectral filtering.
AI.motivation: Explain the empirical success and theoretical legitimacy of spectral GNNs for node classification; identify foundational glitches and assess whether spectral formulations provide real advantages over message-passing baselines.
AI.method: Theoretical critique of graph Fourier bases vs classical Fourier bases; demonstration that high-degree Vandermonde interpolation can realize any spectral response; analysis of low-/high-pass behavior as emerging from message-passing dynamics rather than spectral transforms; evaluation of MagNet and HoloNet to determine whether their effectiveness is spectral or due to architectural/implementation factors; comparison under consistent spectral algorithm implementations.
AI.result: Two core issues undermine the spectral interpretation: (i) the presumed graph Fourier bases are not true Fourier bases for graph signals; (ii) high-degree interpolation via Vandermonde systems invalidates the claim that polynomial approximations justify spectral filtering. Consequently, the perceived low-pass effects of spectral GNNs are artifacts of message passing, not the spectrum. MagNet and HoloNet do not exhibit genuine spectral advantages when implemented consistently; their reported performance largely reduces to MPNN-like behavior or to flawed/cheating implementations that diverge from their intended spectral design.
AI.conclusion: For node classification, Spectral GNNs do not reliably capture the graph spectrum nor consistently improve performance over neighborhood-message-passing methods. Reported gains can be explained by equivalence to MPNNs or by implementation choices inconsistent with the claimed spectral design; serious re-evaluation of the spectral GNN paradigm is warranted, focusing on principled spectrum-aware approaches only when grounded in solid theoretical foundations.

title: Unsupervised Contrastive Learning for Efficient and Robust Spectral Shape Matching
summary: Estimating correspondences between pairs of non-rigid deformable 3D shapes remains a significant challenge in computer vision and graphics. While deep functional map methods have become the go-to solution for addressing this problem, they primarily focus on optimizing pointwise and functional maps either individually or jointly, rather than directly enhancing feature representations in the embedding space, which often results in inadequate feature quality and suboptimal matching performance. Furthermore, these approaches heavily rely on traditional functional map techniques, such as time-consuming functional map solvers, which incur substantial computational costs. In this work, we introduce, for the first time, a novel unsupervised contrastive learning-based approach for efficient and robust 3D shape matching. We begin by presenting an unsupervised contrastive learning framework that promotes feature learning by maximizing consistency within positive similarity pairs and minimizing it within negative similarity pairs, thereby improving both the consistency and discriminability of the learned features.We then design a significantly simplified functional map learning architecture that eliminates the need for computationally expensive functional map solvers and multiple auxiliary functional map losses, greatly enhancing computational efficiency. By integrating these two components into a unified two-branch pipeline, our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both accuracy and efficiency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach is not only computationally efficient but also outperforms current state-of-the-art methods across various challenging benchmarks, including near-isometric, non-isometric, and topologically inconsistent scenarios, even surpassing supervised techniques.

AI.tldr: An unsupervised two-branch approach for efficient and robust non-rigid 3D shape matching: (1) contrastive feature learning to improve embeddings, and (2) a simplified functional-map module that avoids heavy solvers, yielding state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency, often surpassing supervised methods.
AI.motivation: Address the limited embedding quality and computational cost of existing deep functional map methods for non-rigid 3D shape matching by enhancing feature representations with unsupervised contrastive learning and replacing costly functional map solvers with a lean alternative.
AI.method: Two-branch architecture: (1) unsupervised contrastive learning to learn discriminative, consistent embeddings by maximizing positive pair similarity and minimizing negative pair similarity; (2) a simplified functional map learning module that eliminates expensive solvers and multiple auxiliary losses; ends with an integrated pipeline for efficient, end-to-end correspondence estimation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in accuracy and efficiency across challenging benchmarks (near-isometric, non-isometric, topologically inconsistent), outperforming existing methods and even some supervised approaches.
AI.conclusion: Coupling unsupervised contrastive feature learning with a lean functional-map pipeline yields robust and scalable 3D shape matching, delivering high accuracy with significant computational efficiency.

title: On Optimizing Multimodal Jailbreaks for Spoken Language Models
summary: As Spoken Language Models (SLMs) integrate speech and text modalities, they inherit the safety vulnerabilities of their LLM backbone and an expanded attack surface. SLMs have been previously shown to be susceptible to jailbreaking, where adversarial prompts induce harmful responses. Yet existing attacks largely remain unimodal, optimizing either text or audio in isolation. We explore gradient-based multimodal jailbreaks by introducing JAMA (Joint Audio-text Multimodal Attack), a joint multimodal optimization framework combining Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) for text and Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) for audio, to simultaneously perturb both modalities. Evaluations across four state-of-the-art SLMs and four audio types demonstrate that JAMA surpasses unimodal jailbreak rate by 1.5x to 10x. We analyze the operational dynamics of this joint attack and show that a sequential approximation method makes it 4x to 6x faster. Our findings suggest that unimodal safety is insufficient for robust SLMs. The code and data are available at https://repos.lsv.uni-saarland.de/akrishnan/multimodal-jailbreak-slm

AI.tldr: Joint Audio-text Multimodal Attack (JAMA) introduces a gradient-based joint optimization over text and audio to jailbreak SLMs, achieving markedly higher rates than unimodal attacks; a sequential approximation speeds training by 4–6x.
AI.motivation: Spoken Language Models combine speech and text and inherit broader attack surfaces from LLMs; existing studies focus on unimodal prompts, leaving cross-modal vulnerabilities underexplored. Understanding multimodal jailbreaks is essential for robust safety.
AI.method: JAMA jointly optimizes text perturbations via Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) and audio perturbations via Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) to craft adversarial prompts that jailbreak SLMs; evaluated across four state-of-the-art SLMs and four audio types; analyzes dynamics; proposes a sequential approximation to speed up optimization.
AI.result: JAMA increases unimodal jailbreak rate by 1.5x–10x across tested models and audio types; a sequential approximation yields 4x–6x speedup, indicating practical feasibility of multimodal attacks.
AI.conclusion: Unimodal safety is insufficient for robust SLMs; defenses must account for cross-modal adversarial manipulation. The authors provide code and data for replication.

title: VGGT-360: Geometry-Consistent Zero-Shot Panoramic Depth Estimation
summary: This paper presents VGGT-360, a novel training-free framework for zero-shot, geometry-consistent panoramic depth estimation. Unlike prior view-independent training-free approaches, VGGT-360 reformulates the task as panoramic reprojection over multi-view reconstructed 3D models by leveraging the intrinsic 3D consistency of VGGT-like foundation models, thereby unifying fragmented per-view reasoning into a coherent panoramic understanding. To achieve robust and accurate estimation, VGGT-360 integrates three plug-and-play modules that form a unified panorama-to-3D-to-depth framework: (i) Uncertainty-guided adaptive projection slices panoramas into perspective views to bridge the domain gap between panoramic inputs and VGGT's perspective prior. It estimates gradient-based uncertainty to allocate denser views to geometry-poor regions, yielding geometry-informative inputs for VGGT. (ii) Structure-saliency enhanced attention strengthens VGGT's robustness during 3D reconstruction by injecting structure-aware confidence into its attention layers, guiding focus toward geometrically reliable regions and enhancing cross-view coherence. (iii) Correlation-weighted 3D model correction refines the reconstructed 3D model by reweighting overlapping points using attention-inferred correlation scores, providing a consistent geometric basis for accurate panoramic reprojection. Extensive experiments show that VGGT-360 outperforms both trained and training-free state-of-the-art methods across multiple resolutions and diverse indoor and outdoor datasets.

AI.tldr: VGGT-360 is a training-free, panoramic depth estimation framework that unifies per-view reasoning into a 3D-consistent panorama via multi-view 3D models and three plug-and-play modules (uncertainty-guided projection, structure-saliency attention, and correlation-weighted 3D correction) to achieve state-of-the-art zero-shot depth estimation.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of view-independent training-free methods by exploiting 3D consistency and panoramic reprojection, enabling robust zero-shot depth estimation without training.
AI.method: Formulates panoramic depth estimation as a panorama-to-3D-to-depth pipeline using multi-view reconstructed 3D models, with three modules: (i) adaptive projection slices with gradient-based uncertainty to allocate dense views to geometry-poor regions; (ii) structure-saliency guided attention to improve 3D reconstruction; (iii) correlation-weighted 3D correction to refine overlapping points; integrated into a VGGT-like foundation-model framework.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show VGGT-360 outperforms trained and training-free state-of-the-art methods across multiple resolutions and diverse indoor and outdoor datasets.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of a training-free, geometry-consistent panoramic depth estimation framework and suggests a generalizable approach for leveraging foundation-model priors in 3D-aware vision tasks.

title: CRAFT: Aligning Diffusion Models with Fine-Tuning Is Easier Than You Think
summary: Aligning Diffusion models has achieved remarkable breakthroughs in generating high-quality, human preference-aligned images. Existing techniques, such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and DPO-style preference optimization, have become principled tools for fine-tuning diffusion models. However, SFT relies on high-quality images that are costly to obtain, while DPO-style methods depend on large-scale preference datasets, which are often inconsistent in quality. Beyond data dependency, these methods are further constrained by computational inefficiency. To address these two challenges, we propose Composite Reward Assisted Fine-Tuning (CRAFT), a lightweight yet powerful fine-tuning paradigm that requires significantly reduced training data while maintaining computational efficiency. It first leverages a Composite Reward Filtering (CRF) technique to construct a high-quality and consistent training dataset and then perform an enhanced variant of SFT. We also theoretically prove that CRAFT actually optimizes the lower bound of group-based reinforcement learning, establishing a principled connection between SFT with selected data and reinforcement learning. Our extensive empirical results demonstrate that CRAFT with only 100 samples can easily outperform recent SOTA preference optimization methods with thousands of preference-paired samples. Moreover, CRAFT can even achieve 11-220$\times$ faster convergences than the baseline preference optimization methods, highlighting its extremely high efficiency.

AI.tldr: CRAFT: A lightweight diffusion model fine-tuning approach using Composite Reward Filtering to curate high-quality data and an enhanced SFT, achieving strong performance with limited data and significantly faster convergence.
AI.motivation: Address data and compute bottlenecks in diffusion model alignment: expensive high-quality images for SFT and large, inconsistent preference datasets for DPO-style methods.
AI.method: Introduce Composite Reward Filtering (CRF) to construct a high-quality training set, followed by an enhanced supervised fine-tuning (SFT). The approach is theoretically linked to the lower bound of group-based reinforcement learning, bridging SFT with RL.
AI.result: With only 100 samples, CRAFT outperforms state-of-the-art preference optimization methods trained on thousands of preference-paired samples and achieves 11–220× faster convergence than baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: CRAFT offers a data- and compute-efficient fine-tuning paradigm for diffusion models, aligning human preferences with reduced data requirements and establishing a principled RL connection.

title: Adaptive Regime-Aware Stock Price Prediction Using Autoencoder-Gated Dual Node Transformers with Reinforcement Learning Control
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems. 17 pages, 9 figures, 10 tables
summary: Stock markets exhibit regime-dependent behavior where prediction models optimized for stable conditions often fail during volatile periods. Existing approaches typically treat all market states uniformly or require manual regime labeling, which is expensive and quickly becomes stale as market dynamics evolve. This paper introduces an adaptive prediction framework that adaptively identifies deviations from normal market conditions and routes data through specialized prediction pathways. The architecture consists of three components: (1) an autoencoder trained on normal market conditions that identifies anomalous regimes through reconstruction error, (2) dual node transformer networks specialized for stable and event-driven market conditions respectively, and (3) a Soft Actor-Critic reinforcement learning controller that adaptively tunes the regime detection threshold and pathway blending weights based on prediction performance feedback. The reinforcement learning component enables the system to learn adaptive regime boundaries, defining anomalies as market states where standard prediction approaches fail. Experiments on 20 S&P 500 stocks spanning 1982 to 2025 demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves 0.68% MAPE for one-day predictions without the reinforcement controller and 0.59% MAPE with the full adaptive system, compared to 0.80% for the baseline integrated node transformer. Directional accuracy reaches 72% with the complete framework. The system maintains robust performance during high-volatility periods, with MAPE below 0.85% when baseline models exceed 1.5%. Ablation studies confirm that each component contributes meaningfully: autoencoder routing accounts for 36% relative MAPE degradation upon removal, followed by the SAC controller at 15% and the dual-path architecture at 7%.

AI.tldr: Adaptive regime-aware stock prediction framework routing data to specialized predictors using an autoencoder-based anomaly detector, dual-path transformers for stable and event-driven regimes, and a Soft Actor-Critic controller to tune regime thresholds and blending. Shows improved one-day MAPE and directional accuracy over a baseline; ablations confirm contributions of routing, RL controller, and dual-path design.
AI.motivation: Stock markets exhibit regime shifts and volatility where models optimized for stable conditions fail; manual regime labeling is costly and becomes stale as dynamics evolve. An adaptive, label-free routing system can maintain performance across regimes.
AI.method: An autoencoder trained on normal market conditions detects anomalies via reconstruction error. Dual node transformer networks specialize for stable vs. event-driven regimes. A Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning controller adaptively tunes the regime-detection threshold and the blending weights between pathways based on prediction performance. Anomalies are defined as market states where standard prediction approaches fail. Evaluations on 20 S&P 500 stocks from 1982–2025 compare the adaptive system to a baseline integrated node transformer.
AI.result: MAPE for one-day predictions: 0.68% without the RL controller; 0.59% with the full adaptive system, vs 0.80% baseline. Directional accuracy: 72%. Robustness during high volatility: MAPE below 0.85% when baselines exceed 1.5%. Ablation results: autoencoder routing accounts for 36% relative MAPE degradation when removed; SAC controller 15%; dual-path architecture 7%.
AI.conclusion: An adaptive, regime-aware architecture that routes predictions through specialized pathways guided by an autoencoder-based anomaly detector and reinforced by an adaptive controller yields improved accuracy and stability across regimes. Each component meaningfully contributes to performance gains, with routing and the RL controller providing substantial benefits.

title: Unleashing the Power of Simplicity: A Minimalist Strategy for State-of-the-Art Fingerprint Enhancement
summary: Fingerprint recognition systems, which rely on the unique characteristics of human fingerprints, are essential in modern security and verification applications. Accurate minutiae extraction, a critical step in these systems, depends on the quality of fingerprint images. Despite recent improvements in fingerprint enhancement techniques, state-of-the-art methods often struggle with low-quality fingerprints and can be computationally demanding. This paper presents a minimalist approach to fingerprint enhancement, prioritizing simplicity and effectiveness. Two novel methods are introduced: a contextual filtering method and a learning-based method. These techniques consistently outperform complex state-of-the-art methods, producing clearer, more accurate, and less noisy images. The effectiveness of these methods is validated using a challenging latent fingerprint database. The open-source implementation of these techniques not only fosters reproducibility but also encourages further advancements in the field. The findings underscore the importance of simplicity in achieving high-quality fingerprint enhancement and suggest that future research should balance complexity and practical benefits.

AI.tldr: Minimalist fingerprint enhancement with contextual filtering and a learning-based method outperforms complex approaches on latent fingerprints, highlighting simplicity and reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Improve robustness of minutiae extraction for low-quality fingerprints while reducing computational demands; address challenges in latent fingerprint data.
AI.method: Propose two novel techniques—a contextual filtering method and a learning-based method—within a minimalist framework, accompanied by open-source implementation to promote reproducibility.
AI.result: Shows superior performance to state-of-the-art methods, delivering clearer, more accurate, and less noisy fingerprint images; validated on a challenging latent fingerprint database.
AI.conclusion: Supports the value of simplicity in achieving high-quality fingerprint enhancement; advocates balancing model complexity with practical benefits; open-source code fosters reproducibility and community-driven advancement.

title: Hierarchical Latent Structure Learning through Online Inference
summary: Learning systems must balance generalization across experiences with discrimination of task-relevant details. Effective learning therefore requires representations that support both. Online latent-cause models support incremental inference but assume flat partitions, whereas hierarchical Bayesian models capture multilevel structure but typically require offline inference. We introduce the Hierarchical Online Learning of Multiscale Experience Structure (HOLMES) model, a computational framework for hierarchical latent structure learning through online inference. HOLMES combines a variation on the nested Chinese Restaurant Process prior with sequential Monte Carlo inference to perform tractable trial-by-trial inference over hierarchical latent representations without explicit supervision over the latent structure. In simulations, HOLMES matched the predictive performance of flat models while learning more compact representations that supported one-shot transfer to higher-level latent categories. In a context-dependent task with nested temporal structure, HOLMES also improved outcome prediction relative to flat models. These results provide a tractable computational framework for discovering hierarchical structure in sequential data.

AI.tldr: HOLMES enables online, hierarchical latent structure learning by combining a nested-CRP prior with sequential Monte Carlo, achieving competitive predictions with more compact representations and improved transfer in nested tasks.
AI.motivation: Learning systems must balance generalization across experiences with discrimination of task-relevant details. Online latent-cause models support incremental inference but assume flat partitions; hierarchical Bayesian models capture multilevel structure but typically require offline inference. A scalable online method for discovering hierarchical structure is needed.
AI.method: Introduce HOLMES, a hierarchical online learning model: a variation on the nested Chinese Restaurant Process prior combined with sequential Monte Carlo inference to perform tractable trial-by-trial inference over hierarchical latent representations without explicit supervision.
AI.result: In simulations, HOLMES matched the predictive performance of flat models while learning more compact representations that supported one-shot transfer to higher-level latent categories. In a context-dependent task with nested temporal structure, HOLMES improved outcome prediction relative to flat models.
AI.conclusion: HOLMES provides a tractable computational framework for discovering hierarchical structure in sequential data and enables online learning of multiscale latent representations.

title: Generalized Hand-Object Pose Estimation with Occlusion Awareness
summary: Generalized 3D hand-object pose estimation from a single RGB image remains challenging due to the large variations in object appearances and interaction patterns, especially under heavy occlusion. We propose GenHOI, a framework for generalized hand-object pose estimation with occlusion awareness. GenHOI integrates hierarchical semantic knowledge with hand priors to enhance model generalization under challenging occlusion conditions. Specifically, we introduce a hierarchical semantic prompt that encodes object states, hand configurations, and interaction patterns via textual descriptions. This enables the model to learn abstract high-level representations of hand-object interactions for generalization to unseen objects and novel interactions while compensating for missing or ambiguous visual cues. To enable robust occlusion reasoning, we adopt a multi-modal masked modeling strategy over RGB images, predicted point clouds, and textual descriptions. Moreover, we leverage hand priors as stable spatial references to extract implicit interaction constraints. This allows reliable pose inference even under significant variations in object shapes and interaction patterns. Extensive experiments on the challenging DexYCB and HO3Dv2 benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in hand-object pose estimation.

AI.tldr: GenHOI introduces a generalized occlusion-aware hand-object pose estimation framework that uses hierarchical semantic prompts and multi-modal masked modeling with hand priors, achieving state-of-the-art results on DexYCB and HO3Dv2.
AI.motivation: 3D hand-object pose estimation under heavy occlusion and with unseen objects/interactions remains challenging; RGB cues are insufficient alone, and prior knowledge plus structured representations can improve generalization and robustness.
AI.method: GenHOI builds a framework with a hierarchical semantic prompt encoding object states, hand configurations, and interaction patterns via textual descriptions. It uses a multi-modal masked modeling strategy over RGB images, predicted point clouds, and textual descriptions, and leverages hand priors as stable spatial references to constrain interactions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on DexYCB and HO3Dv2 show state-of-the-art performance, with improved generalization to unseen objects and novel interactions under occlusion.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that combining hierarchical semantic prompts, multi-modal masked modeling, and hand priors yields robust, generalizable hand-object pose estimation under occlusion, validated on challenging benchmarks.

title: SHAPCA: Consistent and Interpretable Explanations for Machine Learning Models on Spectroscopy Data
summary: In recent years, machine learning models have been increasingly applied to spectroscopic datasets for chemical and biomedical analysis. For their successful adoption, particularly in clinical and safety-critical settings, professionals and researchers must be able to understand and trust the reasoning behind model predictions. However, the inherently high dimensionality and strong collinearity of spectroscopy data pose a fundamental challenge to model explainability. These properties not only complicate model training but also undermine the stability and consistency of explanations, leading to fluctuations in feature importance across repeated training runs. Feature extraction techniques have been used to reduce the input dimensionality; these new features hinder the connection between the prediction and the original signal. This study proposes SHAPCA, an explainable machine learning pipeline that combines Principal Component Analysis (for dimensionality reduction) and Shapely Additive exPlanations (for post hoc explanation) to provide explanations in the original input space, which a practitioner can interpret and link back to the biological components. The proposed framework enables analysis from both global and local perspectives, revealing the spectral bands that drive overall model behaviour as well as the instance-specific features that influence individual predictions. Numerical analysis demonstrated the interpretability of the results and greater consistency across different runs.

AI.tldr: A PCA-SHAP hybrid pipeline (SHAPCA) yields explanations in the original spectral space for spectroscopy ML, enabling stable global/local interpretability with consistency across runs.
AI.motivation: Spectroscopy data are high-dimensional and strongly collinear, which hinders model training and undermines the stability and interpretability of explanations. Practitioners need explanations that map back to physically meaningful spectral features.
AI.method: Apply Principal Component Analysis for dimensionality reduction, then use SHAP (Shapley Additive exPlanations) for post-hoc explanations. Reconstruct explanations in the original input space using PCA loadings to allow interpretation in terms of the original spectra. Provide both global (dataset-wide) and local (instance-specific) explanations.
AI.result: Numerical analyses show interpretability of results and greater consistency of explanations across different runs; identified spectral bands and features that drive model behavior and individual predictions.
AI.conclusion: SHAPCA offers an interpretable, stable pipeline for spectroscopy ML that links model decisions back to original spectral features, supporting use in clinical/safety-critical settings; further validation across more datasets and models would strengthen generalizability and benchmark comparisons.

title: Rethinking MLLM Itself as a Segmenter with a Single Segmentation Token
summary: Recent segmentation methods leveraging Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown reliable object-level segmentation and enhanced spatial perception. However, almost all previous methods predominantly rely on specialist mask decoders to interpret masks from generated segmentation-related embeddings and visual features, or incorporate multiple additional tokens to assist. This paper aims to investigate whether and how we can unlock segmentation from MLLM itSELF with 1 segmentation Embedding (SELF1E) while achieving competitive results, which eliminates the need for external decoders. To this end, our approach targets the fundamental limitation of resolution reduction in pixel-shuffled image features from MLLMs. First, we retain image features at their original uncompressed resolution, and refill them with residual features extracted from MLLM-processed compressed features, thereby improving feature precision. Subsequently, we integrate pixel-unshuffle operations on image features with and without LLM processing, respectively, to unleash the details of compressed features and amplify the residual features under uncompressed resolution, which further enhances the resolution of refilled features. Moreover, we redesign the attention mask with dual perception pathways, i.e., image-to-image and image-to-segmentation, enabling rich feature interaction between pixels and the segmentation token. Comprehensive experiments across multiple segmentation tasks validate that SELF1E achieves performance competitive with specialist mask decoder-based methods, demonstrating the feasibility of decoder-free segmentation in MLLMs. Project page: https://github.com/ANDYZAQ/SELF1E.

AI.tldr: Decoder-free segmentation in MLLMs via SELF1E: preserve uncompressed-resolution features, refine with residuals, apply pixel-unshuffle, and dual-path attention to match specialist decoders.
AI.motivation: Address reliance on external mask decoders and address resolution loss from pixel-shuffled MLLM features by enabling segmentation directly from MLLMs with a single segmentation embedding.
AI.method: (1) Keep image features at original resolution and refill with residuals from MLLM-processed compressed features. (2) Apply pixel-unshuffle to features with/without LLM processing to recover details and amplify residuals at uncompressed resolution. (3) Redesign attention mask with dual pathways: image-to-image and image-to-segmentation for rich interaction between pixels and the segmentation token. (4) Achieve segmentation without external decoders (SELF1E).
AI.result: Experiments across multiple segmentation tasks show SELF1E achieves competitive performance compared with specialist mask decoder-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of decoder-free segmentation in MLLMs and suggests a simpler, potentially more efficient segmentation pipeline.

title: SEM: Sparse Embedding Modulation for Post-Hoc Debiasing of Vision-Language Models
summary: Models that bridge vision and language, such as CLIP, are key components of multimodal AI, yet their large-scale, uncurated training data introduce severe social and spurious biases. Existing post-hoc debiasing methods often operate directly in the dense CLIP embedding space, where bias and task-relevant information are highly entangled. This entanglement limits their ability to remove bias without degrading semantic fidelity. In this work, we propose Sparse Embedding Modulation (SEM), a post-hoc, zero-shot debiasing framework that operates in a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) latent space. By decomposing CLIP text embeddings into disentangled features, SEM identifies and modulates bias-relevant neurons while preserving query-relevant ones. This enables more precise, non-linear interventions. Across four benchmark datasets and two CLIP backbones, SEM achieves substantial fairness gains in retrieval and zero-shot classification. Our results demonstrate that sparse latent representations provide an effective foundation for post-hoc debiasing of vision-language models.

AI.tldr: Introduces Sparse Embedding Modulation (SEM), a post-hoc, zero-shot debiasing framework that operates in a Sparse Autoencoder latent space to disentangle bias-related vs. query-relevant features in CLIP text embeddings, enabling targeted, non-linear debiasing.
AI.motivation: Vision–language models like CLIP are trained on large, uncurated data and exhibit social and spurious biases. Debiasing in dense embedding spaces entangles bias with semantic information, risking performance loss. A disentangled, sparse latent space could enable more precise interventions.
AI.method: Apply a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) to CLIP text embeddings and decompose them into disentangled features. Identify bias-relevant neurons in the SAE latent space and perform zero-shot modulation to suppress bias cues while preserving query-relevant neurons, enabling non-linear debiasing in a post-hoc setting. Evaluate across multiple backbones and datasets.
AI.result: SEM achieves substantial fairness gains in retrieval and zero-shot classification across four benchmark datasets and two CLIP backbones, demonstrating that sparse latent representations are a robust foundation for post-hoc debiasing of vision–language models.
AI.conclusion: Sparse latent representations in SAE spaces provide an effective foundation for targeted, post-hoc debiasing of vision–language models without severely compromising semantic fidelity.

title: Rigorous Error Certification for Neural PDE Solvers: From Empirical Residuals to Solution Guarantees
summary:
Uncertainty quantification for partial differential equations is traditionally grounded in discretization theory, where solution error is controlled via mesh/grid refinement. Physics-informed neural networks fundamentally depart from this paradigm: they approximate solutions by minimizing residual losses at collocation points, introducing new sources of error arising from optimization, sampling, representation, and overfitting. As a result, the generalization error in the solution space remains an open problem.
  Our main theoretical contribution establishes generalization bounds that connect residual control to solution-space error. We prove that when neural approximations lie in a compact subset of the solution space, vanishing residual error guarantees convergence to the true solution. We derive deterministic and probabilistic convergence results and provide certified generalization bounds translating residual, boundary, and initial errors into explicit solution error guarantees.

AI.tldr: This work develops generalization bounds for physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) in PDEs, linking residual control to solution-space error and providing convergence guarantees.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of theoretical generalization guarantees for PINNs in uncertainty quantification for PDEs, by establishing how residual, boundary, and initial errors translate into actual solution error.
