AI.method: Theoretical analysis establishing bounds for PINNs under the assumption that neural approximations lie in a compact subset of the solution space; derives deterministic and probabilistic convergence results and certified generalization bounds.
AI.result: Provide explicit generalization bounds that translate residual, boundary, and initial errors into solution error guarantees, and prove that vanishing residual error implies convergence to the true solution within the compact set.
AI.conclusion: Under the stated conditions, PINNs exhibit convergence guarantees: if residual errors vanish and the approximation remains in a compact subset, the neural solution converges to the true PDE solution with quantifiable bounds."

title: FUMO: Prior-Modulated Diffusion for Single Image Reflection Removal
summary: Single image reflection removal (SIRR) is challenging in real scenes, where reflection strength varies spatially and reflection patterns are tightly entangled with transmission structures. This paper presents a diffusion model with prior modulation framework (FUMO) that introduces explicit guidance signals to improve spatial controllability and structural faithfulness. Two priors are extracted directly from the mixed image, an intensity prior that estimates spatial reflection severity and a high-frequency prior that captures detail-sensitive responses via multi-scale residual aggregation. We propose a coarse-to-fine training paradigm. In the first stage, these cues are combined to gate the conditional residual injections, focusing the conditioning on regions that are both reflection-dominant and structure-sensitive. In the second stage, a fine-grained refinement network corrects local misalignment and sharpens fine details in the image space. Experiments conducted on both standard benchmarks and challenging images in the wild demonstrate competitive quantitative results and consistently improved perceptual quality. The code is released at https://github.com/Lucious-Desmon/FUMO.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-model-based SIRR with prior modulation (FUMO) using two priors (intensity and high-frequency) and a coarse-to-fine training plan to improve spatial controllability and structural fidelity, achieving competitive quantitative results and enhanced perceptual quality.
AI.motivation: Single image reflection removal in real scenes is hard due to spatially varying reflection strength and entangled reflections with transmission; existing methods struggle to balance quantitative accuracy with perceptual fidelity.
AI.method: Proposes FUMO: a diffusion model guided by explicit priors extracted from the mixed image. Two priors: intensity prior estimating reflection severity, and high-frequency prior capturing detail-sensitive responses via multi-scale residual aggregation. Training has two stages: Stage 1 gates conditional residual injections to focus conditioning on reflection-dominant and structure-sensitive regions; Stage 2 a refinement network corrects local misalignment and sharpens details in image space.
AI.result: On standard benchmarks and challenging wild images, achieves competitive quantitative performance and consistently improved perceptual quality.
AI.conclusion: The prior-modulated diffusion framework with coarse-to-fine strategy yields controllable, faithful SIRR, demonstrating the efficacy of explicit priors derived from the mixed image and a two-stage refinement; code released.

title: DyMoE: Dynamic Expert Orchestration with Mixed-Precision Quantization for Efficient MoE Inference on Edge
summary: Despite the computational efficiency of MoE models, the excessive memory footprint and I/O overhead inherent in multi-expert architectures pose formidable challenges for real-time inference on resource-constrained edge platforms. While existing static methods struggle with a rigid latency-accuracy trade-off, we observe that expert importance is highly skewed and depth-dependent. Motivated by these insights, we propose DyMoE, a dynamic mixed-precision quantization framework designed for high-performance edge inference. Leveraging insights into expert importance skewness and depth-dependent sensitivity, DyMoE introduces: (1) importance-aware prioritization to dynamically quantize experts at runtime; (2) depth-adaptive scheduling to preserve semantic integrity in critical layers; and (3) look-ahead prefetching to overlap I/O stalls. Experimental results on commercial edge hardware show that DyMoE reduces Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) by 3.44x-22.7x and up to a 14.58x speedup in Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT) compared to state-of-the-art offloading baselines, enabling real-time, accuracy-preserving MoE inference on resource-constrained edge devices.

AI.tldr: DyMoE enables real-time, accuracy-preserving MoE inference on edge devices by dynamic mixed-precision quantization guided by expert importance skew and depth-dependent sensitivity, featuring importance-aware prioritization, depth-adaptive scheduling, and look-ahead prefetching.
AI.motivation: MoE models offer high expressivity but incur large memory and I/O costs, hindering real-time inference on resource-constrained edge devices. Static quantization trades latency and accuracy rigidly and fails to adapt to skewed, depth-dependent sensitivity across experts.
AI.method: DyMoE introduces three components: (1) importance-aware prioritization to quantize experts at runtime based on their importance; (2) depth-adaptive scheduling to preserve semantic integrity in critical layers by adjusting quantization/allocation along depth; (3) look-ahead prefetching to overlap I/O stalls and hide memory latency.
AI.result: On commercial edge hardware DyMoE achieves 3.44x–22.7x reduction in Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) and up to 14.58x speedup in Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT) over state-of-the-art offloading baselines, enabling real-time MoE inference while preserving accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic mixed-precision MoE quantization with expert-skew-aware and depth-aware design, plus I/O-aware scheduling, can substantially reduce latency and memory overhead for edge MoE inference without sacrificing accuracy.

title: TerraScope: Pixel-Grounded Visual Reasoning for Earth Observation
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown promise in earth observation (EO), yet they struggle with tasks that require grounding complex spatial reasoning in precise pixel-level visual representations. To address this problem, we introduce TerraScope, a unified VLM that delivers pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning with two key capabilities: (1) modality-flexible reasoning: it handles single-modality inputs (optical or SAR) and adaptively fuses different modalities into the reasoning process when both are available; (2) multi-temporal reasoning: it integrates temporal sequences for change analysis across multiple time points. In addition, we curate Terra-CoT, a large-scale dataset containing 1 million samples with pixel-level masks embedded in reasoning chains across multiple sources. We also propose TerraScope-Bench, the first benchmark for pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning with six sub-tasks that evaluates both answer accuracy and mask quality to ensure authentic pixel-grounded reasoning. Experiments show that TerraScope significantly outperforms existing VLMs on pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning while providing interpretable visual evidence.

AI.tldr: TerraScope is a unified VLM for pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning enabling modality-flexible and multi-temporal analysis; paired with Terra-CoT dataset and TerraScope-Bench benchmark, it outperforms baselines with interpretable pixel-level evidence.
AI.motivation: EO VLMs struggle to ground complex spatial tasks at pixel-level and to fuse heterogeneous modalities (optical/SAR) and temporal data; need a unified framework and evaluative benchmarks.
AI.method: Propose TerraScope architecture: (i) modality-flexible reasoning with adaptive fusion of optical and SAR inputs; (ii) multi-temporal reasoning across time sequences; train/align with Terra-CoT (1M samples with pixel-level masks embedded in reasoning chains); evaluate with TerraScope-Bench (six sub-tasks focusing on accuracy and mask quality).
AI.result: TerraScope significantly outperforms existing VLMs on pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning and provides interpretable visual evidence; TerraScope-Bench enables comprehensive evaluation of reasoning and mask fidelity.
AI.conclusion: TerraScope advances pixel-grounded geospatial reasoning by integrating flexible modality fusion and temporal reasoning, supported by a large reasoning dataset and a dedicated benchmark; it sets a new standard for interpretable, pixel-grounded EO VLMs.

title: SOL-ExecBench: Speed-of-Light Benchmarking for Real-World GPU Kernels Against Hardware Limits
summary: As agentic AI systems become increasingly capable of generating and optimizing GPU kernels, progress is constrained by benchmarks that reward speedup over software baselines rather than proximity to hardware-efficient execution. We present SOL-ExecBench, a benchmark of 235 CUDA kernel optimization problems extracted from 124 production and emerging AI models spanning language, diffusion, vision, audio, video, and hybrid architectures, targeting NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs. The benchmark covers forward and backward workloads across BF16, FP8, and NVFP4, including kernels whose best performance is expected to rely on Blackwell-specific capabilities. Unlike prior benchmarks that evaluate kernels primarily relative to software implementations, SOL-ExecBench measures performance against analytically derived Speed-of-Light (SOL) bounds computed by SOLAR, our pipeline for deriving hardware-grounded SOL bounds, yielding a fixed target for hardware-efficient optimization. We report a SOL Score that quantifies how much of the gap between a release-defined scoring baseline and the hardware SOL bound a candidate kernel closes. To support robust evaluation of agentic optimizers, we additionally provide a sandboxed harness with GPU clock locking, L2 cache clearing, isolated subprocess execution, and static analysis based checks against common reward-hacking strategies. SOL-ExecBench reframes GPU kernel benchmarking from beating a mutable software baseline to closing the remaining gap to hardware Speed-of-Light.

AI.tldr: SOL-ExecBench is a hardware-grounded GPU-kernel benchmark for NVIDIA Blackwell, featuring 235 CUDA optimization problems across multiple AI domains, introducing SOL Score and SOLAR-based Speed-of-Light bounds, plus a sandboxed evaluation harness to assess progress toward hardware efficiency and deter reward hacking.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks emphasize software baselines and speedups, which can misalign optimization with true hardware efficiency. A hardware-grounded metric (SOL-bound) and robust evaluation harness are needed to guide agentic optimizers toward hardware-efficient kernel execution.
AI.method: Assembles 235 CUDA kernel optimization tasks drawn from 124 models spanning language, diffusion, vision, audio, video, and hybrids; covers forward and backward workloads in BF16, FP8, and NVFP4 for NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs; computes hardware SOL bounds via SOLAR, and defines SOL Score as the remaining gap closed toward these bounds; provides a sandboxed harness with GPU clock locking, L2 cache clearing, isolated subprocesses, and static-analysis checks to deter reward hacking.
AI.result: Introduction of SOL-ExecBench, the SOL Score metric, and the SOLAR-based SOL-bounds framework, along with a sandboxed evaluation environment. The abstract presents the benchmarking framework but does not report empirical results.
AI.conclusion: Reframes GPU kernel benchmarking from surpassing a mutable software baseline to closing the gap to hardware Speed-of-Light, enabling more robust evaluation of agentic optimizers and hardware-efficient kernel development.

title: Measuring 3D Spatial Geometric Consistency in Dynamic Generated Videos
summary: Recent generative models can produce high-fidelity videos, yet they often exhibit 3D spatial geometric inconsistencies. Existing evaluation methods fail to accurately characterize these inconsistencies: fidelity-centric metrics like FVD are insensitive to geometric distortions, while consistency-focused benchmarks often penalize valid foreground dynamics. To address this gap, we introduce SGC, a metric for evaluating 3D \textbf{S}patial \textbf{G}eometric \textbf{C}onsistency in dynamically generated videos. We quantify geometric consistency by measuring the divergence among multiple camera poses estimated from distinct local regions. Our approach first separates static from dynamic regions, then partitions the static background into spatially coherent sub-regions. We predict depth for each pixel, estimate a local camera pose for each subregion, and compute the divergence among these poses to quantify geometric consistency. Experiments on real and generative videos demonstrate that SGC robustly quantifies geometric inconsistencies, effectively identifying critical failures missed by existing metrics.

AI.tldr: A new metric (SGC) quantifies 3D geometric consistency in dynamic videos by comparing estimated camera poses across localized static background subregions; depth-informed local poses yield a divergence score that detects geometric failures ignored by fidelity metrics.
AI.motivation: Fidelity-only metrics (e.g., FVD) miss 3D geometric distortions, while consistency benchmarks often penalize valid foreground motion. A geometry-focused evaluation is needed for generative videos.
AI.method: 1) Segment video into static and dynamic regions. 2) Partition the static background into spatially coherent sub-regions. 3) Predict depth per pixel. 4) Estimate a local camera pose for each sub-region. 5) Compute divergence among poses to quantify geometric consistency.
AI.result: Experiments on real and generative videos show SGC robustly quantifies geometric inconsistencies and identifies critical failures that existing metrics miss.
AI.conclusion: SGC fills a gap in evaluating 3D geometric consistency in dynamically generated videos and complements fidelity metrics, providing a tool to diagnose and benchmark geometric failures in generative models.

title: MIDST Challenge at SaTML 2025: Membership Inference over Diffusion-models-based Synthetic Tabular data
summary: Synthetic data is often perceived as a silver-bullet solution to data anonymization and privacy-preserving data publishing. Drawn from generative models like diffusion models, synthetic data is expected to preserve the statistical properties of the original dataset while remaining resilient to privacy attacks. Recent developments of diffusion models have been effective on a wide range of data types, but their privacy resilience, particularly for tabular formats, remains largely unexplored. MIDST challenge sought a quantitative evaluation of the privacy gain of synthetic tabular data generated by diffusion models, with a specific focus on its resistance to membership inference attacks (MIAs). Given the heterogeneity and complexity of tabular data, multiple target models were explored for MIAs, including diffusion models for single tables of mixed data types and multi-relational tables with interconnected constraints. MIDST inspired the development of novel black-box and white-box MIAs tailored to these target diffusion models as a key outcome, enabling a comprehensive evaluation of their privacy efficacy. The MIDST GitHub repository is available at https://github.com/VectorInstitute/MIDST

AI.tldr: MIDST evaluates privacy of diffusion-model-based synthetic tabular data against membership inference attacks (MIAs) and develops novel black-box and white-box MIAs tailored to these models, enabling comprehensive privacy assessment; code is available.
AI.motivation: Address privacy risks in releasing synthetic tabular data generated by diffusion models and assess their resistance to MIAs, filling a gap in tabular data privacy evaluation.
AI.method: Assess multiple MIAs across diffusion-based targets (single-table mixed-type data and multi-relational tables with constraints); design and implement novel black-box and white-box MIAs tailored to these diffusion models; provide a comparative evaluation framework.
AI.result: Introduces new MIA techniques tailored to diffusion-based tabular generators; enables a comprehensive privacy evaluation; provides a public repository (MIDST) for reproducibility; specific quantitative privacy gains depend on data/model.
AI.conclusion: The MIDST initiative advances systematic privacy evaluation for diffusion-generated tabular data and offers methodological tools to quantify privacy gains, highlighting that tabular privacy with diffusion models remains dataset- and model-dependent and warrants further study.

title: SwiftTailor: Efficient 3D Garment Generation with Geometry Image Representation
summary: Realistic and efficient 3D garment generation remains a longstanding challenge in computer vision and digital fashion. Existing methods typically rely on large vision- language models to produce serialized representations of 2D sewing patterns, which are then transformed into simulation-ready 3D meshes using garment modeling framework such as GarmentCode. Although these approaches yield high-quality results, they often suffer from slow inference times, ranging from 30 seconds to a minute. In this work, we introduce SwiftTailor, a novel two-stage framework that unifies sewing-pattern reasoning and geometry-based mesh synthesis through a compact geometry image representation. SwiftTailor comprises two lightweight modules: PatternMaker, an efficient vision-language model that predicts sewing patterns from diverse input modalities, and GarmentSewer, an efficient dense prediction transformer that converts these patterns into a novel Garment Geometry Image, encoding the 3D surface of all garment panels in a unified UV space. The final 3D mesh is reconstructed through an efficient inverse mapping process that incorporates remeshing and dynamic stitching algorithms to directly assemble the garment, thereby amortizing the cost of physical simulation. Extensive experiments on the Multimodal GarmentCodeData demonstrate that SwiftTailor achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and visual fidelity while significantly reducing inference time. This work offers a scalable, interpretable, and high-performance solution for next-generation 3D garment generation.

AI.tldr: SwiftTailor introduces a two-stage, geometry-image-based 3D garment generation framework that pairs PatternMaker (vision-language sewing-pattern prediction) with GarmentSewer (dense-prediction transformer) to produce a Garment Geometry Image and an efficient inverse-mapping-based mesh, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy with much faster inference.
AI.motivation: To address the slow, LM-dependent 3D garment generation pipelines that serialize 2D sewing patterns for mesh synthesis, aiming for faster, end-to-end, interpretable and scalable generation.
AI.method: Two lightweight modules: PatternMaker predicts sewing patterns from multi-modal inputs; GarmentSewer converts patterns into a Garment Geometry Image representing the 3D garment surface in a unified UV space; an efficient inverse mapping with remeshing and dynamic stitching reconstructs the 3D mesh, amortizing physics-based simulation costs.
AI.result: On Multimodal GarmentCodeData, SwiftTailor achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and visual fidelity while significantly reducing inference time compared to prior 30s–1min pipelines.
AI.conclusion: SwiftTailor offers a scalable, interpretable, and high-performance solution for next-generation 3D garment generation, unifying pattern reasoning and geometry-based mesh synthesis.

title: Improving RCT-Based Treatment Effect Estimation Under Covariate Mismatch via Calibrated Alignment
summary: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) are the gold standard for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects, yet they are often underpowered for detecting effect heterogeneity. Large observational studies (OS) can supplement RCTs for conditional average treatment effect (CATE) estimation, but a key barrier is covariate mismatch: the two sources measure different, only partially overlapping, covariates. We propose CALM (Calibrated ALignment under covariate Mismatch), which bypasses imputation by learning embeddings that map each source's features into a common representation space. OS outcome models are transferred to the RCT embedding space and calibrated using trial data, preserving causal identification from randomization. Finite-sample risk bounds decompose into alignment error, outcome-model complexity, and calibration complexity terms, identifying when embedding alignment outperforms imputation. Under the calibration-based linear variant, the framework provides protection against negative transfer; the neural variant can be vulnerable under severe distributional shift. Under sparse linear models, the embedding approach strictly generalizes imputation. Simulations across 51 settings confirm that (i) calibration-based methods are equivalent for linear CATEs, and (ii) the neural embedding variant wins all 22 nonlinear-regime settings with large margins.

AI.tldr: CALM uses representation learning to align RCT and observational study covariates into a common space, transferring OS outcome models to the RCT embedding and calibrating with trial data to estimate CATE under covariate mismatch. Linear-calibration variants match imputation performance; neural embeddings excel in nonlinear settings; sparse linear cases show embedding generalizes imputation; extensive simulations (51 settings) support these claims.
AI.motivation: RCTs are underpowered for detecting treatment-effect heterogeneity; OS data can help but covariate mismatch between sources hinders reliable transfer. A method that aligns covariates without imputation while preserving randomization-based causal identification is needed.
AI.method: Learn embeddings that map each source's features into a shared representation; transfer OS outcome models into the RCT embedding space and calibrate them with trial data to preserve causal identification; derive finite-sample risk bounds decomposed into alignment, outcome-model, and calibration terms; analyze both calibration-based linear and neural variants; show that under sparse linear models the embedding approach generalizes imputation; evaluate via simulations (51 settings).
AI.result: Calibration-based linear variant yields equivalent CATE estimates to imputation in linear settings; the neural embedding variant outperforms in nonlinear regimes with large margins; under substantial distributional shift the neural variant can be vulnerable; in sparse linear regimes the embedding method strictly generalizes imputation; simulations across 51 setups show strong performance, especially for nonlinear CATEs.
AI.conclusion: Embedding-based alignment under covariate mismatch is a viable strategy to improve CATE estimation by leveraging OS data without imputing missing covariates. Calibration helps mitigate negative transfer, while linear and neural variants offer complementary strengths (linear aligns with imputation; neural excels with nonlinearity). The framework provides guidance on when to deploy each variant and highlights the importance of distributional shift considerations.

title: Em-Garde: A Propose-Match Framework for Proactive Streaming Video Understanding
summary: Recent advances in Streaming Video Understanding has enabled a new interaction paradigm where models respond proactively to user queries. Current proactive VideoLLMs rely on per-frame triggering decision making, which suffers from an efficiency-accuracy dilemma. We propose Em-Garde, a novel framework that decouples semantic understanding from streaming perception. At query time, the Instruction-Guided Proposal Parser transforms user queries into structured, perceptually grounded visual proposals; during streaming, a Lightweight Proposal Matching Module performs efficient embedding-based matching to trigger responses. Experiments on StreamingBench and OVO-Bench demonstrate consistent improvements over prior models in proactive response accuracy and efficiency, validating an effective solution for proactive video understanding under strict computational constraints.

AI.tldr: Em-Garde decouples semantic understanding from streaming perception to improve proactive video understanding under resource constraints, by turning queries into perceptual proposals and using lightweight embedding-based matching to trigger responses.
AI.motivation: Current proactive VideoLLMs rely on per-frame triggering, causing a persistent efficiency-accuracy trade-off. A framework that separates perception from reasoning can improve efficiency while maintaining accuracy.
AI.method: At query time, an Instruction-Guided Proposal Parser converts user queries into structured, perceptually grounded visual proposals. During streaming, a Lightweight Proposal Matching Module performs embedding-based matching to efficiently trigger responses.
AI.result: On StreamingBench and OVO-Bench, Em-Garde achieves consistent improvements in proactive response accuracy and efficiency compared to prior models operating under strict computational constraints.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling semantic understanding from streaming perception enables efficient, accurate proactive video understanding in resource-limited settings; the framework demonstrates effective trade-offs and practical viability.

title: Robustness, Cost, and Attack-Surface Concentration in Phishing Detection
summary:
Phishing detectors built on engineered website features attain near-perfect accuracy under i.i.d.\ evaluation, yet deployment security depends on robustness to post-deployment feature manipulation. We study this gap through a cost-aware evasion framework that models discrete, monotone feature edits under explicit attacker budgets. Three diagnostics are introduced: minimal evasion cost (MEC), the evasion survival rate $S(B)$, and the robustness concentration index (RCI).
  On the UCI Phishing Websites benchmark (11\,055 instances, 30 ternary features), Logistic Regression, Random Forests, Gradient Boosted Trees, and XGBoost all achieve $\mathrm{AUC}\ge 0.979$ under static evaluation. Under budgeted sanitization-style evasion, robustness converges across architectures: the median MEC equals 2 with full features, and over 80\% of successful minimal-cost evasions concentrate on three low-cost surface features. Feature restriction improves robustness only when it removes all dominant low-cost transitions. Under strict cost schedules, infrastructure-leaning feature sets exhibit 17-19\% infeasible mass for ensemble models, while the median MEC among evadable instances remains unchanged. We formalize this convergence: if a positive fraction of correctly detected phishing instances admit evasion through a single feature transition of minimal cost $c_{\min}$, no classifier can raise the corresponding MEC quantile above $c_{\min}$ without modifying the feature representation or cost model. Adversarial robustness in phishing detection is governed by feature economics rather than model complexity.

AI.tldr: Cost-aware adversarial evaluation shows phishing detectors' robustness is driven by feature costs; with budgeted feature edits, several detectors converge to similar minimal evasion costs; most evasion targets a few cheap features; feature design matters more than model complexity.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between high i.i.d. detection performance and deployment security by modeling discrete, monotone feature edits under attacker budgets; quantify robustness with new diagnostics.
AI.method: Introduce MEC (minimal evasion cost), evasion survival rate S(B), and robustness concentration index (RCI); evaluate on UCI Phishing Websites with 11,055 instances, 30 ternary features; compare LR, RF, GBDT, XGBoost; analyze budgeted sanitization evasion and feature restriction; derive theoretical bound on MEC quantiles given c_min and feature representation.
AI.result: Under static evaluation AUC ≥ 0.979 for all models; under budgeted evasion, median MEC ≈ 2 with full features; >80% of successful evasions involve three low-cost features; feature restriction helps robustness only if all low-cost transitions are removed; with strict costs, 17-19% of ensemble mass is infeasible; MEC among evadable instances unchanged; convergence formalized: if any fraction can evade via a single transition of cost c_min, MEC quantile cannot exceed c_min without changing representation or cost model.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial robustness in phishing detection is governed by feature economics rather than model complexity; robustness can degrade even with strong models due to cheap, exploitable features; to improve robustness, change feature set or cost model rather than rely on more complex classifiers.

title: SignAgent: Agentic LLMs for Linguistically-Grounded Sign Language Annotation and Dataset Curation
summary: This paper introduces SignAgent, a novel agentic framework that utilises Large Language Models (LLMs) for scalable, linguistically-grounded Sign Language (SL) annotation and dataset curation. Traditional computational methods for SLs often operate at the gloss level, overlooking crucial linguistic nuances, while manual linguistic annotation remains a significant bottleneck, proving too slow and expensive for the creation of large-scale, phonologically-aware datasets. SignAgent addresses these challenges through SignAgent Orchestrator, a reasoning LLM that coordinates a suite of linguistic tools, and SignGraph, a knowledge-grounded LLM that provides lexical and linguistic grounding. We evaluate our framework on two downstream annotation tasks. First, on Pseudo-gloss Annotation, where the agent performs constrained assignment, using multi-modal evidence to extract and order suitable gloss labels for signed sequences. Second, on ID Glossing, where the agent detects and refines visual clusters by reasoning over both visual similarity and phonological overlap to correctly identify and group lexical sign variants. Our results demonstrate that our agentic approach achieves strong performance for large-scale, linguistically-aware data annotation and curation.

AI.tldr: SignAgent is an LLM-driven agentic framework for scalable, linguistically-grounded sign language annotation and dataset curation, using an orchestrator for reasoning and a grounding graph for lexical/linguistic grounding; it achieves strong results on pseudo-gloss annotation and ID glossing.
AI.motivation: Manual linguistic annotation is slow and expensive, and existing SL methods often operate at the gloss level, missing phonological nuances; there is a need for scalable, phonologically-aware SL datasets.
AI.method: SignAgent Orchestrator (a reasoning LLM) coordinates a suite of linguistic tools, while SignGraph (a knowledge-grounded LLM) provides lexical/linguistic grounding. The framework is evaluated on two downstream tasks: (1) pseudo-gloss annotation via constrained assignment using multi-modal evidence to extract and order gloss labels for signed sequences, and (2) ID glossing by detecting and refining visual clusters through reasoning over visual similarity and phonological overlap to group lexical sign variants.
AI.result: The agentic framework demonstrates strong performance for large-scale, linguistically-aware data annotation and curation, indicating effective integration of reasoning and grounding components for sign language data labeling.
AI.conclusion: SignAgent provides a viable, scalable approach to linguistically-informed SL data annotation and curation, reducing manual annotation bottlenecks and enabling larger, phonologically-aware SL datasets; the architecture supports extensibility to additional SL annotation tasks.

title: Online Learning and Equilibrium Computation with Ranking Feedback
summary: Online learning in arbitrary, and possibly adversarial, environments has been extensively studied in sequential decision-making, and it is closely connected to equilibrium computation in game theory. Most existing online learning algorithms rely on \emph{numeric} utility feedback from the environment, which may be unavailable in human-in-the-loop applications and/or may be restricted by privacy concerns. In this paper, we study an online learning model in which the learner only observes a \emph{ranking} over a set of proposed actions at each timestep. We consider two ranking mechanisms: rankings induced by the \emph{instantaneous} utility at the current timestep, and rankings induced by the \emph{time-average} utility up to the current timestep, under both \emph{full-information} and \emph{bandit} feedback settings. Using the standard external-regret metric, we show that sublinear regret is impossible with instantaneous-utility ranking feedback in general. Moreover, when the ranking model is relatively deterministic, \emph{i.e.}, under the Plackett-Luce model with a temperature that is sufficiently small, sublinear regret is also impossible with time-average utility ranking feedback. We then develop new algorithms that achieve sublinear regret under the additional assumption that the utility sequence has sublinear total variation. Notably, for full-information time-average utility ranking feedback, this additional assumption can be removed. As a consequence, when all players in a normal-form game follow our algorithms, repeated play yields an approximate coarse correlated equilibrium. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithms in an online large-language-model routing task.

AI.tldr: This work analyzes online learning with ranking feedback (instantaneous vs. time-average rankings) under full-information and bandit settings. It proves sublinear regret is impossible in general for instantaneous rankings, and also for deterministic time-average rankings under a Plackett-Luce model with low temperature. It then designs algorithms achieving sublinear regret under sublinear total variation of the utility sequence, with full-information time-average feedback allowing removal of this assumption. Consequences include convergence to an approximate coarse correlated equilibrium in repeated normal-form games and practical demonstration on an online LLM routing task.
AI.motivation: To address online learning when only ranking feedback is available due to privacy or feedback constraints, bridging online learning with equilibrium computation in games.
AI.method: (i) Formalize ranking feedback mechanisms (instantaneous vs. time-average utility rankings) under full-information and bandit regimes. (ii) Prove impossibility results for sublinear regret in general. (iii) Propose algorithms that achieve sublinear regret assuming sublinear total variation (TV) of the utility sequence; show TV-free results for full-information time-average ranking. (iv) Extend implications to repeated games yielding approximate CCE. (v) Validate with an online LLM routing experiment.
AI.result: Impossibility of sublinear regret in general for instantaneous rankings and for deterministic time-average rankings under Plackett-Luce with low temperature. Sublinear regret achievable under sublinear total variation; TV assumption unnecessary in full-information time-average case. Repeated play yields approximate coarse correlated equilibrium; demonstrated on an online LLM routing task.
AI.conclusion: Ranking-based online learning can achieve sublinear regret and equilibrium-like outcomes under mild smoothness assumptions, expanding applicability to privacy-preserving settings and human-in-the-loop systems; results combine regret bounds, algorithm design, game-theoretic implications, and empirical validation.

title: SAVeS: Steering Safety Judgments in Vision-Language Models via Semantic Cues
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world and embodied settings where safety decisions depend on visual context. However, it remains unclear which visual evidence drives these judgments. We study whether multimodal safety behavior in VLMs can be steered by simple semantic cues. We introduce a semantic steering framework that applies controlled textual, visual, and cognitive interventions without changing the underlying scene content. To evaluate these effects, we propose SAVeS, a benchmark for situational safety under semantic cues, together with an evaluation protocol that separates behavioral refusal, grounded safety reasoning, and false refusals. Experiments across multiple VLMs and an additional state-of-the-art benchmark show that safety decisions are highly sensitive to semantic cues, indicating reliance on learned visual-linguistic associations rather than grounded visual understanding. We further demonstrate that automated steering pipelines can exploit these mechanisms, highlighting a potential vulnerability in multimodal safety systems.

AI.tldr: Semantic cues can steer safety decisions in vision-language models (VLMs) and expose a vulnerability to semantic manipulation.
AI.motivation: To understand what visual evidence governs multimodal safety judgments and whether those judgments can be controlled by simple semantic cues, which has implications for real-world safety in VLM deployments.
AI.method: Introduce a semantic steering framework with controlled textual, visual, and cognitive interventions that do not alter scene content, and develop SAVeS (Situational Safety under Semantic cues) with an evaluation protocol that separates behavioral refusal, grounded safety reasoning, and false refusals.
AI.result: Safety decisions in VLMs are highly sensitive to semantic cues, indicating reliance on learned visual–linguistic associations rather than grounded visual understanding; automated steering pipelines can exploit these mechanisms, revealing a vulnerability in multimodal safety systems.
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a robustness gap in multimodal safety systems to semantic manipulation and underscores the need for approaches that ground safety judgments in actual visual understanding and resist semantically driven steering.

title: TAU-R1: Visual Language Model for Traffic Anomaly Understanding
summary: Traffic Anomaly Understanding (TAU) is important for traffic safety in Intelligent Transportation Systems. Recent vision-language models (VLMs) have shown strong capabilities in video understanding. However, progress on TAU remains limited due to the lack of benchmarks and task-specific methodologies. To address this limitation, we introduce Roundabout-TAU, a dataset constructed from real-world roundabout videos collected in collaboration with the City of Carmel, Indiana. The dataset contains 342 clips and is annotated with more than 2,000 question-answer pairs covering multiple aspects of traffic anomaly understanding. Building on this benchmark, we propose TAU-R1, a two-layer vision-language framework for TAU. The first layer is a lightweight anomaly classifier that performs coarse anomaly categorisation, while the second layer is a larger anomaly reasoner that generates detailed event summaries. To improve task-specific reasoning, we introduce a two-stage training strategy consisting of decomposed-QA-enhanced supervised fine-tuning followed by TAU-GRPO, a GRPO-based post-training method with TAU-specific reward functions. Experimental results show that TAU-R1 achieves strong performance on both anomaly classification and reasoning tasks while maintaining deployment efficiency. The dataset and code are available at: https://github.com/siri-rouser/TAU-R1

AI.tldr: TAU-R1 introduces Roundabout-TAU, a real-world roundabout video dataset for traffic anomaly understanding, and a two-layer vision-language framework with a two-stage training regime (decomposed-QA fine-tuning and TAU-GRPO) to classify and reason about traffic anomalies with efficiency.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in traffic anomaly understanding (TAU) research by providing a dedicated benchmark and task-specific methodology, using real-world roundabout footage to improve safety and interpretability in intelligent transportation systems.
AI.method: Two-layer architecture: Layer 1 - lightweight anomaly classifier for coarse anomaly categorisation; Layer 2 - larger anomaly reasoner for detailed event summaries. Two-stage training: decomposed-QA-enhanced supervised fine-tuning; TAU-GRPO post-training with TAU-specific reward functions. Dataset: 342 clips with >2,000 QA pairs from real-world roundabouts; code and data released at the project site.
AI.result: TAU-R1 achieves strong performance on both anomaly classification and reasoning tasks, while maintaining deployment efficiency (no numerical metrics provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: The work contributes a dedicated TAU benchmark and a practical TAU-R1 framework, paving the way for TAU-focused research using vision-language models and specialized training strategies.

title: CustomTex: High-fidelity Indoor Scene Texturing via Multi-Reference Customization
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026. This version integrates the main paper and supplementary material
summary: The creation of high-fidelity, customizable 3D indoor scene textures remains a significant challenge. While text-driven methods offer flexibility, they lack the precision for fine-grained, instance-level control, and often produce textures with insufficient quality, artifacts, and baked-in shading. To overcome these limitations, we introduce CustomTex, a novel framework for instance-level, high-fidelity scene texturing driven by reference images. CustomTex takes an untextured 3D scene and a set of reference images specifying the desired appearance for each object instance, and generates a unified, high-resolution texture map. The core of our method is a dual-distillation approach that separates semantic control from pixel-level enhancement. We employ semantic-level distillation, equipped with an instance cross-attention, to ensure semantic plausibility and ``reference-instance'' alignment, and pixel-level distillation to enforce high visual fidelity. Both are unified within a Variational Score Distillation (VSD) optimization framework. Experiments demonstrate that CustomTex achieves precise instance-level consistency with reference images and produces textures with superior sharpness, reduced artifacts, and minimal baked-in shading compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our work establishes a more direct and user-friendly path to high-quality, customizable 3D scene appearance editing.

AI.tldr: A new framework, CustomTex, enables instance-level, high-fidelity 3D scene texturing guided by reference images. It uses dual distillation (semantic-level with instance cross-attention and pixel-level) within a Variational Score Distillation (VSD) framework to produce a unified, high-res texture map with accurate per-instance appearance and reduced artifacts/shading compared to prior methods.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of text-driven scene texturing—namely lack of fine-grained, per-instance control, texture quality issues, artifacts, and baked-in shading—by enabling reference-guided, instance-consistent high-fidelity textures for untextured 3D scenes.
AI.method: Input: untextured 3D scene + reference images for each object instance. Process: generate a unified, high-resolution texture map. Core technique is dual-distillation within a VSD framework: (1) semantic-level distillation with instance cross-attention for semantic plausibility and reference-instance alignment; (2) pixel-level distillation for high visual fidelity. The two distillations are integrated into a single optimization objective (VSD) to ensure consistency across semantics and pixels.
AI.result: Empirical results show precise instance-level consistency with references, textures with superior sharpness, fewer artifacts, and minimal baked-in shading relative to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: CustomTex offers a more direct, user-friendly pathway to high-quality, customizable 3D scene appearance editing by combining reference-guided control with high-fidelity texture synthesis via a unified VSD-based dual-distillation framework.

title: Revisiting Autoregressive Models for Generative Image Classification
summary: Class-conditional generative models have emerged as accurate and robust classifiers, with diffusion models demonstrating clear advantages over other visual generative paradigms, including autoregressive (AR) models. In this work, we revisit visual AR-based generative classifiers and identify an important limitation of prior approaches: their reliance on a fixed token order, which imposes a restrictive inductive bias for image understanding. We observe that single-order predictions rely more on partial discriminative cues, while averaging over multiple token orders provides a more comprehensive signal. Based on this insight, we leverage recent any-order AR models to estimate order-marginalized predictions, unlocking the high classification potential of AR models. Our approach consistently outperforms diffusion-based classifiers across diverse image classification benchmarks, while being up to 25x more efficient. Compared to state-of-the-art self-supervised discriminative models, our method delivers competitive classification performance - a notable achievement for generative classifiers.

AI.tldr: Order-marginalized any-order autoregressive visual classifiers outperform diffusion-based methods and rival self-supervised discriminative models, with up to 25x efficiency.
AI.motivation: Overcome the fixed-token-order inductive bias inherent in autoregressive visual classifiers by aggregating predictions across multiple token orders, aiming to unlock the full potential and improve robustness and efficiency.
AI.method: Utilize any-order autoregressive models to estimate order-marginalized predictions by averaging across multiple token orders; compare against diffusion-based classifiers on diverse image classification benchmarks; report efficiency gains.
AI.result: Consistently outperforms diffusion-based classifiers across diverse benchmarks; achieves up to 25x efficiency; competitive with state-of-the-art self-supervised discriminative models.
AI.conclusion: Order-marginalization in any-order AR models enables competitive performance for generative classifiers, offering superior efficiency and strong accuracy relative to diffusion models and approaching discriminative methods.

title: ADAPT: Attention Driven Adaptive Prompt Scheduling and InTerpolating Orthogonal Complements for Rare Concepts Generation
comment: Accepted in CVPR 2026 (findings). 10 pages, 4 figures; supplementary material included (8 pages, 10 figures)
summary: Generating rare compositional concepts in text-to-image synthesis remains a challenge for diffusion models, particularly for attributes that are uncommon in the training data. While recent approaches, such as R2F, address this challenge by utilizing LLM for prompt scheduling, they suffer from inherent variance due to the randomness of language models and suboptimal guidance from iterative text embedding switching. To address these problems, we propose the ADAPT framework, a training-free framework that deterministically plans and semantically aligns prompt schedules, providing consistent guidance to enhance the composition of rare concepts. By leveraging attention scores and orthogonal components, ADAPT significantly enhances compositional generation of rare concepts in the RareBench benchmark without additional training or fine-tuning. Through comprehensive experiments, we demonstrate that ADAPT achieves superior performance in RareBench and accurately reflects the semantic information of rare attributes, providing deterministic and precise control over the generation of rare compositions without compromising visual integrity.

AI.tldr: ADAPT is a training-free method that deterministically plans prompt schedules to improve rare compositional concepts in diffusion-based text-to-image generation, using attention-based planning and orthogonal components to guide generation without additional training, outperforming R2F on RareBench.
AI.motivation: Rare attributes are scarce in training data, causing poor compositional generation. R2F introduces variance due to LLMs and iterative prompt switching; a deterministic, training-free approach is desired for reliable rare concept composition.
AI.method: Deterministic prompt planning that leverages attention scores and orthogonal components to semantically align prompts. No training or fine-tuning required. The framework guides diffusion models through planned prompt schedules to emphasize rare concepts.
AI.result: ADAPT achieves superior performance on the RareBench benchmark, yielding more accurate semantic representation of rare attributes and deterministic, precise control over rare compositions while preserving visual quality, without extra training.
AI.conclusion: ADAPT offers a training-free, deterministic alternative for prompting diffusion models to compose rare concepts, providing stable guidance and improved compositional accuracy without compromising image fidelity.

title: Adaptive Auxiliary Prompt Blending for Target-Faithful Diffusion Generation
comment: Accepted in CVPR 2026 (main track). 10 pages, 6 figures; supplementary material included (14 pages, 11 figures)
summary: Diffusion-based text-to-image (T2I) models have made remarkable progress in generating photorealistic and semantically rich images. However, when the target concepts lie in low-density regions of the training distribution, these models often produce semantically misaligned or structurally inconsistent results. This limitation arises from the long-tailed nature of text-image datasets, where rare concepts or editing instructions are underrepresented. To address this, we introduce Adaptive Auxiliary Prompt Blending (AAPB) - a unified framework that stabilizes the diffusion process in low-density regions. AAPB leverages auxiliary anchor prompts to provide semantic support in rare concept generation and structural support in image editing, ensuring faithful guidance toward the target prompt. Unlike prior heuristic prompt alternation methods, AAPB derives a closed-form adaptive coefficient that optimally balances the influence between the auxiliary anchor and the target prompt at each diffusion step. Grounded in Tweedie's identity, our formulation provides a principled and training-free framework for adaptive prompt blending, ensuring stable and target-faithful generation. We demonstrate the effectiveness of adaptive interpolation over fixed interpolation through controlled experiments and empirically show consistent improvements on the RareBench and FlowEdit datasets, achieving superior semantic accuracy and structural fidelity compared to prior training-free baselines.

AI.tldr: AAPB is a training-free framework that adaptively blends an auxiliary anchor prompt with a target T2I prompt during diffusion using a closed-form coefficient derived from Tweedie's identity, to stabilize generation in low-density regions and improve semantic fidelity and structural edits on RareBench and FlowEdit.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image diffusion models struggle with rare concepts due to long-tailed training data, leading to semantic misalignment or structural inconsistencies in low-density regions. AAPB aims to provide semantic and structural guidance to improve fidelity for rare prompts and editing tasks.
AI.method: Introduce Adaptive Auxiliary Prompt Blending (AAPB) that uses auxiliary anchor prompts for semantic/structural support. Derive a closed-form adaptive interpolation coefficient at each diffusion step via Tweedie's identity, enabling principled, training-free prompt balancing between the target and auxiliary prompts.
AI.result: Controlled experiments show adaptive interpolation outperforms fixed interpolation. Empirical results on RareBench and FlowEdit indicate improved semantic accuracy and structural fidelity compared to prior training-free baselines.
AI.conclusion: AAPB offers a principled, training-free approach to adaptive prompt blending that stabilizes diffusion in low-density regions, enabling faithful generation for rare concepts and edits.

title: ARIADNE: A Perception-Reasoning Synergy Framework for Trustworthy Coronary Angiography Analysis
summary: Conventional pixel-wise loss functions fail to enforce topological constraints in coronary vessel segmentation, producing fragmented vascular trees despite high pixel-level accuracy. We present ARIADNE, a two-stage framework coupling preference-aligned perception with RL-based diagnostic reasoning for topologically coherent stenosis detection. The perception module employs DPO to fine-tune the Sa2VA vision-language foundation model using Betti number constraints as preference signals, aligning the policy toward geometrically complete vessel structures rather than pixel-wise overlap metrics. The reasoning module formulates stenosis localization as a Markov Decision Process with an explicit rejection mechanism that autonomously defers ambiguous anatomical candidates such as bifurcations and vessel crossings, shifting from coverage maximization to reliability optimization. On 1,400 clinical angiograms, ARIADNE achieves state-of-the-art centerline Dice of 0.838, reduces false positives by 41% compared to geometric baselines. External validation on multi-center benchmarks ARCADE and XCAD confirms generalization across acquisition protocols. This represents the first application of DPO for topological alignment in medical imaging, demonstrating that preference-based learning over structural constraints mitigates topological violations while maintaining diagnostic sensitivity in interventional cardiology workflows.

AI.tldr: Two-stage ARIADNE framework uses preference-aligned perception with DPO and RL-based reasoning to enforce topological vessel structures, yielding high centerline Dice and reduced false positives with external validation.
AI.motivation: Conventional pixel-wise losses neglect topology, leading to fragmented coronary vessels despite high pixel accuracy; there is a need for topologically coherent, reliable stenosis detection.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) perception uses DPO to fine-tune Sa2VA with Betti-number constraints as preferences to prioritize complete vessel topology; (2) reasoning models stenosis localization as a Markov decision process with an explicit rejection mechanism to defer ambiguous candidates (e.g., bifurcations, crossings), optimizing reliability over coverage.
AI.result: Achieves centerline Dice of 0.838 on 1,400 clinical angiograms and reduces false positives by 41% versus geometric baselines; external validation on ARCADE and XCAD shows generalization across acquisition protocols.
AI.conclusion: First application of diffusion policy optimization (DPO) for topological alignment in medical imaging; preference-based learning over structural constraints mitigates topological violations while preserving diagnostic sensitivity in interventional cardiology workflows.

title: Reconstruction Matters: Learning Geometry-Aligned BEV Representation through 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) perception serves as a cornerstone for autonomous driving, offering a unified spatial representation that fuses surrounding-view images to enable reasoning for various downstream tasks, such as semantic segmentation, 3D object detection, and motion prediction. However, most existing BEV perception frameworks adopt an end-to-end training paradigm, where image features are directly transformed into the BEV space and optimized solely through downstream task supervision. This formulation treats the entire perception process as a black box, often lacking explicit 3D geometric understanding and interpretability, leading to suboptimal performance. In this paper, we claim that an explicit 3D representation matters for accurate BEV perception, and we propose Splat2BEV, a Gaussian Splatting-assisted framework for BEV tasks. Splat2BEV aims to learn BEV feature representations that are both semantically rich and geometrically precise. We first pre-train a Gaussian generator that explicitly reconstructs 3D scenes from multi-view inputs, enabling the generation of geometry-aligned feature representations. These representations are then projected into the BEV space to serve as inputs for downstream tasks. Extensive experiments on nuScenes and argoverse dataset demonstrate that Splat2BEV achieves state-of-the-art performance and validate the effectiveness of incorporating explicit 3D reconstruction into BEV perception.

AI.tldr: Gaussian splatting-based BEV framework (Splat2BEV) that uses explicit 3D reconstruction to produce geometry-aligned BEV features, achieving state-of-the-art performance on nuScenes and Argoverse.
AI.motivation: End-to-end BEV methods treat perception as a black box and lack explicit 3D geometry and interpretability, which can limit accuracy.
AI.method: Pre-train a Gaussian generator to reconstruct 3D scenes from multi-view inputs, producing geometry-aligned features; project these features into BEV for downstream tasks.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on nuScenes and Argoverse; experimental validation that explicit 3D reconstruction benefits BEV perception.
AI.conclusion: Explicit 3D representation via Gaussian splatting enhances BEV perception by yielding semantically rich and geometrically precise features; the approach improves performance and interpretability.

title: Tinted Frames: Question Framing Blinds Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have been shown to be blind, often underutilizing their visual inputs even on tasks that require visual reasoning. In this work, we demonstrate that VLMs are selectively blind. They modulate the amount of attention applied to visual inputs based on linguistic framing even when alternative framings demand identical visual reasoning. Using visual attention as a probe, we quantify how framing alters both the amount and distribution of attention over the image. Constrained framings, such as multiple choice and yes/no, induce substantially lower attention to image context compared to open-ended, reduce focus on task-relevant regions, and shift attention towards uninformative tokens. We further demonstrate that this attention misallocation is the principal cause of degraded accuracy and cross-framing inconsistency. Building on this mechanistic insight, we introduce a lightweight prompt-tuning method using learnable tokens that encourages the robust, visually grounded attention patterns observed in open-ended settings, improving visual grounding and improving performance across framings.

AI.tldr: VLMs exhibit framing-dependent selective blindness: constrained framings reduce visual attention and degrade reasoning; a lightweight prompt-tuning with learnable tokens aligns attention with open-ended settings, improving grounding and cross-framing performance.
AI.motivation: To understand why Vision-Language Models underutilize visual input, how linguistic framing modulates visual attention, and whether attention misallocation explains performance drops; and to propose a mitigation to achieve robust visually grounded reasoning across framings.
AI.method: Analyze attention as a probe across framings (open-ended vs constrained such as multiple choice and yes/no). Quantify the amount and distribution of attention over image regions. Correlate attention patterns with accuracy and cross-framing consistency. Develop a lightweight prompt-tuning approach using learnable tokens to promote robust, visually grounded attention; evaluate across framings.
AI.result: Constrained framings induce substantially lower attention to image context, reduce focus on task-relevant regions, and shift attention toward uninformative tokens. This attention misallocation correlates with degraded accuracy and cross-framing inconsistency. The proposed prompt-tuning method improves visually grounded attention and enhances performance across framings.
AI.conclusion: Attention misallocation driven by linguistic framing explains degraded performance; a lightweight prompt-tuning with learnable tokens can align attention patterns across framings, yielding improved visual grounding and cross-framing robustness.

title: RPiAE: A Representation-Pivoted Autoencoder Enhancing Both Image Generation and Editing
summary: Diffusion models have become the dominant paradigm for image generation and editing, with latent diffusion models shifting denoising to a compact latent space for efficiency and scalability. Recent attempts to leverage pretrained visual representation models as tokenizer priors either align diffusion features to representation features or directly reuse representation encoders as frozen tokenizers. Although such approaches can improve generation metrics, they often suffer from limited reconstruction fidelity due to frozen encoders, which in turn degrades editing quality, as well as overly high-dimensional latents that make diffusion modeling difficult. To address these limitations, We propose Representation-Pivoted AutoEncoder, a representation-based tokenizer that improves both generation and editing. We introduce Representation-Pivot Regularization, a training strategy that enables a representation-initialized encoder to be fine-tuned for reconstruction while preserving the semantic structure of the pretrained representation space, followed by a variational bridge which compress latent space into a compact one for better diffusion modeling. We adopt an objective-decoupled stage-wise training strategy that sequentially optimizes generative tractability and reconstruction-fidelity objectives. Together, these components yield a tokenizer that preserves strong semantics, reconstructs faithfully, and produces latents with reduced diffusion modeling complexity. Experiments demonstrate that RPiAE outperforms other visual tokenizers on text-to-image generation and image editing, while delivering the best reconstruction fidelity among representation-based tokenizers.

AI.tldr: Represents a representation-based tokenizer for diffusion models, called Representation-Pivoted AutoEncoder (RPiAE), with a Representation-Pivot Regularization and a variational bridge to compress latent space, enabling better generation and editing with lower diffusion modeling complexity through stage-wise training.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches that reuse frozen pretrained representations as tokenizers yield limited reconstruction fidelity and high-dimensional latents, hurting editing quality and tractability. A representation-based tokenizer that preserves semantics, reconstructs faithfully, and yields compact latents is needed.
AI.method: 1) Representation-Pivot Regularization to fine-tune a representation-initialized encoder for reconstruction while preserving semantic structure of the pretrained space. 2) A variational bridge to compress latents into a compact latent space for diffusion modeling. 3) Stage-wise, objective-decoupled training that first optimizes generative tractability and then reconstruction fidelity.
AI.result: RPiAE outperforms existing visual tokenizers on text-to-image generation and image editing and achieves the best reconstruction fidelity among representation-based tokenizers, while reducing diffusion modeling complexity by using compact latents and a semantically stable tokenizer.
AI.conclusion: A representation-based tokenizer with representation-preserving fine-tuning and latent compression yields improved generation and editing, preserving semantics, faithful reconstruction, and more tractable diffusion modeling.

title: DreamPartGen: Semantically Grounded Part-Level 3D Generation via Collaborative Latent Denoising
summary: Understanding and generating 3D objects as compositions of meaningful parts is fundamental to human perception and reasoning. However, most text-to-3D methods overlook the semantic and functional structure of parts. While recent part-aware approaches introduce decomposition, they remain largely geometry-focused, lacking semantic grounding and failing to model how parts align with textual descriptions or their inter-part relations. We propose DreamPartGen, a framework for semantically grounded, part-aware text-to-3D generation. DreamPartGen introduces Duplex Part Latents (DPLs) that jointly model each part's geometry and appearance, and Relational Semantic Latents (RSLs) that capture inter-part dependencies derived from language. A synchronized co-denoising process enforces mutual geometric and semantic consistency, enabling coherent, interpretable, and text-aligned 3D synthesis. Across multiple benchmarks, DreamPartGen delivers state-of-the-art performance in geometric fidelity and text-shape alignment.

AI.tldr: DreamPartGen introduces semantically grounded, part-aware text-to-3D generation using Duplex Part Latents (geometry+appearance) and Relational Semantic Latents for inter-part dependencies, with synchronized co-denoising to ensure text-aligned, coherent 3D synthesis; achieves state-of-the-art across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address that most text-to-3D methods neglect semantic-part structure and language-grounded relations, resulting in geometry-centric and semantically misaligned outputs.
AI.method: Propose Duplex Part Latents (DPLs) to jointly model per-part geometry and appearance; Relational Semantic Latents (RSLs) to encode inter-part dependencies derived from language; implement a synchronized co-denoising process to enforce mutual geometric and semantic consistency during diffusion-based generation.
AI.result: Empirically surpasses baselines on geometric fidelity and text-shape alignment across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DreamPartGen provides a semantically grounded, interpretable, part-aware text-to-3D generation framework that coherently integrates language guidance with part-level geometry/appearance, enabling improved 3D synthesis aligned with textual descriptions.

title: LVOmniBench: Pioneering Long Audio-Video Understanding Evaluation for Omnimodal LLMs
summary: Recent advancements in omnimodal large language models (OmniLLMs) have significantly improved the comprehension of audio and video inputs. However, current evaluations primarily focus on short audio and video clips ranging from 10 seconds to 5 minutes, failing to reflect the demands of real-world applications, where videos typically run for tens of minutes. To address this critical gap, we introduce LVOmniBench, a new benchmark designed specifically for the cross-modal comprehension of long-form audio and video. This dataset comprises high-quality videos sourced from open platforms that feature rich audio-visual dynamics. Through rigorous manual selection and annotation, LVOmniBench comprises 275 videos, ranging in duration from 10 to 90 minutes, and 1,014 question-answer (QA) pairs. LVOmniBench aims to rigorously evaluate the capabilities of OmniLLMs across domains, including long-term memory, temporal localization, fine-grained understanding, and multimodal perception. Our extensive evaluation reveals that current OmniLLMs encounter significant challenges when processing extended audio-visual inputs. Open-source models generally achieve accuracies below 35%, whereas the Gemini 3 Pro reaches a peak accuracy of approximately 65%. We anticipate that this dataset, along with our empirical findings, will stimulate further research and the development of advanced models capable of resolving complex cross-modal understanding problems within long-form audio-visual contexts.

AI.tldr: Introduces LVOmniBench, a long-form cross-modal benchmark for OmniLLMs with 275 videos (10–90 minutes) and 1,014 QA pairs, revealing current models struggle with long-form audio–visual comprehension.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between real-world long-form videos and existing short-clip benchmarks, by evaluating long-term memory, temporal localization, fine-grained understanding, and multimodal perception in OmniLLMs.
AI.method: Curated open-platform videos (high-quality, rich audio-visual dynamics); rigorous manual selection and annotation; 275 videos, 10–90 minutes each, 1,014 QA pairs; cross-modal evaluation across open-source models and Gemini 3 Pro.
AI.result: OmniLLMs show significant challenges on long-form inputs; open-source models <35% accuracy; Gemini 3 Pro about 65% peak accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Dataset and findings should spur development of advanced models capable of robust long-form cross-modal understanding, with emphasis on memory, localization, and multimodal fusion.

title: Rethinking Vector Field Learning for Generative Segmentation
summary: Taming diffusion models for generative segmentation has attracted increasing attention. While existing approaches primarily focus on architectural tweaks or training heuristics, there remains a limited understanding of the intrinsic mismatch between continuous flow matching objectives and discrete perception tasks. In this work, we revisit diffusion segmentation from the perspective of vector field learning. We identify two key limitations of the commonly used flow matching objective: gradient vanishing and trajectory traversing, which result in slow convergence and poor class separation. To tackle these issues, we propose a principled vector field reshaping strategy that augments the learned velocity field with a detached distance-aware correction term. This correction introduces both attractive and repulsive interactions, enhancing gradient magnitudes near centroids while preserving the original diffusion training framework. Furthermore, we design a computationally efficient, quasi-random category encoding scheme inspired by Kronecker sequences, which integrates seamlessly with an end-to-end pixel neural field framework for pixel-level semantic alignment. Extensive experiments consistently demonstrate significant improvements over vanilla flow matching approaches, substantially narrowing the performance gap between generative segmentation and strong discriminative specialists.

AI.tldr: Reframes diffusion segmentation as vector field learning by correcting flow-based velocity with a distance-aware term and employing a quasi-random category encoding to align pixel-level semantics, yielding faster convergence and better class separation.
AI.motivation: There is an intrinsic mismatch between continuous flow matching objectives used in diffusion models and discrete perception tasks. This leads to gradient vanishing, slow convergence, and weak class separation in diffusion-based segmentation.
AI.method: 1) Vector field reshaping: augment the learned velocity field with a detached distance-aware correction term that creates attractive/repulsive interactions, boosting gradient magnitudes near centroids while preserving the diffusion training framework. 2) Efficient category encoding: a quasi-random encoding scheme inspired by Kronecker sequences for end-to-end pixel neural field alignment with minimal computational overhead.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant improvements over vanilla flow matching, substantially closing the performance gap between generative segmentation and strong discriminative methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed vector-field-based diffusion segmentation provides a principled, efficient path to bridge generative diffusion with discriminative segmentation performance, combining velocity-field corrections with efficient category encoding.

title: DriveTok: 3D Driving Scene Tokenization for Unified Multi-View Reconstruction and Understanding
summary: With the growing adoption of vision-language-action models and world models in autonomous driving systems, scalable image tokenization becomes crucial as the interface for the visual modality. However, most existing tokenizers are designed for monocular and 2D scenes, leading to inefficiency and inter-view inconsistency when applied to high-resolution multi-view driving scenes. To address this, we propose DriveTok, an efficient 3D driving scene tokenizer for unified multi-view reconstruction and understanding. DriveTok first obtains semantically rich visual features from vision foundation models and then transforms them into the scene tokens with 3D deformable cross-attention. For decoding, we employ a multi-view transformer to reconstruct multi-view features from the scene tokens and use multiple heads to obtain RGB, depth, and semantic reconstructions. We also add a 3D head directly on the scene tokens for 3D semantic occupancy prediction for better spatial awareness. With the multiple training objectives, DriveTok learns unified scene tokens that integrate semantic, geometric, and textural information for efficient multi-view tokenization. Extensive experiments on the widely used nuScenes dataset demonstrate that the scene tokens from DriveTok perform well on image reconstruction, semantic segmentation, depth prediction, and 3D occupancy prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: DriveTok introduces a 3D driving scene tokenizer that unifies multi-view reconstruction and understanding via 3D deformable cross-attention and a multi-view transformer, enabling RGB, depth, semantic, and 3D occupancy outputs from scene tokens; demonstrated on nuScenes.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiency and inter-view inconsistency of monocular/2D tokenizers in high-resolution multi-view driving scenes; enable scalable, unified tokenization for vision-language-action models and world models in autonomous driving.
AI.method: Extract semantically rich features from vision foundation models, convert to scene tokens using 3D deformable cross-attention; decode via a multi-view transformer to reconstruct multi-view features and outputs (RGB, depth, semantic); add a 3D semantic occupancy head on the scene tokens; train with multiple objectives to learn unified tokens that fuse semantic, geometric, and textural cues.
AI.result: Experiments on nuScenes show that the DriveTok scene tokens perform well across tasks: image reconstruction, semantic segmentation, depth prediction, and 3D occupancy prediction.
AI.conclusion: DriveTok offers a unified, efficient tokenization framework for multi-view driving scenes, integrating semantic, geometric, and texture information in a token representation, with strong cross-task performance on standard benchmarks.

title: Spectrally-Guided Diffusion Noise Schedules
summary: Denoising diffusion models are widely used for high-quality image and video generation. Their performance depends on noise schedules, which define the distribution of noise levels applied during training and the sequence of noise levels traversed during sampling. Noise schedules are typically handcrafted and require manual tuning across different resolutions. In this work, we propose a principled way to design per-instance noise schedules for pixel diffusion, based on the image's spectral properties. By deriving theoretical bounds on the efficacy of minimum and maximum noise levels, we design ``tight'' noise schedules that eliminate redundant steps. During inference, we propose to conditionally sample such noise schedules. Experiments show that our noise schedules improve generative quality of single-stage pixel diffusion models, particularly in the low-step regime.

AI.tldr: Per-instance, spectrally-informed noise schedules for pixel diffusion; derive bounds on extreme noise levels and create tight schedules to remove redundant steps; enable conditional sampling during inference; improves quality in low-step regimes.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion noise schedules are handcrafted and need per-resolution tuning, leading to inefficiency and suboptimal results. Tailoring schedules to image content via spectral properties aims to boost quality and reduce steps.
AI.method: Theoretically bound the efficacy of minimum and maximum noise levels; design tight, per-instance noise schedules based on image spectral properties; enable conditional sampling of these schedules during inference.
AI.result: Improved generative quality of single-stage pixel diffusion models, especially when the number of sampling steps is low.
AI.conclusion: Spectrally-informed, per-instance noise schedules can enhance diffusion efficiency and quality by removing redundant steps; practical benefits are most pronounced in low-step sampling. Potential trade-offs include the need to compute spectral characteristics per image and possibly broader generalization considerations.

title: EffectErase: Joint Video Object Removal and Insertion for High-Quality Effect Erasing
summary: Video object removal aims to eliminate dynamic target objects and their visual effects, such as deformation, shadows, and reflections, while restoring seamless backgrounds. Recent diffusion-based video inpainting and object removal methods can remove the objects but often struggle to erase these effects and to synthesize coherent backgrounds. Beyond method limitations, progress is further hampered by the lack of a comprehensive dataset that systematically captures common object effects across varied environments for training and evaluation. To address this, we introduce VOR (Video Object Removal), a large-scale dataset that provides diverse paired videos, each consisting of one video where the target object is present with its effects and a counterpart where the object and effects are absent, with corresponding object masks. VOR contains 60K high-quality video pairs from captured and synthetic sources, covers five effects types, and spans a wide range of object categories as well as complex, dynamic multi-object scenes. Building on VOR, we propose EffectErase, an effect-aware video object removal method that treats video object insertion as the inverse auxiliary task within a reciprocal learning scheme. The model includes task-aware region guidance that focuses learning on affected areas and enables flexible task switching. Then, an insertion-removal consistency objective that encourages complementary behaviors and shared localization of effect regions and structural cues. Trained on VOR, EffectErase achieves superior performance in extensive experiments, delivering high-quality video object effect erasing across diverse scenarios.

AI.tldr: Presents VOR, a large-scale paired video dataset for video object removal with five effect types, and EffectErase, an effect-aware removal model that uses reciprocal learning, task-guided region guidance, and insertion-removal consistency to achieve high-quality erasing across diverse scenarios.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based video inpainting often fails to erase object-induced effects (deformation, shadows, reflections) and reconstruct coherent backgrounds, and there is a lack of comprehensive datasets that capture common object effects across varied environments for training and evaluation.
AI.method: VOR provides 60K high-quality video pairs (captured and synthetic) with masks and five effect types. EffectErase treats video object insertion as the inverse auxiliary task in a reciprocal learning framework, with task-aware region guidance and an insertion-removal consistency objective, trained on VOR.
AI.result: EffectErase demonstrates superior performance in extensive experiments, delivering high-quality object-effect erasing across diverse scenarios.
