AI.conclusion: The work furnishes a dataset (VOR) and a method (EffectErase) addressing data and methodological gaps, enabling robust effect-aware video object removal and paving the way for improved training and evaluation.

title: Under One Sun: Multi-Object Generative Perception of Materials and Illumination
summary: We introduce Multi-Object Generative Perception (MultiGP), a generative inverse rendering method for stochastic sampling of all radiometric constituents -- reflectance, texture, and illumination -- underlying object appearance from a single image. Our key idea to solve this inherently ambiguous radiometric disentanglement is to leverage the fact that while their texture and reflectance may differ, objects in the same scene are all lit by the same illumination. MultiGP exploits this consensus to produce samples of reflectance, texture, and illumination from a single image of known shapes based on four key technical contributions: a cascaded end-to-end architecture that combines image-space and angular-space disentanglement; Coordinated Guidance for diffusion convergence to a single consistent illumination estimate; Axial Attention applied to facilitate ``cross-talk'' between objects of different reflectance; and a Texture Extraction ControlNet to preserve high-frequency texture details while ensuring decoupling from estimated lighting. Experimental results demonstrate that MultiGP effectively leverages the complementary spatial and frequency characteristics of multiple object appearances to recover individual texture and reflectance as well as the common illumination.

AI.tldr: MultiGP is a diffusion-based inverse rendering framework that jointly samples reflectance, texture, and illumination from a single image of known-shape objects by exploiting a shared illumination across the scene.
AI.motivation: Radiometric disentanglement from a single image is inherently ambiguous; enforcing a scene-wide illumination consensus provides a strong prior to enable stochastic sampling of radiometric constituents across objects.
AI.method: A cascaded end-to-end architecture combining image-space and angular-space disentanglement; Coordinated Guidance to diffusion for a single consistent illumination estimate; Axial Attention enabling cross-object interactions across different reflectances; Texture Extraction ControlNet to preserve high-frequency texture while decoupling from lighting.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate that leveraging the complementary spatial and frequency characteristics of multiple objects’ appearances enables recovery of individual textures and reflectances as well as the common illumination.
AI.conclusion: MultiGP enables stochastic sampling of reflectance, texture, and illumination for multiple known-shape objects in a single image by exploiting a common illumination, providing a principled inverse-rendering pipeline with several architectural innovations.

title: Bridging Semantic and Kinematic Conditions with Diffusion-based Discrete Motion Tokenizer
summary: Prior motion generation largely follows two paradigms: continuous diffusion models that excel at kinematic control, and discrete token-based generators that are effective for semantic conditioning. To combine their strengths, we propose a three-stage framework comprising condition feature extraction (Perception), discrete token generation (Planning), and diffusion-based motion synthesis (Control). Central to this framework is MoTok, a diffusion-based discrete motion tokenizer that decouples semantic abstraction from fine-grained reconstruction by delegating motion recovery to a diffusion decoder, enabling compact single-layer tokens while preserving motion fidelity. For kinematic conditions, coarse constraints guide token generation during planning, while fine-grained constraints are enforced during control through diffusion-based optimization. This design prevents kinematic details from disrupting semantic token planning. On HumanML3D, our method significantly improves controllability and fidelity over MaskControl while using only one-sixth of the tokens, reducing trajectory error from 0.72 cm to 0.08 cm and FID from 0.083 to 0.029. Unlike prior methods that degrade under stronger kinematic constraints, ours improves fidelity, reducing FID from 0.033 to 0.014.

AI.tldr: A three-stage MoTok-enabled framework that unifies discrete token planning with diffusion-based motion synthesis, achieving higher controllability and fidelity using only one-sixth of the tokens.
AI.motivation: Current motion generation methods either excel at kinematic control via continuous diffusion models or rely on discrete token-based generators for semantic conditioning. A unified approach is needed to leverage both strengths without semantic planning being disrupted by fine-grained kinematic details. The paper introduces a diffusion-based discrete motion tokenizer (MoTok) to decouple semantic abstraction from reconstruction.
AI.method: A three-stage framework: (1) Perception for condition feature extraction, (2) Planning for discrete token generation using MoTok, and (3) Control for diffusion-based motion synthesis. MoTok decouples semantic abstraction from fine-grained reconstruction by delegating motion recovery to a diffusion decoder, enabling compact single-layer tokens. Kinematic constraints guide planning at the token level, while fine-grained constraints are enforced during diffusion-based control via optimization.
AI.result: On HumanML3D, the method outperforms MaskControl in controllability and fidelity while using only one-sixth the tokens. Trajectory error decreases from 0.72 cm to 0.08 cm and FID improves from 0.083 to 0.029. Under stronger kinematic constraints, fidelity further improves (FID from 0.033 to 0.014).
AI.conclusion: The proposed MoTok-enabled three-stage framework effectively combines discrete token planning with diffusion-based synthesis, yielding improved controllability and fidelity with substantially fewer tokens. It demonstrates robustness to stronger kinematic constraints and sets a new benchmark for motion generation with unified semantic and kinematic conditioning.

title: SAMA: Factorized Semantic Anchoring and Motion Alignment for Instruction-Guided Video Editing
summary: Current instruction-guided video editing models struggle to simultaneously balance precise semantic modifications with faithful motion preservation. While existing approaches rely on injecting explicit external priors (e.g., VLM features or structural conditions) to mitigate these issues, this reliance severely bottlenecks model robustness and generalization. To overcome this limitation, we present SAMA (factorized Semantic Anchoring and Motion Alignment), a framework that factorizes video editing into semantic anchoring and motion modeling. First, we introduce Semantic Anchoring, which establishes a reliable visual anchor by jointly predicting semantic tokens and video latents at sparse anchor frames, enabling purely instruction-aware structural planning. Second, Motion Alignment pre-trains the same backbone on motion-centric video restoration pretext tasks (cube inpainting, speed perturbation, and tube shuffle), enabling the model to internalize temporal dynamics directly from raw videos. SAMA is optimized with a two-stage pipeline: a factorized pre-training stage that learns inherent semantic-motion representations without paired video-instruction editing data, followed by supervised fine-tuning on paired editing data. Remarkably, the factorized pre-training alone already yields strong zero-shot video editing ability, validating the proposed factorization. SAMA achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models and is competitive with leading commercial systems (e.g., Kling-Omni). Code, models, and datasets will be released.

AI.tldr: SAMA factorizes instruction-guided video editing into semantic anchoring and motion alignment, using sparse semantic anchors and motion-centric pretraining to achieve strong zero-shot and competitive performance via a two-stage training pipeline.
AI.motivation: Current instruction-guided video editing models rely on explicit external priors (e.g., VLM features, structural cues) that hurt robustness and generalization; there is a need to balance precise semantic edits with faithful motion preservation.
AI.method: Introduce Semantic Anchoring to jointly predict semantic tokens and video latents at sparse anchor frames for instruction-driven planning; Motion Alignment via pretraining on motion-centric restoration tasks (cube inpainting, speed perturbation, tube shuffle) to learn temporal dynamics; a two-stage pipeline: factorized pre-training without paired editing data, then supervised fine-tuning on paired editing data.
AI.result: Factorized pre-training yields strong zero-shot video editing capability; SAMA sets state-of-the-art among open-source models and is competitive with top commercial systems (e.g., Kling-Omni); code, models, and datasets will be released.
AI.conclusion: Factorizing editing into semantic anchoring and motion alignment yields robust, generalizable, instruction-guided video editing with strong zero-shot performance and reduced reliance on external priors, enabled by a two-stage training regime.

title: MonoArt: Progressive Structural Reasoning for Monocular Articulated 3D Reconstruction
summary: Reconstructing articulated 3D objects from a single image requires jointly inferring object geometry, part structure, and motion parameters from limited visual evidence. A key difficulty lies in the entanglement between motion cues and object structure, which makes direct articulation regression unstable. Existing methods address this challenge through multi-view supervision, retrieval-based assembly, or auxiliary video generation, often sacrificing scalability or efficiency. We present MonoArt, a unified framework grounded in progressive structural reasoning. Rather than predicting articulation directly from image features, MonoArt progressively transforms visual observations into canonical geometry, structured part representations, and motion-aware embeddings within a single architecture. This structured reasoning process enables stable and interpretable articulation inference without external motion templates or multi-stage pipelines. Extensive experiments on PartNet-Mobility demonstrate that OM achieves state-of-the-art performance in both reconstruction accuracy and inference speed. The framework further generalizes to robotic manipulation and articulated scene reconstruction.

AI.tldr: MonoArt introduces a progressive structural reasoning framework for single-image articulated 3D reconstruction, transforming observations into canonical geometry, structured part representations, and motion-aware embeddings within one network, enabling stable, interpretable articulation inference without external motion templates or multi-view supervision; it achieves state-of-the-art performance and speed on PartNet-Mobility and generalizes to robotics and scenes.
AI.motivation: Direct articulation regression from a single image is unstable due to entanglement between motion cues and object structure; existing solutions rely on heavy supervision (multi-view, retrieval, or video generation) at the cost of scalability.
AI.method: A unified architecture that progressively converts visual input into canonical geometry, then into part-structured representations, and finally into motion-aware embeddings; performs structured reasoning in a single pipeline, avoiding external templates and multi-stage pipelines; end-to-end trainable.
AI.result: State-of-the-art reconstruction accuracy and inference speed on PartNet-Mobility; demonstrated generalization to robotic manipulation and articulated scene reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Progressive, structured reasoning enables stable, interpretable articulation inference for articulated 3D objects from a single image without external priors, and offers scalable applicability to real-world robotics tasks.

title: Cubic Discrete Diffusion: Discrete Visual Generation on High-Dimensional Representation Tokens
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026 main track; Code: https://github.com/YuqingWang1029/CubiD
summary: Visual generation with discrete tokens has gained significant attention as it enables a unified token prediction paradigm shared with language models, promising seamless multimodal architectures. However, current discrete generation methods remain limited to low-dimensional latent tokens (typically 8-32 dims), sacrificing the semantic richness essential for understanding. While high-dimensional pretrained representations (768-1024 dims) could bridge this gap, their discrete generation poses fundamental challenges. In this paper, we present Cubic Discrete Diffusion (CubiD), the first discrete generation model for high-dimensional representations. CubiD performs fine-grained masking throughout the high-dimensional discrete representation -- any dimension at any position can be masked and predicted from partial observations. This enables the model to learn rich correlations both within and across spatial positions, with the number of generation steps fixed at $T$ regardless of feature dimensionality, where $T \ll hwd$. On ImageNet-256, CubiD achieves state-of-the-art discrete generation with strong scaling behavior from 900M to 3.7B parameters. Crucially, we validate that these discretized tokens preserve original representation capabilities, demonstrating that the same discrete tokens can effectively serve both understanding and generation tasks. We hope this work will inspire future research toward unified multimodal architectures. Code is available at: https://github.com/YuqingWang1029/CubiD.

AI.tldr: Proposes Cubic Discrete Diffusion (CubiD), the first discrete diffusion model for high-dimensional representations, enabling fine-grained masking and generation with a fixed, small number of steps, achieving state-of-the-art results on ImageNet-256 and preserving representation utility for both understanding and generation.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between high-dimensional representations (768-1024 dims) and discrete generation, enabling unified token-based multimodal modeling while maintaining semantic richness and scalability.
AI.method: Introduce CubiD: fine-grained masking of high-dimensional discrete tokens across spatial positions; perform diffusion-like masking/reconstruction with a fixed number of steps T, independent of dimensionality; learn correlations within and across positions; evaluate on ImageNet-256; scale model size from 900M to 3.7B parameters; demonstrate tokens support both understanding and generation.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art discrete generation on ImageNet-256; shows good scaling behavior with model size; demonstrates discretized tokens preserve representation capabilities for downstream understanding and generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: CubiD enables high-dimensional discrete generation and unified multimodal architectures; provides a scalable, effective approach bridging high-dimensional representations and discrete diffusion; code released for reproducibility.

title: Matryoshka Gaussian Splatting
summary: The ability to render scenes at adjustable fidelity from a single model, known as level of detail (LoD), is crucial for practical deployment of 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Existing discrete LoD methods expose only a limited set of operating points, while concurrent continuous LoD approaches enable smoother scaling but often suffer noticeable quality degradation at full capacity, making LoD a costly design decision. We introduce Matryoshka Gaussian Splatting (MGS), a training framework that enables continuous LoD for standard 3DGS pipelines without sacrificing full-capacity rendering quality. MGS learns a single ordered set of Gaussians such that rendering any prefix, the first k splats, produces a coherent reconstruction whose fidelity improves smoothly with increasing budget. Our key idea is stochastic budget training: each iteration samples a random splat budget and optimises both the corresponding prefix and the full set. This strategy requires only two forward passes and introduces no architectural modifications. Experiments across four benchmarks and six baselines show that MGS matches the full-capacity performance of its backbone while enabling a continuous speed-quality trade-off from a single model. Extensive ablations on ordering strategies, training objectives, and model capacity further validate the designs.

AI.tldr: Matryoshka Gaussian Splatting (MGS) enables continuous level of detail (LoD) in 3D Gaussian Splatting by learning a single ordered set of Gaussians and using stochastic budget training to optimize both the prefix and full set; it delivers a continuous speed–quality trade-off without changing architecture and matches full-capacity rendering.
AI.motivation: Enable smooth, continuous LoD for 3D Gaussian Splatting while preserving full-capacity rendering quality and avoiding multiple models or architectural changes.
AI.method: Train a single ordered sequence of Gaussians; in each iteration sample a random splat budget k and optimize both the first k splats (the prefix) and the full Gaussian set. This requires only two forward passes and introduces no architectural changes, maintaining compatibility with standard 3DGS pipelines.
AI.result: MGS achieves full-capacity performance at maximum budget while providing a continuous speed-quality trade-off from a single model across four benchmarks and six baselines; ablations validate ordering, objectives, and capacity choices.
AI.conclusion: MGS provides continuous, high-quality LoD for 3D Gaussian Splatting without sacrificing full-capacity rendering, enabling efficient deployment.

title: HISR: Hindsight Information Modulated Segmental Process Rewards For Multi-turn Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: While large language models excel in diverse domains, their performance on complex longhorizon agentic decision-making tasks remains limited. Most existing methods concentrate on designing effective reward models (RMs) to advance performance via multi-turn reinforcement learning. However, they suffer from delayed propagation in sparse outcome rewards and unreliable credit assignment with potentially overly fine-grained and unfocused turnlevel process rewards. In this paper, we propose (HISR) exploiting Hindsight Information to modulate Segmental process Rewards, which closely aligns rewards with sub-goals and underscores significant segments to enhance the reliability of credit assignment. Specifically, a segment-level process RM is presented to assign rewards for each sub-goal in the task, avoiding excessively granular allocation to turns. To emphasize significant segments in the trajectory, a hindsight model is devised to reflect the preference of performing a certain action after knowing the trajectory outcome. With this characteristic, we design the ratios of sequence likelihoods between hindsight and policy model to measure action importance. The ratios are subsequently employed to aggregate segment importance scores, which in turn modulate segmental process rewards, enhancing credit assignment reliability. Extensive experimental results on three publicly benchmarks demonstrate the validity of our method.

AI.tldr: HISR uses hindsight information to modulate segmental process rewards for sub-goals in long-horizon agentic LLM tasks, improving credit assignment by emphasizing significant trajectory segments and avoiding overly granular turn-level rewards.
AI.motivation: Addresses credit assignment in long-horizon, agentic LLM decision making where sparse outcomes and overly fine-grained turn rewards hinder learning.
AI.method: Introduce a segment-level reward model that assigns rewards per sub-goal; develop a hindsight model to quantify the value of actions given the trajectory outcome; compute the ratio of sequence likelihoods between hindsight and policy to gauge action importance; aggregate segment importance to modulate segmental rewards.
AI.result: Empirical validation on three public benchmarks showing improved credit assignment reliability and enhanced performance.
AI.conclusion: HISR aligns rewards with sub-goals through hindsight-guided segment emphasis, improving credit assignment and long-horizon decision-making in LLM-based agents.
